facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on January 30, 2023 that SK Battery America (SKBA) has exceeded its hiring goal of 2,600 employees two years ahead of schedule for its two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Commerce, Georgia.
The company met its hiring goal at the end of 2022 and is further expanding its workforce to approximately 3,000 over the next year to meet the growing demand for electric vehicles.

Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that SK Battery America is one of the first companies to put Georgia on the e-mobility map and thanked them for going above and beyond in delivering on their commitments.
SK Battery America announced its first project in Georgia in 2018, with an initial planned investment of $1.6 billion.

Since 2019, the company has invested $2.6 billion to build two battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County to supply electric vehicles, including the Ford F-150 Lightning and the Volkswagen ID.4.
SKBA started mass production in early 2022.

SK Battery America CEO Timothy Jeong credited the State of Georgia’s Quick Start program for helping attract and train workers with a speed and scale that would be difficult for any company to do on its own.
More than 300,000 new electric vehicles a year will be on American roads because of the work SK Battery America is doing in Georgia.
SKBA has worked with Georgia Quick Start, the state’s top-ranked job training program through the Technical College System of Georgia, since announcing its first project in 2018.

In July 2022, SKBA announced they would partner with the Georgia National Guard’s Work for Warriors Georgia program to focus on hiring veterans, servicemembers, retirees, reservists, and their families in the pursuit of their career goals.
The company has held numerous career fairs, visited schools, and held community events in recent months.
To attract the highest-quality workforce, SKBA has consistently improved wages, benefits, and its overall work environment.

Jackson County Board of Commissioners Chairman Tom Crow expressed appreciation for SK Battery America’s commitment to provide high quality jobs within the county and their substantial financial investment.
The company is still hiring for jobs in production, quality, utility, logistics, ERT, maintenance, and other areas.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson praised SK Battery America for its out-front role in the development of the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and participation in every opportunity presented to them.
SK On is the parent company of SK Battery America and is a global leading electric vehicle battery developer, manufacturer, and solutions provider.
The Georgia facilities will have a combined capacity to make 22 GWh of battery cells a year.
The produced batteries are enough to power the equivalent of about 300,000 EVs annually.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: SK Battery America Exceeds Hiring Goal, On Track to Reach 3,000 Workers | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Following months of behind-the-scenes work by U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff, Hyundai Motor Company formally announced a historic new electric vehicle and electric vehicle battery manufacturing plant in Bryan County, Georgia.
According to Hyundai, the investment amount is $5.54 billion.
The investment will create roughly 8,100 jobs in Georgia.
Sen. Ossoff was widely credited for helping broker a deal between SK Innovation and LG in April 2021 to save an electric vehicle battery production plant in Commerce, Georgia.
According to reports, Hyundai will partner with SK to power select Hyundai EVs with Georgia-made SK batteries.
Hyundai Chairman Euisun Chung thanked Sen. Ossoff for his work and support.
Over the last year, Sen. Ossoff worked closely with Hyundai Chairman Euisun Chung and other Hyundai officials to urge further investment in Georgia and secure this deal.
The deal followed a settlement between SK Innovation and LG that saved the $2.6 billion SK electric vehicle battery plant in Commerce, Georgia.
Sen. Ossoff led an economic delegation to Seoul last November with a goal of securing additional investment in Georgia.
During the delegation, he met with Hyundai Chairman Euisun Chung and top officials, as well as other key Korean companies including SK, LG, and Hanwha.
Hanwha operates the Q Cells solar module manufacturing plant in Dalton, Georgia.
Sen. Ossoff stated that attracting Hyundai’s investment in Georgia was a key goal of his economic delegation to South Korea last fall.
He noted the multibillion-dollar opportunity will create thousands of jobs and grow Georgia’s reputation as a world leader in automotive and clean energy manufacturing.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated that Hyundai is proud to share its plan to open its first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S.
Chung noted that the U.S. holds an important place in Hyundai's global strategy and expressed excitement to partner with the State of Georgia for electrified mobility and sustainability.
He appreciated the continued support of Governor Brian Kemp, Senators Jon Ossoff and Raphael Warnock, and other Georgia elected officials.
According to Hyundai, the EV and battery manufacturing plant will be located on a dedicated 2,923-acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The site has immediate access to I-95 and I-16 highways, providing easy access to 250 major metro areas.
The site is located less than 50 kilometers from the Port of Savannah.
The Port of Savannah is the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S.
The port has two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.
West Point, Georgia, is already home to Kia’s manufacturing hub in the U.S.
Sen. Ossoff has long championed making Georgia the clean energy manufacturing hub in the United States and making the U.S. the world leader in clean energy manufacturing.
Sen. Ossoff worked personally with Hyundai Chairman Euisun Chung and top executives over the last several months to secure this investment in Georgia.
Last November, Sen. Ossoff led an economic delegation to the Republic of Korea where he met with top executives, including Hyundai Chairman Euisun Chung and other Hyundai officials.
During their meeting, Sen. Ossoff and Hyundai executives discussed further investments in clean energy technology manufacturing in Georgia.
The possibility of further investments became more attractive thanks to the SK electric vehicle battery plant in Commerce, which Sen. Ossoff helped save.

After being widely credited for helping negotiate a settlement between SK Innovation and LG last April that saved the 2,600-job electric vehicle battery plant in Commerce, Sen. Ossoff began courting additional Korean foreign direct investment in Georgia and the United States.
Hyundai and SK announced a joint venture last year.

In the weeks and months after Sen. Ossoff’s economic delegation, he and his staff remained in close contact with Hyundai officials in Korea and the U.S. to urge them to choose Georgia for their historic investment.
source_title: NEWS: Sen. Ossoff Helps Secure Historic Korean Investment in Georgia Electric Vehicle Manufacturing – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

entity_name: LG Energy Solution & Hyundai EV Battery Plant
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution-Hyundai Joint Venture EV Battery Plant
facts: U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff announced that LG Energy Solution and Hyundai will jointly build a $4.3 billion EV battery plant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The two companies will jointly invest $4.3 billion to open the new EV battery plant, which will supply Hyundai’s EV manufacturing site in Bryan County.
The investment is expected to create 3,000 jobs.
According to the companies, full-scale production at the plant is expected to be online by the end of 2025.
Sen. Ossoff announced the news on a Thursday, weeks after leading an April trade mission to South Korea where he met with senior executives from both firms.
Sen. Ossoff met again in late April with LG Energy Solution executives in Washington to discuss the deal.

Sen. Ossoff stated that securing the LG-Hyundai battery investment was a key goal of his trade delegation to South Korea and noted that the IRA’s manufacturing incentives continue to bring jobs and investment to Georgia.

Sen. Ossoff expressed his goal to make Georgia the world leader in advanced energy production and thanked LG Chairman Koo Kwang-mo, LG Energy Solutions President David Kim, Hyundai Chairman Chung Eui-sun, Senator Reverend Warnock, and local/state officials for making the announcement possible.
According to the Financial Times, Georgia ranks 2nd nationwide in new investments from IRA manufacturing incentives.

Earlier this year, Sen. Ossoff announced another $2.5 billion investment in solar manufacturing by Qcells in Dalton and Cartersville, incentivized by his solar manufacturing legislation within the IRA.
source_title: Sen. Ossoff: LG Energy Solution & Hyundai to Build $4.3 Billion EV Battery Plant in Georgia, Create 3,000 Jobs – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on March 6, 2023 that PHA will create 402 new jobs and invest more than $67 million in a new facility in Chatham County.
PHA is a global auto parts manufacturer that will supply the Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that in a single month, Georgia’s economic development community announced more than 1,900 new jobs for hardworking Georgians, with the bulk related to the Hyundai Metaplant.
PHA is a Tier-1 automotive parts supplier for Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and many other original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
PHA is a Korea-based company with locations throughout the world, including multiple facilities within the United States.
Since its establishment in 1985, PHA has grown into a global company in automotive door moving systems through research and development, innovative technology, and aggressive investment.
Sangtae Kim is Chairman of PHA.
PHA’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The new manufacturing facility will produce door modules, tailgate latches, and hood latches for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations are expected to begin in 2024.
Trip Tollison is the President and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
PHA is the second Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America supplier to locate in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was completed in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
Pat Wilson is GDEcD Commissioner.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Projects announced to date have exceeded this projection by more than $800 million.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 27,400 jobs in Georgia.
PHA operates in nine countries with more than 3,300 employees worldwide and $750 million in revenue.
source_title: Hyundai Supplier PHA to Create Over 400 Jobs in Chatham County | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced a statewide initiative to strengthen Georgia's position as a national leader in the electric mobility industry on July 20, 2021.
The initiative is driven by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).

The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) includes partners from private and public sectors ranging from government, industry, electric utilities, education, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
GDEcD launched new website assets for the initiative to advance Georgia’s role in developing the electric mobility industry and its supply chain.
Governor Kemp stated that Georgia has a proven record of investing early in resources and infrastructure needed to connect to the world and develop jobs of the future.
EMIA aims to ensure Georgia continues leading the nation in the rapidly growing electric mobility industry through innovative solutions and strategies.
The announcement references nearly $2.6 billion SK Innovation electric vehicle (EV) battery manufacturing facilities located in Jackson County.
In recent months, Georgia has attracted international investments from Dutch e-mobility charging systems leader Heliox.
Georgia has also attracted Turkish EV-parts manufacturer TEKLAS as an international investment.
Georgia has attracted German-owned lightweight automotive-body parts manufacturer GEDIA as an international investment.
Georgia has attracted SK-supplier EnChem of Korea as an international investment.
In 2018, Georgia Made school bus manufacturer Blue Bird introduced their all-electric buses.
Blue Bird recently reported its 500th delivery or sale of electric buses.
EMIA will offer policy recommendations intended to further enhance the state’s attractiveness to the electric mobility industry and foster innovation in related fields.
Innovation areas targeted by EMIA include drones, autonomous vehicles, connected vehicles, battery technology, and more.
EMIA consists of five committees that will develop a multi-step approach setting objectives for Supply Chain, Infrastructure, Workforce, Innovation, and Policy/Initiatives.
The Carl Vinson Institute at the University of Georgia will host EMIA’s committee meetings and integrated research forums.
A report by the Alliance for Automotive Innovation states automakers are planning to invest $250 billion in electrification by 2023.
IHS Markit predicts there will be 130 EV models available in the U.S. by 2026.

Georgia is positioned as the center of the southeast automotive corridor with close proximity to the region’s automotive original equipment manufacturers to capitalize on electrification trends and support the entire EV supply chain.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that EMIA will give Georgia a greater, more comprehensive strategy for the wave of electrification across industry sectors.
Wilson noted that bringing together industry leaders and public and private sector partners is proactively developing a roadmap to keep Georgia at the forefront of electric mobility and innovation.

links.context: Press Secretary Carter Chapman contact email
Deputy Press Secretary Annalise Morning contact email
Communications Manager Jessica Atwell contact email

source_title: Gov. Kemp Announces Statewide Initiative for Electric Mobility | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on January 26, 2023 that SK Battery America will open a regional IT hub facility in Roswell.
The new IT center will create 200 high-tech jobs.
SK Battery America is investing approximately $19 million over the next few years for the IT center.

Governor Brian Kemp stated Georgia is the No. 1 state for business and praised SK Battery America's continued growth, noting it brings jobs of the future that benefit Georgians in manufacturing and taps into tech talent from top-ranking universities.
SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The Jackson County facilities will supply electric vehicles such as the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen ID.4.
The SK Battery America facility is one of SK Group’s three major investments in the State of Georgia.
SK Group was founded in 1976 and is the second-largest conglomerate in South Korea.

Jason Choi, Head of Information Technology at SK Battery America, said the decision to open the IT regional center in Roswell aims to strengthen SK’s position as a leader in the battery industry and help build and operate an advanced manufacturing system per further expansion of battery production bases in the United States.
The new IT center will serve as an integrated IT management center for the company’s battery manufacturing facilities across the United States, including its Georgia facilities.
The IT center is located at 1110 Sanctuary Parkway in Roswell.
Operations at the IT center are expected to begin in January.
SK Battery America will be hiring IT experts for battery manufacturing execution system development and operations, as well as data analysts.

City of Roswell Mayor Kurt Wilson welcomed SK Battery America to the community, stating his priority since taking office a year ago has been recruiting businesses that provide high-quality job opportunities and enhance economic strength.
Mayor Kurt Wilson called Roswell the perfect location for the IT Regional Center and expressed pride in SK Battery America's choice.
Robb Pitts, Chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners, welcomed SK Battery America to Fulton County, calling it a destination for the best and brightest.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this project in partnership with the City of Roswell, Fulton County, and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that SK On established SK Battery America as its U.S. production base in 2019, and the new IT regional center is a natural result of the company’s commitment to Georgia.

Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted the ripple effects of economic development, noting suppliers, support centers, and expansions create opportunities beyond a company's original scope, location, and industry.
Electric mobility related projects have contributed more than $21 billion in investments and more than 26,700 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.

SK Battery America’s new IT regional center will support battery production operations, build on the state’s existing automotive assets and developing workforce, and help ensure a domestic supply chain for the electric mobility industry.
SK On is the parent company of SK Battery America and is a global leading electric vehicle battery developer, manufacturer, and solutions provider.
SK Battery America produces safe, fast charging, and long-lasting batteries currently installed in the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen ID.4.
The company will have a combined capacity to make 22 GWh of battery cells a year, enough to power the equivalent of about 300,000 EVs annually.
Since 2019, SK Battery America has invested a total of $2.6 billion to build EV battery plants in Commerce, Georgia, creating 2,600 high-skilled jobs.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: SK Battery America to Open U.S. IT Center, Create 200 High-Tech Jobs in Roswell | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Gov. Brian P. Kemp signed FY24 Budget (HB 19) and HB 408 on May 5, 2023, at a signing ceremony on the site of the Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County, Ellabell, GA.
Georgia has been named the number one state to do business for nine consecutive years.
Georgia's unemployment rate is 3.1 percent.
In less than 365 days prior to May 2023, four of the largest economic development projects in Georgia history were announced.
Hyundai announced a project bringing 8,100 jobs and over $5.54 billion in investment to Bryan County.
Suppliers for the Hyundai Metaplant are relocating to Georgia, collectively announcing over 4,500 new jobs and roughly $1.96 billion in investment.
The supplier investment figure is nearly double the original estimate, driven by Hyundai's decision to locate in Georgia.
Hyundai has formed a joint venture with SK On in Bartow County for an EV battery facility that will bring 3,500 new jobs and roughly $5 billion in investment to Georgia.
HB 408 extends the sales and use tax exemption for projects designated as competitive projects of regional significance.
Most communities benefiting from the HB 408 tax exemption tool are located outside metro Atlanta.
Representative Bruce Williamson introduced HB 408, with co-sponsors Representatives Shaw Blackmon and Matt Gambill, and Senator Chuck Hufstetler carried it in the Senate.
FY24 Budget (HB 19) funds economic development priorities and aims to maintain Georgia's status as the No. 1 state for business.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Signs FY24 Budget to Invest in Public Safety, Healthcare, the Workforce, and More | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Shinsung Petrochemical will invest $11.2 million in a new manufacturing facility in Toombs County.
The investment will create more than 30 new jobs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment on September 25, 2024.
Eighty-three percent of expansions and new locations from the past fiscal year are located in rural communities.
These rural expansions delivered more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment to Georgians.
Shinsung was established in 1974.
The company specializes in products like automotive sealant.
Shinsung will be a key supplier for Hyundai Motor Group and the Metaplant in Bryan County.
Construction on Shinsung’s new manufacturing facility is expected to finish in 2025.
The facility is located at 1407 Highway 1 North in Lyons, within the Toombs Corporate Center at U.S. Highway 1.
Tim Truxel, Chair of the Toombs County Development Authority, commented on the transformation of the industrial park over the last few years.
Lyons Mayor Willis NeSmith expressed excitement about the investment to the Toombs Corporate Center at U.S. Highway 1 in Lyons.
David Sikes, Chair of the Board of Commissioners of Toombs County, welcomed Shinsung Petrochemical to the county.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Toombs County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Power.
Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted historic generational investments in automotive and other key industries in Georgia over the last few years.
Since 2018, Georgia has attracted more than 36,000 new jobs and over $28 billion in investments in e-mobility, clean energy, and battery-related supply chains.
For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices and positioned itself as a leader in developing emerging technologies for the mobility and energy industries.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Automotive Supplier Invests $11.2 Million in Toombs County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Sewon America is a subsidiary of Korean-based Sewon Precision Industry Co.
Sewon America will create 740 new jobs in Rincon.
Sewon America will invest more than $300 million in a new Effingham County manufacturing facility.
This project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon to-date.
The announcement fulfills the commitment to create jobs and investment in all four Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority (JDA) counties.
Hyundai's Metaplant location in Bryan County is described as the largest economic development project in state history.
Suppliers for the Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment, surpassing initial estimates by at least $800 million.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the project on February 21, 2023.
Governor Kemp discussed continuing to work with Sewon America during his first international trip as governor to Korea.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns represents part of Effingham County and played a role in securing the project.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones stated the investment builds on Georgia's commitment to remain the No. 1 state in which to do business while attracting a robust workforce.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Following the completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
Vice Chairman Kim stated Sewon America was founded 15 years ago in west Georgia and is building another facility in east Georgia.
Sewon’s new facility will be located at the Grande View industrial park in Rincon.
The new manufacturing location will produce electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations are expected to begin in 2025.

Rincon Mayor Ken Lee welcomed Sewon America to the community and commended the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority for bringing the largest employment and private investment project in the city’s history.
Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (IDA) Troy Smith stated the IDA selected Grande View industrial park as the best location for Sewon's significant investment.

Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority Griff Lynch expressed excitement to support Sewon’s growth and noted the Hyundai announcement continues to bring new economic opportunities to Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD partnered with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC for this project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted Sewon has integrated into and lent its own industry experience to Georgia’s automotive community through local colleges and Georgia Quick Start.
On October 25, 2022, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
Sewon America was established in 2008 in LaGrange as a Georgia company.
Sewon primarily operates in the Motor Vehicles and Car Bodies industry within the Transportation Equipment sector, producing stamped body components.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Sewon America Announces 740 New Jobs in Effingham County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: In May 2022, Hyundai Motor Group announced it was investing $5.4 billion to build a smart factory dedicated to making electric vehicles in Georgia.
The facility will be built on a 2,900-acre megasite in Bryan County, located just a 30-minute drive from the downtown historic district of Savannah, Ga.
The project agreement signed by State of Georgia officials and Hyundai executives will create more than 8,000 jobs in the region.
This project is considered the largest economic development project in the state’s history.
Georgia Quick Start is committed to providing customized workforce training for Hyundai, affiliated companies, and supplier companies.
Georgia Quick Start will design, build, and operate an 80,000-sq. ft. training center directly adjacent to the new facility.
Georgia Quick Start will construct a second building at its Advanced Manufacturing Training Center in Pooler, Georgia, to support training for supplier companies.
The project involves overcoming unknown challenges as this type of megaproject had never been done before.
Hyundai’s “Metaplant” concept is a vision of mobility where journeys are measured by more than just mileage.
Georgia Quick Start is training future General Assembly Meta Pros to be ready for the production floor on day one.
The Georgia Quick Start team visited South Korea and spent days in discussion with Hyundai Motor Group to develop company-specific training ahead of the megaproject.
The facility will include the Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia, where thousands of future Meta Pros and supplier employees will be trained.
Hyundai is partnering with dozens of suppliers for the project, including Mobis, which is receiving customized training from Quick Start.
Georgia Quick Start continues its partnership with HYUNDAI TRANSYS, which will supply powertrains and seating systems to both Kia in West Point, Ga., and HMGMA.
Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated that Hyundai’s mission is “progress for humanity” and part of a shared vision for a brighter, more innovative future.
source_title: Strong Currents - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Located in Pooler, Georgia.
Designs, develops, and delivers customized, company-specific workforce training for qualified companies investing and creating jobs in Georgia.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: 'It’s impressive. I think this facility just continues to give us something that we can offer to really any kind of company that would want to come do advanced manufacturing in Georgia.'
The Advanced Manufacturing Training Center was built in 2015 on a site just outside Savannah, Georgia, directly astride Interstate 16.
Built to address the vexing issue facing companies today: finding the skilled workforce able to perform successfully in an increasingly complex manufacturing environment.
In 2022, the largest economic development project in the state’s history was announced.

The center is currently providing training for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and other companies locating to supply HMGMA’s operation with parts needed to assemble electric vehicles and new products yet to come as the company pioneers future mobility solutions.

Dedicated to supporting any qualified new or expanding company, continuing to provide training for distribution operations, food manufacturers, and the wide range of industries that make up the robust powerhouse that is the Coastal Empire.
Hyundai Transys is currently training at the Advanced Manufacturing Training Center, focusing on one of the important aspects of automobile choice: comfort.
Building a state-of-the-art 90,000-sq.ft. training center to help prepare the Meta Pros of tomorrow, as well as Hyundai affiliates.
Quick Start and McKnight Construction Company celebrated the 'topping off' of the steel frame following a tradition dating back to the 600s that is still honored today.
The design of the Quick Start Advanced Manufacturing Training Center pays homage to the spirit of Historic Savannah while meeting the needs of a 21st century smart factory.
Expanding to make way for new trainees and new technologies as Georgia becomes a leader in clean technology.
Develops company-specific training by conducting project studies complete with detailed analysis of materials, technology, and corporate culture.
source_title: Transforming the Coastal Empire - Georgia Quick Start

facts: La Regina Atlantica hosted a training plan signing on January 13, 2026 at 10 a.m. to commemorate its partnership with Georgia Quick Start and Coastal Pines Technical College.
Over the past six years, the family-owned company has grown its Georgia facility to 103 employees.
La Regina Atlantica plans to hire up to 50 more employees in the coming years.
The company manufactures Rao’s homestyle pasta sauce at its manufacturing facility in Alma, Georgia.
Georgia Quick Start is the state’s top-ranked workforce training program and will provide customized training for the manufacturer.
La Regina Atlantica announced its Georgia facility in Alma in 2020.
Scott McMurray, Deputy Commissioner at Georgia Quick Start, stated they are honored to support a company that chose Georgia for its first-ever US production facility.
The partnership extends all the way from Italy to South Georgia to the shelves of local supermarkets.
In addition to safety training, Quick Start will provide job-specific training on how to operate specialized machines that combine fresh ingredients to create sauces.

Emidio Rinaldi, COO of La Regina Atlantica, stated they look forward to beginning training with Quick Start and that the support will ensure growth while maintaining safety standards and family brand recipe standards.
The training program involves three distinct partners: Georgia Quick Start, La Regina Atlantica, and Coastal Pines Technical College.
Coastal Pines Technical College will serve as a long-term partner for ongoing training and provide a pipeline of qualified graduates to meet future workforce needs.

Lonnie Roberts, President of Coastal Pines Technical College, stated they are eager to partner with companies committed to growing Georgia’s workforce and that the collaboration reflects a shared commitment to quality through skilled people.

The Bacon County Development Authority and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) worked with Coastal Pines Technical College and Georgia Quick Start to qualify La Regina Atlantica for training support.
Quick Start training is scheduled to begin in early 2026.
source_title: La Regina Agreement Signing - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Bojangles has partnered with Alyath EV, Smart Big Box, and Energy and Environmental Design Services to install turnkey electric vehicle (EV) charging stations at most of its locations.
The initiative represents a first-of-its-kind deployment in the quick-service restaurant (QSR) industry.
The new charging stations will cater to the growing number of EV drivers seeking reliable charging options while on the go.
Charging station speeds will vary between level two (medium) and level three (high) efficiency with over 97% uptime.
Each restaurant location will feature a minimum of four charging spots.

Richard Del Valle, Chief Information Officer at Bojangles, stated that working with Alyath and Smart Big Box introduces a new convenience aligning with evolving customer needs and that the top-of-the-line EV chargers will improve guest accessibility and help drive an eco-friendlier future.

Cristiane Rosul, CEO of Alyath, stated that the partnership brings fast, reliable, and convenient EV charging to Bojangles locations nationwide and positions the chain as a leader in the future of quick-service dining.
Smart Big Box has contracted Energy and Environmental Design Services as the exclusive installer and maintenance partner for all EV chargers to ensure optimal performance and industry-leading uptime.
The rollout of EV charging stations at Bojangles will begin in Q4 of 2025.
Fans can expect the EV charging stations to be largely accessible in 2026.

Bojangles is a Carolina-born restaurant chain specializing in Southern food, founded in 1977 as a single location in Charlotte, N.C., and currently operates around 800 company-owned and franchised restaurants.

Alyath is building one of the fastest-growing electric vehicle charging networks in the U.S., focusing on high-demand locations including city centers, highways, retail hubs, and commercial properties.
Alyath has deployments underway at over 5,500 private sites and aims to build America's largest and most reliable EV charging network while adhering to sustainability and ESG principles.

Smart Big Box delivers outcome-driven solutions that enhance efficiency and reduce energy consumption through a holistic, integrated approach connecting strategic partners and leveraging cutting-edge technologies.

links.context: Media contact email for Katie Combs at Luquire
Media contact phone number for Katie Combs at Luquire
Media contact email for Stacey McCray at Bojangles
Media contact phone number for Stacey McCray at Bojangles

source_title: Bojangles Partners With Alyath to Bring Electric Vehicle Charging Stations to Most Locations

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Daesol Ausys Georgia will establish a new manufacturing facility in West Point.
The facility will create more than 140 new jobs.
The project represents $72 million in investment in Harris County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that West Point has become an automotive capital in its own right.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed excitement that Daesol Ausys will join other innovators in Harris County.
Georgia’s automotive industry currently employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the state’s automotive industry will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for future jobs.
Daesol Ausys was established in Korea in 2017.
The company specializes in designing and manufacturing motor and electric vehicle interior components and accessories.
Daesol Ausys is a key supplier for Kia Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group, and General Motors.
Min Ho Kwon serves as the CEO of Daesol Ausys.
Min Ho Kwon stated the company is committed to delivering world-class services that exceed client requirements and expectations.
The new facility will be located in the Northwest Harris Business Park.
Min Ho Kwon expressed excitement about the project and looks forward to continuing the partnership with the State of Georgia and Harris County.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in December 2024.
The facility will produce luggage boards and luggage covers.
The company will be hiring for positions in production, purchasing, quality control, maintenance, human resources, and management.
Robert Grant serves as the Chairman of the Harris County Commission.
Robert Grant stated that Daesol Ausys’ decision to locate in the Northwest Harris Business Park represents a commitment to Harris County.
West Point and Harris County have a wonderful relationship with Daesol Ausys’ sister companies Daehan Solutions and Daesol Materials.
Local officials have been working closely with company officials to bring the new venture to West Point.
Craig Greenhaw serves as the Chairman of the Harris County Development Authority.
Craig Greenhaw extended a welcome to Daesol Ausys and expressed excitement over a long and mutually beneficial partnership with the company.
West Point and Harris County continue to work well together on successful ventures that bring jobs and capital investment into the region.
Alyce Thornhill is the Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team.
The project was a competitive effort developed in partnership with the Harris County Commission, Harris County Development Authority, City of West Point, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that economic development is a team sport driven by the local level.
Pat Wilson congratulated Harris County and community partners for helping bring Daesol Ausys to West Georgia.
The automotive industry is providing generational change in the region and across the state.
Pat Wilson expressed excitement over the continuation of growth and the opportunities Daesol Ausys will provide to the people of Harris County.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the market transitions to electric vehicles, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Daesol Ausys Georgia is a leading manufacturer of automotive components and a key supplier of luggage boards and luggage covers.
The company is committed to exceeding customer expectations with award-winning products and services through innovation and efficiency.
The company has a long history as one of the top suppliers to Kia and Hyundai worldwide.
Coach Robert (Rob) M. Grant Sr. serves as a Harris Co. Commissioner for District 2.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Automotive Supplier Daesol Ausys Georgia to Invest $72 Million in Harris County - Harris County, Georgia

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On Joint Venture for Bartow County EV Battery Facility
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and SK On have selected a site in Bartow County for a new electric vehicle (EV) battery manufacturing facility.
The new facility will supply Hyundai Motor Group's plants in the U.S.
Stakeholders estimate the project will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4-5 billion of investment in Bartow County.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) regarding the partnership for a new EV battery facility in the U.S.
Details of the partnership are still in development.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia's ever-growing automotive industry.

Governor Brian Kemp noted his administration has focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to overlooked communities and expressed pride in the companies investing further in the No. 1 state for business.
SK On was established in 2021 as the lithium-ion battery subsidiary branch of SK Innovation.
SK On currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce.
In July, SK Battery America announced a partnership with the Work for Warriors Georgia program to hire veterans, servicemembers, and their families at its Georgia operations.
The new facility's construction is subject to execution of relevant agreements and final board decisions by HMG and SK On.
The facility will be located at Bartow Centre, a zoned manufacturing and industrial site on Highway 411.
The companies are aiming to begin operations in 2025.
Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed pleasure with the decision and thanked the owners of the former Bartow Centre Industrial property.

Commissioner Steve Taylor stated the project will have regional benefit for job seekers and establish Bartow in Georgia's EV ecosystem, noting Bartow's strong history and talent base in advanced manufacturing and innovation.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project involved partnerships with Georgia EMC, Development Authority of Bartow County, Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated HMG and SK have been pioneering partners for Georgia for decades as major drivers for the state's automotive renaissance and as the first Korean manufacturer to locate in the state, respectively.

Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that supporting cooperation across the EV ecosystem is creating a fully integrated supply chain for automotive OEMs while connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers to close the loop on battery manufacturing.
Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed excitement for the future jobs this will create for Bartow County and northwest Georgia, and gratitude for community and utility partner support.
Georgia's prime location, major ports system, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and pro-business climate have made it attractive for sustainable industries.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric transportation industry.
Excluding this announcement, EV-related projects announced in Georgia since 2020 total approximately $17 billion in investment and more than 22,800 new jobs.
Hyundai Motor Group separately announced in May plans to invest $5.54 billion in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, an EV manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on the Metaplant America facility in October with Governor Kemp, other state leaders, and local and federal officials.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise that has created a value chain based on mobility, steel, construction, logistics, finance, IT, and service.
Hyundai Motor Group has about 250,000 employees worldwide.
Hyundai Motor Group's mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
SK On is a global leading electric vehicle (EV) battery developer, manufacturer, and solutions provider.
SK On aims to become a world leader in the clean energy industry by leveraging its global production base and R&D capabilities, as well as production and quality management know-how.
SK On was launched as an independent company in 2021 after SK Innovation, South Korea's largest energy company, split off its battery business.
SK On is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea, with a worldwide presence of battery plants currently operating or under construction across the United States, Europe, and Asia.

links.context: More information about Hyundai Motor Group

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On To Build EV Battery Facility in Bartow County

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) celebrated the one-year anniversary of its groundbreaking in Bryan County on Oct. 25, 2023.
The company officially released the latest design renderings of the electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility, also known as the Metaplant.
Oscar Kwon is the CEO of HMGMA.
Production at the facility is on track to start in early 2025, or possibly sooner.
José Muñoz is a colleague of Oscar Kwon at HMGMA who commented on the production timeline.
Approximately 2,000 construction workers are working at the nearly 3,000-acre megasite.
Construction has already begun on all HMGMA production-related buildings.
99.9 percent of all foundation work is complete.
An estimated 81 percent of steel framing has been installed, using 27,045 tons of steel.
Roof, floor, and wall construction have begun and are in varying stages of completion.
Architects designed the Metaplant to incorporate technologically advanced elements and features representing Hyundai's brand identity, the greater Savannah area, nature, and renewable energy.
The exhibition hall at the front of the plant was designed as a square to pay homage to historic squares in downtown Savannah.
The building will feature an exhibition space with its own central courtyard and water features for artful exhibitions and tour meetings.
All work zones, from office to production areas, are designed to encourage communication and team collaboration.
Designs will feature open layouts with ample daylight and indoor landscaping.
Production areas such as the Assembly Building will have respite zones with naturally lit skylights at the center for employee breaks and meetings.
A solar parking lot at the front of the site will provide 5.2 megawatts of energy.
Solar panels will be positioned over the top of 1,878 vehicle spots to protect vehicles from direct sun while generating power.
Beginning with mass production, electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent renewable sources.
This aligns with Hyundai's commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
The 3,050-foot-long front exterior of the building will be lit in two parallel lines inspired by a stream.
The lighting will outline an area where interstate travelers can view vehicles as they travel by conveyor from paint to general assembly.
A 41-acre ecological park will be prominently featured in front of the Metaplant, including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, picnic areas, and a pond for employees.
HMGMA is hiring for all positions from production to administrative roles.
The company has currently hired 290 employees (referred to as Meta Pros), consisting of 182 salary and 108 hourly workers.
An additional 53 South Korean expatriates are assisting with initiating operations.
At full capacity, 8,500 employees will be employed on-site between HMGMA, the battery JV, and on-site suppliers.
In total, 14,476 people will be employed between the Metaplant campus and all off-site suppliers.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group's first dedicated Electric Vehicle mass-production plant located in Bryan County, GA.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The Georgia facility will operate as an intelligent manufacturing plant where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production are optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise with about 250,000 employees worldwide.
Hyundai Motor Group's mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Celebrates Anniversary of Groundbreaking

facts: U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff celebrated the start of production for Kia’s first American-made electric vehicle in West Point, Georgia.
The production start occurred less than two years after the passage of manufacturing policies championed by Sen. Ossoff.
In July 2023, one year after the passage of domestic manufacturing policies, Kia announced it would build its first American EV production line in Georgia.
A Kia representative confirmed that the company plans to ensure EV9s produced in Georgia qualify for incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act.
The Inflation Reduction Act incentivizes domestic production of electric vehicles and advanced energy technologies.

Sen. Ossoff stated: “I congratulate KIA Georgia on this huge milestone reached less than two years after the enactment of made-in-America manufacturing incentives we passed into law. I thank all at the state, local, and Federal levels who have for years supported KIA’s success in our state. Georgians are proud these beautiful cars are produced in Georgia and made in America.”
Sen. Ossoff cautioned: “I caution those who have called for the repeal of made-in-America manufacturing policies: repealing these incentives will hurt Georgia.”
Former President Trump and some Georgia politicians have supported the repeal of the made-in-American manufacturing incentives.
The made-in-American manufacturing incentives are intended to make Georgia-produced electric vehicles more affordable for consumers.
According to a recent study, Georgia has benefitted more than any other state from the Federal manufacturing incentives.
source_title: U.S. Federal Manufacturing Incentives Working for Georgia: KIA Begins EV9 Production in West Point – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Sewon America will create 740 new jobs in Rincon.
Sewon America will invest more than $300 million in a new Effingham County manufacturing facility.
This project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon to-date.
The announcement fulfills the commitment to create jobs and investment in all four Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority (JDA) counties.
Hyundai's Metaplant location in Bryan County is the largest economic development project in state history.
Suppliers for the Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment.
The suppliers' investment commitment surpasses initial estimates by at least $800 million.
Governor Brian Kemp congratulated Chairman Kim on his company's recent expansion during his first international trip as governor to Korea and discussed continuing to work together.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns represents part of Effingham County and played a role in securing the project and engaging Sewon America executives.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended Governor Kemp, Sewon America, and stakeholders on the historic economic development announcement for Rincon.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Following the completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
Vice Chairman Kim stated that Sewon America was founded 15 years ago in west Georgia and is excited to build another facility in east Georgia.
The growth is possible thanks to full support from Governor Kemp, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Effingham County.
Sewon's new facility will be located at the Grande View industrial park in Rincon.
The new manufacturing location will produce electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2025.

Rincon Mayor Ken Lee welcomed Sewon America and commended the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority for bringing the largest employment and private investment project in the city's history.
Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (IDA) Troy Smith stated that Grande View industrial park was selected as the best location for significant investment.
The investment will allow current and future generations to have fulfilling, well-paying careers without leaving Effingham County.
Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority Griff Lynch called the announcement terrific news for Effingham County and the State of Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD worked in partnership with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Sewon has integrated into and lent its industry experience to Georgia’s automotive community through working with local colleges and Georgia Quick Start.
Sewon is reinvesting in Georgia as they support Hyundai Motor Group’s operations from Effingham County, which has the skilled workforce, infrastructure, and collaborative spirit to support growth.
On October 25, 2022, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
Sewon America was established in 2008 in LaGrange as a Georgia company.
Sewon primarily operates in the Motor Vehicles and Car Bodies industry within the Transportation Equipment sector, producing stamped body components.
Sewon Group is celebrated for high-quality automotive parts and will continue its tradition of excellence in the new Rincon facility.

links.context: Press contact for Governor Brian P. Kemp
Deputy Press Secretary for Governor Brian P. Kemp
Communications Manager for Georgia Department of Economic Development

source_title: Sewon America Announces 740 New Jobs in Effingham County | Georgia

facts: A Norwegian battery upstart could receive more than $358 million in grants, tax breaks and other inducements from state and local governments to build its planned Coweta County factory where it will build giant energy storage systems for utilities and other clients.
The state and local incentives offered to Freyr Battery are detailed in documents obtained by The Atlanta Journal-Constitution through the Georgia Open Records Act.
Freyr announced plans in November for a $2.57 billion factory in Coweta where it employ 724 workers making energy storage systems and lithium-ion batteries.
Freyr ranks among the legion of companies moving to Georgia to produce electrified products, be it electric vehicles or batteries, a priority among state leaders.

Freyr told the AJC that the state’s education system and ability to recruit workers capable of thriving in the burgeoning clean-tech sector were the deciding factors when choosing Georgia over more than two dozen states for its first North American factory.
The package of incentives offered by Georgia and Coweta leaders helped tip the scales for Freyr's location decision.
Much of the global supply chain for batteries and EV components is based in China.

The Biden administration has pumped billions of dollars into grants, tax credits and other federal incentives to entice companies into establishing battery and EV manufacturing in the U.S. as part of ambitious goals to fight climate change, create jobs and reduce the nation's dependency on its geopolitical rival.
Local and state governments have clamored to win these corporate investments, dangling billions more in property tax breaks and other freebies.

State and local incentives are a common though controversial currency in the war for jobs, with critics saying the inducements come at the expense of existing residents and businesses who have to shoulder expenses from supporting these new industries.

Greg LeRoy, executive director of left-leaning incentives watchdog Good Jobs First, said he worries Coweta County residents will have to cover local government costs incurred by Freyr’s new factory until the tax abatement ends.

Greg LeRoy stated: “You’re going to have to hire more teachers, pick up more trash, widen more lanes and answer more 911 calls,” adding that if the company isn’t paying for a share of those induced costs, then everybody else gets stuck with the bill.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, said Freyr represents a new niche within the electric battery and vehicle ecosystem and helps diversify the state’s fast-growing sector.

Pat Wilson stated: “You have a great company with a technology that is vital to this transition to electrification,” noting it is a battery sector that Georgia and the U.S. as a whole currently do not have.
Georgia’s increasing talent pool cemented Coweta County as Freyr’s final choice, according to Freyr CEO Tom Einar Jensen.

Freyr CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated: “The most important factor for selecting our first US plant location was talent,” and that the total package of financial incentives for Giga America played an important role in the decision.
The single largest component of the Freyr inducements were about $228 million in local property tax breaks that will stretch over 20 years.
Coweta County offered Freyr a $20 million grant.
The state agreed to provide nearly $90 million in sales tax exemptions for qualified machinery to be installed in the factory and construction materials used in building the plant.
Nearly $11 million in tax savings will come from state tax credits granted for new jobs created.
A $7 million state grant will offset land costs.

The Freyr incentive package could grow in value over time, with no value assigned to other potential tax breaks for clean room equipment, energy used in manufacturing and a handful of other incentives based on future expenditures.
The sprawling Freyr factory, which will be called Giga America, will rise near Amazon and Shopify distribution centers of I-85 near Newnan.
Sarah Jacobs, president of the Coweta County Development Authority, said Freyr will help add some industrial diversity to an area flush with logistics and shipping companies.
Within the incentive package offer, Freyr will need to employ 724 workers at the new factory with an average salary of more than $60,000.
Sarah Jacobs noted that the average salary is 127% of the average wage in Coweta.
Sarah Jacobs stated: “Those are exactly the kind of jobs we want to attract to the county.”
Coweta County and Arizona were the final two options Freyr considered for its factory location.
The Freyr incentives are large but pale by comparison to inducements offered for EV factories like Hyundai Motor Group ($1.8 billion) and Rivian ($1.5 billion).
Greg LeRoy questioned why Georgia is willing to financially incentivize more job creation when unemployment remains near record-lows.
Greg LeRoy stated: “The vast majority of job takers will be people coming from elsewhere, even people moving into the labor market.”
Greg LeRoy said it’ll be nearly impossible for each new Freyr employee to generate enough local spending and tax revenue to justify the incentives averaging roughly $490,000 per job.
Freyr was founded in 2018 and is also building a plant in Norway.
Freyr’s energy storage systems use lithium-ion batteries to store electricity from renewable forms of energy, such as wind and solar.
Freyr, publicly traded since last year, reported a $93.9 million net loss in the third quarter.
The company and project are supported by Koch Industries, a company better known for fossil fuels and leaders who have funded groups that questioned climate change.
Koch has emerged as a major player in battery technology, and the project will benefit from incentives in President Joe Biden’s signature climate bill, the Inflation Reduction Act.
The 870,000-square-foot facility is expected to open in 2026, according to the incentives package.
Freyr’s first phase will focus on initial battery cell production, and a second phase of cell production is expected to be ready by 2029.
Pat Wilson said Freyr will attract companies that produce battery parts such as cathodes and anodes.
Pat Wilson stated: “This gives us a great opportunity to go recruit those pieces as well.”

On Wednesday relative to the publication date, Qcells confirmed a $2.5 billion expansion of Georgia production in what officials called the largest ever investment in clean energy manufacturing in U.S. history.

The total value of Freyr's incentives is $358.5 million, including approximately $228 million in local property tax breaks over 20 years, nearly $90 million in sales tax exemptions on machinery and construction materials, a $20 million local quality job creation grant, $11 million in state quality job creation credits, a $7 million Regional Economic Business Assistance (REBA) grant, and $3.3 million in Georgia Quick Start funding.
The total incentive value does not include certain incentives based on future potential expenditures.
source_title: Georgia Offered $358M in Incentives for EV Battery Factory Bid

facts: On May 20, 2021, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Korean automotive manufacturer Duckyang will open its first U.S. facility in Jackson County.
Duckyang will invest $10 million in opening the new manufacturing facility.
The new facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton, Georgia.
The facility will be located at 984 Broadway Avenue in Braselton.
Duckyang’s new facility will be 230,000-square-foot.
Duckyang is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
Duckyang supplies cockpit modules to customers around the world including Hyundai, Kia, Renault Samsung Motors, Genesis, and Mobis.
Cockpit modules are integrated auto parts comprised of more than 130 subcomponents found near the driver’s seat of a vehicle.
Duckyang is an electric mobility parts supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America (SKBA).
By opening a facility in Georgia, Duckyang will be able to better serve several of its automotive customers within the region.

Dong-in Son, CEO of Duckyang Ind. Co. Ltd., stated that entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA will allow cooperation with other automotive companies like Hyundai and Kia Motors that already have a manufacturing base in the U.S.

Duckyang expects business expansion opportunities to supply not only electric vehicle parts but also interior parts, including cockpit modules, door trim panel modules, and other flagship parts to global automakers based in the United States.
The company will be hiring for positions in production, maintenance, logistics, and warehouse management.
Town Manager and Clerk of Braselton Jennifer Scott stated that the town looks forward to welcoming Duckyang to their community.

John Scott, Director of Economic Development for the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce, stated that companies like Duckyang are helping Jackson County and Georgia secure a place in the automotive industry's future as it moves toward electric vehicles.

Yoonie Kim, Director of Korean Investment, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on this project in partnership with the Jackson County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia is focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain and using relationships with SK Battery America and Korean companies like Duckyang to secure local supply of needed parts for their customers.
During 2020 alone, Georgia facilitated more than $1.3 billion in investment and more than 2,700 jobs from South Korean companies.
Duckyang Industrial was established in July 1977 and is the first company to develop and produce automobile cockpit modules in Korea.
Duckyang solidified its position through a strategic alliance with Visteon, an American global automotive supplier that was spun off from Ford in 1999.
Duckyang plans to expand production capabilities for interior automotive parts in addition to electronics in the future as it continuously promotes research and development on new technologies.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Announces Korean Automotive Manufacturer Duckyang to Open Company's First U.S. Facility in Jackson County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: The U.S. electric vehicle (EV) industry is undergoing a transformative phase, driven by federal incentives, supply chain reconfiguration, and a push for domestic manufacturing dominance.
Rivian Automotive's $5 billion Georgia EV plant is a $6.6 billion federal loan-backed project poised to redefine the company's trajectory and the broader EV supply chain.
The facility is supported by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
Rivian's Georgia plant is a cornerstone of its strategy to scale production and achieve profitability in a fiercely competitive market.
The facility is expected to produce 400,000 R2 and R3 vehicles annually by 2030.
The plant is designed to reduce reliance on global supply chains.
According to a report by the U.S. Department of Energy, the plant's construction is supported by a conditional loan of $5.98 billion in principal and $592 million in capitalized interest.
This funding aligns with the IRA's goals to incentivize clean energy production and reduce foreign dependency, particularly in critical components like battery cells and semiconductors.
The company has engaged local Georgia suppliers and technical colleges to co-locate operations near the plant.
Co-locating operations with local suppliers and technical colleges is creating nearly 8,000 indirect jobs.
Rivian plans to transition battery cell production to Arizona by 2027.
The transition to Arizona aims to mitigate risks from international trade policies and tariffs.
These moves reflect a broader industry trend of automakers diversifying sourcing strategies to meet IRA requirements.
IRA requirements mandate that 60% of battery components be manufactured in North America to qualify for tax credits.
The Georgia plant is emblematic of the U.S. manufacturing renaissance, fueled by the IRA's $132 billion in clean energy and EV-related investments since 2022.
Georgia has emerged as a hub for EV production, with 22 facilities representing $24.4 billion in private investment and 26,400 jobs.
Rivian's $1.5 billion in state incentives are tied to 7,500 direct jobs and $56,000 average annual wages.
The IRA's tax credits and loans have spurred collaboration among automakers and suppliers.
Rivian's $5.8 billion joint venture with Volkswagen Group provides access to shared supplier networks and technological integration.
This partnership reduces component costs and enhances supply chain resilience.
This partnership mirrors broader industry trends, such as Honda and LG New Energy's Ohio battery plant and Ford's Michigan lithium iron phosphate facility.
These facilities aim to meet IRA sourcing thresholds.
Despite its strategic advantages, Rivian's Georgia project faces headwinds from tariffs on non-North American parts.
Tariffs have added up to $10,000 per vehicle in production costs.
This prompted the company to revise its 2025 delivery guidance downward.
Rivian has responded by streamlining manufacturing processes.
The company eliminated 100 battery assembly steps and reduced body shop components by 50%.
These changes aim to cut costs by $22,600 per vehicle.
The company's Q1 2025 free cash flow remains negative at $526 million.
This underscores the financial risks of scaling production.
Environmental concerns and local opposition pose challenges to the project.
Residents near the Georgia plant have raised alarms about groundwater contamination risks.
