facts: Rivian has announced construction of a $120m supplier park attached to its EV manufacturing plant in Illinois.
Construction of the supplier park is expected to be completed by the end of 2026.
Expansion of Rivian's main manufacturing plant in Normal, Illinois is already underway.
The supplier park will be located a short distance from the main manufacturing plant in Normal, Illinois.
The supplier park is expected to host several of Rivian's key suppliers.
Co-locating suppliers aims to drastically reduce timelines and costs associated with logistics, especially shipping and warehousing.
Nearshoring production, including parts and materials for its EVs, will improve supply chain resilience through domestic production.
Nearshoring will also increase the speed at which units can be produced.
The supplier park is intended to reduce supply chain costs and improve efficiency for Rivian, an EV-only OEM based in the US.
Rivian claims the supplier park will be a “key enabler” to its operations as it begins manufacture of the R2 van.
Rivian founder and CEO RJ Scaringe stated that the supplier park will be a “key enabler” in increasing the plant’s output in 2026 as product lines diversify.
source_title: Rivian invests $120m in EV supplier park at US plant | Automotive Logistics

facts: Hyundai Motor Group promised its electric vehicle manufacturing facility near Savannah would be the “factory of the future.”
The manufacturing campus is located in Ellabell, near Savannah.
The South Korean automaker debuted what it calls the Metaplant to the public on Wednesday.
Robots both big and small stamped, welded and assembled autos at the facility.
EVs moved about the shop floor on self-propelled dollies that use GPS and motion sensors for navigation.
Four-legged robots that resembled medium-sized dogs inspected the work.
Employees, known as Meta Pros, monitored equipment or performed tasks too tactile for the machines to handle.
The peek inside was part of the ceremonial grand opening for the $7.59 billion manufacturing campus.
The facility is the largest economic development project in Georgia state history.
The facility is projected to employ 8,500 at full build out.
Of the projected employees, 2,600 will be building automobiles.
The assembly facility spans 16 million square feet.
The facility is designed to produce up to 500,000 vehicles per year.
The annual production capacity was revised from 300,000 at Wednesday’s event.
source_title: Peek inside Hyundai’s new electric vehicle ‘metaplant’ in Georgia

facts: Automotive supplier Magna International is investing over $790 million to build three facilities in Tennessee.
Two of the three facilities will be part of Ford’s BlueOval City supplier park, state officials announced on Thursday.
The three facilities will create approximately 1,300 new jobs.
Approximately 1,050 of the new jobs will be at a battery enclosures facility and a new seating plant in Stanton, where the supplier park is located.
Approximately 250 of the new jobs will be at a stamping and assembly plant in Lawrenceburg.
The Stanton facilities will supply the $5.6 billion BlueOval City campus with battery enclosures, truck frames and seats for Ford’s second-generation electric truck efforts, nicknamed “Project T3.”
One Stanton site will include an 800,000-square foot frame and battery enclosures facility.
Magna already produces battery enclosures for Ford’s electric F-150 in Ontario, Canada.
The second Stanton facility will span 140,000 square feet and produce polyurethane foam, and assemble and sequence just-in-time complete seats.
Production at all three sites is expected to begin in 2025.
The production start is in time for the automaker to begin production on its Project T3.
Ford plans to produce 500,000 EV pickup trucks yearly at its BlueOval City metaplant.
Magna is the first Ford supplier to be added to the automaker’s upcoming supplier park, Tennessee Gov. Bill Lee said in a statement.

Stuart McWhorter, commissioner of the Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development, stated that the $790 million investment from Magna will further shape the economic landscape of Lawrence and Haywood counties and strengthen the electric vehicle supply chain in Tennessee for years to come.
Ford has been making big investments over the past year to facilitate future EV production.
In addition to the upcoming metaplant in Tennessee, Ford is also building a $3.5 billion EV plant in Marshall, Michigan.
Ford is investing $1.3 billion to transform its Oakville Assembly Complex in Ontario, Canada, into an EV production facility.
Automakers have been building large EV campuses in the U.S. to meet the rising demand, and bringing their supplier facilities with them.
Hyundai is building a $5.5 billion EV metacampus in Ellabell, Georgia.
Hyundai is also building a $4.3 billion battery cell plant with LG Energy nearby in Georgia.
Honda is retooling three of its existing Ohio plants to produce EVs.
Honda is adding a $3.5 billion battery plant with LG Energy nearby in Ohio.
source_title: Ford supplier invests $790M to build 3 Tennessee plants | Manufacturing Dive

facts: The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is the official name of the new factory.
The facility is located a few minutes outside Savannah in Ellabell, Georgia.
HMGMA represents the largest investment ever made in the state of Georgia.
The investment is part of Hyundai’s broader expansion of its US manufacturing footprint.
The company owns nearly 3,000 acres of land at the site and continues to build out additional business units as operations expand.
The initial production scope was set at 300,000 vehicles per year.
Just last year, Hyundai committed to expanding operations and increasing its total output capacity to 500,000 vehicles per year.
The facility is currently operational and producing the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 for the US market.
Hyundai has created 8,500 direct jobs at the facility.
Hyundai has created 6,950 indirect jobs through regional suppliers and supporting businesses.
The factory plan includes co-locating core vendors at the same site to optimize logistics and increase partner agility.
Co-located vendors include Hyundai conglomerate companies like Hyundai Steel as well as external vendors building their own factories within the Metaplant.
Co-locating vendors creates a shorter supply chain that ties up less money and inventory, enabling faster response times to changes.
The plant utilizes autonomous guided vehicles (AGVs) instead of traditional massive conveyors to carry vehicles through the factory floor.
AGVs provide greater flexibility for vehicle queuing and production line changes but require individual maintenance, charging, and direction.
Hyundai’s stamping press features extensive noise dampening and shock absorption, allowing workers to operate in the area without hearing protection.
The body shop welds stamped panels together using a fleet of robots and checks them for quality.
The state of Georgia was instrumental in securing the large parcel of land for the Hyundai Metaplant.
Georgia built a state-owned and run training facility that provides initial training modules for Hyundai’s new hires.
This state support enabled Hyundai to invest significantly in Georgia with confidence in building a sizable local workforce.
Hyundai’s CEO stated he sees significant potential for the company to expand in the US market in the near term.
source_title: A Look Inside Hyundai's Metaplant - CleanTechnica

facts: Kia at its West Point, Georgia plant rolled out the first-ever electric vehicle manufactured in the state of Georgia, an EV9 SUV.
The rollout occurred just 10 months after Kia invested $200 million to expand operations at its West Point, Georgia plant.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp drove the newly assembled EV9 off the production line and congratulated the Kia Georgia team for boosting the state’s EV portfolio, strengthening the battery belt, and advancing the climate agenda.
The fossil-fuel, emission-free vehicle went on sale at the end of 2023.
Prior to Georgia production, the vehicles were shipped from South Korea and sold in all 50 states.
The EV9 has received industry accolades including World Car of the Year, World EV of the Year, and North American Utility Vehicle of the Year.
At the ceremony, U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff congratulated the Kia Georgia team on the “huge milestone.”
Ossoff stated that Georgians are proud of these beautiful cars, produced in Georgia and made in America.
Manufacturing the vehicle on American soil makes it at least partially eligible for a $7,500 EV tax credit under the Inflation Reduction Act.
The EV tax credit requires the vehicle to be made in America and meets specific battery component requirements.
Anne Blair, VP of the Electrification Coalition, noted that fifty percent of an EV's mineral components must now be sourced and processed in the U.S. or a trade partner country.
Kia has not confirmed whether the Georgia-assembled EV9 will qualify for the full tax credit.
Kia Georgia spokesman Patrick Sands stated the company expects the Georgia-assembled EV9 to qualify for available incentives in the near future and will provide additional details at a later date.
According to Sands, the EV9 batteries take eight hours to charge on a 220v level 2 circuit.
The EV9 battery comes with a 10-year/100,00- mile warranty, while federal regulation mandates a minimum of an eight-year warranty.
The MSRP for the EV9 ranges between $55,000 and $70,000, accounting for incentives and trim level.
Kia Georgia spokesman Patrick Sands did not address whether Georgians will receive a hometown discount on the vehicle.
The plant's expansion last July added 200 jobs to support EV production alongside the current Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage lineup.
The West Point plant is listed as the fifth largest employer on the ‘Choose Columbus’ website.
Approximately 86 people in Columbus work for Kia Georgia at the West Point plant, according to LinkedIn.
source_title: Kia creates first-ever Georgia electric vehicle at West Point plant | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

facts: SK Battery is cutting nearly 1,000 jobs at its Commerce plant amid tepid demand for battery-electric vehicles.
SK Battery America announced March 6 that it would lay off 958 workers “to align operations to market conditions.”
SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The company is pursuing future customers, including the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The company will retain about 1,600 workers at the $2.6 billion site.
The Commerce plant received an incentives package valued at a combined $300 million from state and local governments.
SK Battery announced the layoffs in a WARN notice filed with the Technical College System of Georgia.
Top roles affected are assembly stacking operators (137 jobs), assembly nothing operators (130) and formation operators (129).
Employees’ last day worked is Friday, but they will receive full pay for another 60 days.
Chief Human Resources Officer Chuck Moore sent a letter regarding the layoffs and continued pay.
The SK project was announced in 2017 during the waning days of the Nathan Deal administration.
Newly elected Gov. Brian Kemp picked up the baton and pushed for the state to press the accelerator in its efforts to drive supplier investments.
The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 created a complex landscape for SK, as it was still relying heavily on materials from China.
To qualify for the full $7,500 tax credit under the IRA, a battery had to be both assembled in the U.S. and made from a minimum 50 percent content sourced from the U.S. or select trading partners.
President Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, removed the purchase incentives.
Demand for new electric vehicles has cratered since, with Cox Automotive research pointing to a 30 percent decline year over year in January.
SK On is working with Hyundai on a $5 billion battery joint-venture plant in Cartersville.
An SK spokesperson said the company is “unaware of any changes” regarding the Cartersville JV.
Work continues on a $4.3 billion LG-Hyundai site on the footprint of the Korean auto maker’s Metaplant near Savannah.

The Savannah site was the subject of a September Immigration and Customs Enforcement raid that created a diplomatic spat with Korea after nearly 500 people were detained, causing work to pause for two months.
Georgia’s U.S. senators, both Democrats, blamed Trump policies for Friday’s job cuts.

Sen. Raphael Warnock said the administration put politics over people and slashed clean energy investments that helped bring good-paying manufacturing jobs to the state, and made manufacturing in Georgia more expensive with harmful tariff taxes.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff warned that Trump’s policies threatened the EV boom in Georgia.

Ossoff stated: “Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone... As predicted, Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy. We were booming and building new plants. Now Georgians are losing our jobs.”
Gov. Brian Kemp traveled to Korea in October to calm nerves after the ICE raid.
Kemp has enjoyed a strong relationship with Hyundai, SK and other Korean investors, often pointing out that their commitments were made before the IRA’s passage.
Kemp told Global Atlanta just after meetings in Seoul that Biden-era incentives were an inflationary market distortion that artificially propped up electric-vehicle demand.
Kemp added that stalled EV ecosystem projects across the state were too reliant on government largesse.
Kemp said: “Their whole business model up on incentives, versus, if we let the market work with the companies that were coming before, they would have still built the supply chains out.”
The SK plant in Commerce spans 2.7 million square feet.
The SK plant represents $2.6 billion in capital investment.
The SK plant maintains 22 GW in production capacity.
The plant's capacity is enough to power 300,000 all-electric vehicles.
source_title: SK Battery Cuts Nearly 1,000 Georgia Jobs Amid Declining EV Demand - Global Atlanta

facts: Georgia is becoming a critical state for EV and battery manufacturing.
Significant investment from automakers, battery suppliers, energy companies, and government funding is driving economic output in the state.
As of August 2024, $24.4 billion in private investments have been poured into the Georgia EV manufacturing industry.
These investments were spurred by approximately $347 million in federal funding.
The investments will result in the creation of more than 26,40o jobs for Georgians.
Prominent locations of these jobs are shown on a map on the page.
Investments in Georgia’s EV economy are bolstering American manufacturing and supply chains at a time when China has a head start in the global transition to electric transportation.
The City of Atlanta developed the 2025 EV Readiness Workbook in close consultation with the Electrification Coalition (EC).
The workbook serves as a practical guide for property owners, developers, fleet managers, and community partners to accelerate EV readiness.
Georgia is currently a leader in electric vehicle adoption within the state.
There are more than 100,000 EV drivers on the road in Georgia.
The state has more than 2,200 publicly available EV charging stations.
The National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) program provides federal funds to build a reliable, accessible network of public EV charging stations across all 50 states.
Georgia’s Department of Transportation was allotted $135 million in NEVI funds to build chargers along major highways and in communities across the state through public-private partnerships.
NEVI funds were frozen in February 2025.
The Federal Highway Administration issued updated guidance in August 2025, allowing states to regain access to their allotted funds.
The new NEVI guidance will streamline the program and provide states with greater flexibility when deciding how to use the funds.
Georgia submitted an updated state plan for NEVI in September 2025.
Georgia announced a second round of NEVI awards in November 2025.
The Electrification Coalition will continue working alongside the state and NEVI awardees to support the construction of a robust national charging network.
Outdated laws in Georgia currently restrict consumers’ freedom to buy vehicles of their choice.
These vehicle purchase restrictions are obstructing economic growth and blocking Georgians’ access to the latest automotive technologies.
The Electrification Coalition’s policy priorities for EV expansion in Georgia include supporting the expansion of medium- and heavy-duty electric vehicle adoption.
EC priorities also include promoting EV-friendly rates and rate design best practices for charging, including the sale of electricity by kilowatt hours.
Additional EC priorities involve supporting utility transportation electrification planning and effective implementation of the state’s NEVI plan.
The EC aims to support effective implementation of federal funding opportunities for school bus and transit electrification.
The coalition supports direct-to-consumer vehicle sales and service to expand vehicle choices and provide greater access to maintenance.
The EC opposes punitive EV taxes that discourage EV adoption.
On August 7, 2025, regional leaders and industry experts gathered at the Savannah Visitors Center in Savannah, GA, for an event titled "How EV Investments Are Powering Prosperity in Georgia".
This event highlighted the region’s emergence as a national hub for EV innovation, manufacturing, and workforce development.
On October 10, 2024, the EC hosted a workshop on the future of port and medium- and heavy-duty (MHD) vehicle electrification in Georgia and Florida.

On October 4, 2023, the EC convened policymakers and industry representatives from across the Southeast in Charlotte, NC, for a Rural Roundtable discussing rural transportation electrification in Florida, Georgia, and the Carolinas.
The October 4, 2023 roundtable focused on workforce development, the role of utilities in the transition, battery recycling, and related issues.

On May 24, 2023, the EC hosted a roundtable on vehicle electrification in rural Georgia to discuss opportunities and challenges associated with electric transportation investment and how rural communities can benefit.
source_title: Georgia EV Policy | Electrification Coalition

facts: Batterie Resourcers is a US battery recycling startup building a commercial lithium-ion battery recycling plant in Covington, Georgia.
The company is supported by Jaguar Land Rover among other investors.
The Covington plant is scheduled to begin operations as early as August 2022.
The facility will be set up in an existing hall and have a processing capacity of 30,000 tonnes.
Battery Resourcers is investing $43 million (around €38 million) into the project.
The project will create 150 jobs in Covington, Georgia.
Battery Resourcers states the site is strategically located near several EV production centers and lithium-ion gigafactories.
Covington is connected to Atlanta, approximately 50 kilometers away, via Interstate 20.
SK Innovation is building a battery factory in Georgia to supply Volkswagen, Ford, and other customers.
Nearby automotive manufacturing facilities include Volkswagen in Chattanooga, Mercedes-Benz in Tuscaloosa (producing EQE SUV and EQS SUV), and BMW in Spartanburg (producing PHEV models).
The Volvo plant in Ridgeville, South Carolina (producing the electric XC90 successor and Polestar 3) remains accessible from the site.
Michael O’Kronley, CEO and director of Battery Resourcers, states that automotive OEMs currently lack responsible and cost-effective disposal options for discarded batteries and scrap.
The company claims its next-generation technology and U.S. location provide a sustainable solution that minimizes mining needs and returns battery-grade materials to the lithium-ion supply chain.
Battery Resourcers has long-term plans to open additional recycling facilities in North America, Europe, and Asia.
The global network aims to process up to 150,000 tonnes of lithium-ion material annually.
Battery Resourcers is considering opening an additional facility to produce cathode materials in 2023.
The facility's current recycling processes will primarily recover cathode materials from NMC, NCA, and LFP batteries.
Jaguar Land Rover participated in a $20 million funding round for Battery Resourcers in April 2021.
source_title: Recycling-Startup Batterie Resourcers to build recycling plant in Georgia - electrive.com

canonical_name: HL-GA Battery Company / Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA)
facts: On 4 September, US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), along with multiple other law enforcement organisations, raided Hyundai Motor and LG Energy Solution’s electric vehicle (EV) battery cell plant in Ellabell, Georgia.
Hyundai and LG ES each have a 50% stake in the US$4.3 billion HL-GA Battery Company.
HL-GA is part of the larger Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) campus.
The HMGMA campus cost US$7.6 billion and has over 16 million square feet of factory floor space.
HMGMA began producing the Hyundai Ioniq 5 in October 2024.
The Associated Press noted the project as “the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.”
The Savannah Morning News reported that HMGMA had plans to hire 8,100 people by 2031.
According to Politico, 17 suppliers in the surrounding area announced factories to support HMGMA.
These supplier factories represent an estimated additional investment of US$2.7 billion and an additional 7,000 jobs.
The large-scale investments spurred upgrades to surrounding infrastructure from the Georgia Department of Transportation.
Earlier this year, Hyundai said it would invest an additional US$5 billion in the US overall.
A reported 500 agents from ICE, Homeland Security Investigations, Georgia State Patrol, the FBI, ATF, DEA, and IRS raided the factory.
475 individuals were detained during the raid, of whom approximately 300 were South Korean nationals.
The detained workers were held in an ICE detention centre in Folkston, Georgia.
Treatment of the workers led South Korea’s Foreign Ministry to investigate claims of human rights violations.
Immigration attorney Charles Kuck said two of his clients were working under a visa waiver programme for tourism and business without violating it.
Some detained workers allegedly illegally crossed the US border, while others had entered legally but had expired visas or visa waivers that prevented them from working.
Some detained individuals were employed by contractors and subcontractors at the construction site, not directly by Hyundai or LG ES.
On 10 September, The Guardian reported that one detained Korean worker had a valid working visa but was “mandated” to agree to removal from the US.
ICE claimed that all 475 individuals were either working illegally or in violation of their visas, which has not been proven true.
South Korean battery manufacturers currently produce 45% of all Li-ion batteries sold in the US.
South Korea’s stationary storage market is expected to require around 50GWh of batteries this year, with projected growth to 200GWh by 2028.
Multiple South Korean companies have outfitted factories made for EV cell manufacturing for the production of BESS cells.

A market expert source noted that South Korean battery manufacturers partnering with US entities are repurposing existing operational or nearly finished facilities to avoid sunk costs due to potential EV demand decline.
These repurposed factories are about 90% complete, with manufacturers shifting some capacity to stationary storage applications.
In July, LG ES and General Motors joint venture Ultium Cells announced plans to upgrade its battery cell plant in Spring Hill, Tennessee, to increase production of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells.
The Ultium Cells upgrade is based on a US$2.3 billion investment initially announced in 2021.
Conversion of battery cell lines at Spring Hill to manufacture LFP cells will begin later this year, with commercial production expected by late 2027.
Spring Hill was established to double Ultium’s production of nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) battery cells for vehicles like the Cadillac LYRIQ.
LG ES stated that its newly opened LFP production lines are expected to reach an annual capacity of 17GWh by the end of 2025.
LG ES plans to increase this LFP capacity to over 30GWh by the end of the following year.
LG ES expects continued energy storage growth and a continued slowing in the EV market, citing increased renewable energy deployment and AI data centre demand as key drivers.
New foreign entity of concern (FEOC) restrictions limiting Chinese equipment in US energy storage projects eligible for tax credits will likely benefit non-Chinese competitors like LG ES.
In July, South Korean companies SK On and L&F agreed on a memorandum of understanding regarding the supply of LFP cathode materials for the North American market.
SK On stated its partnership with L&F aims to address rising demand for LFP batteries in the US and strengthen its position in the ESS market.
In September at the RE+ trade show in Las Vegas, Samsung SDI debuted its new Battery Box (SBB) 1.7 and SBB 2.0 BESS products, scheduled to begin US-based production next year.

An anonymous source noted that importing batteries from China is complicated by tariffs and FEOC restrictions, while domestic batteries produced by South Korean companies pose no FEOC issues and may qualify for advanced manufacturing tax credits.
There are no tariffs on finished batteries imported from South Korea, only on inputs like graphite or lithium from China.
If South Korean factories face delays of three years, available capacity could drop to 30GWh against demand, creating a 70% gap.
A one-year delay would reduce available capacity from about 25% to 40%, potentially causing prices to go up and projects to be delayed.

Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority, said there is no other entity in the world with the proprietary technology that has to be installed by certain individuals, stating “We are relying on South Korea.”

Jose Munoz, president and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, told CBS News the raid would cause a minimum two to three months delay because displaced workers want to return and need replacement, mostly not being in the US.
A representative from LG ES stated there are no plans to delay the factory and that they aim to start production at HL-GA Battery Company next year.

Following a US-Korea working group meeting agreement that B-1 and ESTA holders can perform installation, inspection, and maintenance of equipment at US factories, essential personnel business trips resumed starting 13 October.
In the weeks following the raid, Hyundai announced it would move forward with expansion plans at the Ellabell plant.
Hyundai updated its original production plans at HMGMA from 300,000 EVs per year to 500,000 EVs per year.

The New York Times reported that South Korea’s Chosun Ilbo editorial urged officials to recognize the seriousness of the situation and questioned whether South Korea should continue investing in America.
Donald Trump wrote on Truth Social calling on all foreign companies investing in the US to respect immigration laws and encouraging them to legally hire and train American workers.
In July, the US agreed to reduce tariffs on South Korean exports from 15% to 25% in return for Korean companies pledging US$350 billion in investments in the US.
Of the pledged investment, US$150 billion is specifically allocated to boost America’s shipbuilding industry.
On 27 October, Trump stated he was opposed to the raid, noting that complex factory equipment requires brought-in expertise to teach American workers.

About 200 individuals detained during the weeklong ordeal are planning to sue ICE, accusing the organisation of racial discrimination, human rights abuses, and excessive force, while pursuing compensation for damages.
source_title: What an ICE raid on a Georgia EV factory could mean for BESS - Energy-Storage.News

facts: A Kennesaw State University researcher is leading development of a new materials approach to make next-generation solid-state batteries safer and more sustainable.

Beibei Jiang, an assistant professor in the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at Kennesaw State University, leads the research team developing a sulfur-modified solid electrolyte for solid-state batteries.
The sulfur-modified solid electrolyte is designed to improve lithium ion movement through solid-state batteries, increasing performance while reducing safety risks.
Dr. Beibei Jiang stated the team's goal is to replace flammable components in batteries to make them much safer.
The team aims to reduce the risk of overheating, short circuits, and fires by removing the liquid electrolyte and redesigning the solid materials inside the battery.
Solid-state batteries replace flammable liquid electrolytes with a solid material that separates electrodes and allows lithium ion movement during charging and discharging.
The solid-state battery design improves safety but limits charging speed because lithium ions move more slowly through solids.
Jiang's team addressed slow ion movement by adding sulfur-based chemical groups to a composite solid electrolyte made of ceramic and polymer components.
The added sulfur improves bonding between the ceramic and polymer components, reducing resistance and allowing lithium ions to move more easily.
Dr. Beibei Jiang compared lithium ions to cars on a highway, stating their sulfur modification smooths the path for faster ion movement, enabling faster charging and better performance.
The research team discovered a strong interaction between sulfur and zirconium in the ceramic portion of the electrolyte.
Dr. Jiang's group is the first to identify and document this strong sulfur-zirconium interaction.
Dr. Beibei Jiang stated they believe the sulfur-zirconium interaction is the main reason for the improved battery performance.
The discovery originated from students noticing a chemical reaction occurring far more quickly than expected during early experiments.

Dr. Beibei Jiang described the discovery as almost accidental, noting the reaction happened in just a few seconds and got out of control before they investigated how to slow it down and make it controllable.
Lawrence Whitman, dean of the Southern Polytechnic College of Engineering and Engineering Technology at Kennesaw State University, stated the work reflects the college's applied research focus.

Dean Lawrence Whitman stated Dr. Jiang's research addresses a pressing challenge in energy storage by improving safety and performance to move solid-state battery technology closer to real-world application.
The research project is supported by a $200,000 grant from the National Science Foundation.
The project also receives seed funding from Kennesaw State University's Office of Research.
Most of the research takes place in Dr. Jiang's lab on Kennesaw State University's Marietta Campus.
Students at the lab synthesize materials, assemble prototype batteries, and test performance using small coin-cell designs.
Dr. Beibei Jiang stated the team's current focus is proving the design works, is stable, and is reliable before considering scaling and manufacturing.
Dr. Beibei Jiang believes the solid-state battery research has strong potential applications in electric vehicles, renewable energy storage, and consumer electronics.
Dr. Beibei Jiang encourages students from a range of engineering backgrounds to get involved in the research.
Dr. Beibei Jiang stated that students from any background can contribute and emphasized the importance of getting started and gaining hands-on experience.

Dr. Beibei Jiang's work represents a promising step toward making solid-state batteries a practical alternative to current lithium-ion technology as demand for safer and more efficient energy storage grows.
source_title: Kennesaw State researcher develops safer, faster solid-state battery design  - News

canonical_name: Prof. Matthew McDowell's Lab at Georgia Institute of Technology
facts: The lab's research goal is to understand how materials behave and transform in real-life environments within energy storage devices to engineer improved materials for better performance.
In energy storage devices, mass/electron transport, high temperatures, and reactive environments are the norm, causing materials to change, transform, or degrade in response to operating conditions.
The group focuses on using in situ experimental techniques to probe materials transformations under realistic conditions to understand how changes influence properties.
Fundamental scientific advances from these studies guide the engineering of materials for breakthrough new devices.
Students and other researchers in the group synthesize materials, analyze their properties, and utilize a variety of in situ characterization methods to understand dynamic structure and chemistry.

Current Project 1 investigates transformation and degradation mechanisms in next-generation battery materials that require higher charge storage capacity or entirely new materials beyond lithium-ion, such as sodium-ion or multivalent batteries.
The reaction and phase transformation mechanisms of new materials determine how much charge they can store and how long they last within batteries.
It is critical to understand these reaction mechanisms across multiple length scales from the atomic scale to the mesoscale within battery electrodes.
The group uses powerful in situ experimental techniques including X-ray tomographic imaging and electron microscopy to reveal structural, chemical, and morphological transformations in real time.

Current Project 2 focuses on understanding interfaces in solid-state batteries, which could be safer and have higher energy density than Li-ion batteries but face performance limits from chemical and structural instabilities at interfaces between solid-state electrolytes and electrode materials.
The group is working on understanding the evolution of interfaces between electrodes and solid electrolyte materials in solid-state batteries.
With this knowledge, the group is designing new materials for solid-state batteries with improved performance.
Current Project 3 investigates and develops sustainable materials for battery systems that are more sustainable, lower cost, and have improved energy storage properties.
The group is investigating the use of these sustainable materials in lithium-ion batteries, sodium-ion batteries, and grid-scale energy storage flow batteries.
Current Project 4 focuses on understanding and developing high-capacity electrode materials for use at low temperatures beyond that which is possible with conventional Li-ion batteries.
This low-temperature battery work could be important for spaceflight, electrified aircraft, and other applications involving extreme environments.
Bryan County is located along Interstate 16 and Interstate 95.
Bryan County provides direct access to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Bryan County is an optimal location for Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers serving Hyundai, other Southeastern auto manufacturers, and the broader U.S. automotive market.
Boston Dynamics’ all-electric Atlas robot, powered by advanced chips, is being tested on real industrial tasks at Hyundai’s Georgia plant.
Bryan County is situated in the heart of the booming Southeastern automotive corridor where major OEMs and automotive suppliers are investing billions into EV production and advanced manufacturing.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is located in Bryan County.
Mercedes-Benz Vans is located 115 miles from Bryan County in Charleston, SC.
Kia Manufacturing Plant is located 180 miles from Bryan County in West Point, GA.
BMW Manufacturing Facility is located 210 miles from Bryan County in Greenville, SC.
General Motors is located 460 miles from Bryan County in Spring Hill, TN.
Ford’s BlueOval City is located 500 miles from Bryan County in Memphis, TN.
Bryan County provides easy interstate access and rail connectivity for suppliers serving multiple OEMs across the region.
Interstate 16 provides direct connections to Atlanta and the Southeastern U.S.
Interstate 95 serves as a major north-south corridor connecting suppliers to the entire East Coast.
Bryan County is located less than 20 miles from the Port of Savannah, one of the busiest and fastest-growing deepwater ports in the U.S.
Bryan County is located 80 miles from the Port of Brunswick, which is the largest import/export automotive facility in the country.
The Ro/Ro terminal at the Port of Brunswick handles thousands of imported and exported vehicles annually.
Containerized logistics provide efficient transport for parts and components.
CSX and Norfolk Southern Class I rail service ensures cost-effective transportation for inbound materials and outbound shipments.
Direct rail connectivity to the Port of Savannah integrates Bryan County into the global automotive supply chain.
Savannah/Hilton Head International Airport provides cargo and passenger services.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the busiest airport in the world, is within easy reach.
Bryan County’s multi-modal logistics network ensures on-time, cost-efficient supply chain operations for just-in-time and just-in-sequence delivery capabilities.
Hyundai’s $12.6 billion electric vehicle manufacturing facility in Bryan County is one of the largest economic development projects in U.S. history.
The Hyundai Metaplant involves a $12.6 Billion investment.
The Hyundai Metaplant will create 8,500 direct jobs and thousands more in supplier support.
The Hyundai Metaplant will produce 500,000 electric vehicles annually.
The facility creates massive demand for parts and components.
The expanding EV ecosystem opens opportunities for battery, electronics, and component manufacturers.
The Hyundai Metaplant serves as the anchor for an entire supply chain ecosystem.
Bryan County’s workforce development programs ensure suppliers have access to talent trained in advanced automation, robotics, and precision assembly.
Regional Industry Support Enterprise (RISE) supports seven initiatives: military, underrepresented, housing, education, transportation, childcare and marketing.
Savannah Technical College provides industry-specific training in mechatronics, welding, industrial maintenance, and advanced manufacturing.
Fort Stewart and Hunter Army Airfield produce a steady pipeline of transitioning military personnel with technical skills in logistics, engineering, and operations.
Georgia Quick Start offers customized workforce training at no cost to qualifying businesses.
There are more than 500,000 workers available in the Savannah metro area.
Automotive manufacturing employment in Georgia has grown 20 percent in the past decade.
There is strong demand for EV and battery technology skills in the region.
Bryan County offers Job Tax Credits providing up to $5,000 per new job created.
Bryan County offers Investment Tax Credits to help offset costs for equipment, R&D, and infrastructure upgrades.
Bryan County offers Fast-Track Permitting to ensure businesses can start production faster.
Bryan County offers Utility and Infrastructure Grants to help fund site development and facility expansion.
Bryan County offers industrial parks and shovel-ready sites designed for automotive supply chain operations.
source_title: Automotive | Development Authority of Bryan County

facts: Hyundai Motor Group entered an agreement with the State of Georgia to build full electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Bryan County.
The new facilities represent an investment of approximately USD 5.54 billion.
Commercial production at the new facility is expected to begin in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a wide range of innovative full electric vehicle models for U.S. customers at the new Georgia plant.
Details regarding specific production models will be shared at later dates.
The local EV production aims to increase U.S. consumer accessibility to the Group’s innovative EVs.
The battery manufacturing facility will be established through a strategic partnership, with details to be disclosed at a later stage.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain and build a healthy EV ecosystem in the U.S.
The EV and battery manufacturing plant will be located on a dedicated 2,923-acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The site has immediate access to I-95 and I-16 highways, providing easy access to 250 major metro areas.
The site is located less than 50 kilometers from the Port of Savannah, which is the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S.
Two Class I rail facilities are on-site at the port, provided by Georgia Central Railway.
West Point, Georgia is already home to the Group’s Kia manufacturing hub in the U.S.
Hyundai Motor Group selected Georgia due to favorable business conditions including speed-to-market, a talented workforce, and an existing network of Group affiliates and suppliers.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registration per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia already offers more publicly available EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.
The project is expected to create about 8,100 new jobs.
This investment is part of the Group’s 2021 announcement to invest USD 7.4 billion by 2025 to foster future mobility in the U.S., including EV production and smart mobility solutions.
Hyundai Motor Group aims to become a leader in the U.S. auto market’s EV transition with the new facility.
The Group has a global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize this goal, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network to ensure a stable supply of EVs worldwide.
With additional EV and battery production capabilities in the U.S., the Group aims to become one of the top three EV providers in the U.S. by 2026.
The Group plans to lead the EV market in sales, design, technologies, and mobility solutions optimized for the electric mobility era.
The investment aligns with the U.S. government’s roadmap to accelerate electrification as global OEMs expand local U.S. EV production.
Last year, the U.S. government announced an initiative requiring zero-emission vehicle sales to account for at least half of OEM new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The Group plans to implement advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at its innovation hub, the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center in Singapore (HMGICS).
The Georgia facility will operate as an intelligent manufacturing plant where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production processes are optimized using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system aims to create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
As part of its sustainability commitment, the plant will mainly rely on renewable energy sources and use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung joined the official signing ceremony virtually in Bryan County.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the signing ceremony along with other Georgia officials.
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jaehoon Chang attended the signing ceremony.
Hyundai Motor President and Chief Operating Officer José Muñoz attended the signing ceremony.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the project is the largest in the state’s history and delivers high-quality jobs on the leading edge of mobility to hardworking Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp noted it is incredible that within a year of Georgia’s investment in the Bryan County Megasite, they are locating a world-class project with a company with great Georgia history.
Euisun Chung stated that the U.S. holds an important place in the Group’s global strategy and expressed excitement to partner with the State of Georgia for electrified mobility and sustainability.
Jaehoon Chang stated that America embraces change and drives innovation, making it the right location for their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S.
Jaehoon Chang added that the new EV plant is the future of their business and will help meet growing demands from U.S. customers for leading-edge design, safe, zero-emissions vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise with a value chain based on mobility, steel, construction, logistics, finance, IT, and service.
The Group has about 250,000 employees worldwide and its mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
source_title: Hyundai to Establish Its First Dedicated EV Plant and Battery Manufacturing Facility in the U.S.

facts: Hyundai Motor Company plans to manufacture their line of electric vehicles on nearly 3,000 acres at the Bryan County megasite.
The Bryan County megasite is located off Highway 80 in Bryan County, Georgia.
Local resident Jennifer Fichthorn's home is just minutes away from the site.
Fichthorn moved to the area more than two decades ago when there was little-to-no traffic in her neighborhood.
Fichthorn worries the plant will add more speedsters and traffic to an area that was previously relatively quiet.
source_title: Bryan County, GA residents, officials react to Hyundai auto plant

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant
facts: Federal agents arrested about 475 workers at the Hyundai–LG Energy Solution construction site for a Hyundai battery factory in Ellabell on September 4.
The arrest followed the serving of a judicial search warrant focused on alleged unlawful employment practices.
Officials said most of the detained workers were South Korean nationals employed by subcontractors rather than Hyundai or LG themselves.
The operation was led by ICE’s Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) with support from the FBI, DEA, ATF, and the Georgia State Patrol.
It is the largest single-site immigration raid in HSI history.
The raid signals a sharp turn toward targeting employers seen as hiring illegal workers at high-profile industrial projects in the United States.
The raid triggered an immediate work stoppage at the $4.3 billion electric-vehicle battery project.
The project is expected to create thousands of jobs once completed.
The scale of the enforcement shocked local leaders and sent a warning to firms building major facilities about hiring and verification practices across entire contracting chains being under scrutiny.
Diplomatic talks followed quickly between U.S. and South Korean officials for the release and repatriation of many detained workers.
Authorities arranged charter flights to return those who agreed to leave.
At least 300 South Korean nationals were among those held, underscoring the cross-border nature of labor pipelines at global projects.
The White House has made worksite enforcement a priority in 2024–2025, with raids focusing on large, foreign-invested sites.
White House border czar Tom Homan said the administration would “continue to ramp up raids on U.S. workplaces,” stressing a zero-tolerance approach toward employers who hire undocumented labor.
President Trump praised foreign investment but drew a clear line on hiring, saying: “Your Investments are welcome… What we ask in return is that you hire and train American Workers.”
Hyundai Motor Group responded that it has a “zero tolerance” policy for unlawful employment.
The company said it would review its contractor and subcontractor vetting to ensure compliance.
Hyundai said it would cooperate with investigators and re-check onboarding systems used by third-party labor firms.
Staffing for the project is built through lengthy chains of subcontractors, where verification steps can break down if not policed at each layer.
The United Auto Workers (UAW) condemned Hyundai for unsafe conditions and the use of immigrant labor in ways the union called exploitative.
The UAW criticized the militarized feel of the raid and said, “workers are not the problem. Exploitative corporations are.”
Union leaders urged more oversight by OSHA and the NLRB and called for better training and safety compliance on big job sites that depend on rushed schedules and far-flung contractors.
South Korea dispatched a ministerial delegation to work with U.S. agencies on the release and safe return of its citizens.
The talks and quick arrangement of repatriation flights showed how worksite enforcement can become a diplomatic issue when large numbers of foreign nationals are involved.
Legal experts say the size of the raid and multi-agency presence set a new baseline for major actions under Trump’s second term.
Industry analysts note that construction often depends on unauthorized labor, with some estimates putting the share near 23%.
This raises the odds of further disruption at big-ticket projects that draw attention.
VisaVerge.com reports that employers should expect wider use of joint operations at critical infrastructure sites, along with deeper audits of every employer in the chain, not just the marquee brand.

Federal agencies described the Ellabell action as a coordinated, court-authorized search targeting unlawful employment schemes, possible document fraud, and other federal violations tied to hiring practices.
Officials did not state that Hyundai or LG directly employed the workers in question.
Most of those detained were linked to subcontractors and labor brokers who supplied crews for the Hyundai battery-factory project.
The Department of Homeland Security has said future worksite actions will concentrate on companies in key sectors: energy, high-tech manufacturing, and transportation.
The Ellabell raid fits that plan and took place at a headline project backed by foreign investment and promoted as part of a broader push to onshore advanced manufacturing.
Hyundai pledged a full compliance review and promised swift action if contractors broke the rules.
The company pointed to its internal standards on safety and lawful hiring.
It remains to be seen whether federal investigators will bring criminal charges, seek civil fines, or push for debarment from federal contracts.
The work stoppage alone may set the project back and expose the companies to new scrutiny from lenders, insurers, and state partners.
People detained may face deportation and repatriation under current law.
Some detainees could serve as witnesses in federal cases against employers or recruiters.
Others may seek legal advice, but available defenses are often narrow.
Families face sudden separation, lost income, and fear about returning home with debt from recruitment fees.
For local communities, the stoppage means a halt in paychecks and a ripple effect that hits small businesses tied to the site.
Local officials in Georgia now face the fallout from a paused mega-project.
The region had banked on the Hyundai battery-factory as an anchor for growth, from supply plants to service jobs.
With work halted, counties and small towns may feel a short-term pinch.
Over the long term, developers will likely restart after sorting out staffing, because the site remains central to national goals on EV supply chains and domestic manufacturing.
The Ellabell raid signals tougher enforcement and deeper scrutiny across contracting chains.
Practical steps employers should take include conducting internal audits of worker files across all tiers of contractors.
Employers should re-check Form I‑9 records for errors and missing re-verification.
Employers should require E‑Verify enrollment by all staffing partners and monitor results.
Employers should engage outside counsel with immigration and labor expertise.
Employers should cooperate with any federal inquiries and preserve records.
Employers should improve site safety and training to address union and regulator concerns.
According to analysis by VisaVerge.com, the Ellabell case shows that cost-saving through lax verification is now a high-risk choice.
Scrutiny will fall not only on who is on the payroll but also on who oversees the crews, who checks the documents, and who profits from labor arrangements that skirt the law.
VisaVerge.com recommends that firms map their contractor networks and assign clear compliance owners for each tier.
Employers should expect more site visits, subpoenas, and worker interviews by multi-agency teams combining immigration, labor, and financial probes.
Even firms that pass basic I-9 checks can be vulnerable if contractors ignore rules or if document fraud is present.
Construction firms may face labor shortages if unauthorized workers are removed; responses could include raising wages, expanding local recruitment, or investing in training.
For foreign investors: build U.S. hiring rules into contracts from day one, require E‑Verify down the chain, perform surprise audits, and watch for document fraud rings.
Worker advocates argue that raids tend to punish the most vulnerable while the biggest gains go to companies.

They call for stronger protection for whistleblowers, limits on retaliation, clear paths for workers who report safety or wage theft, and increased involvement by OSHA and NLRB at sites flagged by immigration raids.
The Ellabell raid may become a template: a large, multi-agency operation at a flagship site, followed by fast diplomatic coordination and rapid repatriation for many detainees.
It demonstrates how immigration enforcement, labor policy, and industrial strategy are tightly linked.
For employers: treat hiring rules as seriously as engineering plans — tighten hiring and verification across every contracting tier to avoid liability and project delays.
For workers: the risks of unauthorized employment are high and can change lives in a day.
For policymakers: the challenge is to keep the country’s building boom moving while ensuring hiring stays within the law.

For companies with ongoing or planned U.S. investments, now is the time to test systems, deploy detailed contractor questionnaires, conduct random interviews with crew members, and immediately correct any I‑9 or E‑Verify errors.
source_title: Hyundai Battery Plant Raid Signals Trump Target Hiring Illegals

facts: Two more Hyundai suppliers will establish manufacturing sites in Georgia, following in the automaker’s footsteps to establish operations in the state.
Auto thermal systems manufacturer Hanon Systems will invest over $40 million in a new manufacturing facility, according to an announcement last month.
The South Korea-based company’s new plant will be located near Statesboro and assemble automotive thermal solutions.
The company aims to open the site in May 2024, creating 160 jobs.
Another South Korea-based company, Woory Industrial Co., is also headed to the state, investing $18 million to establish its first manufacturing facility in the U.S.

The new plant will be located in Dublin and will produce electrical heaters, control units and actuators for EV and internal combustion automakers in North America, according to a June 7 announcement.
The site will create more than 130 jobs and operations are expected to commence in November.
Woory Industrial CEO Jungwoo Kim stated: “The transition to electric vehicles is the future, and the U.S. EV market is the key market among all automotive markets.”
Woory Industrial CEO Jungwoo Kim added: “Georgia will be a good opportunity for Woory to take another step forward as part of the vanguard of EV development.”

The two factories are following Hyundai to the state after the automaker announced a $5.5 billion manufacturing campus in Ellabell last year, the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Georgia officials had predicted Hyundai’s Metaplant would attract approximately $1 billion to the state via offsite supplier investments.
The automaker’s suppliers have outpaced that estimate, with plans to invest nearly $2 billion in the state, officials said in April.
More suppliers are still anticipated to be announced, according to Angela Hendrix, senior vice president of marketing and public relations of the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
Hyundai continues to expand its own investment in the state alongside supplier announcements.
In late May, Hyundai and LG Energy Solution struck a joint venture deal to build a $4.3 billion battery cell plant, which will be adjacent to the automaker’s EV campus.
The two South Korea-based companies will each take a 50% stake in the venture and facility.
Hyundai will assemble battery packs using cells from the joint venture plant to be sent to its U.S. manufacturing sites.
source_title: Hyundai’s Georgia supplier network expands to 9 manufacturers | Manufacturing Dive

facts: PHA announced it would invest USD 67 million at a site about 15 miles (24 kilometers) west of Savannah.
PHA plans to hire 400 workers for the new Georgia plant.
The company already has a plant in Montgomery, Alabama, near the Hyundai facility in that city.
PHA is a South Korean company that makes latches, hinges and other parts for vehicle doors and hoods.
PHA will become the sixth major supplier to locate near a Hyundai Motor Group plant being built in coastal Georgia.
Hyundai said in May it would build a USD 5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia.
The Hyundai plant site could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
PHA said operations at its new facility would begin in 2024.
Hyundai suppliers have now announced USD 1.8 billion of investments in the region.
The state will pay to train workers, but the total incentive package from state and local governments wasn't immediately clear Tuesday.
The local community will donate land for the plant, according to Angela Hendrix, a spokesperson for the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
Chatham County would abate property taxes for 15 years, saving the company a projected 60% on taxes.
Angela Hendrix did not release a dollar value for the abatements.
PHA also could qualify for nearly USD 8 million in state income tax credits, at USD 4,000 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least USD 31,300 a year.
Sewon Precision Industry Co. plans a USD 300 million metal stamping plant near Savannah, with 740 workers.
Seoyon E-HWA plans a USD 76 million plant near the city to make interior and exterior trim parts, hiring 730.
Hyundai Mobis announced a USD 926 million powertrain plant in Richmond Hill, employing 1,500.
Joon Georgia plans a USD 317 million plant near Statesboro, employing 630.
Ecoplastic plans a USD 205 million plant with 456 workers in Register.
source_title: Hyundai supplier plans USD 67 mn Georgia plant, 400 jobs, ETAuto

facts: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Homeland Security Investigation (HSI) officers came onto the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America megasite Thursday to serve a criminal warrant at one of the plant’s suppliers being built at the site.
ICE and Homeland Security agents arrived at the HL-GA Battery Company construction site in Ellabell, which sits next to Hyundai’s electric-vehicle producing Metaplant.
LG Energy Solutions is the parent of HL-GA Battery and is partnering with Hyundai to build the battery plant at the site.
Officials announced they had a warrant and ordered construction to stop immediately.
Mary Beth Kennedy, senior public relations specialist for HL-GA Battery Company, issued a statement at 3 p.m.
HL-GA Battery Company stated it is cooperating fully with appropriate authorities regarding activity at its construction site.
HL-GA Battery Company paused construction to assist law enforcement's work.
HL-GA Battery Company does not have further details on the situation at the time of the statement.
A Hyundai spokesman stated the company is cooperating with law enforcement and committed to abiding by all labor and immigration regulations.
Multiple buses and other law enforcement vehicles were seen at the megasite, but no information about people being detained has been released.
ICE and Homeland Security officials have not released any details regarding the raid.
Georgia State Patrol confirmed they were assisting federal agencies in serving the warrant.
Georgia State Patrol assisted with traffic and air control around the Bryan County plant off Interstate 16.
source_title: Federal agents raid Hyundai supplier at Bryan Co. megasite - Statesboro Herald

facts: Georgia leaders are attempting to recruit a battery manufacturer to open an innovation center in Atlanta.
The deal could involve a Fulton County agency granting the company hundreds of thousands of dollars in tax incentives.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development is trying to woo a “state-of-the-art global innovation center” with 110 workers by an undisclosed battery company.
Details come from a Development Authority of Fulton County meeting agenda.
The agency, rebranded as Develop Fulton and commonly known as DAFC, will consider whether to approve a nearly $870,000 tax break to the battery company.
The consideration is scheduled for a Tuesday meeting.
The recruitment effort uses the code name “Project Phoenix.”
The innovation center recruitment effort is shrouded in mystery with few public details.
The project would add another battery-related project to Georgia’s fast-growing e-mobility and electric vehicle sector.
source_title: Georgia in talks to land battery manufacturing innovation center in Atlanta - Southern Business & Development

facts: Hanon Systems, a global automotive thermal and energy management solutions provider, invested over $40 million in a new Georgia facility.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment while on an economic development trip to Israel.
Hanon Systems will create 160 new jobs.
The new manufacturing facility is located in Bulloch County, Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as they welcome Hanon Systems to the state.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Georgia is furthering its position as the EV capital of America, with new jobs and opportunities coming to hardworking Georgians across the state.
Min Sung, President and CEO of Hanon Systems, stated the company is pleased to contribute to Bulloch County’s local economy through job creation.
Min Sung thanked the State of Georgia and the cooperation between its agencies for guiding the project.
Min Sung said the announcement aligns with Hanon Systems’ strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
Hanon Systems will build a new facility at Gateway Regional Industrial Park in Bulloch County to assemble automotive thermal solutions.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in May 2024.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, stated Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon Systems’ decision to locate its new facility there.
Billy Allen expressed excitement to welcome another automotive supplier to the community, particularly one working closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and the electric vehicle industry.
Billy Allen cited the decision as an example of the resources offered by their community and region to help companies succeed.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), stated the announcement further solidifies Georgia’s position as a growing hub for auto manufacturing.
Griff Lynch noted that GPA is proud to support the operations of Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers to deliver jobs and opportunity for Georgia residents.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the GDEcD’s Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD partnered with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start for this project.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hanon Systems can successfully support larger industry ecosystems in Georgia through partnership-driven solutions ranging from workforce training to logistics systems efficiencies.
Pat Wilson noted that Bulloch County, as part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, has been part of this growing success story from the beginning.
Pat Wilson highlighted how Georgia’s history of partnership continues to create opportunity for current and future generations.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, over 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $22.7 billion in investment and 28,400 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Hanon Systems was formed in 1986 and has grown into a leading supplier of automotive thermal and energy management solutions for electrified and conventional vehicles.
Hanon Systems’ offerings include heating ventilation and air conditioning, powertrain cooling, compressor, fluid transport, and electronics and fluid pressure solutions.
source_title: Hanon Systems invest over $40M in GA facility

facts: Pilot operates a growing network of EV charging canopies along major interstates.
The network takes the guesswork out of route planning and eliminates range anxiety with reliable EV charging.
Pilot connects travelers to the places they want to go and the conveniences they deserve along the way.
Pilot's chargers feature clear, upfront pricing with no hidden fees.
Charging is designed to be simple: start when you're ready, stop when you're done, and return to the open road.
Pilot collaborates with GM Energy to leverage advanced electric vehicle battery technology.
Pilot collaborates with EVgo to utilize extensive knowledge of charging networks and stations.
The collaboration between Pilot, GM Energy, and EVgo makes it easy for travelers to go farther, worry less, and enjoy the journey.
Pilot has helped travelers move across North America for over 65 years.
Pilot continuously adapts its variety of services, amenities, fuel, and energy offerings to stay ahead of the curve and be a trusted travel center.
source_title: Electric Vehicle Charging Stations Across the Country | Pilot

facts: Vietnamese automaker VinFast announced in March 2022 that it would open an electric vehicle assembly plant in North Carolina.
The battery manufacturing plant will be built in Chatham County and is expected to eventually create 7,500 jobs.
It’s the largest economic development announcement in the state’s history.

Nearly nine months after VinFast broke ground on its planned $4 billion electric vehicle factory in North Carolina, construction at the Chatham County site has stalled while local officials await updated building plans from the Vietnamese carmaker.

Chatham County confirmed Tuesday what News & Observer drone footage makes clear: No significant vertical construction has occurred at the campus, which sits about 30 miles southwest of downtown Raleigh near the unincorporated town of Moncure.
The current holdup dates back to last July when Chatham approved a foundation permit for VinFast to build a 995,000-square-foot general assembly area.

Later that month, VinFast welcomed local and state leaders, including Gov. Roy Cooper, to celebrate what the company promised would be its first factory outside of Vietnam — a facility capable of churning out 150,000 electric vehicles a year while employing 7,500 workers.
On Dec. 8, VinFast revised its assembly plant designs.
Instead of a 995,000-square-foot structure, it now asked Chatham for one that only covered 782,250 square feet.

Where the original assembly area design made a U-shape, the new layout is more rectangular, said David Camp, Chatham’s director of permits and inspections, as a truck loading dock featured in the middle of the original design was cut out.

Camp said the smaller footprint doesn’t necessarily indicate VinFast will scale back its promised production or employment at the site, noting it “could mean that they’re being more efficient about the space inside.”
On Dec. 13, Chatham County posted comments on the revised design.
Nine days later, the car company submitted additional vertical building plans that Camp said “did not address our comments.”
On Dec. 28, Chatham asked VinFast to submit a comprehensive plan for the assembly plant’s foundation and vertical design.
VinFast has not yet done this.
“No construction is being done until this permit revision is issued,” county spokesperson Kara Lusk said in an email Tuesday.
The general assembly building is one of several structures the car company has said it hopes to erect at the Moncure site.
Site plans submitted last summer included almost 3 million square feet spread across eight structures.
The Chicago-based developer Clayco is leading the project.
Chatham County is currently reviewing designs for an 850,000-square-foot body shop, and VinFast has received permits for retaining walls, which Lusk said are under construction.

VinFast announced the site in March 2022 after North Carolina and Chatham offered combined incentives of $1.25 billion, including more than $300 million in payroll tax benefits if the company meets its hiring and investment targets.
Under its state job development investment grant, VinFast pledged to create at least 1,997 jobs by the end of this year.
But the absence of building construction calls this aim into question as well as VinFast’s stated goal of producing electric vehicles in North Carolina by 2025.
The company previously postponed the plant’s scheduled opening from 2024.

New to manufacturing electric vehicles, VinFast has lost billions over the past three years as it seeks to overcome poor initial reviews, wobbling electric vehicle demand and a hypercompetitive auto market.
The automaker’s stock finished Thursday at an all-time low of around $3 a share.
This week, VinFast reported delivering 9,689 electric vehicles during the first three months of 2024, a 28% decrease over the previous quarter.
About half of these sales were made to Green and Smart Mobility, a Vietnamese taxi service controlled by VinFast’s parent company.

VinFast declined to share whether it still believes production can begin in Chatham County next year, though a company spokesperson said VinFast would share more information about its North Carolina operations on a quarterly earnings call Wednesday.

On the call Wednesday morning, company officials did not mention the Chatham factory in their prepared remarks, but in answering an investor’s question about the site, chairwoman Le Thi Thu Thuy said “North Carolina is still ongoing.”
“We’re still on track to start the operation by the end of next year,” Thuy said.
She added that hiring and putting in operations would happen by the end of next year, but full operations will take a couple of months.
source_title: Construction has stopped at VinFast’s NC site. Here’s why.  | Raleigh News & Observer

facts: VinFast had a record-setting economic development project in North Carolina.
North Carolina is suing VinFast over its failed assembly plant.
source_title: Timeline of VinFast plans in North Carolina - Triangle Business Journal

facts: The Chatham County Unified Development Ordinance (UDO) is a comprehensive set of regulations designed to guide the county’s growth and development while balancing environmental concerns, economic development, and community needs.
The UDO aims to ensure that new developments are compatible with the rural character of Chatham County while promoting sustainability.
Chapter 4 of the UDO imposes strict limits on building expansions for businesses in Chatham County.
Under the new regulations, any business that expands its physical footprint by more than 10% must bring its entire building up to current code.
Planning board member Eric Andrews states that a 10% expansion threshold is too restrictive compared to the national norm of 25 to 50%.
Andrews warns the 10% rule could discourage local businesses from expanding or upgrading facilities, potentially driving them to neighboring counties with more flexible regulations.
Businesses expanding under the new rules may face expensive upgrades for parking, landscaping, lighting, and energy efficiency.
The UDO mandates that large commercial buildings of 100,000 square feet or more install solar energy infrastructure.
Eric Andrews questions making solar installations mandatory for smaller developers who may lack the financial resources of large corporations.
Andrews advocates for strong recommendations for solar power instead of hard-and-fast rules to allow businesses flexibility.
The UDO mandates significant landscaping investments, including the installation of 10-foot trees and irrigation systems.
Andrews notes that requiring large trees adds hundreds of thousands of dollars to project costs, which gets passed on to consumers.
Andrews argues that research shows smaller plants have a higher survivability rate than the large trees required by the UDO.
The ordinance requires businesses to surround parking lots with hedges and other vegetation.
Andrews raises security concerns about vegetation in parking lots providing hiding spots for criminals.
The UDO includes a requirement for electric vehicle (EV) charging stations in new developments.
Eric Andrews cautions that demand for EV charging stations may not yet be high enough to justify the mandate.
Andrews argues the free market should dictate whether apartment complexes and other developments include EV charging stations to attract tenants.
Forcing developers to include EV charging stations now could result in unnecessary expenses passed on to consumers as higher rent or purchase prices.
Chapter 6 of the UDO redefines open space, stating only 10% of riparian buffers can be counted as open space in new developments.
Andrews cites American Planning Association guidelines, noting that 50 to 100% of riparian buffers are typically counted as open space across most of the country.
Limiting riparian buffer counting to 10% will force developers to set aside more prime land for open space, potentially driving up costs and limiting development land.
Andrews warns the new open space rules will have a huge impact on affordable housing by forcing developers to raise prices.
Local leaders face challenges managing development to preserve rural character while promoting economic growth and sustainability.
Andrews emphasizes the need to strike a better balance between promoting sustainability and allowing businesses/developers flexibility.
Andrews advocates for a more flexible, market-driven approach that considers the needs of local businesses, developers, and residents.
Andrews urges the county to listen to feedback from the business community and developers to ensure development makes sense for everyone.
source_title: Unpacking the challenges of Chatham County's Unified Development Ordinance - Chatham Journal Newspaper

facts: Battery company SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.

Friday marked the last working day for 958 plant employees, about 37% of its workforce, according to a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Korean company notably supplied the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the truck in December.

The U.S. electric vehicle market is at a standstill amid the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification in favor of more lax automotive emissions policies and a broader agenda supporting oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier said the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.
Collier stated SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
SK is pursuing future customers, including in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The City of Commerce and Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Ford said in December it would scrap the fully-electric version of its iconic pickup truck and opt for an extended-range version.
A Ford spokesperson could not comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford had together previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S.
The battery maker ended the joint venture with Ford in December.
SK is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff (Democrat) stated: “Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone.”
Ossoff added: “As predicted, Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy. We were booming and building new plants. Now Georgians are losing their jobs.”

SK has invested significantly in Jackson County, Georgia, in recent years as automakers shored up plans to spend billions to develop and build EVs with federal support under former President Joe Biden.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said the expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated a rush of green energy projects.
The 33 additional projects announced by the end of 2024 were the most nationwide, according to E2.
Projects in Georgia topped $20 billion, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.

Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, resumed normal production on Friday after temporarily reducing hours and pay last year because U.S. customs officials detained imported components needed to make solar panels.

facts: SK Innovation is constructing a new EV battery manufacturing plant in Commerce, Georgia.

facts: Steelmart Inc. processes and supplies steel to customers in Georgia, Alabama, South Carolina, North Carolina, and Tennessee.

facts: Rivian invested nearly $120 million to develop a 1.2 million square-foot supplier park and warehousing/logistics facility near its existing plant in Normal, Illinois.
