facts: Rivian Automotive has received a conditional commitment from the U.S. Department of Energy’s Advanced Technology Vehicle Manufacturing (ATVM) loan program for a loan of up to $6.6 billion (including $6 billion of principal and approximately $600 million of capitalized interest).
The loan is intended to accelerate Rivian's growth and leadership in electric vehicle design, development, and manufacturing in the United States.
If finalized, the loan would support the construction of Rivian’s next facility in Stanton Springs North, near the city of Social Circle, Georgia.
The new facility will substantially expand Rivian’s domestic production capacity to support demand from U.S. and international markets.
The funding will provide significant support for the production of Rivian's midsize platform, which underpins the R2, a midsize SUV, and the R3/R3X, a midsize crossover.
Rivian states that its R2 and R3 vehicle lines will be critical drivers in the company’s long-term growth and profitability.

Rivian plans to design and build a fully modern manufacturing facility employing modern construction techniques and advanced environmental management while preserving natural spaces and investing actively in surrounding communities.
Over the history of the ATVM loan program, DOE has provided billions in financing for American EV manufacturing, including loans to General Motors and Tesla.

The conditional commitment indicates DOE’s intent to finance the project, but technical, legal, environmental, and financial conditions must be satisfied before definitive financing documents are executed and funds are disbursed.
If finalized, the loan would be secured by all assets of the project, fixed assets, and guarantees of parent company Rivian Automotive, Inc. and certain of its subsidiaries.

A full-time Quality Inspector position is currently open in Pendergrass, GA.

The Quality Inspector role is responsible for quality inspection and control throughout the production process, including incoming materials, in-process operations, and final products.
The role ensures compliance with specifications and standards, identifies and controls non-conforming products, and supports continuous improvement to prevent defects.
The inspector performs incoming (IQC), in-process (IPQC), and final inspections (FQC) throughout the production process, from receiving through assembly, integration, testing, and shipment.

The inspector supports root cause analysis and corrective action activities.

The inspector supports cross-functional quality improvement and on-site support activities.

facts: A Norwegian firm has pulled the plug on a $2.6 billion factory in Newnan that was set to make utility-scale battery energy storage systems.
Freyr Battery informed the Coweta County Industrial Development Authority of its pullback in a letter last month.
Executives officially announced the plan during the authority’s board meeting on Feb. 6.
The company said it would offload the 368-acre site to an undisclosed buyer for $50 million.
The proceeds from the site sale will be used to repay the state and county for grants totaling $27 million toward site acquisition and preparation.
Coweta County contributed $20 million to win the investment.
A state deal-closing fund contributed another $7 million.
According to the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, Freyr’s state and local incentive package was valued at $358 million.
Property tax abatements and statutory incentives made up the majority of the total incentive package.
Statutory incentives are doled out over time and are tied to jobs created and ongoing expenditures on manufacturing equipment, salaries, and research and development.
The withdrawal wipes out 724 promised jobs.
Freyr’s departure deals a blow to Georgia’s booming new-energy sector, which has landed more than $20 billion in projects focused on electric vehicles, batteries, and solar power.
The $7.6 billion Hyundai Meta Plant near Savannah has already kicked into high gear.
SK Battery’s $2.6 billion complex is located in Commerce.
Hanwha Q Cells is located in Dalton and has helped the firm become the U.S.’s largest solar panel producer.
Other major projects in Georgia remain either under construction or stuck in neutral.
Rivian’s $5 billion automotive plant east of Atlanta was delayed last March.
A $6.6 billion Department of Energy loan approved in the final days of the Biden administration could revive the Rivian project.
Joint-venture battery investments by Hyundai in partnership with SK in Cartersville are valued at $5 billion.
Joint-venture battery investments by Hyundai in partnership with LG Energy Solution, co-located with the Meta Plant, are valued at $4.3 billion.
These joint-venture projects have yet to come on line.
The longer such projects remain in development, the greater the chance supportive underlying policies could shift with trade patterns and political winds.
Even before taking office in January, President Trump called to kill the $7,500 EV tax incentive included in the Biden administration’s Inflation Reduction Act.
Freyr executives reportedly said in August that a glut of Chinese batteries was reducing prices.
The glut of Chinese batteries is impeding efforts to raise capital for battery production.
In announcing the Georgia factory in 2022, Freyr framed domestically produced energy storage in national security terms.
This framing was given China’s dominant hold on the global battery supply chain.
Freyr’s Newnan project was initially meant to address the mismatch between when renewable energy is produced and when it is eventually used.
Battery-energy storage systems (BESS) allow the grid to store renewable energy when produced and release it when demand spikes.
Freyr’s new leadership decided to move upstream by producing solar panels.
Executives said producing solar panels would generate immediate sales and bolster the startup’s financial position.
Producing solar panels could also increase overall demand for the energy storage systems Freyr hopes to eventually bring to the United States.
In December, Freyr acquired the U.S. manufacturing assets of Trina Solar.
Trina Solar is a Chinese firm that had set up a factory in Wilmer, Texas, almost 20 miles south of Dallas.
The $340 million deal gave Freyr immediate access to 5GW worth of solar module production.
The acquisition made Freyr the third-largest domestic module producer right off the bat.
Freyr is searching for a separate U.S. site to make solar cells in a push for vertical integration in that supply chain.
American-made modules, cells, and raw materials qualify for tax rebates under the IRA.
These IRA tax rebates were a key driver of Freyr’s move to Newnan.
The pending sale of the Newnan property was set to close on Feb. 15.
Georgia does not seem to be in consideration for Freyr’s new solar manufacturing facility.
Freyr said it is moving its corporate headquarters to Austin, Texas.
Austin is a tech hub just a few hours’ drive from its newly acquired Dallas factory.
The Dallas factory employs more than 1,000 people.
Q Cells is the No. 1 panel producer in the U.S.
Q Cells is spending $2.5 billion across two sites in Cartersville and Dalton to build out more module capacity.
The project includes domestic sourcing for solar components like wafers, cells, and ingots in Cartersville.
Freyr went public on the New York Stock Exchange in 2021 via a merger with Alussa Energy.
The merger raised $704 million, including $115 million from Koch Strategic Platforms.
A few months after going public, Freyr and KSP launched a $70 million joint venture.
The joint venture aimed to create a U.S. counterpart to Freyr’s European planned giga-factory above the Arctic Circle.
They licensed battery technology from 24M.
Freyr has since ended its commercial arrangement with 24M.
Freyr has retained its cathode active material factory in Finland.
The Finnish factory is backed by a €122 million grant.
The Newnan project was announced in November 2022.
The project aimed to produce 34 GW worth of battery cells in its first phase.

facts: FREYR Battery, a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity, announced the selection and purchase of a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its planned Giga America battery plant on November 11, 2022.
The company plans to create 723 new jobs through the project.
FREYR Battery will invest more than $2.57 billion in Coweta County through 2029.
The total investment in Coweta County could be well over $2.57 billion, including planned value-enhancing upstream and downstream modules.
The new flagship manufacturing plant will be located at the Bridgeport Industrial Park.
FREYR Battery is based in Luxembourg.
The company’s battery cells have a variety of uses, including stationary energy storage (ESS) and electric mobility.
To allow for an expedited start to construction, FREYR is creating a strategy to carry out the project in a phased approach.
The new project execution plan takes into account the recently passed U.S. Inflation Reduction Act and soaring incremental customer demand for clean, next-generation batteries.
The project is expected to be developed in multiple phases beginning with an initial battery cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
SemiSolidTM technology from FREYR’s American licensing partner, 24M Technologies, will serve as the foundation for the project.
Lithium-ion battery manufacture can be done at scale with a high level of capital efficiency thanks to the simplified SemiSolidTM platform.
With local utility providers involvement, FREYR is currently reviewing clean power supply options for the project.
The long-term goal of the company is to adopt the same approach used at Giga Arctic to create some of the cleanest next-generation batteries.

Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman Bob Blackburn welcomed FREYR Battery to the community, stating that with its addition, Coweta County is quickly becoming a premier destination for top-tier manufacturing industries that support the U.S. economy and beyond.
The site of the new FREYR facility will be in the Bridgeport Industrial Park, where a mix of heavy and light industrial has been permitted since 2014.
FREYR chose to invest in Coweta County after an extensive and competitive site selection process.
The County’s workforce, location, and business-friendly climate helped it stand out from other locations.
Coweta County has a strong manufacturing workforce with the ability to attract talent from universities and technical colleges.
The robust transportation infrastructure of highway and rail provides easy access to ATL and the Port of Savannah.

Coweta County Development Authority Chairman John Daviston stated that collaboration was integral to the success of this project and that teamwork and intelligence sharing among local and statewide stakeholders built credence in the location business case.
FREYR Battery is looking to fill openings for both administrative, technical, and managerial positions.
FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen commented that expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from its inception.
FREYR Battery aims to provide industrial scale clean battery solutions to reduce global emissions.
FREYR Battery is listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
FREYR intends to install 50 GWh of battery cell capacity by 2025.
FREYR intends to install 100 GWh annual capacity by 2028 and 200 GWh of annual capacity by 2030.
FREYR has commenced building the first of its planned factories in Mo i Rana, Norway.
FREYR announced potential development of industrial scale battery cell production in Vaasa, Finland, and the United States.

facts: FREYR Battery purchased a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its planned Giga America battery plant.

facts: Faces $160,727 in Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) fines for exposing employees to unsafe levels of lead.
OSHA announced the fines on September 27 based on personal air monitoring of workers and lead accumulation on respirators and counters in lunch break areas.
Cited for two repeat violations after inspections found the company failed to have engineering and work practice controls in place to reduce exposures to lead.
Failed to prevent lead from accumulating on surfaces at the facility.
Cited for two serious violations after finding that the company exposed employees to lead levels in concentrations above the permissible exposure limit (PEL).
Did not ensure employees’ respirators were properly cleaned and disinfected.
Since 2018, federal safety inspections at the Augusta facility have identified numerous violations related to lead exposures.
In 2018, OSHA issued citations for nine serious violations, one repeat violation, and one other-than-serious violation at the facility.
In a 2019 follow-up inspection, OSHA found one serious, one repeat, and one other-than-serious violation.
Founded in 1926, designs and manufactures deep-cycle batteries for scissor lifts, golf carts, boats, and other uses.
Company has locations in Corona, California, and Evans and Augusta, Georgia.

OSHA’s Atlanta-east area office director Josh Turner stated that elevated lead levels can cause debilitating and permanent health issues and employers are legally responsible for minimizing exposure via engineering controls, safe work practices, and clean protective equipment.

facts: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells announced a multi-year commitment to install reliable, affordable, American-made storage products for Qcells’ development projects across the country.
The agreement provides American-made energy storage projects across a portfolio of utility-scale sites.

All projects will meet U.S. domestic content requirements.
The agreement includes 5GWh of lithium-ion energy storage projects for utility-scale projects.
The agreement includes lifecycle services from LG Energy Solution Vertech.
This agreement supports Qcells EPC projects scheduled from 2028 – 2030.
In May 2024, Qcells entered into battery purchase agreements with LG Energy Solution Vertech totaling 4.8 GWh of BESS projects developed across the U.S.
The partnership secures energy storage systems for Qcells that meet domestic content requirements.
LG Energy Solution’s battery energy storage products are made in Michigan.
Qcells’ solar panels are made in Georgia.
Leveraging made-in-America products supports U.S. manufacturing jobs, strengthens the nation’s clean-energy supply chains, enhances grid reliability, and reduces exposure to trade risks and tariffs.
Chris Hodrick is CEO of Qcells EPC.
Qcells operates the largest solar panel manufacturing facility of its kind in the United States, based in Dalton, Georgia.
The company is building a second factory in Cartersville, Georgia.
The Cartersville factory will manufacture ingots, wafers, cells and finished panels.
This second factory represents the largest investment in U.S. solar history to create an integrated, sustainable domestic supply chain.
Once fully operational, Qcells’ annual solar panel production capacity in the US will reach 8.4 gigawatts.
This capacity equals nearly 46,000 panels every day.
The production is enough to power more than 1.3 million American homes each year.
Jaehong Park is CEO and President at LG Energy Solution Vertech.
The energy storage systems included in this commitment are built using the latest LG Energy Solution containerized lithium-ion battery solutions with LFP cells manufactured at U.S. facilities.

facts: Two workers were killed this week in separate workplace accidents at two of Georgia’s most high-profile manufacturing sites: the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America battery plant near Savannah and the Hanwha Qcells solar panel plant in Cartersville.
The deaths occurred within six weeks of the death of Ronald Adams, Sr. at the Stellantis Dundee Engine Complex in Michigan.
Adams, a 63-year-old worker, was killed on April 7 when an overhead gantry engaged, pinning him to a conveyor and causing fatal crushing injuries to his upper torso.
On Tuesday, May 20, a construction worker was killed at the HL-GA Battery facility, part of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America complex in Ellabell, Georgia.
The worker was fatally injured when an unsecured load fell from a forklift during loading operations shortly before 11:00 a.m.
The identity of the worker, who was employed by a subcontractor involved in the construction of the battery plant, has not been released.
The HL-GA Battery plant is not yet operational and is a key part of Hyundai’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
The HL-GA Battery plant is slated to supply batteries for Hyundai’s new electric vehicle assembly plant.
In March of this year, Sunbok You, a 67-year-old worker, was struck and dragged by a forklift at the Hyundai Metaplant construction site, resulting in fatal injuries.
In April 2023, Victor Gamboa, a 43-year-old contractor, died after falling 60 feet when his safety line failed at the same site.
An OSHA investigation cited contractors for failing to provide workers with adequate safety gear.
Repeated workplace deaths indicate that worker safety is being subordinated to Hyundai Motor Group’s drive to speed up the project and cut costs ahead of the battery facility opening.
On Monday, May 19, a fatality occurred at the Hanwha Qcells solar panel plant in Cartersville, Georgia.
Firefighters responded to a call at 7:15 p.m. and found Marion Jose Rugama, a 33-year-old resident of Norcross, dead on top of a tank.
It has not been established if Rugama was a direct employee of Hanwha Qcells or a contractor, though contract labor use is widespread at the facility.
Local authorities reported the oxygen level at the top of the tank was only 15 percent, far below the minimum required to sustain life.
A nitrogen leak is suspected as the cause of Rugama’s death, pending autopsy and toxicology screening.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Ellabell, Georgia is the largest single economic development project in Georgia’s history.
HMGMA is backed by Hyundai and partners including LG Energy Solution and SK On.
The sprawling complex is expected to employ 8,500 workers by 2031 and includes both vehicle assembly and battery manufacturing operations.
The facility is being constructed at breakneck speed with the goal of making Georgia a hub for electric vehicle and battery production.
The Hanwha Qcells plant in Cartersville is part of a $2.5 billion investment by the South Korean clean energy giant.
Hanwha Qcells also operates a major facility in Dalton, Georgia.
Together, the Hyundai and Hanwha plants form the largest solar manufacturing operation in the Western Hemisphere.
There are plans to expand the Cartersville facility even further in 2025.
Neither the workforce at Hyundai Metaplant nor the Hanwha Qcells Cartersville facility are unionized.
The use of contract labor allows companies to cut costs, evade responsibility, and fragment the workforce.
Contract workers are often paid less, receive fewer benefits, have less job security than direct employees, and are less likely to receive adequate safety training.
According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, 5,486 workers died on the job nationwide in 2022, with transportation, construction, and manufacturing ranking among the deadliest industries.
In February 2024, OSHA reported an investigation of the Columbus, Georgia facility of Aludyne Columbus LLC, a prominent global manufacturer of auto parts with 30 production facilities worldwide.
OSHA’s findings uncovered 22 serious violations and were followed by proposed penalties of $182,344.
Violations included failure to provide protective gear and enforce safety protocols where employees worked dangerously close to energized power lines without insulated tools.
Employees at the Aludyne facility were exposed to airborne silica at levels up to 15 times higher than the permissible limit.
Workers lacked proper respiratory equipment, and the company failed to conduct fit testing for those exposed to silicosis hazards.
OSHA Atlanta-West Area Office Director Jeffery Stawowy stated that Aludyne Columbus LLC’s failure to prioritize employee safety nearly cost a worker their life and allowed overexposure to silica.
Workers at StarPlus Energy, a joint venture between Stellantis and Samsung SDI in Kokomo, Indiana, exposed dangerous working conditions on social media.
Workers reported no medical emergency response, no safety data sheets, and no workers’ comp doctors in the facility.
Injured workers at StarPlus Energy must climb four flights of stairs to seek assistance.
The International Workers Alliance of Rank-and-File Committees (IWA-RFC) launched an independent investigation into the death of Ronald Adams.
Stellantis, the UAW apparatus, and MIOSHA have refused to release information about the circumstances or official investigation into Adams’s fatality.
source_title: 2 workers killed in separate fatal workplace accidents in Georgia - World Socialist Web Site

facts: Electrification will have a cascading impact on energy infrastructure, extending from the electric grid to homes and buildings.
Eaton is developing comprehensive electric vehicle charging infrastructure (EVCI) solutions and services.
Eaton's EVCI solutions unite the power needs of buildings and electric vehicles with on-site renewable energy generation.

Eaton provides planning, deployment, and management services for sustainable systems supporting safe and reliable electric vehicle charging to building owners/managers, builders, consultants, designers, homeowners, and fleet managers.
Eaton's approach utilizes scalable, secure, resilient energy hubs to power buildings, homes, facilities, and charge EVs with reliable, sustainable, cost-effective energy.
Eaton offers a customizable decentralized energy approach to help customers prepare for upcoming demand.
Eaton is developing a comprehensive portfolio of EVCI software, services, and hardware.
Eaton designs its comprehensive solution to integrate EV charging infrastructure and distributed energy resources (DERs).
Eaton's solutions can optimize sustainability, resiliency, and cost-effectiveness.
Planning and careful design are required to add electric vehicle charging to new or existing buildings for efficient, tailored infrastructure.
Eaton leverages more than 100 years of experience, technology leadership, and extensive research to provide end-to-end project management capabilities.
Eaton supports its project management with field engineers and teams across the region.
eMobility is a new business within Eaton that combines elements of its Electrical and Vehicle businesses.
The eMobility business delivers electric vehicle solutions to passenger car, commercial vehicle, and off-highway OEMs.
Eaton is helping companies and communities around the world simplify their energy systems and safely transition to a low-carbon future as the energy transition advances.

facts: Duracell Inc., a leading U.S. battery manufacturer, has announced a $56 million investment to establish its new R&D global headquarters at Georgia Tech’s Science Square in Atlanta, Georgia.
The relocation of Duracell's R&D facility from Bethel, Connecticut, to Atlanta is scheduled to open in Summer 2026.
The investment will create 110 high-paying jobs with average projected salaries of $120,000.
Georgia Tech president Ángel Cabrera stated the collaboration bridges academia and industry to foster innovative ideas and groundbreaking advancements.
The new hub is located at Science Square, a research innovation district adjacent to Georgia Tech.
Science Square is developed in partnership with commercial real estate firm Trammell Crow Co.
The Science Square development spans 18 acres and features more than 1.8 million square feet of lab and office space, along with apartments and retail space.
The first phase of Science Square opened last year, and Duracell's new hub will occupy a substantial portion of the building.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp noted that Georgia has set itself apart as a leader in attracting innovative companies due to its research institutions, world-class logistics network, and pro-business environment.
Duracell's relationship with Georgia dates back to 1980, when the company began running a manufacturing facility in LaGrange.
Duracell operates a distribution plant in Fairburn that began operation in 2000.
Duracell chief technology officer Liben Hailu described the move to Atlanta as a significant milestone for driving innovation in battery technology for years to come.
Duracell's presence is expected to enhance battery research, development, and manufacturing activities already thriving in Georgia.

facts: EnviroSpark Energy Solutions announced it has secured a $50 million investment from funds advised by Basalt Infrastructure Partners LLP.
Basalt is a leading mid-market infrastructure equity investment firm focusing on investments in Europe and North America.
The investment will fuel EnviroSpark’s mission to revolutionize the EV charging landscape, enabling rapid expansion of its owned and operated network across the US.
The funding will support innovation of cutting-edge technologies and enhance accessibility and sustainability of EV infrastructure.
This funding builds upon the momentum of a $15 million funding round led by Ultra Capital in 2022.
EnviroSpark is a Georgia-based company that has established itself as a key player in the EV charging market.
The company maintains a broad footprint of over 8,200 charging plugs across North America.
Aaron Luque serves as co-founder and CEO of EnviroSpark.
Aaron Luque stated that securing the funding marks a pivotal moment for the company and reinforces its commitment to making electric mobility accessible and convenient for all.
Andrew Marsden serves as Basalt’s Head of Energy Transition.
Andrew Marsden stated that Basalt is investing in compelling scale-up opportunities in low carbon infrastructure and is partnering with EnviroSpark to drive their next phase of growth.
Andrew Marsden noted that EnviroSpark's industry-leading team is accelerating the build-out of the EV-charging network across North America at a pivotal time for the clean transportation sector.
EnviroSpark has cultivated strategic partnerships with RaceTrac, Waffle House, IHG Hotels & Resorts, Ford Dealerships, HHHunt, and Asset Living.
EnviroSpark maintains longstanding partnerships with Tesla, Volkswagen, Volta (recently acquired by Shell), and Starwood Capital Group.
EnviroSpark has partnered with the U.S. Federal Government through the General Services Administration to drive widespread commercial and government EV adoption.
EnviroSpark secured National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) awards in Georgia and Tennessee.
The Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) selected EnviroSpark to construct significant DCFC charging projects in Tifton and Metter, Georgia.
The Tennessee Department of Transportation (TDOT) selected EnviroSpark for installations at a Waffle House in Lakeland, Tennessee.
The NEVI awards are funded by the Federal government through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
EnviroSpark was founded in Atlanta, Georgia, by Aaron and Stephanie Luque in 2014.
EnviroSpark is an industry leader in the turnkey design, installation, and operation of EV charging solutions.
The company was founded with two missions: to raise awareness around the benefits of electric vehicles and to build a more robust EV charging infrastructure.
EnviroSpark has been responsible for more than 8,200 installations of charging plugs across North America to date.
EnviroSpark addresses pain points of property owners and drivers by enhancing EV accessibility in customer-friendly ways.
source_title: EV Charging Company EnviroSpark Secures $50 Million Investment from Basalt Infrastructure Partners - Latest - Atlanta Tech News - Technology Headlines, Job Listings, and Events in Georgia

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia selected West Point in Troup County for a $1.1 billion assembly plant in 2006, attracted by available land, interstate access via I-85, and state incentives including tax abatements.
The Kia facility opened in 2009, creating 2,700 direct jobs and spurring over 15,000 indirect positions in suppliers and services.
The plant's expansions include a 2023 $200 million investment for electric vehicle production like the EV9, adding nearly 200 jobs.
Kia Georgia (now Hyundai) assembly plant has attracted approximately 30 related supplier projects since 2007, bolstering automotive and component manufacturing.
Manufacturing accounts for approximately 30% of private-sector employment in Troup County as of early 2025, with 6,754 workers based on 2023 data.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia is the state's sole dedicated automotive assembly plant, directly employing over 3,000 workers and generating more than 15,000 regional jobs including suppliers.
Other significant manufacturing subsectors include batteries, films, and chemicals, supported by companies like Duracell and Milliken.
Hyundai TRANSYS announced a $240 million transmission facility in 2020, operational by 2022, adding 300 jobs.
Lee Kum Kee's $288 million sauce manufacturing plant (2024) projects 267 positions.
Walmart and UPS distribution centers are major logistics employers leveraging I-85 access.
Caterpillar has a heavy equipment production facility in the county.
A March 2025 proposal for Project West, a $9.7 billion data center campus spanning up to 1.5 million square feet near LaGrange, is expected to generate hundreds of high-wage positions.
County-wide economic development initiatives have exceeded $800 million in commitments, leveraging industrial parks like the Greater LaGrange Industrial Business Park.
The county's labor market featured an annual average unemployment rate of 3.2% in 2023, rising to 3.6% in 2024 from historic lows of 2.4% in 2023.
Building a robust workforce pipeline remains critical due to gaps in talent development hindering scaling for high-tech projects.
Challenges persist in workforce training and adapting to automation at the Kia plant expansions.
Gross domestic product increased from $3.68 billion in 2020 to $4.77 billion in 2023, underscoring resilient growth amid national disruptions.

The county's economy transitioned from textiles to automotive manufacturing following the Kia plant opening, with sustained economic momentum despite housing supply deficits and labor market pressures.
source_title: Troup County, Georgia — Grokipedia

facts: A 3MW solar photovoltaic (PV) project has been completed and commercialized by United Renewable Energy at Kimberly-Clark’s facility in LaGrange, Georgia.

The project was a collaboration between Kimberly-Clark’s facility, United Renewable Energy LLC as the project developer and builder, and NextEra Energy Resources, LLC through a subsidiary acting as the long-term project owner.
All output from the new solar facility and all renewable energy credits (RECs) are sold to Georgia Power as part of Georgia Power’s Renewable Energy Development Initiative.
The associated RECs will be retired on behalf of all Georgia Power customers.
The 3MW solar project includes over 8,600 solar panels.
Kimberly-Clark leases the land for the project and entered a separate agreement to purchase RECs from NextEra Energy Resources to offset its greenhouse gas emissions.
Jeremy Cannady serves as mill manager at Kimberly-Clark’s LaGrange facility.
Kimberly-Clark is pursuing a global goal of a 40% offset in greenhouse gas emissions by 2022.
William Silva is the CEO of United Renewable Energy.
Matt Ulman is the vice president of distributed generation for NextEra Energy Resources.
NextEra Energy Resources has approximately 21,000 megawatts of net generating capacity as of year-end 2018.
NextEra Energy Resources operates clean, emissions-free nuclear power generation facilities in New Hampshire, Iowa and Wisconsin.
NextEra Energy Resources is a subsidiary of Juno Beach, Florida-based NextEra Energy, Inc.
source_title: Kimberly-Clark, United Renewable Energy® LLC and NextEra Energy Resources Team Up to Construct Solar Project in LaGrange, Georgia – United Renewable Energy

facts: Kia is expanding its West Point assembly plant in Georgia to produce the fully electric EV9 SUV.
The facility expansion represents a $200 million investment.
Production of the model-year 2024 EV9 will begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Expanding the production line will create around 200 jobs in Troup County, Georgia.
The Kia Georgia plant currently produces the Telluride, Sorento, Sportage, and K5 models, comprising three gasoline SUVs and one gasoline sedan.
The EV9 will mark Kia's first entry into electric vehicle production across North America.
Sean Yoon, president and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that the EV9 has the potential to be another 'game changer' for Kia, similar to the Telluride.
Yoon noted that the EV9 will be the most sophisticated vehicle Kia has ever built and will stand out in the EV market and on the road.
The EV9 combines design elements from the award-winning Telluride SUV with electric vehicle features from the award-winning EV6, aiming to mix capability, technology, and refined elegance.
Assuming a $10,000 profit per unit, Kia would need to sell 20,000 units to break even on the expansion investment.
source_title: Kia Georgia Production Plant Expanding For EV9 - CleanTechnica

facts: Federal government conducted a raid on a construction site at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America compound near Savannah, Georgia.
More than 400 individuals were reportedly detained during the immigration enforcement action.
A vast majority of the detained individuals are said to be South Korean nationals.
The US Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives claimed the federal government apprehended around 450 people in the raid on Thursday.
Around 300 of those apprehended individuals are from South Korea.

ICE spokesperson Lindsay Williams stated the raid focused on the construction site for a new large EV battery plant, which had been previously touted as a major manufacturing win for the state and for America.
The raid is part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.
The raid involved officers from the Georgia State Patrol, FBI, DEA, ATF, Homeland Security, ICE, and possibly more in one of the largest immigration enforcement actions taken by any agency in years.
A Hyundai spokesperson stated the company is cooperating fully but doesn't believe any of the detained individuals were Hyundai employees.
Hyundai confirmed awareness of the incident at the HL-GA Battery Company construction site in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai stated it is closely monitoring the situation and working to understand specific circumstances.
Hyundai understands that none of those detained are directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai prioritizes the safety and well-being of everyone working at the site and complies with all laws and regulations wherever it operates.
Hyundai is committed to full compliance with employment verification requirements and immigration laws in every market where it operates.
Hyundai expects the same commitment from all partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
The safety and well-being of everyone working at Hyundai sites, whether directly employed or for suppliers, is a top priority.
Incidents like this highlight the importance of robust oversight throughout Hyundai's entire supply chain and contractor network.
Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure all parties maintain high standards of legal compliance.
This includes thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
North America Chief Manufacturing Officer Chris Susock will assume governance of the entire megasite in Georgia.
Hyundai will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and their subcontractors comply with laws and regulations.
Hyundai has zero tolerance for those who don't follow the law.

Hyundai continues to invest in American manufacturing and create thousands of jobs in full accordance with U.S. law and in a manner that reflects its values of treating all people with dignity and respect.
CNN reports that video showed US agents having construction workers in hard hats line up to show documentation.
The nationalities and identities of hundreds of apprehended people have not been released.
South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong confirmed a 'large' number of South Korean nationals were detained by the US government.
Lee Jaewoong stated that business activities of investors and rights of nationals must not be unjustly infringed during US law enforcement.
Diplomats from South Korea's Washington D.C. embassy were dispatched to Atlanta to form an on-site response team for the situation.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama has reportedly stopped producing the Electrified GV70, possibly for good.
Construction activity at the new battery plant has ceased.
The battery plant is being built through a joint venture between Hyundai and LG, dubbed HL-GA Battery Company.
The joint venture stated it was cooperating fully with appropriate authorities.
The new battery plant was slated to go online next year.
The plant is said to be the largest single economic development in the history of the State of Georgia when completed and lumped into the rest of the 3,000-acre facility.
The attached Georgia EV manufacturing plant did not have operations interrupted by the raid.
The EV plant went online in March with production of Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 EVs.
The EV plant is expanding to more capacity for producing Kia-branded models next year.
The EV plant will add Genesis-branded vehicle production further in the future.
Hyundai's current investment total for its Georgia manufacturing footprint is around $7.6 billion and growing.
source_title: ICE Raid Detains Hundreds Of Workers At Hyundai's Georgia Battery Factory

facts: Hyundai's Georgia EV and hybrid plant utilizes AI-powered robots, drones, and quality-control systems.
The facility's technology manages inventory, final inspections, and the parking of finished vehicles.
At America's largest car manufacturing plant, nearly all operations are powered by AI.
Drones are used to buzz and scan stacked pallets to inventory parts.
Robotic arms swivel through programmed arcs to assist in manufacturing.
Cameras direct sanding tools to paint out blemishes on vehicles.
Uncrewed taxis transport vehicles and parts to their next manufacturing destination.
A four-legged Boston Dynamics AI dog named Spot walks through the facility scanning components to ensure they are snapped into place.
The facility, called Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, is roughly equivalent to 278 football fields in size and carries a nearly $7.6 billion price tag according to the company.
The HMGMA facility celebrated its grand opening in March.
Unlike other US automakers that are retrofitting decade-old plants, Hyundai built HMGMA from the ground up with AI, Nvidia chips, and robotics at its core.
Each vehicle built in the plant runs through at least 23 AI-based or robotic systems before it is finalized.
Industry analysts told Business Insider that Hyundai's AI-first approach could help navigate policy changes and customer trends impacting the carmaker's bottom line.
The technology improves quality control measures and reduces manufacturing costs in ways traditional manufacturing cannot match.
Erin Keating, an executive analyst at Cox Automotive, told Business Insider that Hyundai's integration of humanoid robots sets a new benchmark for smart manufacturing.
Keating noted that the AI integration potentially offers cost savings and allows Hyundai and others to respond faster to industrial disruptions.
At the center of Hyundai's Georgia operation is an integrated digital command hub acting as a digital twin, or a computer simulation mirroring the physical plant in real time.

Miles Johnson, Hyundai's communications representative for its electric-powered Ioniq-brand vehicles and tech, stated that sensors collect data across the production floor and an AI algorithm creates a virtual replica of each manufacturing step.
Johnson told Business Insider that AI plays a significant role in predicting optimized outcomes and identifying root causes of production issues.
If a quality issue arises, the AI system searches past trained data to identify the likely cause and suggest a corrective action plan.
Keating said Hyundai's AI can detect defects in real time, analyze root causes, and suggest corrective actions that significantly reduce rework, scrap, and warranty claims.
The brand's two retro-modern electric vehicles, the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 SUVs, are built using these highly technical processes.
The plant will eventually serve Hyundai's other brands, with Kia production set to begin in 2026 and Genesis vehicles planned for an unspecified future date.
In a press release, Hyundai stated that AI and robotics systems will assist humans, who will remain in charge of on-the-ground robotics maintenance, data analysis, and parts installation.
Hundreds of roles now involve programming the factory's 850 robots, troubleshooting AI, or interpreting production data instead of performing repetitive physical tasks.
The company aims to hire 8,500 employees at HMGMA by 2031 and has dubbed the project the largest single business investment in Georgia's history.
In March, Hyundai reported it had already hired 1,000 full-time staff members at the plant.
The company built a nearby Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia to train a pipeline of college-level talent for the plant.
The training program is in coordination with three local technical colleges and is expected to open this year.
Hyundai's massive AI investment faces significant challenges in the current automotive market.
Both the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 were positioned to benefit from then-President Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act.
The cars are eligible for $7,500 tax credits for EVs assembled in American plants.
Americans' uptake of EVs has underperformed analysts' predictions since Hyundai broke ground on HMGMA in October 2022.
EV adoption is facing federal pushback as subsidies for EVs are set to end in September.
Hyundai is betting that its EVs, including the three-row Ioniq 9 which launched in May 2025, will attract buyers without needing tax incentives.
Some industry analysts say Hyundai's AI-powered manufacturing can still succeed even if it currently relies on EV sales.
AI advantages like real-time quality control and faster problem solving could prove crucial as automakers face pressure from increasingly cheap Chinese vehicles and ongoing supply chain volatility.
Morningstar analyst David Whiston said companies adopting AI for manufacturing and logistics are better positioned to manage costs and respond to disruptions.

Keating said automakers leveraging AI for smart factories, autonomous logistics, and predictive analytics will be better positioned to scale production efficiently and meet regulatory and consumer demands faster.
Keating added that AI and robotics are becoming non-negotiable for competitiveness.
source_title: Hyundai built its newest factory around AI-powered technology like robotics and digital twins - AOL

facts: Hyundai’s first three-row electric SUV, the IONIQ 9, has officially entered production at its massive new EV plant in Georgia.
The company held the grand opening on Wednesday at its massive new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The facility will build upcoming Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV and hybrid vehicles.
Construction began less than two years prior to the opening ceremony.
The new EV plant is part of a $7.6 billion investment in Georgia.
This investment represents the state’s largest economic development project to date.
The facility is expected to create about 8,500 direct jobs by 2030.
Including battery joint ventures with SK On and LG Energy Solutions plus 18 suppliers, Hyundai’s new facility has attracted over $12.6 billion in investments.
According to the Center for Automotive Research, Hyundai’s investments will create nearly 40,000 jobs in Georgia.
During the grand opening, Hyundai Motor Group executive chair Euisun Chung said, “Together with the talented workforce at HMGMA, we are building the future of mobility with America, in America.”
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz, and Kia CEO Ho Sung Song attended the opening ceremony.
The new plant will eventually produce up to 500,000 EVs and hybrids under the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands each year.
Initially, the facility will produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
Despite the recent opening ceremony, the first model, the 2025 Hyundai IONIQ 5, rolled off the assembly line in October.
The IONIQ 9 features an upgraded electric SUV with up to 318 miles of range, a new interior and exterior style, and an NACS port for charging at Tesla Superchargers.
Hyundai confirmed the IONIQ 9 is now officially in production and expects it to launch any day or this spring.
Like the 2025 IONIQ 5, the IONIQ 9 will include an NACS port.
The IONIQ 9 packs a massive 110.3 kWh battery, providing an EPA-estimated range of up to 335 miles.
The vehicle’s “lounge-like” interior boasts up to 1,322 liters of cargo space, which is more than Kia’s three-row EV9 (up to 1,233 liters).
The IONIQ 9 measures 5,060 mm (199.2″) long, 1,980 mm (78″) wide, and 1,790 mm (70.5″) tall, making it slightly bigger than the EV9.
Hyundai will reveal prices closer to launch, but the IONIQ 9 is expected to start at about $60,000.
The IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 will be joined by the first Kia vehicle built at the facility in 2026, possibly Kia’s EV4 electric sedan.
The opening ceremony follows Hyundai’s landmark $21 billion investment in the US announced earlier this week.
Hyundai will use $9 billion of that investment to boost Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brand output to 1.2 million vehicles.
Another $6 billion from the US investment will be used to build a new steel plant in Louisiana.

GA Senator Jon Ossoff warned during a press conference on Wednesday that the Trump Administration’s war on electric vehicles poses a threat to EV sales, the EV market, and the automobile industry in Georgia and across the country.
Ossoff stated that killing off federal tax credits will only put the US further behind China and others in the global auto and tech industry.
source_title: Hyundai IONIQ 9 enters production as Georgia EV plant opens

facts: Homeland Security Investigations targeting four individuals led to the detainment of about 475 people on Thursday, Sept. 4.
A U.S. Department of Justice press release referred to the HSI and ICE raid at HL-GA Battery Company, LLC as Operation Low Voltage.
The battery plant is located at 9730 US Highway 280 in Ellabell, Ga.
The search warrant named Andreina Fuentes-Tovar, Kevin Zavaleta-Ramirez, David Zavaleta-Ramirez and Julio Gonzalez Alvarado as Target Persons of the Sept. 4 raid.
Approximately 400 officers across multiple federal and Georgia agencies assisted with the operation.
The battery plant was first announced on Friday, May 26, 2023 as a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution.
The plant is part of the state's economic agreement with Hyundai to build an electric vehicle manufacturing plant and affiliate facilities in Bryan County.
Production of EV batteries was slated to begin later in 2023 once construction was completed.
LG was responsible for the governance of the HL-GA facility, including during its construction, until the raid.
LG officials announced it is cooperating fully with authorities and has paused construction on the site.
The warrant identifies the HL-GA plant as a 35-acre site within the larger Hyundai megasite.
The megasite houses or will house five Hyundai affiliates: Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, Hyundai Transys, Hyundai Steel, and Hyundai Material.
Hyundai's economic agreement with Georgia requires all companies on the megasite to create and sustain a combined 6,800 to 8,500 permanent jobs by Dec. 31, 2031.
The agreement mandates that the jobs' salaries must average $58,105.
The battery plant initially committed to fulfilling 1,600 of those required jobs and later committed to 400 more for a total of 2,000.
HMGMA officials stated it aims to hire around 2,600 workers.
Hyundai's affiliates will cover the remaining positions, though exact hiring numbers for each have not been made public.
Roughly 300 of the detainees were Korean.
LG Communications and Government Affairs Manager Phil Lienert confirmed that 47 of those detained are LG employees, with 46 being Korean and one Indonesian.
About 250 detainees are affiliated with HL-GA's partner companies, with most being Korean but exact numbers and nationalities needing confirmation.
Individuals detained in the Sept. 4 operation were immediately taken to Folkston Detention Center in South Georgia.
In June, Charlton County approved a $50 million contract with ICE to expand Folkston, which would make it the largest detention center in the country.
Some workers may have relied on the U.S. Visa Waiver Program and ESTA for stays of 90 days or less without obtaining a visa.
Reuters reported that many Korean detainees had After-Sales Service and Installation Visas, otherwise known as B-1 Visas.
Atlanta-based immigration lawyer Charles Kuck affirmed many Korean detainees were in the country on B-1 visas for business consulting, contract negotiation, or short-term training.
No evidence had been shared confirming that any detained person used these authorizations to work or contract at the HL-GA plant.
The warrant for Operation Low Voltage was filed Tuesday, Sept. 2, commanding execution on or before Sept. 14.
U.S. Magistrate Judge Christopher Ray of the Southern District of Georgia signed the warrant.
Assistant United States Attorney Tania Groover filed a request to unseal the warrant on behalf of U.S. Attorney Meg Heap.
Investigators on the case include Groover, Ryan Bondura, Kelsey Scanlon, and E. Greg Gilluly, Jr.
The warrant sought to seize documents related to ownership and management of HL-GA and contractors TK LLC, Autorica LLC, SBY America, Beyond Iron Construction LLC, and Steel Brothers Development Inc.
Sought documentation included time cards, payroll information, photographs of employees, Visas, permanent resident cards, employment authorization cards, passports, and birth certificates.
Beyond Iron's site address is listed as 1 LG Road, Ellabell, Georgia, 31308 with a mailing address at 3229 Weyhill Trail, Buford, Georgia, 30519.
Whether the pause on construction will impact EV production at HMGMA remains unknown.
HMGMA had asserted it will produce 500,000 units annually once fully operational.
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz stated in March that those units would include hybrid vehicles at what was initially touted as an all-EV plant.

The Trump Administration froze $5 billion for EV charger infrastructure for America's highways in February, which lasted about six months until a federal judge ruled in favor of states suing the government.
Aspen Aerogels Inc. pulled out of its contract to construct a $325-million plant in Statesboro in February.
The Trump Administration removed federal tax credits for EVs, making $4,000 for used EVs or $7,500 for new ones unavailable after Sept. 30.
source_title: LG battery plant ICE operation: Details emerge after raid of Hyundai megasite in Georgia

facts: Kia America has officially started production of the Sportage Hybrid (HEV) at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The production start marks three major milestones for HMGMA: its first Kia model assembly, its first hybrid electric vehicle assembly, and the third vehicle to join the plant's production line.
The Sportage Hybrid joins the fully electric Hyundai IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 at the Savannah facility.
HMGMA was designed to integrate diverse powertrains with minimal modifications.
HMGMA has the structural capacity to build up to 10 different vehicle models across the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
The Metaplant represents Kia’s second major industrial investment in Georgia, complementing its existing assembly plant in West Point.
With the addition of HMGMA, Kia now commands a total annual production capacity of up to 550,000 vehicles in the United States.

Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that the Metaplant in Savannah is Kia's second major investment in Georgia and a testament to confidence in the state’s future as an automotive powerhouse.
Sean Yoon expressed confidence that Metaplant will continue the strong momentum achieved by the Sportage Hybrid since it was updated last year to quickly become one of Kia's highest volume models.
To prepare for production, HMGMA personnel underwent extensive training, including travel to Kia’s West Point facility and operations in South Korea to align with global manufacturing standards.
HMGMA was engineered from day one with a highly flexible manufacturing system that allows seamless integration of different powertrains with minimal modifications.
The start of local hybrid assembly allows Kia to optimize its logistics and expand the volume of SUVs built directly within the U.S. market.
Production duties across Georgia are split between two facilities.
Kia West Point assembles the internal combustion (ICE) versions of the Sportage and Sorento, alongside the Telluride, EV6, and EV9.
HMGMA in Savannah assembles the Sportage Hybrid (HEV), alongside the Hyundai IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
During the official launch ceremony, the first production Sportage Hybrid was delivered to the stage via an Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR), showcasing the factory's advanced automation systems.
Company officials stated that future product allocation plans for the plant are not being released at this time.
source_title: Kia Begins Sportage Hybrid Production in Georgia

facts: A massive electric truck factory is coming to rural Georgia, with plans to create thousands of jobs and produce 400,000 emissions-free trucks each year.
The project is called the Rivian Electric Vehicle Factory.
Rivian has promised to invest $5 billion in the project.
The project is being hailed as one of the largest economic development projects in Georgia’s history.
It aims to accelerate the transition to cleaner energy and create jobs.
It will be the state’s first electric vehicle factory.
The government of Georgia and the Georgia state government are trying to foster this development.
They’re investing $150 million in incentives for an EV assembly plant to be built in the state.
It’s expected to create 7,500 jobs.
It’s unclear whether this new factory will be able to meet those goals.
The state’s economic development officials have promised incentives to the company.
Some local residents have voiced their concerns over the project.
Some are worried about the potential environmental impact.
Others say that the factory will change the character of the rural community.
While there is much controversy surrounding the project, the state has been making strides to attract the growing electric vehicle industry to the state.
The governor of Georgia, Brian Kemp, has launched the Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (GEMIA), which aims to attract more EV manufacturing to the state.
President Biden has made electric vehicles a priority and a part of his climate change initiative.
Rivian has announced plans to build a new truck factory in Georgia.
The 2,000-acre site will span Morgan and Walton counties, about 45 miles east of Atlanta.
It will be able to produce up to 400,000 electric vehicles annually.
Rivian already operates a plant in Illinois.
The company is planning to break ground on the new plant this summer.
Production is expected to begin in 2024.
When it reaches full production, the factory will employ 8,000 people.
It will be the first major automotive assembly facility in Georgia since 2006.
The company is currently upgrading its original plant in Normal, Illinois.
It has approved an expansion of 623,000 square feet, bringing the total to four million square feet.
The factory is currently home to 800 to 1000 employees.
Plans are to employ a further 800 to 1,000 workers there by the second quarter of next year.
Although the factory hasn’t even broken ground, the plan has been controversial with local residents.
Almost 2,000 Stanton Springs residents have expressed their disapproval of the project.
The state of Georgia has been supportive of the project.
It is even taking the project over to avoid public opposition.
The project will create up to 7,500 jobs and produce 400,000 emissions-free vehicles per year.
The project is facing a lot of opposition from the local community.
State officials have not disclosed how much the project is going to cost.
The state is putting up a big incentive package for the company, but critics of the incentives are urging caution.
The plant will include 19 million square feet of interior space, waste and recycling facilities, and storm-water management ponds.
The state’s Department of Transportation is also likely to build a new interchange near the facility.
Employees will be given dark-sky principles for parking areas, which will minimize light pollution.
The project will also include at least a 100-foot buffer from nearby residential areas.
The second electric truck plant will be built in Georgia by Rivian, the Amazon-backed electric vehicle company.
It will be built on a 2,000-acre plot of land between Rutledge and Social Circle.
The new plant will be close to Atlanta.
The company hopes to start production there in 2024.
Rivian has a factory in Normal, Illinois, where it builds the electric pickup trucks and SUVs that make up the Rivian brand.
The company also has a commitment to make 100,000 custom delivery vans for Amazon.
The factory is set to be one of the largest in the world when it begins production in 2024.
With gas prices on the rise, more people are looking into electric vehicles.
Ford plans to build an electric truck factory in Kentucky.
The automaker will invest $11.4 billion in the new plant, $7 billion of it with a joint venture partner.
The new factory will create jobs and produce batteries for electric trucks.
The company also plans to expand to other states.
Aside from manufacturing the trucks, it plans to open a battery manufacturing plant in North Carolina, where it will manufacture EV batteries.
This plant will be the first of its kind in the United States.
Rivian has partnered with Republican Gov. Brian Kemp.
The project has already become entangled in partisan politics.
Opponents of the plant have organized online and held rallies, and some have even gotten into conspiracy theories.
The debate is emblematic of broader tensions in environmental issues.
A new electric car factory in Georgia is drawing a lot of attention.
The state has received more than $300 million in incentives for the project.
Rivian, an upstart electric automaker, plans to build a plant five times larger than the Pentagon and make up to 400,000 emissions-free trucks every year.
It is one of the largest projects in the state’s history.
It is expected to create thousands of jobs and accelerate the transition away from fossil fuels.
The announcement comes after Hyundai Motor Company announced plans to build a new EV factory in Bryan County, near Savannah.
The company says the $5 billion investment will create 8,100 jobs and bring billions in new investment from suppliers.
Hyundai executives met with local and state officials to make the deal official.
The company’s CEO, Jay Chang, thanked the people of Bryan County for their “warm welcome.”
The company also announced that it will build a second factory in Georgia to build electric vehicles.
The company will employ 7,500 people at the plant when it is fully operational.
It will be able to produce 400,000 vehicles annually.
Construction will begin next summer.
The company hopes to be producing vehicles in 2024.
Rivian is an American automaker that specializes in electric vehicles.
The company originally intended to release their R1T electric pickup truck in June of 2021.
A global shortage of semiconductor chips and a pandemic delayed the production schedule.
The company has since moved their research and production facilities from Michigan to Irvine, California.
Ultimately, the company is planning to produce consumer products in its normal Illinois factory, where it once built Mitsubishi Eclipses.
The company currently has one EV on the market and two more on the way.
Amazon has placed a large order with them as part of a deal to invest in the company.
Rivian’s current focus is developing solid-state battery packs.
It also has plans to build a $5 billion factory in Georgia.
Rivian plans to start production of electric trucks in Georgia in 2024.
When fully operational, the factory will produce up to 400,000 vehicles a year.
The company plans to expand its production facility with future models.
In late May, Rivian announced plans to build a new factory near Atlanta.
Construction is set to begin this summer.
Production is expected to begin in 2024.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp has asked the state for $125 million to help pay for land and training costs for the new factory.
The Rivian company has also been lobbying for rule changes that would allow it to sell its vehicles directly to consumers.
This is similar to the push Tesla made to sell their cars directly to consumers in 2015.
While the state’s government is still unsure about whether or not this would work, Rivian has lobbied for the ability to sell its vehicles directly to consumers.
A recent bill filed in the state legislature would have allowed the company to sell vehicles directly to customers.
The bill didn’t receive a vote in committee.
It’s likely to be back on the legislative agenda next year.
Rivian’s plans are a good example of how the company focuses on local job creation.
They’re investing $5 billion in Georgia to build their factory.
This is an unprecedented number for an automotive plant.
It will create thousands of new jobs.
As a state, Georgia is seeking to attract the next generation of electric trucks with incentives.
A new factory will create thousands of middle-class jobs and will probably create spinoff jobs through suppliers.
The incentive package is expected to be worth hundreds of millions of dollars and will include infrastructure, training, and job creation.
While Rivian is the well-funded darling of Wall Street, Georgia will have to compete with a thriving electric truck industry that already includes Tesla.
Rivian Automotive has proposed building an electric truck factory on 2,000 acres near Atlanta.
The company plans to hire 7,500 workers and produce 400,000 emissions-free vehicles a year.
The company’s plans are a subject of political debate.
Rivian and the state’s governor Brian Kemp have both defended the plant as an economic boon.
The state’s business-friendly climate has made it possible for EV manufacturers to establish factories here.
The state’s recent economic development has seen a rise in investments in manufacturing and energy.
The state has long been known for its golf cart industry.
In 2018, Blue Bird introduced its first all-electric bus.
source_title: How Electric Truck Factory Became Georgia? [Answered 2026] | Prettymotors

facts: Rivian broke ground Tuesday on a $5 billion electric vehicle facility in Georgia.
The groundbreaking occurred east of Atlanta.
The project starts despite President Donald Trump’s successful push to roll back electric vehicle tax credits.
Starting Sept. 30, buyers will no longer qualify for savings of up to $7,500 per car.
CEO RJ Scaringe said Rivian will sell its R2 sport utility vehicles because it is a superior vehicle, not for environmental or tax incentive reasons.
Scaringe described the R2 as an incredible five-passenger offroad machine that happens to be electric.
The Georgia plant was first announced in 2021 and is key to Rivian’s profitability.
Rivian currently makes the high-end R1T pickup truck and R1S SUV in Normal, Illinois, as well as delivery vans for Amazon and others.
Rivian’s truck prices start at $71,000.
The Illinois plant will begin making the smaller R2 next year with prices starting at $45,000.
An expanded Illinois plant will be able to assemble 215,000 vehicles yearly.
If the R2 is a hit and Rivian successfully produces an even smaller R3, it will need more capacity.
The Georgia operation will be able to make 200,000 vehicles yearly starting in 2028.
Scaringe called the Georgia operation the foundation for growth.
Rivian plans another 200,000 in capacity in phase two of the Georgia plant.
Rivian expects to deliver 40,000 to 46,000 vehicles this year, down from 52,000 last year.
The company is limiting production in part to launch 2026 models.
Alex Oyler, North American director of auto research firm SBD Automotive, said it’s do-or-die time for Rivian and that scale is key to profitability.
EV sales growth in the U.S. rose only 1.5% in the first half of 2025, according to Cox Automotive.
Tesla accounted for almost 45% of U.S. electric vehicle sales in that period, according to Cox.
General Motors’ slice of American EV sales climbed to 13%.
Rivian had a 3% share in the first half of the year, behind Tesla and six traditional automakers.
Excluding Tesla, Rivian is the most successful startup automaker.
Rivian initially targeted demand for electric pickups and SUVs.
Competition now includes Ford’s F-150 Lightning and the electric Chevrolet Silverado.
After an initial public offering in 2021, Rivian shares have fallen by more than 80%.
Rivian lost $1.66 billion in the first half of 2025.
Stellantis canceled Ram’s electric truck program last week.
Ford delayed production at a new Tennessee plant.
General Motors abandoned plans to build electric vehicles in suburban Detroit.
Sam Fiorani, vice president at AutoForecast Solutions, noted slowing EV growth doesn’t play in Rivian’s favor but said there is still an EV market.
Georgia pledged $1.5 billion in incentives to Rivian for 7,500 company jobs paying at least $56,000 a year on average.
Rivian can only benefit from most incentives if it meets employment goals.
The state is already spending $175 million to buy and grade land and improve roads.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp wants to make Georgia the electric mobility capital of America.
Kemp acknowledged the road has not been smooth but voiced confidence Rivian can deliver an innovation revolution benefiting Georgians.
Rivian was supposed to be making trucks by now at the 2,000-acre (800-hectare) site near Social Circle, about 45 miles (70 kilometers) east of Atlanta.
The company paused construction in 2024 as it burned through cash.
German automaker Volkswagen agreed to invest $5.8 billion in Rivian for software and electrical technology.
Then-President Joe Biden’s administration agreed to loan Rivian $6.6 billion to build the Georgia plant.
Scaringe said Rivian has built a very close relationship with the U.S. Department of Energy and that goals align with Trump’s focus on U.S. manufacturing and technology.
Kemp urged Energy Secretary Chris Wright to back Rivian.
Some residents oppose the plant, citing it as an inappropriate neighbor to farms and potential groundwater pollution.
Eddie Clay, a resident living less than a mile away, said his well water turned mud-choked after excavation at the site.
Tariffs are costing Rivian $2,000 per vehicle.
The Trump administration ending a tax-credit program will cost the company $140 million in revenue this year.
Long-term threats include low-priced, cutting-edge Chinese EVs.
Scaringe said starting construction shows Rivian is working through its challenges and that they have clear sight to finish and launch.
source_title: Rivian breaks ground on $5 billion Georgia electric vehicle plant | AP News

facts: Rivian has moved from a production pause into a controlled restart phase at the Georgia plant, restarting key assembly lines after targeted inspections and process updates.
The plant reopening directly impacts EV production rates, supplier scheduling, and regional employment numbers.
Production will ramp up measuredly rather than immediately returning to peak volumes.
Customers can expect prioritized allocations, clearer VIN timelines, and possible short delays for new orders as throughput stabilizes.
The restart signals operational progress but raises near-term risks to earnings guidance until yield rates and cycle times improve.
The restart moves job rehiring and local supplier activity back into motion, with short-term boosts to logistics and services near the campus.
Vehicle deliveries should edge up over several weeks as line optimization and supplier confirmations reduce bottlenecks.
Full manufacturing throughput will depend on sustained parts flow and consistent quality metrics.
Initial production pause occurred after internal quality audits flagged inconsistent fit-and-finish on early production units, prompting a temporary shutdown to diagnose root causes.
Engineering teams ran targeted inspections, sample-first vehicle checks, and process audits to determine layout and tooling adjustments required before resuming assembly.
Public restart announcement followed verification milestones: completion of rework actions on key stations, supplier confirmations for critical components, and successful low-rate pilot runs.
Operational triggers for the pause included elevated defect counts on early builds, component fit issues that required retooling, and localized supply hiccups that risked line stoppages.

Regulatory and logistical milestones required before restart included cleared safety inspections, updated environmental handling protocols for battery systems, and confirmed inbound freight windows from key suppliers.

Rework and retooling focused on line rebalancing and process standardization: workcells were adjusted to match takt time, jigs and fixtures were calibrated, and assembly procedures tightened to reduce variation.
Line optimization included new quality gates at critical stations, clearer operator-pull signals, and revised handoff checks to prevent downstream rework during manufacturing ramp.

Quality assurance and validation used staged test runs: low-volume pilot builds, instrumented test vehicles, and a sample-first protocol where the first units of each run receive detailed inspection before ramping up.
Battery pack and component readiness required supplier confirmations of lot traceability, incoming materials QA, and the establishment of short-term inventory buffers to prevent immediate shortages.
Validation covered electrical integrity tests, humidity and temperature controls on battery storage, and burn-in procedures for high-voltage systems before vehicles left the plant.

Headcount plan emphasizes a phased rehiring timeline: contractors and temporary staff cover immediate needs while permanent hires return as throughput stabilizes, with clear targets for staffing ramp at each production milestone.
Retention incentives likely include attendance bonuses, shift differentials, and expedited conversion offers for long-term contractor hires to reduce turnover during the ramp.
Skill refresh programs focus on on-the-job training for updated assembly procedures, first-pass quality techniques, and critical inspection tasks to minimize early defects.
Health and safety measures reinforce hazardous-material handling rules for battery work, provide PPE refreshers, and use tightened lockout/tagout procedures to keep shift changeovers safe.
Shift scheduling balances overtime limits with coverage needs and includes defined escalation channels to address recurring stoppages without overburdening crews.

Supplier coordination prioritized dual sourcing for critical components, expedited air or priority sea shipments for shortfalls, and tightened vendor quality gates to prevent bad lots from reaching the line.
Parts lead times are being monitored daily; suppliers provided rolling confirmations of shipment windows to match the plant’s staged production targets and to limit warehouse congestion.
Regional logistics planning accounted for port access, inland freight capacity, and additional on-campus warehousing to smooth inbound flow and speed outbound deliveries.
Contingency plans include trigger points for secondary sourcing, temporary production throttles, or scheduled downtime if a supplier misses consecutive deliveries or fails quality checks.
Short-term production target is low double-digit daily units as systems stabilize and first-pass quality improves.
Mid-term production target is steady weekly cadence with progressive increases to monthly volumes as supplier flow and line optimization deliver consistent yields.
Long-term production target is scaled manufacturing capacity tied to published guidance, contingent on sustained supplier performance and no repeat stoppages.
Key KPIs to watch include yield rates, first-pass quality, cycle time per vehicle, and delivered-vehicle backlog; improvement across these metrics signals a healthy manufacturing ramp.
Realistic constraints include parts lead times, remaining rework on legacy builds, and potential requalification cycles for suppliers; any of these can delay hitting advertised production targets.
Direct employment at the plant grows as hires return; indirect effects include supplier hiring, logistics jobs, and increased local service demand from contractor staff and new vendors.
Public incentives and state partnerships tied to the plant are performance-based and typically include employment thresholds and investment milestones that influence local tax abatements and grants.

Opportunities for Georgia businesses center on qualifying as Tier 1 or Tier 2 suppliers: companies should prepare certifications, pass vendor audits, and demonstrate capacity to meet parts lead times and quality standards.
Local suppliers benefit from early engagement, flexible scheduling, and readiness to scale production quickly to meet incremental demand during ramp phases.
Permitting and state inspections verify that process changes meet safety codes, equipment upgrades have proper approvals, and hazardous-material handling aligns with environmental compliance rules.
Battery-handling protocols were reinforced: enhanced storage segregation, spill-response drills, and updated transport declarations for lithium-ion modules before outbound shipments.

To demonstrate compliance and transparency, Rivian should document inspection results, maintain clear traceability of test records, and publish status updates to regulators and local authorities as milestones are met.
Expect prioritized fulfillment: higher-availability allocations and previously confirmed orders are likely dispatched first while new orders may face longer lead times during the initial ramp.

Order status updates will focus on VIN allocation, revised delivery windows, and explicit notes about eligibility for priority scheduling; customers should monitor their account portal for VIN assignment and estimated delivery dates.
Warranty coverage remains in force; expect expanded diagnostics at delivery centers to catch early defects and reduce post-delivery service visits.
Customers should request clear escalation paths for delayed deliveries, ask for date-stamped VIN confirmations, and confirm any trade-in or financing deadlines tied to delivery timing.

Short-term market signals include potential revisions to production guidance, effects on near-term margins from rework costs, and possible shifts in capital allocation to shore up supplier resilience.
Analysts will watch weekly production figures, yield improvements, and supplier confirmations; consistent progress reduces production miss risk and supports investor confidence.
A successful restart preserves Rivian’s model rollout timeline and defends supplier commitments; delays could open share for competitors to capture order intake and supplier capacity.
Sustained ramp success would improve route-to-market for future models, reduce per-unit cost through higher throughput, and support a stronger valuation trajectory over time.
Concrete signs of stable recovery include consistent weekly production numbers, falling defect rates, stable cycle times, and timely supplier deliveries without emergency shipments.
Early warning indicators of trouble include repeated line stoppages, rising scrap rates, missed supplier windows, and widening backlogs of unfinished vehicles at inspection stations.
Reliable public sources and filings to watch include company statements, earnings call transcripts, SEC filings for material updates, and local reporting on employment and permitting actions.

Employees should confirm onboarding dates, review updated safety and assembly procedures, and use established channels to report persistent quality concerns; keep up-to-date on shift assignments and benefits changes.

Customers should check order portals regularly, request VIN confirmation if delivery timing is critical, and document communications about delays or revised windows to support any warranty or contract questions.
Suppliers should verify capacity commitments, keep parts lead-time confirmations current, and be prepared to offer expedited shipments or buffer stock if requested to sustain the ramp.
Policymakers should monitor reported employment numbers against incentive targets and ensure regulatory inspections are completed on schedule to keep the restart compliant and transparent.
Confirmed VIN holders generally retain priority while new orders may shift later in the queue during early ramp delays.
The plant is subject to safety inspections and reinforced battery-handling protocols; workforce safety measures, documented inspections, and retraining are part of the restart checklist.
Full-scale production depends on consistent supplier deliveries and quality metrics; expect a phased ramp where capacity increases over weeks to months rather than days.
Verify company statements against SEC filings and earnings call remarks for production guidance and timelines before publishing updates.
Confirm local employment numbers and rehiring plans with state economic development contacts and plant HR for accurate job counts.
Track weekly production KPIs published by the company or cited in investor updates: units produced, yield rates, and first-pass quality.

Suggested cadence for follow-up articles includes initial technical deep-dive within 48–72 hours, weekly production snapshots for the first month of the ramp, and a 30–60 day assessment on supplier performance and delivery impacts.
Trigger an immediate update if regulators issue major findings, if there is a material production miss compared with guidance, or if a supplier collapse forces a significant halt to output.
source_title: Rivian Georgia Factory Restart — Latest Updates

facts: Kia Georgia marked the start of production for the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride at its West Point facility on Feb. 24, 2026.
The ceremonial vehicle represented the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) assembled in the state of Georgia.
The same vehicle marked the 5 millionth vehicle produced at Kia Georgia.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the ceremony alongside team members and state/community leaders.
Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that Kia Georgia is the only plant in the world that builds the Telluride.
