Reaching five million vehicles and introducing Kia's first US-assembled HEV reflects Kia's commitment to building the future of mobility in Georgia, according to Sean Yoon.
Since beginning production in 2009, Kia Georgia has grown into one of the region’s largest employers and a cornerstone of automotive manufacturing in the Southeast.
The Telluride has been exclusively Georgia-made since its introduction in 2019.
Governor Brian Kemp credited the vehicle's success to a proven partnership approach between Kia Georgia, the local community, and the state.
Stuart Countess, Kia Georgia’s President and CEO, stated that producing world-class gasoline-powered vehicles, EVs, and HEVs demonstrates the plant's versatility and talent.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Seoul-based Kia Corporation.
The facility has an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
The plant currently produces the Telluride, Sorento (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage (excludes HEV and PHEV models) SUVs, and the all-electric EV9 and EV6.
source_title: Kia Georgia celebrates trio of major milestones, including Start of Production for 2027 Kia Telluride

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp drove the new hybrid vehicle during its rollout on Tuesday.
The Kia Sportage Turbo-Hybrid is the first Kia and first hybrid electric vehicle produced at the Ellabell plant.
It is the third vehicle manufactured at the site since production began in 2022, joining the Hyundai IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
This marks the first time the Sportage Hybrid is being manufactured in the U.S., though it has been produced in Korea.

Gov. Kemp, Kia North America and Kia America President and CEO Seungkyu (Sean) Yoon, and Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess joined about 200 Metaplant employees to celebrate the vehicle reveal.
The hybrid Kia Sportage debuted on an Autonomous Mobile Robot, a machine used throughout HMGMA's production processes.
Two yellow "robot dogs" greeted guests at the event.
Gov. Kemp called the reveal a historic moment and emphasized the partnership between Kia America, HMG's Metaplant, the local community, and Georgians to support jobs for years to come.
The new vehicle's production marks the first step in the Metaplant's pivot to producing hybrid vehicles.
Chris Susock, chief manufacturing officer for Hyundai Motor North America, stated the plant's original intention was to manufacture all-electric vehicles.
Susock cited a sharp decline in U.S. electric vehicle demand, especially following the expiration of federal tax credits outlined in President Donald Trump's "big beautiful bill."
Midway through construction, HMGMA officials decided to shift focus to make the plant adaptable to hybrid electric vehicle production to balance market shifts.
Susock highlighted the flexibility in automation and technology, allowing the plant to switch powertrain mixes quickly based on market demand.
Last month, HMGMA shipped about 6,000 units outside the facility.
This shipment volume represents about 50% of the plant's current capacity.
The plant is ramping up to reach full capacity of about 580,000 units by 2028.
Product planning will now look at forecasts to determine what models to build, with capability to quickly switch between Kias, different hybrids, Genesises, or other models.
Kia President and CEO Seungkyu (Sean) Yoon noted the timing aligns with higher demand for hybrids, especially SUVs like the Sportage, Carnival, and Telluride.
HMGMA President and CEO Tony Heo stated employees received extensive training to produce the new vehicle.
Many team members traveled to Kia facilities in West Point, Georgia, and South Korea to learn best practices for Sportage Hybrid production.
Both the Ellabell Metaplant and Kia's plant in West Point emphasize creating high-quality jobs and economic opportunities for their local communities and across Georgia.

Yoon stated the Savannah Metaplant testifies to confidence in Georgia's future as an automotive powerhouse and expects it to maintain strong momentum with the Sportage Hybrid becoming one of Kia's highest volume models.
source_title: Hyundai Metaplant begins producing a third vehicle - Statesboro Herald

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) celebrated its official grand opening on February 26, 2010, in West Point, Georgia.
The ceremony was attended by officials from KMMG, Hyundai-Kia Automotive Group, the State of Georgia, local city and county officials, and approximately 500 VIPs from Korea and the U.S.
Attendees included Hyundai-Kia Automotive Group Chairman Mong-Koo Chung, Group President and CEO of KMMG and Kia Motors America Byung-Mo Ahn, and Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue.
Byung-Mo Ahn stated that Kia is proud to call West Point, Georgia home and looks forward to many years of growing together.
Byung-Mo Ahn noted that continued diligent work has helped elevate Kia to one of the fastest growing automobile brands around the globe.

Byung-Mo Ahn stated that with the successful launch of the 2011 Kia Sorento CUV and establishment of their first U.S. manufacturing plant, the Kia brand is well positioned for further growth and to become a worldwide leader in quality and innovation.
The project represents a $1 billion investment by Kia Motors Corporation.
The automobile manufacturing plant spans 2.2 million square feet and is situated on more than 2,200 acres of land between Exit 2 and Exit 6 along Interstate 85 in West Point.
KMMG consists of four main shops: stamping, welding, paint, and assembly.
The site also includes a transmission shop, a module shop, and a two-mile test track where every produced vehicle is thoroughly tested.
KMMG’s general assembly area features more than a half mile of height-adjustable conveyors to optimize installation positions and reduce physical fatigue for team members.
The assembly area is outfitted with wood flooring to provide a more comfortable work environment.
Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue noted the event continues a mutually beneficial partnership between the State of Georgia and Kia that will benefit the state in years to come.
Governor Perdue stated that Kia has brought thousands of new jobs to the area and is delivering as a tremendous corporate citizen.
Governor Perdue pledged to continue providing specialized training through the Kia Georgia Training Center to guarantee a quality workforce and future plant success.
KMMG currently employs more than 1,200 team members.
KMMG is expected to nearly double its workforce by the end of the year.
Onsite and nearby suppliers are expected to create up to 7,500 additional jobs in the region.
Randy Jackson, director of human resources and administration, highlighted KMMG's role in job creation, community development, and tourism.
The plant will open its doors to the community beginning in spring for tours to see the quality and craftsmanship of Kia vehicles.
Mass production of the all-new 2011 Kia Sorento CUV began on November 16, 2009.
The 2011 Sorento officially went on sale in the U.S. in January 2010.
The 2011 Sorento sold 7,398 units in its first month, making it Kia’s best-selling vehicle in January.
Byung-Mo Ahn noted the outstanding early performance of the first vehicle from the West Point plant is a significant achievement for KMMG and the Kia brand.
Kia plans to continue expanding its line of vehicles at the facility while adhering to brand tenets of value, quality, safety, and technology.
KMMG is the first manufacturing site in North America for Seoul-based Kia Motors Corporation.
At full capacity, the plant will be able to build 300,000 vehicles annually using U.S. and globally sourced parts.
source_title: Kia Celebrates Grand Opening of $1 Billion State-of-the-Art Automobile Manufacturing Plant in Georgia - Kia Georgia

facts: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications to support the future of sustainable mobility.
The partnership will focus on developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing more than $12 billion in EV manufacturing and battery production in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $12.6 billion in new dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia.
The investment includes the $7.59 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The HMGMA site will create more than 8,500 direct jobs.
The investment includes a $5 billion battery manufacturing facility joint venture with SK On in Bartow County, Georgia.
The SK On joint venture facility will employ approximately 3,500 people.
Proximity of the new facilities to Georgia Tech was one of the important considerations in deciding their locations.
Kia Georgia’s West Point assembly plant began vehicle production in 2009.
Kia Georgia's plant was established under the leadership of Honorary Chairman Mong-Koo Chung and Executive Chair Euisun Chung.
University System of Georgia Chancellor Sonny Perdue participated in the HMG-Georgia Tech MOU ceremony.
Sonny Perdue was the Governor of Georgia at the time of the Kia Georgia plant’s opening.
A recent study by the Center for Automotive Research reveals Hyundai Motor’s automotive operations contributed $20.1 billion and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy in 2021 alone.
According to the study, Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs every year.
According to the study, Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.
Hyundai’s economic impact in Georgia will be tens of billions of dollars in the years to come.
The partnership with Georgia Tech builds upon Hyundai Motor Group’s long history in Georgia.
The partnership is expected to last for decades and was one of the reasons Hyundai chose Georgia for its EV investments.

Future mobility concepts explored through the collaboration include zero emissions hydrogen-powered vehicles on campus, advanced air mobility shuttles to Hartsfield International Airport, and hands-free autonomous vehicles on I-75 and I-85.
The expanded relationship with Georgia Tech will include opportunities for student professional development and cooperative work programs.
Hyundai will collaborate with Georgia Tech on joint research and development programs focused on the future of sustainable mobility.
Joint R&D efforts will focus on battery technology and expanding the hydrogen economy.
Georgia Tech and Hyundai will create technical training and leadership development programming for Hyundai employees.
The partnership will initiate engagement activities to stimulate interest in STEM degrees among students.
Hyundai will actively participate in career recruitment events at Georgia Tech.
Hyundai will create learning programs for both undergraduate and graduate students.
Hyundai’s partnership with Georgia Tech includes field-naming recognition at Bobby Dodd Stadium, which is now known as Bobby Dodd Stadium at Hyundai Field.
The partnership provides a vital pathway for Georgia Tech to expand its stature as a leading research entity in the electrification of the automotive industry through world-class hydrogen research.
The partnership offers an opportunity for Georgia Tech to contribute more broadly to Georgia’s commitment as a hub for electric vehicle production.
The partnership will generate new revenue for Georgia Tech Athletics.
This athletic revenue will provide student-athletes and teams with resources needed to compete at the highest levels athletically and academically.
source_title: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group Sign MoU for Future Mobility Collaboration

facts: Electric Vehicle battery manufacturer SK Battery America has laid off workers at its primary facility in Commerce, Georgia.
Affected employees will continue receiving pay through May 6, 2026, in line with the 60-day notice period required under the WARN Act.
SK Battery America, Inc. submitted a WARN notice on March 6, 2026.
The filing states that 958 employees were laid off at the company’s Commerce, Georgia facility.
The last working day for employees was March 6, 2026, and their employment will terminate on May 6, 2026.
The company said affected employees will receive pay equivalent to the 60-day notice period.
The facility is located at 1760 SK Blvd in Commerce, Georgia.
The company described the layoffs as part of a workforce reduction.
The layoffs come amid broader shifts in the EV industry.
SK Battery America (SKBA) is an electric vehicle battery producer with its headquarters in Commerce, Georgia.
It is the production base of SK On, a battery company based in South Korea.
The company specializes in the production of high-technology lithium-ion batteries to use in electric vehicles.
SKBA belongs to the SK Group, which is among the largest industrial conglomerates in South Korea and has businesses in energy, telecommunications, finance, and construction.
The company was established in 2019 and serves the expanding electric mobility market in North America.
Its business focuses on EV battery technology, including high-energy-density lithium-ion cells, NCM9 battery technology, and power battery electric vehicles (BEVs).
SK Battery America has invested approximately $2.6 billion in constructing its EV battery facilities in Georgia.
This move created multiple high-profile job positions in the area.
The company is the provider of battery cells to various automotive companies across the world, such as Ford, Volkswagen, and Hyundai.
The SK Battery America layoffs reflect the difficulties that the electric vehicle sector is going through.
Manufacturing companies re-strategize production strategies in response to the changing demand.
source_title: Major EV Battery Producer Lays Off 958 Workers at Georgia Plant

facts: SK On is close to securing additional large-scale energy storage system (ESS) orders in North America.
SK On is accelerating preparations for mass production of ESS batteries.
According to industry sources on April 10, SK On is in talks with multiple customers for supply deals totaling several tens of gigawatt-hours (GWh).
There is a high likelihood of contracts being finalized.
SK On entered the ESS market later than rivals LG Energy Solution and Samsung SDI.
SK On has begun to gain traction in the ESS market since the second half of last year.
SK On is building a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery production line for ESS at its standalone plant in Georgia (SKBA).
Mass production at the Georgia plant is scheduled to begin in October.
Key material supplies for the Georgia plant are expected to start in early second half.
A source familiar with SK On's ESS business said the company is actively negotiating with multiple U.S.-based firms.
Demand for domestically produced batteries is rising rapidly due to proposed legislation restricting imports of Chinese-made ESS products.
Demand is also rising due to the impact of investment tax credits (ITC).
Existing supply chains are struggling to keep pace with demand growth.
SK On has established its ESS supply chain framework ahead of production.
Major material suppliers are set to begin deliveries between late May and early June.
Cathode supplier is L&F.
Separator maker is WCP.
Electrolyte provider is Enchem.
Chinese suppliers such as BTR may be used for anode materials.
SK On has completed the configuration of ESS systems.
SK On is working with DeltaX on battery module design.
Key components such as structures and containerized direct current (DC) blocks are being supplied in partnership with Seojin System.
In February, SK On signed a procurement contract worth about 2 trillion won with Seojin System for ESS components through 2030.
SK On is applying electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technology to detect battery anomalies at an early stage.
SK On's battery management system (BMS) is developed by Hunate.
Key diagnostic semiconductors are sourced from Analog Devices.
SK On's ESS order pipeline includes a global target of 20 GWh for 2026.
SK On has secured a supply agreement of up to 7.2 GWh with U.S. renewable energy developer Flatiron Energy Development.
SK On secured 284 megawatts (MW) in South Korea's second centralized ESS contract market.
SK On secured 60 megawatt-hours (MWh) for a carbon-neutral industrial complex in Gumi.
SK On secured 500 MWh for a distributed energy project in Busan.
SK On's first North American ESS contract came in September last year with Flatiron for 1 GWh.
The Flatiron contract was later expanded to a potential 7.2 GWh over four years.
Domestically, SK On secured 284 MW—about 50.2% of total capacity—in a 565 MW ESS bidding program overseen by the Korea Power Exchange.
SK On secured government-backed projects in Gumi and Busan.
Beyond the Georgia plant, SK On plans to expand ESS production capacity.
SK On's Seosan Plant 2 will convert two electric vehicle battery lines, totaling 3 GWh, for ESS production.
Mass production at Seosan Plant 2 is scheduled for early next year.
SK On plans to utilize its Tennessee facility to meet North American demand.
The Tennessee facility was secured after restructuring the BlueOval SK joint venture with Ford.
SK On is expected to post an operating loss of around 300 billion won in the first quarter.
The operating loss is due to costs associated with slowing electric vehicle demand and production restructuring.
Earnings improvement is anticipated in the second half.
Earnings improvement will be supported by increased EV sales from key customers Hyundai Motor and Kia.
Earnings improvement will also be supported by the ramp-up of ESS production lines.
source_title: SK On Nears Additional ESS Orders as Georgia LFP Line Prepares for October Start < Battery < 기사본문 - The Elec Inc.

facts: The raid on Hyundai’s Savannah-area electric vehicle plant was part of a lengthy investigation into the unlawful hiring of illegal aliens at the industrial site that Georgia officials had promised would create thousands of jobs for locals, federal officials said.
Operation Low Voltage resulted in more than 475 people detained — more than 300 of whom were Koreans — for alleged immigration violations.
Federal agents executed a criminal search warrant to gather evidence at the battery plant jointly owned by Hyundai Motor Group and LG, two of South Korea’s largest companies.

According to the search warrant, federal agents were seeking employment records from the lithium battery factory that could reveal illegal immigration and hiring practices at HL-GA Battery Company LLC and five other companies identified by federal officials as subcontractors.
Federal officials had identified four people with Hispanic names as part of the basis for the warrant signed by a magistrate judge in the Southern District of Georgia.

Steven Schrank, the special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations for Georgia and Alabama, said the raid at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America was the largest single site enforcement operation ever carried out by the Department of Homeland Security’s enforcement division.

Schrank told reporters that while some of the detained workers illegally crossed the U.S. border, others had entered the country legally but had expired visas or had entered on a visa waiver that prohibited them from working at the $7.6 billion site where Hyundai Motor Group and an interconnected group of suppliers are building vehicles.

Hyundai broke ground at the site two years ago after Georgia promised billions of dollars in tax credits and tax abatements if the world’s largest carmaker met its goals of creating more than 8,000 jobs at an average salary of approximately $58,000.
As of March, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America reported that it had 1,200 people currently employed, not counting Koreans, and was operating one shift at the plant.
At that time, the battery facility was not operational and remains under construction.
No criminal charges had been filed.
Federal officials said the majority of workers detained this week have been sent to Folkston, the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency’s detention center in Charlton County.
The governor’s office responded to the raid by saying that state agencies remain committed to the rule of law.
Spokesman Carter Chapman stated that all companies operating within the state must follow the laws of Georgia and our nation.
There was no immediate comment from Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr.
The South Korean government expressed “concern and regret” over the operation.

South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong said in a televised statement from Seoul that the business activities of investors and the rights of nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of U.S. law enforcement.

Trump stated that the people arrested were immigrants who entered the country illegally, adding, “We had as I understand it a lot of illegal aliens... Some not the best of people. But we had a lot of illegal aliens working there.”

South Korean officials told The Wall Street Journal that many of the Koreans detained worked for LG Energy and were at the metaplant on a business trip with visas that allowed them to train employees in Georgia.

In addition to Koreans, foreign nationals from Guatemala, Colombia, Chile, Venezuela, and Mexico had been detained, according to Migrant Equity Southeast, a Coastal Georgia migrant workers’ organization.

The operation involved 10 federal agencies, including the Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Internal Revenue Service’s criminal investigation unit, according to Margaret Heap, the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of Georgia.

Schrank noted that the operation was more complex than other ICE enforcement actions since Trump took office earlier this year, describing it as a multi-month criminal investigation where evidence was developed, interviews conducted, documents gathered, and presented to a court to obtain a judicial search warrant.

The Current has previously reported on a pattern of workplace deaths and injuries affecting both American and foreign workers at the metaplant, including the death of a Korean technical worker in March.
There is also a reported pattern by key Hyundai suppliers of not reporting those incidents to the federal agency responsible for workplace safety.
HMGMA negotiated nearly $2 billion in state tax abatements and credits with Georgia and has previously stated that it was not responsible for the actions of its supplier companies on the site.

In response to the ongoing federal investigation, Hyundai Motor Group announced that North America Chief Manufacturing Officer Chris Susock would assume governance of the entire EV site in Bryan County.
The company stated it will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and subcontractors comply with all laws and regulations.

Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure all parties working on its projects maintain high standards of legal compliance, including thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
source_title: Hyundai Plant Detentions Linked to Illegal Hiring - Postindustrial

facts: Hyundai Mobis invested US$926 million for an electric vehicle components factory in Bryan County in the US state of Georgia.
The plant will begin construction in January 2023, with production due to start in 2024.
Hyundai Motor Group, the parent company of Hyundai Mobis, recently started construction of its electric vehicle factory in the US.
Hyundai Mobis follows a concept tried and tested by other manufacturers in the group by building its own components factory around Hyundai Motor Group's electric vehicle factory.
The components produced will include various sensors for assisted or autonomous driving, as well as chassis, steering and braking systems, and electrification components.
The components will supply not only the Hyundai electric car plant in Bryan County, but also the Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama, and Kia’s plant in Georgia.

H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated that the investment project reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
H.S. Oh stated that Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market and that this will trigger more growth within the sector.
H.S. Oh noted that Mobis is looking forward to providing high-quality work opportunities for the growing local workforce.
The Hyundai Mobis production facility in Georgia is expected to employ at least 1,500 people.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that region of the state.
Governor Kemp noted that they announced the second supplier to locate in the Bryan County area in just two weeks.
The electric vehicle components produced by Hyundai Mobis include ICCUs, electric drive units, and battery systems including BMS.
Georgia is generally positioned as the center of the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed investment of more than $13.3 billion and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially debuted in Bryan County at its first dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai Motor Group project are expected to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations throughout the state.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis to invest $926 million for electric vehicle components plant in USA

facts: Hyundai Mobis’ Alabama plant begins production of chassis modules which will be installed in four all-electric Mercedes-Benz SUVs.

Hyundai Mobis has begun supplies of chassis modules for Mercedes-Benz’s electric vehicle models in the US market, becoming the first non-European supplier for chassis modules for the German luxury carmaker.
This is the second time that large-scale module orders have been won overseas by Hyundai Mobis, after Chrysler in the United States.
Hyundai Mobis has built a plant in McAlla, Alabama near Mercedes-Benz's US plant for this chassis module supply and started mass production from the third quarter of this year.

This facility is its fifth production base in the US, following the Alabama and Georgia plants that supply modules to Hyundai Motors and Kia, and the Michigan and Ohio plants that supply Chrysler modules.
A chassis module refers to a large-scale component combination that combines steering, braking, and suspension devices around a frame under the vehicle body.
The chassis module is divided into a front chassis and a rear chassis according to the mounting location and corresponds to a core system that determines the driving stability of a vehicle.
Hyundai Mobis will supply its chassis modules for pure electric vehicles, specifically four SUV models equipped with an electric vehicle-only platform.
According to the mass production schedule for these models, Hyundai Mobis plans to supply front chassis and rear chassis modules sequentially.
In the process of winning the chassis module order, Hyundai Mobis held several technical briefing sessions and directly demonstrated the module factory mass production system.

Since 1999, Mobis has produced three types of modules, including cockpit-modules (control systems of the driver’s seat), front-end modules (integrating system of HVAC, lighting and bumper system), and chassis modules.
The company is currently operating a total of 28 module production sites: 8 in Korea, and 20 overseas.
Hyundai Mobis has been supplying its module systems to Chrysler since 2006 and has been recognised for its module technology and quality excellence in the North American market.
Following the new business from Mercedes-Benz, Hyundai Mobis expects to win orders from other global automakers.
Hyundai Mobis currently supplies key parts such as electric equipment, lamps, and sound systems to GM, Stellantis, Volkswagen and Geely.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis to supply chassis modules for four Mercedes-Benz e-SUVs | Autocar Professional

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is located outside of Savannah, Georgia.
It is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated mass-production Electrified Vehicle (EV) plant.
Order collection, procurement, logistics and production at HMGMA are optimized utilizing AI and data.
HMGMA's innovative manufacturing system creates a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers to build more than 500,000 vehicles per year.
Its first vehicle was the 2025 IONIQ 5 and its second will be the 2026 IONIQ 9.
HMGMA will eventually produce models for Kia and Genesis, which are part of the Hyundai Motor Group family.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that the plant represents the Group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities and commitment to innovation, as well as investment in relationships with partners and communities right here in Georgia.
The company’s plans for its Metaplant date to 2022 when it sought to build its first dedicated fully electrified vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in the United States.

HMG chose the 2,900-acre site in Bryan County, Georgia, for the area’s quality of life, strong regional connectivity, global logistical connections and infrastructure, and existing workforce training programs.
The site is now fully operational.
The site employs more than 8,500 workers.
More than 6,900 jobs will be created at off-site suppliers.
To date, the economic development agreement is Georgia’s largest in state history and totals more than $7.5 billion.
Governor Kemp highlighted that HMGMA is a once-in-a-generation opportunity made possible by working with job creators to build fruitful partnerships for decades.

Collaborations between HMGMA and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges, including Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility, are preparing Georgians for high-quality jobs of today and tomorrow.
Governor Kemp highlighted the company’s award-winning infrastructure, such as its ports, which connect companies to markets around the nation and globe.

The megasite consists of the Metaplant, LG Battery Joint Venture (known as HLA Greenpower) and five affiliates including Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, Hyundai Transys, Hyundai Steel and Hyundai Material.
There are nineteen suppliers located offsite.
The facility has 11 cavernous buildings and a massive 7.5 million square feet of space.
HMGMA employs more than 850 robots and almost 300 Automated Guided Vehicles (AGV) working alongside employees, who the company calls Meta Pros.
HMGMA's meta pros park in nearly 1,900 spaces beneath solar roofs that provide up to 5 percent of the plant’s electricity.
The bulk of finished vehicles are shipped by rail rather than truck, trimming fossil-fuel emissions and the automaker’s carbon footprint.

As part of an AI-based procurement and logistics system, the AGVs automatically allocate and ferry parts to their proper workstations for just-in-time delivery, saving space, time, and money otherwise used to stockpile parts.

One of HMGMA's unique features are the Spots, robotic quadrupeds designed by Hyundai-owned Boston Dynamics that use 360-degree vision and “athletic intelligence” to sniff out potential defects on car welds.

Atlas humanoid robots from Boston Dynamics at HMGMA can crawl, cartwheel, and move in sync with human counterparts, utilizing RGB cameras on their 360-degree swiveling heads to sense environments, avoid collisions, and manipulate parts.
HMGMA’s welding shop houses 475 industrial robots that assemble the bodies in white.
One of the biggest features of the site is its hydrogen ecosystem.
HMGMA has deployed Hyundai Motor XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations.

A total of 21 XCIENT trucks are currently in operation, representing more than a third of the Glovis America truck fleet at HMGMA, which transport vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the 2,906-acre Megasite on a daily basis.
The Group has enabled further growth related to joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK totaling a $12.6 billion investment.
This represents the largest single investment in the state’s history.

The Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs, and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
To date, the Group has invested more than $20.5 billion in the U.S. in the service of these goals, creating or supporting over 570,000 jobs nationwide.

A total of $6 billion will be allocated to enhance the Group’s parts and logistics business, as well as establishing an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill in the U.S. through its affiliate, Hyundai Steel, with an annual steel production capacity of 2.7 million tons.
The mill will supply high-quality automotive steel plates to HMGMA when commercial steel production begins in 2029.
Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated that the state-of-the-art smart plant is officially open.
Hyundai Motor America has been in the U.S. market for 37 years.
Hyundai Motor America's sales volume grew from 168,000 units in 1986 to near 800,000 units in 2016.
HMA’s Alabama plant has been producing U.S.-made vehicles since 2005.
The annual production volume at HMA’s Alabama plant increased from 91,000 units in 2005 to shy of 400,000 units in 2013 and 2014.
HMA’s U.S.-based assembly plant produced about 45 percent of HMA’s vehicles sold in the U.S. market.
Globally, Hyundai Motor Group ranks third, with more than 6.8 million units sold in 2022.
Hyundai Motor Company contributed nearly 4.0 million units of vehicles sold worldwide.

Hyundai has significant operations in the U.S., including its North American headquarters in California, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama assembly plant, the all-new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, and several cutting-edge R&D facilities.
These operations, combined with those of Hyundai’s 850 independent dealers, contribute $20.1 billion annually and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy, according to the company’s economic impact report.
source_title: Meta Dynamics - Industry Today - Leader in Manufacturing & Industry News

facts: Hyundai Mobis is aiming for an average annual revenue growth of 8% and an operating margin of 5~6% by 2027.
The company targets increasing its global automaker customer share in auto component manufacturing from the current 10% to 40% by 2033.
Hyundai Mobis plans to achieve a Total Shareholder Return (TSR) of over 30% by leveraging leading technological competitiveness and improving business structure centered on profitability.

The company is strengthening high-value lineups such as electronic braking systems (EMB) and steering systems (SBW) that utilize electrical signals to attain a 10% global chassis safety market share by 2030.
Hyundai Mobis hosted its 2024 CEO Investor Day on November 19 at the Fairmont Hotel in Yeouido, Seoul, with investors, analysts, and credit-rating agents in attendance.
Lee Gyusuk serves as President and CEO of Hyundai Mobis and unveiled the company’s mid-to-long-term growth direction and comprehensive strategies.
The company declared its intention to raise its average annual revenue growth rate to over 8% by 2027, marking the company’s 50th anniversary.
Hyundai Mobis plans to expand its current TSR of 20% to over 30% within the next three years as part of a balanced and proactive shareholder return policy.
As part of the shareholder return initiative, Hyundai Mobis revealed plans to cancel its currently held treasury shares over the next three years.
The company's strategy to secure market-leading technological competitiveness is grounded in an efficient response to the Software-Driven Vehicles (SDV) mobility trend and electrification.

Hyundai Mobis plans to consolidate its leadership in the electrification sector by proactively developing optimized products for Extended Range Electric Vehicles (EREV) and Economical Electric Powertrain (e-PT) systems.
The EREV product is currently undergoing design verification and evaluation, with mass production targeted for the end of 2026.
Hyundai Mobis intends to develop an economical 120kW e-PT system by next year, which will be priced at approximately 70% of the current 160kW systems dominating the electric vehicle market.
The development of the 120kW e-PT system targets concentrated efforts in small EV-centric markets such as Europe and India.
A 250kW e-PT system for large EVs is in the final stages of development, with the company actively pursuing orders from premium global automakers.
Hyundai Mobis focuses on providing innovative hardware and software solutions, such as integrated control platforms and unified infotainment systems, to enable flexible responses to SDV demands.

The company plans to optimize its business portfolio by categorizing it into growth businesses (electrification and electronics) and stabilization businesses (modules, chassis, safety, lamps, and service components).
Hyundai Mobis announced a commitment to ESG management aiming to realize mobility through responsible innovation and clean technology.
The company plans to achieve a 35% renewable energy transition rate by 2025.
Hyundai Mobis will increase the sustainability audit rate of its manufacturing sites to 100% by 2025 and its component sites by 2027.
From 2025 to 2027, Hyundai Mobis will disclose the results of external evaluations related to board management to expand communication with stakeholders.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis Outlines Value-Up Strategy at CEO Investor Day - fintechfilter

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Co. and LG Energy Solution Ltd. Joint Venture Facility in Georgia
facts: The United States conducted a large-scale immigration raid on a joint electric battery factory operated by Hyundai Motor Co. and LG Energy Solution Ltd. in Georgia.
The raid sparked concerns among Korean enterprises about their planned investments worth tens of billions of dollars in the U.S.
Currently, the largest enterprise groups in Korea are taking emergency measures to mitigate the impact of this event.
During a government meeting on Monday, the Korean Minister of Trade, Industry and Energy acknowledged concerns arising from the immigration crackdown on Korean enterprises investing in the U.S.
The minister stated that the government would collaborate with relevant departments, including the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, to explore ways to improve related systems.
The raid occurred last week, with footage showing multiple workers being handcuffed at the wrists, waist, and ankles.

This operation was part of a broader U.S. effort to combat illegal immigration, coming just two weeks after a summit between the leaders of Korea and the U.S. where the alliance between the two countries was emphasized.
During the summit, a trade agreement was announced that included a 3500 billion dollar fund to support Korean enterprises expanding in the U.S.
Korean enterprises had independently committed to 1500 billion dollars in direct investment in the U.S. to strengthen trade ties between the two nations.
LG Energy Solution has tentatively postponed the start of production for the electric vehicle battery factory it is building with Hyundai in Georgia.
Hyundai has prohibited all employees from traveling to the U.S. for business purposes.
LG Energy Solution had initially planned to commence production later this year, aiming for an annual battery production capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours (GWh).
The company decided to delay the start of production until the first half of next year.
This change could affect Hyundai's and its subsidiary Kia Corp.'s production plans.
On Monday, an LG Energy Solution spokesperson stated that due to market environmental factors, the company postponed the start of production from this year to next year.
It is still too early to determine if last week's event will impact the factory's operations.
Spokespersons for Hyundai and Kia stated that it is currently impossible to determine the impact on their business.
Before the raid, Samsung Electronics issued internal guidelines in May requiring employees using short-term ESTA visas for U.S. business trips not to exceed two weeks.
The raid highlighted risks faced by foreign enterprises that rely on contractors to fill labor gaps while waiting months to obtain U.S. visas.

The chairman of the Korea Chamber of Commerce and SK Group urged the Korean government to secure more U.S. visa quotas to ensure smooth enterprise operations and prevent similar incidents from recurring.
Investors initially reacted calmly, with LG Energy Solution and Hyundai's stock prices performing slightly below the market average.

Analysts noted that news of LG Energy Solution's potential production delay had already been fully disclosed, and Hyundai has flexibility to adjust electric vehicle production and use alternative suppliers.
By the end of trading, LG Energy Solution's stock price rose by 0.2%, while Hyundai's stock price fell by 0.7%.
Some investors expressed growing concerns about the profitability of Korean enterprises investing in the U.S.

The chief investment officer of Life Asset Management Inc. stated that this event demonstrates how difficult it has become for Korean enterprises to profit from investments in the U.S., noting inflation is eroding returns and enterprises face recruitment challenges.
This high-profile immigration enforcement action was the largest single-site enforcement operation in the history of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security's investigative arm.

During a meeting with opposition party leaders in Seoul, the Korean President acknowledged the challenging situation but did not directly address the detention of Korean citizens in the U.S., emphasizing the need to strengthen national power.
The Korean Minister of Foreign Affairs is expected to depart for Washington on Monday to seek the release of 300 Korean citizens detained during the raid.
A Korean government official revealed that Korea is pushing for a "voluntary departure" arrangement to repatriate its citizens using chartered flights.
The Minister of Foreign Affairs stated that detainees returning to Korea with them on Wednesday is not ruled out, depending on negotiation progress.
Enterprises were urged to comply with U.S. immigration laws.

In a post on the social media platform Truth Social, the encouraged enterprises to bring their highly intelligent and technically skilled talent to the U.S. to create world-class products, assuring them that the process would be fast and legal.
The factory in Georgia is a key facility in the U.S.'s efforts to build a domestic electric vehicle supply chain.
Beyond its diplomatic significance, the delay in the factory's production adds new risks for automakers that rely on battery supplies to meet their production goals.

An analyst from Yuanta Securities noted that due to loss of key personnel during equipment installation and trial production phases, achieving mass production of electric vehicle batteries by 2026 is no longer possible.
The analyst predicted that given the lack of alternative employee visa solutions, the factory's production start would be delayed by at least a year through diplomatic channels.
The analyst also stated that given LG Energy Solution's prior warning about potential production delays, the impact of this event on the company's earnings would be limited.
source_title: Korean Enterprises Cautious After U.S. Immigration Raid on Hyundai-LG Factory

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
canonical_name: LGES-HMGMA Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) will invest an additional $2 billion in their battery cell manufacturing joint venture (JV).
The JV is located at the Metaplant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The additional investment raises the JV's total investment value to more than $4.3 billion.
The new investment will create another 400 new jobs.

In May 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LGES signed a memorandum of understanding establishing LGES as the partner for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s (HMGMA) onsite battery cell manufacturing JV.
The combined EV manufacturing facility and battery JV represent an estimated $7.59 billion in total investment.
The combined projects will create 8,500 new jobs for the region over the next eight years.
The battery cell manufacturing facility has a capacity of 30 GWh.
At full operations, the facility will support the production of 300,000 EV units annually.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the Bryan County plant.
The assembled battery packs will be supplied to Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities.
The supplied batteries will be used for the production of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
Georgia State Governor Brian Kemp stated that they broke ground on the largest project in state history in a single year.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that multiple suppliers were landed across the state for Hyundai’s Metaplant.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed LGES to Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated the goal is to make Georgia the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp claimed major investments ultimately go to hardworking Georgians in the form of paychecks, improved schools and infrastructure.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated the incremental investment reflects a continued commitment to create a more sustainable future powered by American workers.
José Muñoz expressed gratitude for the support of Governor Kemp and communities throughout Georgia.
Dong-Myung Kim, president and head of the Advanced Automotive Battery Division of LG Energy Solution, stated the investment underscores a dedication to driving America’s EV transition.
Dong-Myung Kim noted the investment aims to bolster the local economy through the creation of quality jobs.
Dong-Myung Kim thanked the State of Georgia for its support in bringing top-quality products and operational experience to grow with local communities.
source_title: Hyundai and LG Energy Solution up US Investment for Battery JV - World-Energy

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: The new battery cell joint venture will be located in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia, adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America which is currently under construction.
Total investment for the joint venture will exceed USD 4.3 billion (KRW 5.7 trillion).
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will each hold a 50 percent stake in the joint venture.
The joint venture aims to begin battery production at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
Annual production capacity of the new facility will be 30 GWh.
The facility's capacity will support the annual production of 300,000 EVs.
Construction of the joint venture facility is planned to start in the second half of 2023.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the new plant.
Hyundai Mobis will supply the assembled battery packs to Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities for production of Hyundai, Kia and Genesis EV models.
The new facility aims to create a stable regional battery supply and allow the group to respond fast to soaring EV demand in the U.S. market.
This marks LGES's seventh battery plant currently operating or under construction in the U.S., with the company concentrating most of its resources on expanding local production capacity.
Hyundai Motor Group and LGES have long been partners in electrification, having previously supplied EV batteries for vehicles including the Elantra Hybrid, Kona Electric, and IONIQ 6 dedicated EV.
The Hyundai Elantra Hybrid, an LPi hybrid vehicle introduced in 2009, was the group’s first electrified model.
In 2021, both companies started construction of a separate Indonesia battery cell joint venture set to start production in the first half of 2024.
Hyundai Motor Group and LGES signed a memorandum of understanding for U.S. EV battery production on May 26, 2023, at LGES headquarters in Seoul.
Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution, attended the signing ceremony.

“Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on its electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry. We will create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition through establishing a new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution, a leading global battery producer and long-time partner,” said Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company.
“Two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands, and together we are ready to drive the EV transition in America,” said Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution.

links.context: LG Energy Solution news and innovations

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution to Establish Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture in the U.S.

facts: Germany’s largest investor in Georgia declared intentions for an $800 million investment in the state with U.S. interests at heart.
Aurubis AG is focused on “metals for America” at a new smelting operation just south of Augusta.
A ribbon-cutting ceremony was held Sept. 20 with patriotic flair including a marching-band welcome, a national anthem rendition by a classically trained singer, and a James Brown impersonator.
The facility will soon house equipment for sampling material to determine its metal content.
Aurubis Richmond joins a network of plants employing 7,000 people from Germany to Bulgaria and beyond.
It is the first major secondary smelter in the U.S.
New Aurubis global CEO Toralf Haag stated the investment will make supply chains more independent by keeping strategically important metals in the loop for the U.S. economy and its customers.

Aurubis uses shredding and smelting technology with massive rotating furnaces installed by German supplier SMS to separate copper and other metals from printed circuit boards and insulated copper wire.
The resulting slag material fuels up to half of the facility’s own fuel needs.
CEO Toralf Haag stated American copper stays here, powers lives here, and drives futures here, strengthening American industry and entrepreneurship.
Copper can be “infinitely” recycled, creating a closed-loop system that cuts emissions drastically versus mining when paired with the right energy mix.
CEO Toralf Haag stated today's penny could be tomorrow's transmission line, mobile phone, or medical device, and their metals will drive economic growth across Georgia and the U.S.
Aurubis claims its smelters emit half the greenhouse gases of competitors.
The company aims to be carbon-neutral before 2050 with the use of new hydrogen technology.

In December 2022, Aurubis announced a second phase in Augusta, doubling capacity to 180,000 tons of copper through a commitment of a second $400 million that brought the investment to its record-breaking level.
Third and fourth phases are possible, with the company currently only using about a third of the 150 acres it purchased.
Colin Kelly, chair of the Recycled Materials Association (ReMA), said the industry is now viewed as “sustainable, resilient and essential.”
Colin Kelly stated there is no decarbonization or clean economy without this sector, and the facility brings needed materials.
Jasjit Singh, executive director of SelectUSA, said Aurubis exemplifies policies prioritizing the intersection of clean-energy and national security.
Jasjit Singh noted the U.S. has been the No. 1 foreign investment destination for 12 years running, and Georgia has been wonderfully placed over recent years.
Before opening the first phase, the company hired more than 150 of the 230 people it plans to employ.

Aurubis invested heavily in community engagement, helping set up an apprenticeship program and hosting oversubscribed job fairs that helped grow local technical college enrollment and change perceptions around manufacturing.
The company put $200,000 into workforce training and has been an ally in promoting manufacturing as a career path.

Augusta Technical College President Jermaine Whirl noted Aurubis hammered home messages with parents and counselors, and Augusta Tech faculty spent time in Germany learning processes to teach a growing pipeline of students.
Augusta is a beneficiary of federal policies aimed at driving domestic resiliency in the clean-energy sector.
The Inflation Reduction Act provides incentives for domestically sourced materials for electric-vehicle batteries.

The IRA has been a key driver for companies like Denkai, a Japanese producer of copper films, and Syensqo, the Belgian plastics firm that got a $178 million U.S. Department of Energy Grant to make separator films for EV batteries.
Augusta Chamber of Commerce President Angie Cox described the influx of foreign firms as “transformative.”
Augusta is set to welcome an estimated 100,000 guests for the Arts in the Heart festival over that weekend.
Ms. Cox noted Augusta positions itself as a clean energy, EV battery and recycling hub, especially with the Workforce Hub designation from the Biden-Harris administration.
She added the city enjoys a strong industry mix, especially with Fort Eisenhower (formerly Fort Gordon) designated as a U.S. cybersecurity hub.
Gov. Brian Kemp returned to the Aurubis plant two years after a groundbreaking under the hot Georgia sun.
Gov. Kemp said the investment is evidence of the business-friendly environment his government helped create.
Area Development magazine named Georgia its top spot for business for the 11th straight year.
Gov. Kemp stated he talks about this company and project all the time as one of his favorites, showing the value of the state’s international relationships and local community diligence.
Gov. Kemp noted hearing the American national anthem reminded him of going to the symphony in Hamburg while visiting the Aurubis facility on a European mission.
David Schultheis is president and managing director of Aurubis Richmond.
Mr. Schultheis stated American employees join a team with 61 nationalities around the world, many represented at the ribbon-cutting.
He expressed love for working with an international and diverse team, having a foot in both German and American cultures.
Aurubis Richmond used 4,000 tons of structural steel in the building process.
1 million working hours were spent since groundbreaking two years ago.
130 feet is the height of the tallest structure on the premises.
source_title: Aurubis Ribbon Cutting: Germany’s Largest Investor in Georgia Focuses on American Security, Sustainability  - Global Atlanta

facts: Aurubis AG has officially commenced operations at its first U.S. multimetal recycling plant in Richmond County, Georgia.
The facility will produce strategic metals including copper, nickel, tin, and precious metals.
These recovered metals play a key role in expanding data centers, AI applications, energy infrastructure, high-tech manufacturing, and the defense industry.
Global demand for strategic metals, especially copper, continues to surge.
The U.S. industry requires approximately 1.8 million tons of copper annually.
Experts forecast a nearly 30% increase in strategic metal demand over the next five years.
Currently, the U.S. imports about half of the copper it processes.
Aurubis Richmond aims to close the domestic supply gap by leveraging advanced multimetal recycling technologies to enhance the resilience and independence of American supply chains.
Aurubis is investing around $800 million into the Richmond facility.
The investment will create over 240 new jobs in Georgia.
The plant is designed to process up to 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials each year.
Processed inputs will include printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other metal-bearing products.
An expansion phase for the facility is scheduled to begin in 2026.
Aurubis Richmond will recover critical metals directly from recycled materials, effectively closing the value chain on site.
By sourcing materials locally, the plant helps retain valuable raw resources within the U.S. market, supporting sustainability and supply chain security.
source_title: Aurubis Launches First U.S. Multimetal Recycling Plant In Georgia

entity_name: U.S. E-waste and Battery Recycling Industry
canonical_name: U.S. E-waste and Battery Recycling Industry
facts: The U.S. government is pouring real money into recycled electronics to cut reliance on China.
The Pentagon acquired a significant equity stake in MP Materials, the only company currently mining rare earths domestically.
President Trump imposed a 50% import tariff on global copper supplies, pushing prices to an all-time high.
The U.S. is leveraging existing e-waste (dead phones, broken EV batteries, servers, laptops) as an alternative to new mining, which takes decades to scale.
The strategy focuses on recycling metals like neodymium, praseodymium, and terbium from e-waste instead of waiting 30 years for new mines.
Companies including Glencore, Full Circle Electronics, Cyclic Materials, and startup Illumynt are competing to process e-waste as raw material in the geopolitical competition with China.
Recycled rare earth metals are critical components for fighter jets, power tools, MRI machines, wind turbines, and other high-end military and industrial technology.
Kunal Sinha, global head of recycling at Glencore, noted that the recycling industry has only recently gained serious attention.
Kunal Sinha stated that many industry participants underestimate the massive potential of e-waste recycling.
Glencore has operated a smelter in Quebec for decades to process old electronics into copper slabs and extract precious metals like gold, silver, palladium, and platinum.
Recycled electronics now constitute 15% of Glencore's Quebec smelter feedstock.
Glencore entered a multi-year agreement with Cyclic Materials to supply recycled copper sourced from EV motors, data center components, and wind turbines.
Cyclic Materials is investing over $20 million to construct a new facility in Mesa, Arizona.
High demand for recycled materials is driving foreign companies to enter the U.S. recycling market.
German company Wieland launched a $100 million recycling facility in Kentucky in 2022.
Aurubis opened an $800 million multi-metal recycling plant in Georgia.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated that the Richmond facility aims to retain strategically important metals domestically and reduce reliance on China.
John Mitchell, president of the Global Electronics Association, noted that most U.S.-imported electronics contain significant amounts of copper, gold, and steel.
John Mitchell suggested that current tariffs could serve as a catalyst for increased domestic recycling efforts.
E-waste recycling offers a faster supply chain alternative to new mining, as waste streams are continuous and daily.
Full Circle Electronics CEO Dave Daily reported a surge in corporate e-waste disposal as companies rush equipment upgrades before prices increase further.
Dave Daily confirmed a noticeable influx of e-waste entering Full Circle Electronics' warehouses.
Full Circle Electronics disassembles old electronics into 40 to 50 distinct material types for sale to metal recyclers.
Lithium-ion batteries are increasingly used as feedstock due to their high content of lithium, cobalt, nickel, manganese, and aluminum.
Companies including Ascend Elements, Redwood Materials, Cirba Solutions, and American Battery Technology are recovering battery metals to supply EV battery manufacturing.
The viability of these recyclers depends on the Biden-era 45X tax credit, which faces potential elimination under Trump's proposed spending plans.
Kunal Sinha cautioned against building recycling business models solely around temporary tax credits.
Startup Illumynt is extracting rare earth metals from decommissioned hard drives sourced from data centers.
In April, Western Digital partnered with Microsoft, Critical Materials Recycling, and PedalPoint Recycling to recover copper, gold, aluminum, steel, and rare earths from legacy hard drives.

The IT asset disposition (ITAD) sector is expanding rapidly, with companies like Waste Management, Clean Harbors, Sims Lifecycle Services, and Electronic Recyclers International processing and dismantling devices.
Global e-waste production reached nearly 62 million metric tons in 2022, representing an 82% increase since 2010.
The United States generated approximately 8 million metric tons of e-waste in 2022, with only 15–20% being recycled.
The U.S. e-waste recycling market generated $28.1 billion in revenue last year and is expanding at an annual rate of 8%, according to IBISWorld.
In April 2025, China imposed export restrictions on rare earth magnets as a retaliatory measure against new U.S. tariffs.
Ford was forced to halt production at certain factories because of the resulting rare earth magnet shortages.
In June, China issued several six-month export licenses for rare earth magnets to alleviate supply constraints, though volumes remain below normal levels.
China maintains a 90% global market share across the entire rare earth supply chain, including mining, refining, and processing.
The Biden administration previously provided MP Materials with a $45 million grant to support domestic rare earth development.
In April, the U.S. Department of the Interior approved development of the Colosseum rare earth project located within California's Mojave National Preserve.
The Colosseum site, owned by Australian firm Dateline Resources, has the potential to become the second operational rare earth mine in the United States.
Kunal Sinha advised investors to focus on fundamental business metrics rather than industry hype or unproven technologies.
Glencore invested $327.5 million into Toronto-based battery recycler Li-Cycle.
Li-Cycle filed for Chapter 15 bankruptcy protection in May following the commencement of construction on a facility in Rochester, New York.
Glencore is attempting to acquire Li-Cycle's failed project through a $40 million bid.
Kunal Sinha predicted that current market conditions will spur increased startup formation and investment in the recycling sector, noting Glencore's own active investment strategy.
source_title: Battery and hard drive waste becomes new battleground

facts: Construction of Aurubis’ ~$800 million plant in Georgia marks a new beginning for sustainable multimetal recycling in the United States.
The plant will provide over 230 high-quality jobs, ensuring growth and economic stability for families and businesses.
Capacity to process over 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually, including circuit boards and copper cables.
Aurubis’ metals are crucial contributors to renewable energy, e-mobility, battery recycling, and other industries.

The Supervisory Board of Aurubis AG, a European-based global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide, announced a significant and accelerated investment in its U.S. operations.
The company plans to spend an additional € 250 million to fast-track the expansion of its Richmond plant, which is currently under construction in Augusta, Georgia.
This investment is projected to double throughput volume from the previously planned 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials to 180,000 tons per year.
Once both phases are complete, Aurubis anticipates about 200 employees at the facility.
Aurubis broke ground on their Richmond plant in June 2022.

Together with the expansion stage announced today and taking into account a capital expenditure adjustment for infrastructure requirements and inflation of € 90 million for the current construction project, Aurubis plans to invest a total of € 640 million in the U.S. state of Georgia.
Aurubis expects to generate operative earnings (EBITDA) of around € 170 million starting in fiscal year 2026/27.

Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis commented that the world needs metals such as copper – which can be recycled without loss of quality or performance – for our cars, mobile phones, batteries, cables, and computers.
Roland Harings stated that today’s high recycling demand cannot be met by existing facilities.
Roland Harings added that Aurubis’ increased commitment to the U.S. will be a game-changer – for the environment, for manufacturers, and for the larger transition to a renewable economy.
Aurubis Richmond’s first phase is expected to come online in mid-2024 with the completed expansion scheduled for 2026.
Aurubis received the necessary approvals for the operation of both facilities earlier in the year.
Dr. Toralf Haag, Chief Executive Officer, stated that with Aurubis Richmond, they are a pioneer for multimetal recycling in the US.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that through many meetings with Aurubis leadership in Georgia and Germany, they heard how their goals aligned with creating good paying jobs and building a lasting partnership to position them as leaders in an emerging electric industry.
Governor Brian Kemp added that after years of hard work, they get to celebrate cutting the ribbon on the facility and formally welcome the next chapter for Aurubis in the Augusta community.
After breaking ground in June 2022, Aurubis made the strategic decision at the end of 2022 to fast-track the expansion of the plant and invest a total of around $ 800 million.
With the expansion stage, the facility will be able process up to 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually, including circuit boards, copper cables, and other metal-containing products.
David Schultheis is President and Managing Director of Aurubis Richmond.
David Schultheis stated that Aurubis Richmond is going to become a major player in the North American multimetal supply chain and a significant employer in greater Augusta.
David Schultheis expressed pride in the team achieving building this state-of-the-art recycling plant in just over two years, noting there is so much more to come.

David Schultheis stated they are grateful for the support of Georgia officials and leaders that provided a warm welcome and supported them throughout the journey, describing it as a true partnership they look forward to intensifying.
Aurubis’ new Richmond facility is equipped with advanced technology in line with environmental standards set by both the State of Georgia and federal regulators.

Operations are conducted with the highest regard for air, water and wildlife protection, aligning with Aurubis’ mission of being a good environmental steward and its strategic vision to be carbon-neutral well before 2050.

The ribbon-cutting ceremony emphasized Aurubis Richmond’s role in contributing to a circular economy by supplying essential metals to key industries such as renewable energy, electric vehicles, and battery manufacturing.
The facility is expected to play a critical role in the North American supply chain, cementing Aurubis’ position as a global leader in multimetal recycling.
Aurubis Richmond is committed to being a major employer in the greater Augusta area, providing over 230 high-quality jobs and contributing to the local economy.
The company is dedicated to community engagement and providing valuable learning opportunities and scholarships to train tomorrow’s leaders.
Aurubis Richmond has pledged to a community benefit agreement with Augusta Technical College and Aiken Technical College as well as with the Richmond County School System.
As part of this agreement, the company will invest in scholarships and job training programs for young workers.
Christoph Tesch is Head of Corporate Communications.
Meino Hauschildt is Manager Corporate Communications and Spokesperson.
source_title: Aurubis celebrates ribbon-cutting at the first multimetal recycling plant in the United States

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will hold a job fair in Savannah with recruiters for three plants currently under construction in Bulloch County that will supply parts to the Hyundai plant.

Career opportunities at the event will include manufacturing, logistics, technology, and administration roles.

Ecoplastic America Corporation, a supplier of injection-molded plastic automotive body parts to Hyundai Motor Group, is building a $205-million plant off Highway 301 South that will create 456 jobs phased in over eight years.

Ajin USA, a supplier of metal auto body parts to Hyundai, is investing $317 million and will eventually employ 630 people at its Joon Georgia plant on 83 acres in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park off Interstate 16.

Hanon Systems, a manufacturer of vehicle air conditioning systems, will build a plant in Statesboro’s Gateway Regional Industrial Park off Highway 301 South, with a $40 million investment projected to create 160 jobs.

Recruiters from additional suppliers scheduled to attend include DSC Savannah, Glovis EV Logistics America, Hyundai Materials, Hyundai Steel Georgia, Hyundai Transys GA, Kyungshin America, Mobis Alabama Savannah Plant, Mobis North America Electrified Powertrain, PHA Georgia, Seohan Auto Georgia, Seoyon E-Hwa, and Sewon America.

facts: Sewon America will create 100 new jobs in Rincon as part of a $50 million expansion of its manufacturing plant.

facts: Mahle commenced production of e-compressors at its Morristown, Tennessee facility in August 2026.
The Morristown, Tennessee facility is historically known for steel piston manufacturing.
The e-compressor production initiative creates approximately 50 new jobs.
The initiative targets the North American market.
The e-compressors are essential for EV thermal management and will serve both internal combustion engine and electrification applications.
The U.S. is the largest consumer of advanced thermal management systems, driven by its strong automotive sector and rapid EV penetration.
Federal and state-level EV tax credits and infrastructure investments encourage automakers to integrate sophisticated battery cooling, liquid thermal loops, and waste heat recovery systems.
Companies like Tesla, Ford, and GM are investing heavily in R&D to enhance battery longevity, charging efficiency, and thermal safety.
Strict CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) and EPA emission standards push manufacturers to adopt thermal technologies that improve energy efficiency and reduce carbon emissions.
North America is expected to account for 39.9% of the global automotive thermal management market share in 2026.
The presence of automotive giants like General Motors, Ford and Fiat-Chrysler has made the U.S. the largest automotive producer in the world.
Several auto component majors have established their base in North America to cater to OEM needs for innovative thermal management technologies.
Advanced systems such as liquid cooling, phase change materials, and smart thermal control units are being widely integrated to improve fuel efficiency and reduce carbon emissions.
The rapid adoption of electric and hybrid vehicles further drives demand for efficient battery thermal solutions.

Battery thermal management is expected to hold the largest share of the market in 2026 due to the critical need for battery temperature regulation, safety, overheating prevention, and driving range extension.
Leading manufacturers such as Tesla, BYD, Volkswagen, GM, and Hyundai are heavily investing in advanced battery cooling systems, including liquid cooling and phase-change materials.
HVAC and battery cooling can cut EV range by ~5% in moderate heat, with losses modeled at 30–50% under extreme climates.
Parasitic energy consumption from thermal systems is measured at ~3% of total driving energy, escalating sharply in severe weather.
DENSO’s “Everycool” system reports 57% energy savings versus conventional cabin cooling.
source_title: Automotive Thermal Management Market Size & Trend, 2026-2033
