The price tag for the latest plant is $4 to $5 billion.
The facility is expected to create more than 3,500 jobs.
The plant will be built at Bartow Centre, a zoned manufacturing and industrial area located along Highway 411, just west of Cartersville.
The plan is to get the massive facility built and up and running by 2025.
The plant will be one of the largest economic development projects in state history.
HMG and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding regarding the partnership for the new battery facility.
The two companies are still working out specific details.
SK On is the lithium-ion battery subsidiary branch of SK Innovation.
SK On currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), stated that HMG and SK have been pioneering partners for Georgia for decades as major drivers for Georgia’s automotive renaissance and as the first Korean manufacturer to locate in the state, respectively.

GDEcD is supporting cooperation and partnerships across Georgia's growing EV ecosystem to create a fully integrated supply chain for automotive OEMs while connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers.

The state maintains that Georgia’s prime location, major ports system, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and pro-business climate have positioned it as a hub for the electric transportation industry.
Bartow County commissioner Steve Taylor noted the county is very pleased with the decision of Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
Taylor thanked the owners of the former Bartow Centre industrial property.
Taylor highlighted Bartow’s strong history and talent-base built upon advanced manufacturing, the automotive industry, and innovation as reasons the battery partnership should thrive.
Director of Korean investment, Yoonie Kim, represented the GDEcD global commerce team on the project.
The project is in partnership with Georgia EMC, the development authority of Bartow County, the Cartersville-Bartow County department of economic development, and Georgia Quick Start.
Atlanta Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai and SK On are valued partners and key players in the state’s ever-growing automotive industry.
Kemp noted his administration has been focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to communities across the state that may have been overlooked in the past.
In November, Hyundai Mobis announced plans to invest $926 million in an EV power electric system plant in Bryan County.
The Hyundai Mobis plant will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia announced it will create 630 new jobs.
Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia announced it will invest $317 million in a new manufacturing facility at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park near Statesboro in Bulloch County to supply Hyundai.
FREYR Battery announced it will invest $2.57 billion in a manufacturing facility in Coweta County that will create 723 new jobs.
In October, HMG broke ground on a $5.54 billion EV plant in Bryan County near Savannah that will employ at least 8,100 workers.
Rivian Automotive announced plans for a $5-billion electric truck plant east of Atlanta that’s expected to employ about 7,500 workers.
source_title: South Korean conglomerates partner on EV battery plant in Bartow County, Ga.

facts: A Hyundai Motor Group supplier on Tuesday announced a new parts factory in Bulloch County.
It is the first supplier to confirm plans to build a facility to support Hyundai’s sprawling $5.54 billion electric vehicle plant near Savannah.
Joon Georgia, a subsidiary of Ajin USA, will build a parts factory at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park near Statesboro.
The site is located about 30 miles west of the future Hyundai EV plant in neighboring Bryan County.
Joon is expected to hire 630 workers at the facility.
The parts factory represents a $317 million investment.
Gov. Brian Kemp’s office provided a news release regarding the hiring and investment details.
Ajin USA supplies multiple Hyundai plants and makes vehicle body parts and electronics.
The company said the new factory will open in 2024.
source_title: Hyundai supplier announces factory near future coastal EV plant

facts: Ecoplastic Corp., a Korean auto parts manufacturer, is investing $205 million to build a new manufacturing facility in Statesboro, GA.
The company is a tier-1 supplier for Hyundai.
Ecoplastic produces parts such as consoles and bumpers.
The project is expected to bring more than 450 jobs to the region.
Production is slated to begin in October 2024.
Han Shang, president and CEO of Ecoplastic, stated that the company is establishing a production facility for electric vehicle parts in Bulloch County.
Han Shang expressed appreciation for the support of Georgia state and county governments regarding the project.
source_title: Hyundai Supplier to Invest $205 Million in Georgia Manufacturing Plant | ASSEMBLY

facts: Five South Architecture, LLC is leading the architectural design for a new manufacturing facility for Ecoplastic America in Statesboro, Georgia.
The new Ecoplastic America manufacturing facility will be 285,000 square feet.
Ecoplastic America is a major supplier to Hyundai Motor Company.
The new facility will enhance Ecoplastic America's current capacity to deliver high-quality automotive components.
The project strengthens the supply chain for Hyundai’s expanding U.S. operations.
This announcement follows Hyundai Motor Group’s recently announced $20 billion investment plan in the United States.
Hyundai’s $20 billion investment plan includes the development of a new steel plant in Louisiana.
Hyundai’s investment plan also covers ongoing electric vehicle (EV) and battery production initiatives.
Hyundai’s investment aims to create thousands of jobs and foster economic growth across Louisiana, Georgia, and Alabama.
The Ecoplastic America facility will supply critical components for Hyundai’s growing U.S. footprint.
Justin Rogers is the Owner of Five South Architecture, LLC.

Justin Rogers stated that designing a facility of this scale allows his firm to showcase expertise in creating innovative, efficient spaces and contribute to the momentum Ecoplastic America and Hyundai are driving in the U.S. automotive industry.
Five South Architecture is also providing architectural design services for a new 465,000-square-foot Hyundai Mobis spare parts logistics facility in Montgomery, Alabama.
The Hyundai Mobis logistics facility is valued at $52 million.
Groundbreaking for the Hyundai Mobis logistics facility occurred in October 2024.
The Hyundai Mobis facility will serve as a critical hub for aftermarket parts supporting Hyundai and Kia dealerships nationwide.
The Hyundai Mobis facility integrates seamlessly with the existing Hyundai Mobis Campus.
Five South Architecture is based in Birmingham, Alabama.
source_title: Five South Architecture, LLC to Lead Design of New 285,000sf Ecoplastic America Manufacturing Facility in Statesboro, GA

facts: The Covington facility will become North America’s largest battery recycling operation.
Ascend Elements is investing $43 million into its new Covington, Georgia facility.
The facility will be a commercial-scale, lithium-ion battery recycling operation spanning 154,000 SF.
Once complete, the facility will have the capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and scrap per year.
The recycling process will minimize the need for mining while returning valuable, battery-grade lithium, nickel, and cobalt back into the battery supply chain.
The Covington site is strategically located near several electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing hubs and lithium-ion battery producers.
Ascend Elements aims to be fully operational later this year.
SSOE Group is providing architecture, engineering, procurement, construction, and commissioning services for the project.

Dan Russell, Chief Operating Officer of Ascend Elements, stated that SSOE's early engagement for technical evaluation and expertise in battery and automotive markets helped facilitate a seamless transition into facility design.
Over the past six years, SSOE has been engaged in more than 20 battery / EV manufacturing projects across 18 facilities in the U.S. and Mexico.
Rick Fox, PMP, serves as the Project Manager for SSOE on the Ascend project.
Rick Fox noted that despite growth, the EV market is still in its infancy globally.
SSOE has gained experience in all phases of the battery lifecycle, from materials production to cell production, through recycling.
The project involves tight schedules, requiring diligence in working with code officials and environmental permitting agencies to ensure safe delivery.
SSOE is developing alternative execution and procurement strategies to quickly meet Ascend Elements' business objectives.
Scott Hazen, PE, PMP, serves as the Project Director for the project.
The project team has remained agile to adjust plans for issues such as delays in materials and equipment due to global supply chain issues.
SSOE is assisting Ascend Elements with preliminary engineering for future expansions, including greenfield work in Southwestern Kentucky.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Massachusetts, and operates as a vertically integrated battery recycling and engineered materials company.
The company utilizes its patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ technology to directly synthesize new cathode active materials from spent lithium-ion cells.

This technology results in reduced cost, improved performance, lowered GHG emissions, better performance batteries with fewer discarded in landfill, a cleaner manufacturing process, and a sustainable closed-loop circular battery economy.
source_title: SSOE Group Providing Architecture and Engineering Services for Ascend Elements’ Georgia-Based Brownfield Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling Facility - SSOE Group

entity_name: Ascend Elements Georgia Battery Recycling Facility
facts: Ascend Elements, a US American company, has opened its commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Georgia.
The company invested $50 million in the site.
Ascend Elements claims the site is North America’s largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility to date.
The facility can recover up to 98 percent of critical metals used in lithium-ion batteries.
The facility uses Ascend Elements' proprietary 'Hydro-to-Cathode' process for recycling.

Through its Hydro-to-Cathode process, Ascend Elements engineers the composition and microstructure of lithium-ion cathode particles during direct precursor synthesis to deliver custom-made battery materials to precise customer specifications.
The Hydro-to-Cathode process is described as more efficient and environmentally friendly than other recycling methods.
The process recovers 98 percent of critical battery metals from spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The process produces new cathode material at half the cost of similar cathodes made with newly mined and refined metals.
The process generates 90 percent lower greenhouse gas emissions compared to similar cathodes made with newly mined and refined metals.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility has now reached its full operational capacity.
The site can process up to 30,000 tonnes of batteries and manufacturing scrap annually.
Ascend Elements plans to produce sustainable battery materials for electric vehicles at a future facility in Kentucky.
The Kentucky facility is expected to be completed towards the end of the year.
The Kentucky facility will manufacture enough sustainable cathode material and precursors for up to 250,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
source_title: US: Ascend Elements opens battery recycling plant in Georgia - electrive.com

facts: Ascend Elements, a Westborough, Massachusetts-based provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions, has been certified to the ISO 9001:2015 standard for quality management systems at its electric vehicle (EV) battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia.
The certification was issued by DQS Inc., an independent certification body.

Andrew Aberdale, Ascend Elements Chief Financial Officer and Chief Operating Officer, stated that the ISO certification signals a commitment to quality, customer service, and rigorous safety procedures.
The Covington facility is one of the largest EV battery recycling facilities in North America.
The facility has an annual processing capacity of 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and scrap.
The site occupies 154,000 square feet in Newton County.
Operational capabilities include battery discharge and disassembly, battery shredding, lithium extraction, and analytical testing.
From recycled EV batteries, the facility commercially produces lithium carbonate, nickel-manganese-cobalt precursor (pCAM), and cathode active materials (CAM).
source_title: Ascend Elements’ EV battery recycling facility receives certification - Recycling Today

facts: The collaboration combines Ascend Elements’ electric vehicle battery recycling infrastructure and technologies with Call2Recycle’s collection, logistics and tracking capabilities.
The program provides online access to GreenTraxEV, Call2Recycle’s cloud-based all-in-one platform designed to ship mid-life, end-of-life and damaged-defective EV batteries.
The companies plan to collect and recycle the equivalent of over 300,000 electric vehicle battery packs by 2030.
Ascend Elements operates a facility in the US state of Georgia with processing capacity to handle 30,000 metric tons per year.
Ascend Elements' Georgia facility is one of the largest EV battery recycling facilities in North America.
Leo Raudys, CEO of Call2Recycle, stated the partnership aims to build infrastructure for electric vehicle battery transportation and recycling.
The commercial agreement enables a convenient collection process through Call2Recycle's GreenTraxEV logistics platform.
Ian Braime, Chief Commercial Officer at Ascend Elements, stated the expanded partnership will offer a comprehensive EV battery recycling and tracking program to customers.
The new program is designed to help automotive dealerships, repair shops, and recyclers prepare for the expected wave of EV batteries in the coming years.
Ascend Elements previously announced a similar cooperation with SK Ecoplant last September.
Ascend Elements recently opened a plant in Poland.
source_title: Ascend Elements & Call2Recycle launch collaboration in the US - electrive.com

canonical_name: Georgia Mid-Year 2023 EV and Clean Energy Investment Announcements
facts: Georgia officials have landed several gigantic projects promising billions of dollars in investment and thousands of jobs during the first half of 2023.
Clean energy and the electric vehicle sector continue to lead the pack, with companies clustering around Georgia’s pipeline of large EV factories and suppliers.

On Tuesday, during the Paris Air Show, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office announced aircraft and helicopter engine company Pratt & Whitney will expand its Columbus operations, a $206 million project that is expected to spur 400 additional jobs.
Qcells announced a $2.5 billion investment for an expansion and new plant.
Anovion Technologies announced a $1.8 billion investment.
Hyundai suppliers announced a $5.5 billion investment.
Anderson Corp. announced a $249 million investment.
Admares announced a $1.057 billion investment.
Sam’s Club announced a $241.5 million investment.
Meissner announced a $1.052 billion investment.
The state has announced massive projects across multiple industries, from logistics to life sciences.
source_title: Georgia largest job recruitment announcements so far in 2023

facts: Solvay confirmed plans to build a new battery-grade polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) facility in Augusta, Georgia.
The project is pivotal for the U.S. EV battery supply chain and benefits from a grant from the U.S. Department of Energy and a joint venture with Orbia.
More than half of U.S. car sales are projected to be electric by 2030, driving demand for U.S.-produced PVDF to meet the growing EV battery and domestic energy storage markets.
At full capacity, the new operations will provide material for more than 5 million EV batteries per year.
The project will create hundreds of jobs throughout the value chain.
Solvay and Orbia have signed a joint venture agreement for the project.
The partnership secures Orbia's supply of needed materials for Solvay to manufacture suspension-grade PVDF.
Suspension-grade PVDF is used as a lithium-ion binder and separator coating in electric vehicle batteries.
Solvay will bring its process technology and global market know-how to the joint venture.
The combination of Solvay’s Solef® PVDF innovations and Orbia’s raw material assets and production expertise will deliver PVDF that extends EV range, battery life, and battery safety.
Ilham Kadri, CEO of Solvay, stated the project is a key milestone in Solvay's electrification strategy and emphasizes its global commitment to sustainable mobility.
Ilham Kadri noted that DOE support demonstrates Solvay’s pivotal role in advancing technologies that meet market demand and improve energy storage, safety, and power of electric vehicles.
Ilham Kadri added that the partnership with Orbia positions Solvay to shore up an independent, sustainable EV battery supply chain in North America and create clean energy jobs.
Sameer Bharadwaj, CEO of Orbia, stated the partnership underscores Orbia's commitment to enabling the clean energy transition through investments in energy materials.

Sameer Bharadwaj noted Orbia has a unique 'mine-to-market' position with integration in all key materials needed to bolster North America's EV supply chain and comply with the Inflation Reduction Act.
Solvay and Orbia intend to use two production sites in the southeastern U.S., one for raw materials and one for finished products.
Both plants are expected to become operational in 2026.
Solvay and the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains finalized an agreement for a $178 million grant to help build the facility at its site in Augusta, Georgia.
The grant was awarded under the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) to expand domestic manufacturing of batteries for EVs and the electrical grid.
The project will fill a significant supply gap and build upon favorable regulatory conditions promoting regional production and material security.
source_title: Solvay to build PVDF facility | Solvay

facts: Syensqo has completed its spin-off from Solvay, a Belgian chemical company.
Now a stand-alone, Euronext-listed company, Syensqo has more than $8.5 billion in annual sales and 13,200 global employees.
It has 34 industrial sites and 3 research and innovation centers.

The company’s Materials segment makes high-performance, high-margin Specialty Polymers and Composites, mainly for automotive (including solutions for lithium-ion and next-generation batteries) and aerospace markets.
After securing a $178-million DOE grant and forming a joint venture with Orbia, Syensqo is building a North American production facility for EV materials in Augusta, Georgia.
The new operations will provide material for over 5 million EV batteries per year at full capacity.
Mike Finelli, Chief North America Officer, stated that the company focuses on four growth platforms: battery materials, hydrogen, thermoplastic composites, and renewable materials and biotechnology.
source_title: Charged EVs | Syensqo spins off from Solvay, builds EV material factory in Georgia - Charged EVs

facts: Enacted July 4, Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) ends federal tax credits for electric vehicles by the end of September and tax breaks for American battery manufacturers in 2030.
More clean energy projects were cancelled in the first quarter of 2025 than the past two years combined, according to research group Atlas Public Policy.
Since January, cancellations and downsized clean energy projects have amounted to roughly $14 billion in abandoned investments.
The abandoned investments include a $1 billion factory planned to produce thermal barriers for batteries in Statesboro.

Georgia rolled out a red carpet worth billions of dollars in tax breaks for EV manufacturing plants, intended to become Gov. Brian Kemp-proclaimed 'electric vehicle capital of America' by the end of his second term.
Industry leaders have brushed aside Washington's setbacks and boasted the industry's growth, which they claim is headed towards an independent supply chain that will rely less on federal support.
Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols stated: 'The tax credits played a purpose, but that purpose may be over. We may not need a subsidy in the future.'

Electrification Coalition Policy Manager Skye Golann said at an Aug. 7 press briefing titled 'How EV Investments Are Powering Prosperity in Georgia': 'We must develop an American critical mineral supply chain.'

The Biden administration's Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) passed in August 2022 provided tax credits, subsidies and incentives for the purchasing and manufacturing of EVs and their charging facilities.
A 2024 report by the Rhodium Group and MIT found that investments in batteries, fueling equipment, and critical minerals refining and processing have each grown at least three-fold since the IRA.
In 2022, the U.S. Department of Energy granted $178 million for the construction of Solvay Specialty Polymers' battery-grade polyvinylidene fluoride facility in Augusta.
Last year, the DOE awarded $80 million to Fort Valley-based Blue Bird for its electric school bus manufacturing and workforce development.

Cox Automotive Director of Industry Insights Stephanie Streaty noted that EV purchases ticked up in the U.S., spurred by an IRA credit offering a $7,500 discount for a new EV and $4,000 or 30% for a used EV.
Last year was a record year of 1.3 million EVs sold, according to Streaty.
The Georgia Clean Energy Roadshow hosted by Echols at Savannah Technical College on Aug. 12 featured over 60 vehicles, including one of the City of Savannah's Nissan Leafs starting at about $30,000.
When Echols first began the roadshow 15 years ago, only the Tesla Roadster was on display, worth more than $100,000 at the time.
This year, the average EV transaction was $10,000 more expensive compared to a gas vehicle, according to Cox Automotive data.
Before passing the OBBBA, Cox Automotive predicted that EVs would make up 10% of all cars sold for 2025, up 2% from last year.
Cox adjusted its EV sales prediction to 8.5% due to tariffs, interest rates, and other headwinds, according to Streaty.
Skye Golann stated: 'Taking away the federal tax credits will dampen some of the growth. I certainly think that the One Big Beautiful Bill Act is a setback for EVs.'
Lithium-ion batteries could make up 40% of the cost of a car, said Streaty.
China still dominates the battery's mineral processing, admitted Golann.
The U.S. is home to just 1% of the world's lithium reserves, according to the World Economic Forum.
On Aug. 6, GM announced plans to co-manufacture six EV models with Hyundai, including an electric van for the North American market.
A day later, GM inked a deal to import Chinese batteries that will function as a stopgap until it can build its own lithium batteries at lower cost in the U.S., according to the Detroit Free Press.
About half of Kia's manufacturing for EV and gas vehicles remains overseas, said Kia's Head of Department Corporate Services and Information Technology Ted Arnold.
Hyundai, a parent company of Kia, is in the process of bringing suppliers locally so they don't have to import, according to Arnold.

The OBBBA will end the 45X tax credit for U.S. battery manufacturers in five years, a move that could cede growth to China, Mexico, Canada and members of the European Union, according to think tank Energy Innovation.
In January, Norway-based Freyr Battery called off a battery manufacturing plant in Newnan, stating it in a letter to Coweta County.

In February, Aspen Aerogels told investors it halted construction of its second plant manufacturing thermal barriers for EV batteries in Statesboro and will instead increase production in China, per Latitude Media.
In March, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America President José Muñoz announced an expansion focused on hybrid vehicles at its grand opening, a diversion from its expected all-electric operations.
Streaty noted: 'The IRA 45x reduction credit doesn't sunset until 2030, so I think that's a glimmer of light.'
Golann stated that domestic and statewide supply chain growth will take some time.
Dealers are getting aggressive with rebates, deals and discounts as the IRA goes away, according to Streaty.
Echols noted that manufacturers have already spent money to build assembly lines and factories and will want to put incentives on those cars.
Streaty stated that EVs eventually need to be able to sell on their own without constant incentives.
source_title: After Trump pulls the plug, can Georgia still become the "EV capital of America?"

facts: Hyundai Motor Group will build its first dedicated electric vehicle manufacturing plant in Georgia.
The facility site is located on a dusty expanse of vacant land outside Savannah in Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp, a Republican, officially announced the project to political and business figures gathered under a large tent at the site.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “It is my great honor to officially announce that Hyundai Motor Group will build their first dedicated electric vehicle manufacturing plant right here in this good soil in Bryan County.”
The announcement took place on a sweltering Friday during the summer.
Georgia prioritizes creating green jobs over adopting specific climate targets.

links.context: Newsletter signup for unlimited article access

source_title: Georgia doesn’t want climate targets — but it does want green jobs – WABE

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions announced plans late Thursday to spend $4.3 billion to build an EV battery manufacturing plant in Bryan County near Savannah.
The project is described as the largest economic development project in Georgia's state history.
The battery plant will be built adjacent to Hyundai’s massive electric vehicle “Metaplant” under construction in Ellabell.
State officials say the project is part of a previously announced plan by Hyundai to spend $5.5 billion on the new complex and create 8,100 jobs.
The battery plant will account for 3,000 of those jobs.
Batteries made at the forthcoming factory will power Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis models produced at U.S. facilities.
The South Korean-based companies made the announcement in a release.
Georgia is positioning itself as a monolithic green hub for the largest electric vehicle manufacturing complex in the state's history.
The project will receive incentives in exchange for creating local battery and electric vehicle supply chain partnerships.
A Korean American Workers Association was established to create a new workforce development program.
A supplier of local electric vehicle components will have access to all project resources and grants.
The initiative aligns with the Biden Administration's push for domestic battery manufacturing and supply chain partnerships.
source_title: Hyundai, LG to build $4.3B battery plant near Savannah

facts: Hanwha Q Cells announced an investment of more than $2.5 billion to build up the U.S. solar supply chain and create 2,500 jobs in Georgia.
The company and federal and state officials describe this as the largest such investment in U.S. history.
Qcells is a subsidiary of the South Korean industrial group Hanwha Corp.
Qcells unveiled plans to build a second plant in Georgia that will manufacture 3.3 GW of solar components and panels annually.
The company plans to assemble an additional 2 GW per year of solar panels at its original plant in Dalton, Georgia.
Qcells aims to reach 5.1 GW of annual production by 2023.
The company is working toward a goal of 8.4 GW of total annual solar panel production capacity by 2024.
Qcells plans to break ground on the second plant in Bartow County in the first quarter of 2023.
Commercial production at the second plant is scheduled to begin by late 2024.
The Dalton factory was previously producing 1.7 GW of solar modules each year.
The Dalton factory is being overhauled to increase output to 3.1 GW per year.
Qcells is raising its total U.S. output by a combined 6.7 GW over the next two years.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-Georgia) called the expansion “the largest clean energy manufacturing project in American history.”
Sen. Ossoff celebrated the company’s plan to create almost 2,500 jobs in Georgia.
U.S. Sen. Raphael Warnock (D-Georgia) said the announcement follows months of close collaboration with Qcells, Senate leadership, and the administration.
The collaboration aimed to make the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act a reality.
The Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act was sponsored by Sen. Ossoff.
The act was included in the Inflation Reduction Act.
The legislation adds a new tax credit for domestic production of solar components, including photovoltaic cells and wafers.
The solar industry is experiencing global supply constraints from an ongoing tariff investigation.
Global supply constraints are also driven by the enactment of the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act.
Lingering pandemic impacts continue to affect the solar supply chain.
Demand for solar panels is rising as supply has become less certain.
The Inflation Reduction Act extended and increased a number of tax credits for solar projects and manufacturing, contributing to rising demand.
The company stated that demand for American-made solar panels is increasing rapidly driven by efforts to ensure energy independence.
Qcells said its domestic manufacturing expansion will fulfill the growing need for clean energy solutions.
source_title: Qcells to invest $2.5B to build complete US solar supply chain, expand annual capacity to 8.4 GW | Utility Dive

facts: Luxembourg-based FREYR Battery SA purchased a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its Giga America battery plant.
The company projected an initial capital spend of $1.7 billion for the project.
FREYR plans to undertake detailed plant engineering in the coming months.
The company expects to create more than 720 jobs in the region.
Georgia is experiencing a string of electric vehicle (EV) and battery announcements.
SK Innovation has a battery unit among the recent EV and battery developments in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Co is developing a $5.54 billion EV and battery plant in Georgia for which the company broke ground last month.

Chief Executive Officer Tom Jensen said that with the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, the company expects U.S. demand for ESS (energy storage system), passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
The company is evaluating value accretive upstream and downstream modules and additional cell production lines.
These evaluations are expected to bring FREYR's total capital investments to more than $2.6 billion through 2029.
source_title: FREYR buys Georgia site for battery plant, sees $1.7 bln initial capital cost

facts: Freyr solidified its US solar pivot with the cancellation of its Georgia battery factory plan.
A battery firm abandoned its plan for a $2.6 billion plant in Georgia.
Freyr Battery abandoned its proposed $2.6 billion Georgia factory.
The projected investment value for the canceled Georgia facility was $2.6 billion.
News reports regarding the cancellation were published on February 6, 2025, February 7, 2025, and February 10, 2025.
source_title: Google News - News about Georgia - Overview

facts: FREYR Battery (NYSE: FREY) is a developer of next-generation battery cell production capacity.
FREYR Battery announced the purchase of a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its planned Giga America battery plant.
The company selected the Bridgeport Industrial Park site based on key operational, logistical, and financial criteria.
The site is suitable to co-locate modular upstream and downstream projects with the Giga America cell manufacturing facility.
The initial phase of the Giga America factory will include a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
The cell production module will be based on the next-generation of 24M Technologies U.S.-based SemiSolid™ production platform.
FREYR is evaluating value accretive upstream and downstream modules for the project.
FREYR is evaluating additional cell production lines for the project.
Total capital investments are expected to bring more than $2.6 billion through 2029.
FREYR’s Co-Founder and CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated that the announcement underscores FREYR’s ambition to develop a very strong and near-term operational footprint in the United States.
Tom Einar Jensen stated that expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from its inception.

Tom Einar Jensen stated that with the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, FREYR expects U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
The U.S. energy storage market is already the largest in the world.
The U.S. energy storage market is expected to experience exponential growth over the next several years.
The U.S. energy storage market could reach as much as 1.4 TWh by 2030.
FREYR is evaluating clean power supply solutions for the Giga America project with U.S. independent power producers.
FREYR’s long-term ambition is to replicate the strategy employed at Giga Arctic.
At Giga Arctic, FREYR plans to produce next-generation batteries that are among the cleanest anywhere in the world.
Shares of FREYR Battery trade on the NYSE under the symbol FREY.
source_title: FREYR Battery ($FREY) Releases Plans to Build Clean Battery Gigafactory in Georgia - Green Stock News

facts: FREYR Battery scrapped plans to build a $2.6 billion battery energy storage system factory in Georgia.
FREYR Battery has rebranded itself as T1 Energy (NYSE: TE).
T1 Energy is committed to becoming a vertically integrated US solar + battery storage leader from its new corporate headquarters in Austin, Texas.
T1 Energy expects to start trading under its new NYSE symbols on March 3.
In August, then-CEO Tom Einar Jensen told investors that a surplus of cheap Chinese batteries had made it harder to raise money to manufacture batteries.
The company switched its focus to solar panel manufacturing.
On December 24, FREYR announced closing its acquisition of China-headquartered Trina Solar’s 5-gigawatt (GW), 1.35 million-square-foot solar panel factory in Wilmer, Texas.
The acquired factory was renamed G1 Dallas.
The G1 Dallas factory employs more than 1,000 people.
T1 says the solar panel production ramp is on schedule.
Daniel Barcelo serves as T1’s chairman of the board and chief executive officer.
Daniel Barcelo stated that more than 80% of new US electricity capacity in 2024 was solar and batteries.

T1 plans to lead in the solar and battery markets by enabling the growth in energy supply required to support AI, data centers, reshoring, and electrification to restore American industrial capacity and leadership in future advanced industries.
T1 is moving forward with site selection for its upcoming US solar cell factory, G2.
T1 expects to lock in a location for the G2 factory by Q1 2025.
Construction for the G2 factory is still on track to kick off in mid-2025.
source_title: FREYR rebrands after killing its $2.6B Georgia battery factory plans | Electrek

facts: Archer Aviation has announced progress on its high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA.
The company reaffirmed an expected 2024 completion date for the facility.
The 400,000-square-foot facility will enable the production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually.
This makes it one of the aviation industry's largest facilities by volume.
Earlier this year, Archer completed the first build phase, which included grading and foundation work.
Archer is now nearing completion of phase two, which entails erecting the exterior walls and roof.
Phase three, consisting of interior fit-out, is scheduled to be completed in the coming months.
Phase four, consisting of equipment load-in and power-on, is scheduled to be completed in the coming months.

Adam Goldstein, CEO and founder of Archer stated: “Once complete, we’ll focus on ramping production up to 650 aircraft a year, our goal is to be the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing.”
Archer is leading the aviation electrification charge, designing and developing the key enabling technologies and aircraft that are necessary to power the next great transportation revolution.
source_title: Archer Aviation ($ACHR) Nears Completion of High-Volume eVTOL Manufacturing Facility In Georgia - Green Stock News

facts: Archer Aviation is set to finalize the construction of its 400,000-square-foot manufacturing plant in Covington, Georgia by the end of 2024.
The facility will be equipped to produce 650 units of Archer Midnight, the company’s electric aerial vehicle (EAV).
The Midnight is designed for short trips of 20 to 50 miles and can carry four passengers and a pilot.
The vehicle boasts a maximum speed of 150 mph and requires only 10 minutes of battery charging between flights.
The vehicle is intended for low-altitude operations and is designed for rapid, consecutive flights.
CEO and founder Adam Goldstein stated that thanks to the hard work of the Archer and Stellantis teams, the company is tracking towards completing the manufacturing facility by the end of the year.
Once complete, Archer will focus on ramping production up to 650 aircraft a year, with the goal of being the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing.
Archer has signed an agreement with Southwest Airlines to develop an electric air taxi network in California utilizing Archer’s Midnight eVTOLs.
The Southwest network will integrate with the airline’s operations at 14 airports across California, offering a seamless door-to-door travel experience.
Archer secured a $55 million investment from Stellantis following a successful transition flight test in June.
Stellantis had previously invested $110 million in Archer in 2023 and purchased $8 million in Archer stock earlier this year.
The partnership leverages Stellantis’ supply chain and manufacturing resources to advance the development of Archer’s eVTOLs.
Archer announced plans to establish an air mobility network connecting five locations in the San Francisco Bay area.
In collaboration with Signature Aviation, Archer will gain access to major United Airlines hubs and private aviation terminals across the U.S.
Archer has received Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) approval to commence commercial operations.
Archer is now the second air taxi company, following Joby Aviation, to achieve FAA certification.
The company aims to start commercial operations with United Airlines after receiving Type Certification next year.
Archer secured a deal with KakaoMobility in South Korea for up to 50 Midnight EAVs, valued at $250 million, with operations projected to begin in 2026.
Archer has partnered with Etihad Training to recruit and train pilots in the United Arab Emirates, with support from the Abu Dhabi Investment Office.
Archer’s total funding has reached $1.1 billion, bolstered by recent investments from Stellantis, Boeing, United Airlines, and ARK Investment Management.
source_title: Archer Aviation on Track to Complete Georgia Manufacturing Facility by Year-End

facts: Georgia has been dubbed the battery belt of the South, the e-mobility capital of the U.S., and a clean energy manufacturing leader by the governor and business executives across the state.
Chris Clark is president and CEO of the Georgia Chamber of Commerce.
Chris Clark stated: "We're the buckle of the battery belt."

North Carolina has some projects, South Carolina has a few, Tennessee has a few, but no one has near the capacity that Georgia has built out when considering Freyr, SK Battery, Hyundai, KIA, Rivian, and Archer Aviation.
Since 2021, more than $32 billion dollars have been invested in clean energy projects in Georgia, the most of any state in the country – by $14 billion, according to the U.S. Department of Energy.
Columbus and Muscogee County have been left out of the clean energy boom so far due to reasons out of the city's control, such as location and available land to develop.
Business leaders in Columbus are working to create more available land.
Higher education systems in Columbus are creating a force of workers to entice manufacturers.
Greg King is Associate vice president of economic development for Georgia Tech.
Greg King stated: "This is an industry that has added billions of dollars and thousands of jobs to Georgia."
SK Battery, Rivian, and Archer Aviation are in northern Georgia or Atlanta metro.
Q-cells is the U.S.' largest solar panel manufacturer with over 4,000 employees and is located in Dalton, expanding to Cartersville, Georgia, both outside of Atlanta.
Augusta, Savannah, West Point and parts of rural Georgia have all attracted (and in some cases, sustained) clean energy manufacturing.
Climate Power reported Georgia has amassed 30,661 clean energy jobs since 2022 from 41 projects.
The biggest sector in the U.S., by far, is battery manufacturing.
Those batteries are used mainly for utilities to pair with solar to store energy, or for electric vehicles.
Georgia's clean energy ecosystem is a diverse mix of solar panel parts, battery manufacturing, and EV manufacturing and components.
Columbus' big ticket employers are in cybersecurity with TSYS Global Payments and Synovus, and aviation from Pratt and Whitney.
These three combined businesses pull in 8,000 employees.
Pratt & Whitney currently has 1,850 employees.
Pratt & Whitney's $200 million expansion will add 400 employees, according to Mitchell.
Kia in West Point draws about 30% of its 2,700 employees from Muscogee County, according to Mitchell.
Clark said Columbus does have some "unique challenges" when it comes to its location and population.
Land, a skilled workforce and proximity to transportation hubs like airports, shipping ports and railways are what experts say attract these large manufacturing investments.
Jerald Mitchell is president of the Columbus Chamber of Commerce.
Jerald Mitchell stated: "The average economic development project requires 70 to 100 acres of land."
Jerald Mitchell stated: "Large plants are more in the 1,000-acre range."
KIA Georgia in West Point is 2,200 acres.
A large swath of Muscogee County is taken up by the military installation (Fort Moore).
Most of Muscogee County does not have land suitable for construction.
North Muscogee is "covered in rock" and most of Southern Muscogee is "wetlands", according to Mitchell.
Clark said the Norwegian battery company, Freyr, selected Newnan, likely because of location and the workforce that they could get from Georgia Tech.
Clark stated: "(Manufacturing in Columbus) could draw workforce from Harris County and Troup County but when you go further south you don't have the pull nor if you go east."
The $2 billion, 300-acre plant currently under construction in Bridgeport Industrial Park in Newnan will bring 700 jobs, explained Jason Peace.
Jason Peace is senior vice president of business development at Freyr.
Jason Peace stated: "We needed a certain amount of land, we needed an adequate talent pipeline, and we needed good logistic plans via rail."
Jason Peace stated: "We sourced 130 sites in 35 states and narrowed the criteria down. Then narrowed again to 50. Then narrowed to six sites across six states."
The final two site options were between a site in Arizona and Newnan, according to Peace.
Columbus did not come up on the consulting site search team, JLL's radar, he added.
Outside of Savannah, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America poured billions into expanding its Metaplant operations in Bryan County to add EV capacity and 400 jobs to its existing workforce of 8,000.
Michael Stewart is director of public relations for Hyundai.

Michael Stewart stated: "Hyundai's Metaplant is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S. with two Class 1 rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway."
The plant is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access to I-95 and I-16 highways.
Bainbridge, a town of 15,000 people tucked in the Southwest corner of the state, is two hours south of Columbus.
Bainbridge was selected to host Anovion, a synthetic graphite lithium-ion battery plant.
The company invested $800 million and reports that it will add 400 jobs once the 300-acre plant is complete in 2026.
Rick McCaskill is executive director of the Bainbridge and Decatur County Economic Development Authority.
Rick McCaskill stated: "We have excellent rail and available land."
Rick McCaskill stated: "We have CSX and Georgia SW railways that connect to Norfolk Southern."
Columbus Chamber of Commerce President, Jerald Mitchell, believes Columbus is "well positioned" to attract clean energy and has been approached by clean energy groups recently.
Mayor Skip Henderson did not respond to multiple requests asking whether clean energy businesses have inquired about setting up shop in Columbus.
Mayor Skip Henderson stated: "We're doing the hard work as it relates to addressing land and workforce."
Mayor Skip Henderson stated: "We have two sites that are currently ready for industrial construction at the Muscogee Technology Park that are 17 and 36 acres."
The Columbus Chamber of Commerce president said they are in the processing of clearing and grading a 64-acre site and another 62-acre site at the Tech Park.
Martha Todd is president of Columbus Technical College.
Martha Todd explained that the workforce that the EV megaplants, solar cell, or battery manufacturing plants require are "people to manage the robots."
Peace, Freyr's business development leader, said they look for troubleshooters who can solve problems and good collaborators.
Jason Peace stated: "A two-year tech degree with heavy automation background in robots, controllers, sensors, and being able to troubleshoot is our workforce profile."
Computers and robotics are second to the health science associates degree in popularity at Columbus Tech, according to Todd.
To answer the increased need for maintenance technology within the automotive technology program, Columbus Tech created an electric vehicle technology program last year.
Last year there were about a dozen students in the classroom for the EV program, but Todd is confident that this program will continue to grow.
About 75% of the students work part-time while attending Columbus Tech, and the average age is 27.
Martha Todd stated: "Students who work in automotive shops are recognizing this need."
Martha Todd stated: "They say, 'My shop is getting more and more calls for electric repair'."
The needs of manufacturing are different now than 30 or 40 years ago.
It's learning how to maintain systems and keep them operating.
Both Columbus Technical College and Columbus State University (CSU) are the linchpins that are prepping the clean energy workforce.
The robotics program at CSU is just a few years old with about 60 students in the program, according to President Stuart Rayfield.
Stuart Rayfield stated: "We have seen exponential growth in this program."
Stuart Rayfield also said CSU is positioning itself well for the clean energy industry with advanced manufacturing programs.
Stuart Rayfield stated: "Clean energy industries are going to require a bachelor or masters to support the advanced part of advanced manufacturing."
Stuart Rayfield stated: "We're looking at how we can develop curricula to support that degree program that spans across computer science, business, to support the advanced manufacturing industry."
Mitchell emphasized that the workforce is the most critical piece he hears about in every deal.
Mitchell thinks EV ecosystem products could have a place in Columbus.
Mitchell stated: "I believe we will attract these jobs because this industry is hot in the South."
Mitchell noted: "Tire's for EVs will be different from combustion engine vehicles, for example."
Mitchell stated: "The entire EV ecosystem needs to be supported."
source_title: Will Columbus join the rest of Georgia & South in hosting clean energy manufacturing? - AOL

facts: Archer Aviation unveiled a deal earlier last month that marks an important first for the company and may open the door to a new revenue stream.
The eVTOL manufacturer said it will supply partner Anduril and EDGE Group with its electric powertrain for the Omen VTOL drone program.
It is the first time Archer has made its proprietary dual-use battery-electric powertrain technology available to an outside partner.
Canaccord analyst Austin Moeller sees the move as a “material shift in the company’s operating model that introduces a new revenue stream for the business.”

According to Moeller, the announcement highlights the deepening relationship between Archer and the fast-growing defense-tech firm, underscoring the value of the core powertrain technology originally developed for the Midnight aircraft.
Archer’s system will also be adapted into a hybrid configuration to support the military VTOL drone program the company is advancing with Anduril.
While developing Midnight, Archer chose to bring battery-pack production in-house, despite relying on outside aerospace suppliers for most other components.
Moeller believes that “This decision enabled development of powertrain technology that is highly optimized for an array of aviation applications across both commercial aerospace and defense.”
CEO Adam Goldstein says the agreement with Anduril is likely the first of several examples where Archer could provide its powertrain to outside companies.

While the announcement is separate from Archer’s hybrid-electric drone project with Anduril, the new partnership grew out of the time Archer’s engineers have spent working with Anduril’s team over the past year.
That collaboration helped both sides get comfortable with each other’s technology and spot additional chances to work together beyond the hybrid-electric aircraft effort.

Archer is now participating in both the Omen project as the eVTOL powertrain supplier and the hybrid-electric VTOL project with Anduril, while also partnering with Palantir to develop AI-driven air traffic management systems for next-gen aviation.

Over the past three months, the company has acquired Hawthorne Airport – its new eVTOL travel hub near LA – along with Lilium’s IP portfolio, Mission Critical Composites, and Overair’s intellectual property and talent.
At the same time, Archer is ramping up Midnight aircraft production in California and Georgia, aiming for an annual output of 50 Midnights within the next 12 months.
“That all being said,” Moeller added, “investors are closely focused on the certification and flight-testing progress for the Midnight passenger eVTOL.

Management has not provided a recent update on when the piloted full transition flight of the Midnight will occur, but this is a critical milestone to proceed into TIA/for-credit testing with FAA pilots.”
Meanwhile, Moeller keeps his Buy rating on ACHR intact, while his $13 price target implies ~67% upside from current levels.
3 other analysts are also positive on ACHR’s prospects, while 1 Hold can’t detract from a Strong Buy consensus rating.
The average target lands at $12.40, a figure that points toward 12-month gains of 59%.
source_title: ‘New Opportunities Opening Up,’ Says Analyst About Archer Aviation Stock - TipRanks.com

facts: Archer Aviation received the certificate of occupancy for its approximately 400,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Covington, Georgia.
The company plans to begin initial production at the facility in early 2025.
Archer broke ground on the Covington facility in March 2023.
In December, Archer announced it is finalizing an agreement with global automaker Stellantis to serve as its exclusive contract manufacturer.
Archer aims to manufacture two aircraft per month by the end of next year (2026).
By 2030, Archer plans to produce 650 electric takeoff and landing aircraft annually.
During a November 2024 investor call, Founder and CEO Adam Goldstein named New York City, Los Angeles, the San Francisco Bay Area, and Miami as the company’s first U.S. markets.
Archer has received interest from the United Arab Emirates and Japan for its air taxi services.
The company plans to deploy a network of air taxis in Southern California in time for the 2026 FIFA World Cup in Los Angeles.
Archer worked closely with Stellantis to construct the Covington facility.
The facility will produce Archer’s Midnight aircraft, which is optimized to fly routes of 20 to 50 miles at up to 150 mph with a quick recharge between flights.
The Midnight air taxi can carry up to four passengers and their carry-on luggage, plus a pilot.

Stellantis Chief Manufacturing and Supply Chain Officer Arnaud Deboeuf stated that supporting Archer’s electric aircraft growth with deep manufacturing expertise is a great opportunity for both companies.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp issued a statement saying Archer’s announcement reaffirms the state’s commitment to working with innovative companies to create quality jobs and opportunities for years to come.

President-elect Donald Trump expressed support for advanced air mobility technology such as air taxis in a 2023 campaign speech, indicating potential regulatory friendliness from the incoming administration.
source_title: Archer Aviation completes Georgia air taxi factory | Manufacturing Dive

facts: Duckyang is an electric automotive battery and parts manufacturing company based in Korea.
Duckyang is locating its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Braselton, Georgia.
The facility will be located in Jackson County, Georgia.
Duckyang will invest $10 million toward the project to cover costs.
The company plans to create 285 new jobs.
The project is described as a great addition to Jackson County’s economy.
Duckyang is entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market through a partnership with SKBA.
Through this partnership, Duckyang will have the opportunity to cooperate with other automotive companies that already have a U.S. manufacturing base, including Hyundai and Kia Motors.
A Georgia official stated that the state is focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain in Georgia.
The state leverages strong relationships with SK Battery America and other Korean companies, such as Duckyang, to help grow the supply chain.
The state helps bring suppliers to Georgia to secure a local supply of needed parts for automotive manufacturers.
Partners in Jackson County are providing support for these projects.
The project is expected to create new opportunities locally and for the entire U.S. mobility sector.
source_title: Where is Duckyang Locating its First US Manufacturing Facility? | NILRR

facts: Korean auto parts maker Duckyang is investing $10 million to open its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
The 230,000-square-foot facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton.
IC Biomedical, which makes cryogenic freezers to transport medical products like vaccines, is opening a 75,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Bartow County that will bring more than 80 jobs.
FanDuel, a sports betting and digital entertainment company, is investing more than $15 million to open a technology campus in Midtown Atlanta.
The company will create more than 900 jobs over the next five years at the 68,000-square-foot location.
Domtar Corp. is expanding its engineered absorbent materials manufacturing facility in Wayne County.
The $60 million investment will create 75 jobs.
WebstaurantStore, an online supply store for restaurant service professionals, is opening a 475,000-square-foot distribution center in Chatham County, creating more than 100 jobs.
Netherlands-based Heliox, which makes electric vehicle charging systems, is establishing its North American headquarters in Atlanta.
The company will create more than 70 clean-energy jobs in the region over the next year.
Interfor, a forest products company and lumber producer, is investing $30 million to expand sawmill operations in Perry.
The company employs more than 1,200 Georgians and operates seven sawmills across the state.
Bimbo QSR, a subsidiary of Mexican company Grupo Bimbo, is investing more than $25 million to open a food processing facility in Lowndes County.
The facility, which will produce buns, will create 74 new jobs.
GreyOrange, a global leader in AI-enabled software and robotics for fulfillment automation, is investing more than $1 million to relocate its headquarters to Roswell, creating 200 jobs.

facts: KOTRA opened its first Atlanta office on March 31, 2022.
Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Commissioner Pat Wilson joined Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn, Senior Executive Vice President of KOTRA, to announce the new office.
KOTRA is the Government of Korea’s trade and investment organization.

GDEcD and KOTRA signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to support each other’s services and networks and promote cooperation in economic relations, trade, and investment between Georgia and Korea.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Korea is an established strategic market for investment, trade, and tourism for the State of Georgia.
Existing connectivity includes daily direct flights between Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and Incheon International Airport.
Long-standing agreements exist between the Georgia ports of Savannah and Brunswick and five Korean ports.
These connections facilitate joint efforts to create an innovative electric mobility ecosystem in Georgia.
In early March 2022, Commissioner Wilson met with KOTRA President and CEO Mr. Jeoung Yeol Yu during a mission to Korea.
The mission included meetings with key business representatives from Kia and SK Group.
The mission re-confirmed the State of Georgia’s commitment to its Korean companies and international partners.
KOTRA Atlanta will serve as an outpost to support Korean businesses in the Southeastern United States.
Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn stated that KOTRA will expand cooperation in many industrial fields between Korea and the U.S. and support Korean companies’ local investments.
KOTRA acts as the first point of contact for Korean companies settling in new markets like Georgia, providing investment and support resources including information on state or local agency programs.
The new KOTRA office is located at 950 East Paces Ferry Road NE, Suite 999 in Atlanta.
The office will host teams dedicated to facilitating trade and investment opportunities between Georgia and Korea.
Atlanta will be the eighth overseas KOTRA office in the United States and the 10th in North America.
The Republic of Korea has maintained a consular presence in Georgia since 1971.
Mr. Yoonjoo Park has served as Consul General since June 2021.
The State of Georgia has maintained continuous representation in Korea since 1985 with an office in Seoul headed by Managing Director Peter Underwood.
The state also employs a dedicated Georgia-based director of Korean Investment, Yoonie Kim.
Several Georgia companies including American Megatrends, First Advantage Corporation, InComm, Novelis, Unicoi Systems, and UPS have operations in Korea.
Georgia is home to at least 100 Korean facilities.
These Korean-affiliated companies currently employ more than 9,400 Georgians.
Notable Korean-affiliated companies in Georgia include Kia, SK Battery America, Sangsin Technology America, and Q CELLS Korea.
Since 2020, numerous Korean companies have announced projects in Georgia.
SK Group subsidiary company SKC will manufacture glass-based substrates for semiconductor chips in Covington along with several business partners.
SUNGDO ENG USA announced its corporate U.S. headquarters in the state and provides highly technical construction services to many Georgia-based clients.
Duckyang, an electric mobility parts and storage systems supplier for SK Group, is opening a new facility in Jackson County.
Brake pad supplier KB Autosys will open its first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility in west Georgia to serve regional customers such as GM, Hyundai, and Kia.
The Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) in Savannah offers seven regular shipping services to the Port of Busan.
GPA’s Brunswick Port offers shipping services to Incheon, Masan, Pyongtaek, and Kunsan.
In 2021, Georgia exports to Korea totaled $860.59 million.
Korea is the 10th largest export market for Georgia.
Top exports from Georgia to Korea include gas turbines, aircraft, medical instruments, chemical woodpulp, and chemical analysis instruments.
In 2021, Georgia imports from Korea totaled $8.83 billion.
Korea ranks third among Georgia’s importer nations.
Top imports from Korea to Georgia include motor vehicles, tractor parts, bulldozers, engines, and fork-lift trucks.

KOTRA was established in 1962 to contribute to the national economy by promoting trade, facilitating investment between domestic and foreign companies, and supporting industrial technology cooperation.
KOTRA is headquartered in Seoul but operates over 127 Overseas Trade Centers in 84 countries.
source_title: Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency Opens First Atlanta Office | Georgia

facts: The Georgia Export Grant has supported 99 unique Georgia companies, making it possible for them to make international connections.
Georgia has broken export records for five years in a row and is a top 10 state for exports.
The trade team connects Georgia businesses with more than 60 international markets to help local entrepreneurs grow.
The Georgia Export Grant, launched in partnership with the U.S. Small Business Administration, provides reimbursement funding to eligible Georgia exporters for pre-approved export activities.
This competitive grant program is intended to increase the number of small businesses that export, assist small firms with export development and expand international sales.
Services provided include market intelligence, trade show assistance, grant funding opportunities, competitive analysis, distributor and buyer identification, and resource connection.
Upcoming trade shows or missions are open to help participants learn firsthand about a market of interest.
A monthly newsletter provides the latest international sales opportunities for Georgia exporters.
source_title: Georgia Export Services | Georgia

facts: Local governments may use income from the infrastructure surtax to provide loans, grants, or rebates to residential or commercial property owners to install electric vehicle chargers, propane fueling stations, and natural gas fueling stations.
A local government ordinance authorizing this use must be approved by referendum.
The Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services must adopt rules to establish requirements for EV chargers.
Regulation of EV chargers may only be done by the state government.
Local governments may not enact or enforce ordinances or regulations relating to EV chargers.
A person may not stop, stand, or park a vehicle that is not capable of using EV chargers in a parking space designated for EVs.
The Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services must adopt additional rules to provide definitions, methods of sale, labeling requirements, and price-posting requirements for EV chargers.
The Public Utility Commission is authorized to approve voluntary residential, fleet, and public EV charging programs.
Condominium associations may not prohibit or restrict the installation or use of an EV charger or NGV fueling station in a homeowner’s designated parking space.

Condominium associations may put reasonable restrictions on EV chargers or NGV fueling stations, but the policies may not significantly increase the cost of the EV charger or NGV fueling station or prohibit installation.

Homeowners may be required to comply with applicable safety codes and architectural standards, engage a licensed installation contractor, provide a certificate of insurance, and reimburse the cost of any increased insurance premium associated with the EV charger or NGV fueling station.

The homeowner of the parking space equipped with EV chargers or NGV fuel is responsible for the cost of the installation, operation, maintenance, repair, removal, or replacement of the station, as well as any resulting damage to the EV charger or surrounding area.

Insurance companies may not impose surcharges on EVs based on factors such as new technology, passenger payload, weight-to-horsepower ratio, and the types of material used to manufacture the vehicle, unless the Florida Office of Insurance Regulation receives actuarial data that determines the surcharges are justified.

Electric vehicle charging made available to the public by a non-utility is not considered a retail sale of electricity and, therefore, the rates, terms, and conditions of EV charging services are not subject to regulation.
The Florida Department of Management Services must evaluate the use of fuel-efficient and alternative fuel vehicles in the state fleet.
When procuring vehicles, state agencies must consider total life cycle costs, fuel efficiency, and the availability of alternative fuel options, consistent with state purchasing requirements.
The Florida Department of Transportation created an EV Master Plan for the development of electric vehicle chargers along the State Highway System.
FDOT will also establish staging area that will include EV chargers at key locations along the State Highway system to be used as emergency evacuation stops.
Local governments may offer funding to property owners within their jurisdiction to help finance EV charger installations on their property or enter into a financing agreement for the same purpose.

The U.S. Department of Transportation’s NEVI Formula Program required the Florida Department of Transportation to submit an annually updated EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan for fiscal years 2022 through 2026 to their FHWA Division Office.
The submitted plans must describe how the state intends to distribute NEVI funds and their physical and cybersecurity strategies, and include a Community Engagement Outcomes Report.
The submitted plans must address updated NEVI Guidance.

links.context: FDOT Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Funding website

source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Electricity Laws and Incentives in Florida

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced steady growth for business recruitment and expansions in Fiscal Year 2025.
Between July 1, 2024, and June 30, 2025, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 423 facility expansions and new locations.
These commitments represent more than $26.3 billion in investment, a new record for the state.
The projects will create 23,200 new private-sector jobs over the next few years.
Expansions at existing Georgia companies accounted for 74% of the FY25 projects.
These expansions build on an estimated 50,000 jobs already employing Georgians.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones stated that the state continues to invest in workforce programs to ensure a steady talent pipeline with existing industry partners.
Workforce training partners include the University System of Georgia, the Technical College System of Georgia, and College Career Academies.
77% of all expansions and new locations in FY25 occurred in communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region.
Metro Atlanta remains one of the Southeast’s top business hubs.
Companies like TriNet, A.I.G., CRH, Duracell, and Mercedes-Benz established or expanded key corporate functions in the 10-county Atlanta region.
Georgia attracted more than 6,500 new jobs from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S.
The Republic of Korea, Japan, and Canada were the top sources of international investments into Georgia.
International companies expanding or locating in Georgia committed to more than $3 billion in investment.
64% of international investments are slated for expansions within the state.
Notable international investments include Irving Tissue’s expansion in Macon, GreenBox Systems’ new location in Butts County, and KISS USA’s expansion in Bryan County.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Georgia has achieved sustained, steady growth in job creation over the last decade.
Georgia remains the No. 1 state for business according to Area Development magazine, a title claimed for over a decade.
Georgia is investing over $18 million to help rural communities prepare new industrial sites through the Rural Site Development Initiative.
These grants support site improvements and add new locations to the “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” (GRAD) Program.
The GRAD Program now includes more than 70 certified sites.
Speaker Jon Burns highlighted Georgia’s ports at Savannah and Brunswick, extensive rail network, vast energy infrastructure, and world-class roads, bridges, and highways as key business enablers.

From sustainable fuel to battery technology, Georgia's growing energy sector supports innovation with strong infrastructure, expanding markets, and a skilled workforce.

facts: SKC, a subsidiary of SK Group, will manufacture glass-based substrates for semiconductor chips in Covington, Georgia.
The company will invest more than $473 million in this unprecedented venture.
The project will create more than 400 new jobs in Newton County.
Production is expected to ramp up by late summer 2023.
The new facility will be developed on existing SKC property located at 3000 SKC Drive in Covington.
SKC will primarily hire high-tech engineers, skilled technicians, and other semiconductor field-experienced talent.
Dr. Sung Jin Kim, SKC Director of New Business Development, served as a research professor at the Georgia Institute of Technology from 2012 to 2015.

Professor Kim helped develop the glass substrate technology through research conducted at the Georgia Tech Packaging Research Center with the Center Team and global semiconductor supply chain companies.
SKC was founded in 1976 as a specialty material company and established its Newton County, Georgia facility in 1996.
The existing Covington facility is one of the major Polyester films (PET film) producers in North America.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, the Covington facility pivoted manufacturing to produce face shields and other PPE safety products.
SKC has expanded its business to copper foil and next-generation cathode/anode materials for rechargeable batteries.
The company developed the world’s first glass substrate technology for high-performance semiconductors, following high-end blank masks and CMP pads.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the project and signed a ceremonial Memorandum of Understanding with SKC to solidify the location.
Lanier Sims, Chairman of the Newton County Industrial Development Authority, highlighted SK’s long-term partnership with the county.
Ángel Cabrera, President of Georgia Tech, noted that 25-30 years of Georgia Tech’s investment in packaging research and advanced circuitry contributed to Georgia being selected as the best location.
Yoonie Kim, Director of Korean Investment at the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), represented the Global Commerce division on the project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated the investment will directly support desperately needed domestic manufacturing of semiconductors.
SK Group is one of the largest conglomerates in South Korea, and this facility marks its third major investment in Georgia.
The announcement notes that SK Innovation is investing nearly $2.6 billion to develop two battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County to supply electric vehicles.
Governor Kemp described the announcement as a prime example of Georgia addressing a pressing U.S. supply chain roadblock affecting manufacturers.

Dr. Kim stated the new technology will enable utmost performance with minimal power consumption for high-performance computing and high-speed communication applications, serving as a basecamp for SKC’s AI and data center semiconductor applications.
source_title: SK Group to Locate Glass-based Semiconductor-part Venture in Covington | Georgia

canonical_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle and Battery Supply Chain
facts: President Biden’s Investing in America agenda is delivering industries, jobs, and opportunities to communities across the South.

The cornerstone of that agenda is a suite of generous federal tax credits making the U.S. an irresistible destination for companies to manufacture electric vehicles, batteries, semiconductors, solar panels, and other clean energy technologies.
These policies are turning industrial hubs across the Southeast into supermagnets for new investment.
Georgia is Exhibit A of this manufacturing resurgence.
Since President Biden took office, companies have planned $32 billion in electric vehicle, battery and clean energy supply chain investments in the Peach State.
In Dalton, QCells is building America’s first-ever fully integrated solar supply chain.
The QCells project in Dalton is creating 2,500 jobs.
In Bryan County, Hyundai broke ground on its very first U.S. electric vehicle and battery plant.
The Hyundai plant in Bryan County is creating more than 8,000 jobs.
Jennifer M. Granholm, former Secretary of the U.S. Department of Energy, observed that Americans of all stripes want to land future industries, jobs, and opportunities in their communities.
The federal tax credits are designed to ensure American products are made on American soil with American workers.
source_title: Biden’s Energy Policies Help Keep Georgia Manufacturing Humming | Department of Energy
