entity_name: DOE AMMTO $33 Million Smart Manufacturing Funding Opportunity
canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office (AMMTO) Funding Opportunity DE-FOA-0003329
facts: $33 million funding opportunity aims to accelerate the advancement of smart manufacturing technologies and processes necessary to develop and deploy innovative technologies and materials needed for the nation’s clean energy transition.
FOA Number is DE-FOA-0003329.
Deadline for full applications is Nov. 18, 2024, at 5 p.m. ET.
Topics include smart manufacturing for a circular economy, sustainable transportation, high-performance materials, and domestic mining.

Smart manufacturing is defined as the use of advanced technologies and processes, including digitalization and artificial intelligence, to improve technical performance, productivity, quality assurance, and security of the manufacturing sector.

AMMTO Director Christopher Saldaña stated smart manufacturing solutions are critical to domestically manufacture next generation clean energy technologies more economically, efficiently, and at higher quality and scale needed for global competitiveness.
Funding recipients will support research to develop smart manufacturing technologies that improve efficiency and economics of circular supply chains.
Recipients will advance sustainable transportation manufacturing.
Recipients will accelerate development of high-performance materials for the clean energy transition.
Recipients will create more sustainable, safe, and competitive mining in the United States.

Topic 1 focuses on smart manufacturing solutions increasing viability of circular supply chains (Re-X pathways) such as recycling, repairing, remanufacturing, and reuse by improving efficiency and economics.

Area of Interest 1 under Topic 1 covers smart manufacturing technologies for improved sorting and characterization to identify, sort, and characterize materials to facilitate recovery and recycling into new products.

Area of Interest 2 under Topic 1 covers interoperable and open supply chains for expanded Re-X to help repair or remanufacture products not initially designed for reuse, repair, refurbish, remanufacture, or repurpose.

Area of Interest 3 under Topic 1 covers improved data transparency for accurate lifecycle assessments to enhance availability and transparency of data critical to bolstering market acceptance of secondary materials.
Topic 2 focuses on smart manufacturing technologies making tooling and equipment for sustainable vehicles faster and more economical while reducing component defects and enabling new functionalities.
Sustainable transportation in this FOA refers to electric vehicles based on either batteries or fuel cells across all vehicle types from automobiles to fleets to all truck classes.
Area of Interest 1 under Topic 2 covers automation by integrating hardware and software systems into manufacturing tooling and equipment to improve component productivity.

Area of Interest 2 under Topic 2 covers manufacturing asset management for improving system performance to improve cost, quality, and throughput of tooling and equipment, industrial controls, and automation network.
Topic 3 focuses on developing, validating, and prototyping new high-performance materials and their manufacturing processes.

Particular interest is in manufacturing processes and fabrication techniques for high-performance materials for the clean energy transition, such as high-conductivity materials and materials for harsh service conditions.

Area of Interest 1 under Topic 3 covers high-performance materials and process discovery by developing high throughput strategies for conductivity-enhanced and harsh environment materials synthesis/testing used with computational tools.

Area of Interest 2 under Topic 3 covers high-performance materials manufacturing, assembly and scaleup by creating competitive manufacturing and assembly approaches utilizing smart manufacturing tools in materials manufacturing.
Topic 4 focuses on developing novel smart manufacturing technologies for sustainable, safe, and competitive mining in the United States.
Fostering advancements in mining will enable a more resilient, diverse, and secure domestic supply of critical minerals and materials.

Area of Interest 1 under Topic 4 covers sustainable and competitive domestic mining by establishing smart manufacturing technologies such as sensors, controls, and automation for health, safety, environmental, and economic benefits to mining operations.

Area of Interest 2 under Topic 4 covers sensing, analytics, and data-driven decision making in mining by advancing new and transformative mining technologies offering significant benefits over current commercially available analytical instruments and sensors that are more accessible and affordable.
Applicants must submit a concept paper by 5 p.m. ET on Aug. 22, 2024, to be eligible to submit a full application.
Funding Opportunity Announcement issue date was July 18, 2024.
Expected submission deadline for replies to reviewer comments is Jan. 14, 2025, at 5 p.m. ET.
Expected date for selection notifications is April 2025.
Expected timeframe for award negotiations is April–June 2025.
Full funding opportunity can be downloaded on EERE Exchange.
Specific support is available through a dedicated contact channel for this funding opportunity.
Applicants can sign up for the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy email list to get notified of new funding opportunities.
Stakeholders can subscribe to the monthly Manufacturing Moment newsletter to receive latest information on AMMTO funding opportunities, events, and news.

links.url: Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy email list

source_title: $33 Million in Funding Available To Advance Smart Manufacturing Technologies To Help Accelerate a Clean Energy Economy | Department of Energy

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Pratt & Whitney, an RTX business, is investing $200 million to expand its operations in Columbus, Georgia.
The project will initially create 15 new jobs, with more jobs to follow.

Along with Pratt & Whitney President Shane Eddy, Columbus Mayor Skip Henderson, and other public and company leaders, Governor Kemp celebrated the announcement of this new investment and the completion of a prior expansion announced in 2023.
This previously announced project added nearly 400 new jobs and 81,000 square feet to Pratt & Whitney’s Columbus Engine Center.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Pratt & Whitney's decision to invest in Georgia immediately after completing another major expansion is an incredible testament to how companies operating in the state find reliable, ongoing, and long-term success.
Governor Kemp noted that throughout its more than forty years in Columbus, Pratt & Whitney has been a valued partner and driver of the impact the aerospace industry is delivering statewide.
Governor Kemp expressed gratitude that the partnership will continue to grow and flourish, benefiting the job creator, the people of Columbus, and all of Georgia.
The Pratt & Whitney Columbus facility opened in 1984 and includes the Columbus Engine Center and Columbus Forge, both operating on the same campus.
The Columbus Engine Center maintains Pratt & Whitney GTF™ engines for the Airbus A320neo aircraft family, Airbus A220, and Embraer E-Jets E2.
The facility also supports the F117 engine for the Boeing C-17 Globemaster III and the F100 engine for the McDonnell Douglas F-15 Eagle and Lockheed Martin F-16 Fighting Falcon.
The Columbus Forge produces compressor airfoils, and compressor and turbine disks for Pratt & Whitney’s commercial and military engines.
These components include those for the F135 engine, which powers all variants of the Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II fighter.

Pratt & Whitney President Shane Eddy stated that over the past four decades with community, state, and Governor support, Pratt & Whitney’s presence in Georgia has grown from a small manufacturing facility to a state-of-the-art manufacturing and overhaul center.
The facility employs 2,600 dedicated people focused on connecting and protecting the world.
Since 2008, Pratt & Whitney has invested more than $1 billion to continue expanding the footprint and capabilities of the Columbus facility.

This latest investment will increase output of critical parts for growing military and commercial engine programs and underscores an ongoing commitment to ramp industrial capacity to support customers.
The Columbus Engine Center is located at 8987 Macon Road.
The Columbus Forge is located at 8801 Macon Road.
The expansion is expected to be completed by 2028.
Columbus Mayor Skip Henderson thanked Pratt & Whitney for its continued faith in the Columbus community and the Valley region.
Mayor Henderson appreciated Governor Kemp, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Choose Columbus’ help in getting the project across the finish line.
Mayor Henderson described Pratt & Whitney as an outstanding community partner and expressed a desire to continue growing that relationship.
Chairman Selvin Hollingsworth of the Development Authority of Columbus, Georgia thanked Pratt & Whitney for its additional capital investment and creation of high-quality jobs.
He stated the project underscores the strength of the region’s advanced manufacturing sector and the power of partnership in driving economic growth.
Regional Project Manager Christy Bozeman represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was completed in partnership with the Development Authority of Columbus, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Pratt & Whitney’s continued growth in Columbus proves that investing in workforce initiatives is the most important factor for the long-term success of companies in Georgia.
Pratt & Whitney was an early beneficiary of Georgia Quick Start, a partner they have engaged with for nearly every expansion or major investment over their decades-long relationship.

By working with education partners and local communities such as Columbus Technical College and Columbus-Muscogee County, Georgia offers solutions that support decades of accomplishments and milestones.
Pratt & Whitney is an RTX business and a world leader in the design, manufacture and service of aircraft engines and auxiliary power units for military, commercial and civil aviation customers.
Since 1925, its engineers have pioneered the development of revolutionary aircraft propulsion technologies.
Today it supports more than 90,000 in-service engines through a global network of maintenance, repair and overhaul facilities.
source_title: RTX’s Pratt & Whitney Announces New $200M Columbus Expansion, Cuts Ribbon on Prior Expansion | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy's Office of Technology Transitions and the Technology Commercialization Fund (TCF) unveiled a new program—the TCF Open Voucher Call—that connects the public with researchers at DOE national laboratories.
This single-phase call will award up to 21 teams with a technical assistance voucher of up to $100,000.

Vouchers are redeemable at eight national labs: National Renewable Energy Laboratory, Sandia National Laboratories, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, Los Alamos National Laboratory, Ames National Laboratory, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Argonne National Laboratory, and Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
Vouchers must be used within a one-year period.
Awardees will be selected based on innovative ideas and concepts for advancing energy or other lab-supported initiatives.
Participating national labs will provide awardees with expertise and lab validation capabilities to help them advance their concept closer to commercialization.
Eligible hydrogen and fuel cell-related topics include hydrogen production, electrolysis and fuel cell components, safety, storage, systems analysis, and techno-economic analysis.
An informational webinar for the program was scheduled for July 18, 2024, at 2 p.m. ET.
Applications are due October 3, 2024.

The TCF Open Voucher Call is open to private entities (for-profits and nonprofits), non-federal government entities such as states, counties, tribes, and municipalities, academic and research institutions, and individuals.
The goal of this call is to foster a culture of curiosity and collaboration while bridging the gap between scientific expertise and public engagement.
source_title: $2.1 Million Available in National Laboratory Assistance Through Open Voucher Call | Department of Energy

facts: Honda expects to incur significant additional costs in the fiscal year ending March 31, 2026 arising from tariffs on completed vehicles and parts, which will negatively impact business results compared to the fiscal year ended March 31, 2025.

Changes in governmental policy concerning environmental or import and export regulations could possibly result in stoppages or delays in Honda’s production activities or lead to countermeasure costs associated with development, purchasing, sales, and other business activities.

Honda is concerned that battery prices will soar due to a shortage of mineral resources and other supplies as demand for nickel, lithium and cobalt grows rapidly due to the accelerating shift to electrification.
Honda is closely examining the impact on its group with regard to export restrictions on rare earths in China.

The automotive industry is undergoing a period of major change, including intensifying competition due to the rise of emerging Chinese EV companies, changes in environmental policies in North America and Europe, and global trade wars arising from the imposition of additional tariffs by various countries.
Honda revised its sales targets for EVs and FCEVs for 2030 in light of slower-than-expected EV adoption, as announced in May 2025.
source_title: ANNUAL REPORT

entity_name: Science and Energy Determination of Exceptional Circumstances (S&E DEC)
canonical_name: Science and Energy Determination of Exceptional Circumstances (S&E DEC)
facts: On June 8, 2021, the Science and Energy Determination of Exceptional Circumstances (S&E DEC) was announced as part of a series of new policy actions to support U.S. job creation and bolster the domestic manufacturing supply chain.
The S&E DEC was announced in connection with the 100-day review of critical supply chains as directed under E.O. 14017, America’s Supply Chains.

The S&E DEC expands on DOE’s experience in strengthening U.S. manufacturing requirements in DOE-funded grants, cooperative agreements, and research and development contracts by extending the enhancement of U.S. manufacturing requirements to most of the DOE Programs.

The S&E DEC ensures that all innovations, including those relating to advanced batteries, developed with taxpayer dollars through DOE Science and Energy Programs are substantially manufactured in the U.S.

The S&E DEC authorizes inclusion of the U.S. Competitiveness provision, which requires substantial U.S. manufacture of products embodying DOE-funded subject inventions, in agreements funded by DOE Science and Energy Programs.
DOE expects to release additional guidance on the implementation of the Science and Energy DEC in early FY2022.

FAQs for Applicants and Awardees of DOE Financial Assistance and R&D Contracts regarding the Department’s Determination of Exceptional Circumstances (DEC) for DOE Science and Energy Technologies were issued in June of 2021.

DOE includes domestic manufacture requirements for subject inventions in its funding agreements, such as financial assistance awards, Lab contracts, and CRADAs, to help revitalize domestic manufacturing, secure U.S. supply chains, and create good-quality American jobs.

The authority for U.S. manufacturing requirements is statutory, typically taking the form of U.S. Preference (the baseline standard per the Bayh-Dole Act) or U.S. Competitiveness (for CRADAs and entities obtaining patent waivers).

Since 2013, DOE has used the statutory determination of exceptional circumstances process in the Bayh-Dole Act to elevate the U.S. manufacturing requirement to U.S. Competitiveness for Bayh-Dole entities in select programs.

Under the U.S. Preference Provision, recipients agree that neither they nor any assignee will grant exclusive rights to use or sell a subject invention in the United States unless products embodying it are manufactured substantially in the United States, and this provision does not impose a manufacturing commitment on the recipient itself, only on assignees or exclusive licensees.

Under the U.S. Competitiveness Provision, any products embodying a waived invention or produced through its use will be manufactured substantially in the United States regardless of source, and if foreign manufacture is agreed upon, the government’s support must be recognized appropriately.
Recipients may request specific, fact-based, and tailored modifications to or waivers of U.S. manufacturing requirements to accommodate situations like a lack of domestic manufacturing capacity.

DOE evaluates modification requests based on factors including mission support objectives, commercial feasibility of U.S. manufacturing, reasonable efforts to manufacture in the U.S., proposed alternative benefits to the U.S. economy (e.g., investment in U.S.-based plant and equipment, creation of high-quality U.S.-based jobs), scope of the waiver compared to proposed benefits, agreement to provide non-exclusive licenses with commercially reasonable terms, and other relevant factors.
DOE provides a suggested form to assist requestors in identifying and providing necessary information for modification requests.

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Vehicle Technologies Office Fiscal Year 2025 Program Wide Funding Opportunity
canonical_name: DOE VTO Fiscal Year 2025 Program Wide Funding Opportunity
facts: Up to $88 million in funding is available for advanced vehicle research and development.
The funding program is administered by the Vehicle Technologies Office (VTO).
The NOFO number is DE-FOA-0003514.
The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced the Fiscal Year 2025 VTO Program Wide funding program.
This funding opportunity will award up to $88 million for projects seeking innovative transportation solutions for on- and off-road vehicles.
DOE is committed to pushing the frontiers of science and engineering, catalyzing domestic jobs through research, development, demonstration, and deployment.
Topic areas under this funding will advance affordable and reliable energy innovation in mobility and improve U.S. competitiveness.
The NOFO covers 10 distinct topic areas.

Topic Area 1 focuses on technologies for enhanced lithium-ion cell safety, specifically improving abuse tolerance of Li-ion cells with graphite anodes and mass-produced cathodes for plug-in electric vehicles.
Topic Area 2a seeks low-cost production of battery-grade lithium with reduced energy consumption, improved yield, and minimal environmental impact.
Topic Area 2b seeks novel materials to form a beneficial passivation layer on the surface of Li foil for rechargeable Li batteries containing liquid or solid-state electrolytes.

Topic Area 3a aims to accelerate a domestic battery industry supporting lower cost options for long-haul U.S. trucks by seeking Li-ion battery chemistry innovations and design solutions for multilayer pouch, cylindrical, or prismatic cells with extended cycle life.
Topic Area 3b supports innovative standardized modules or scaled packs (cell-to-pack design) suitable for Class 8 long-haul trucks.
Topic Area 4 focuses on thermal technologies for zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), aiming to decrease on-board energy use of vehicle thermal systems for medium- and heavy-duty on-highway ZEVs.

Topic Area 5 aims to research, develop, and validate tools leveraging Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) and smart metering to improve EV charging infrastructure energization, accelerate deployment, and provide an efficient EV load management platform.

Topic Area 6 (CROP) focuses on co-located renewable fuel and off-road vehicle pilots for farms and agricultural communities, demonstrating locally produced renewable fuels from agricultural waste and animal manure.
Topic Area 7 aims to conduct life cycle analysis (LCA) including energy usage, critical materials requirements, and greenhouse gas emissions on up to four vehicles.
Topic Area 8 focuses on quality improvements of battery busbar joining methods for “foil-to-tab” manufacturing in EVs and real-time monitoring of joining performance.
Topic Area 9 targets electric vehicle workforce development through local, state, regional, or national outreach, awareness, and partnership building efforts.
VTO highly encourages project teams for Topic Area 9 to include one or more Clean Cities and Communities coalition.

Topic Area 10 is an open topic for exploring novel solutions to transportation and related energy and infrastructure challenges through demonstration and deployment projects to spur market development.
Project teams for Topic Area 10 must include at least one active and DOE-designated Clean Cities and Communities coalition with a significant project role.
The NOFO Issue Date is January 17, 2025.
The Submission Deadline for Concept Papers is April 1, 2025, at 5:00 p.m. ET.
The Submission Deadline for Full Applications is June 18, 2025, at 5:00 p.m. ET.
The Expected Date for EERE Selection Notifications is November 5, 2025, at 5:00 p.m. ET.
The Expected Timeframe for Award Negotiations is February 2026.
The full funding opportunity document can be downloaded via the EERE Exchange website.
Stakeholders can sign up for the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) email list to get notified of new EERE funding opportunities.
Stakeholders can also sign up for the VTO GO! newsletter to stay current with the latest VTO news.

facts: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding on Sept. 19, 2023, to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility.
The MoU partnership focuses on developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing more than $12 billion in EV manufacturing and battery production in Georgia.
Hyundai's economic impact in Georgia will be tens of billions of dollars in the years to come.
The partnership will help create nearly 40,000 jobs.
The collaboration builds upon Hyundai Motor Group's long history in Georgia.
The Group is investing $12.6 billion in new dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia.
The investment includes the $7.59 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The HMGMA facility will create more than 8,500 direct jobs.
The investment includes a $5 billion battery manufacturing facility joint venture with SK On in Bartow County, Georgia.
The SK On joint venture facility will employ approximately 3,500 people.
Proximity to Georgia Tech was one of the important considerations in deciding the locations for these facilities.
Kia Georgia's West Point assembly plant began vehicle production in 2009 under the leadership of Honorary Chairman Mong-Koo Chung and Executive Chair Euisun Chung.
University System of Georgia Chancellor Sonny Perdue participated in the HMG-Georgia Tech MOU ceremony.
Sonny Perdue was the Governor of Georgia at the time of the Kia Georgia plant's opening.
A recent study by the Center for Automotive Research reveals that Hyundai Motor's automotive operations contributed $20.1 billion and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy in 2021 alone.
The study states Hyundai's investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.
Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera said Hyundai is a global brand synonymous with quality, innovation, and a commitment to advancing technology to make a positive difference in the world.
Cabrera noted that the alignment of values between Georgia Tech and Hyundai has become obvious as they have gotten to know each other.
Cabrera expressed gratitude for the transformative investments Hyundai is making in the state.
Cabrera stated he is proud that the Hyundai brand will feature prominently on the campus.
Cabrera looks forward to working with Hyundai leaders to deepen the partnership, develop exceptional leaders, and produce new ideas that will shape the automotive industry and advance mobility.
Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, said Georgia is like a second home to the company.
Chang described Hyundai as a mission-driven organization with a compelling point of view about the future.
Chang stated the company feels a great responsibility to contribute to a sustainable future where all humankind can have a safe and healthy life while enjoying freedom of movement.
Chang expressed thrill to create a partnership with Georgia Tech including research and applications for sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy, workforce development, and smart cities.
Chang stated the partnership will last for decades and was one of the reasons they chose Georgia for EV investments.

José Muñoz, President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, said Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Muñoz stated proximity to institutions like Georgia Tech was one of the many reasons Hyundai selected Georgia for their new EV manufacturing facility.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise that has created a value chain based on mobility, steel and construction, logistics, finance, IT, and service.
The company has about 250,000 employees worldwide.
Hyundai Motor Group's mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
source_title: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group Sign MoU for Future Mobility Collaboration

facts: At a May 20, 2022 signing ceremony, Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Chairman Chung Euisun announced that Hyundai Motor Group would open a state-of-the-art facility at the Bryan County Megasite.
The initial investment amount announced on May 20, 2022 was $5.54 billion.
The project represents Hyundai Motor Group's first state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory.
The initial announcement included an estimated 8,100 new jobs for Georgia’s coastal region.
At the time of the May 20, 2022 announcement, it was announced that Hyundai suppliers would invest approximately another $2 billion in the project.
On Aug. 31, 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced an additional $2 billion in capital investment.
The Aug. 31, 2023 announcement included another 400 new jobs.
Following the Aug. 31, 2023 announcement, total capital investment reached $7.59 billion and total new jobs reached 8,500.
To date, Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers have announced $10 billon in capital investment.
To date, Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers have announced 15,716 new jobs.

The Bryan County Megasite was purchased by the State of Georgia and the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) for such an investment less than a year before the announcement.
The Bryan County mega site covers 2,923 acres.
The site is located in Bryan County adjacent to Intestate 16.
The site offers immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas including Atlanta, Birmingham, Charlotte, Memphis and Orlando.
The single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S., the Port of Savannah, is 28 miles from the site.
The Port of Savannah offers two Class I rail facilities.
The site was purchased in 2021 through a partnership between the State of Georgia and the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA.
The Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA includes Bryan County, Bulloch County, Chatham County, and Effingham County.
In choosing Georgia, Hyundai Motor Company cited speed-to-market, workforce, and the state’s ability to meet company carbon neutrality standards.
Georgia boasts a large existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group – Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) established technical targets for integrated polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) fuel cell power systems and stacks operating on direct hydrogen for transportation applications.
Targets were developed with input from the U.S. DRIVE Partnership, which includes automotive and energy companies, specifically the Fuel Cell Technical Team.
Detailed target information is available in the Fuel Cells section of the DOE Fuel Cell Technologies Office's Multi-Year Research, Development, and Demonstration Plan.
For 80-kWe (net) integrated transportation fuel cell power systems, the 2015 peak energy efficiency status was 60%, with a 2020 target of 65% and an ultimate target of 70%.
Peak energy efficiency is defined as the ratio of DC output energy to the lower heating value of the input hydrogen fuel, occurring at less than 25% rated power.
For integrated systems, the 2015 power density status was 640 W/L, targeting 650 W/L by 2020 and 850 W/L ultimately.
Specific power for integrated systems stood at 659 W/kg in 2015, with 2020 and ultimate targets set at 650 W/kg.
The cost target for integrated systems was $53/kWnet in 2015, reduced to $40/kWnet by 2020 and $30/kWnet ultimately, projected to high-volume production of 500,000 systems per year.
Cold start-up time to 50% rated power at –20°C ambient temperature was 20 seconds in 2015, with 2020 and ultimate targets set at 30 seconds.
Cold start-up time at +20°C ambient temperature was under 10 seconds in 2015, targeting 5 seconds for both 2020 and ultimate goals.
Start-up and shutdown energy consumption from –20°C ambient temperature was 7.5 MJ in 2015, targeting 5 MJ for 2020 and ultimate phases.
Start-up and shutdown energy consumption from +20°C ambient temperature targets 1 MJ for both 2020 and ultimate phases.
Durability in an automotive drive cycle was projected at 3,900 hours in 2015, increasing to 5,000 hours by 2020 and 8,000 hours ultimately.
Start-up/shutdown durability targets 5,000 cycles for both 2020 and ultimate phases.
Assisted start from low temperatures targets –40°C for both 2020 and ultimate phases.
Unassisted start from low temperatures was –30°C in 2015, maintaining a –30°C target for 2020 and ultimate phases.
System targets explicitly exclude hydrogen storage, power electronics, and electric drive components.

Cost projections are based on state-of-the-art components developed via the DOE Fuel Cells sub-program at the laboratory scale, noting that additional efforts are required to integrate components into complete automotive systems meeting real-world durability requirements.
Cold start status values were averaged from data reported at the 2010 SAE World Congress, with a caveat that these systems may not meet other system-level targets.
Durability metrics represent the average projected time to 10% voltage degradation for the highest durability fleet, based on real-world driving testing rather than simulated cycles.
Catalyst loading was not reported in durability assessments and did not necessarily match the target value of 0.125 mgPGM/cm2.
Durability targets must be met or exceeded at temperatures of 80°C up to peak temperature, based on testing protocols requiring less than a 10% drop in rated power after testing.
Start-up/shutdown durability is measured per protocol with less than a 5% decrease in voltage at 1.2 A/cm2.
An 8-hour soak at stated temperatures must not impact subsequent achievement of targets.
Unassisted start status references Honda's FCX Concept vehicle demonstration (September 25, 2006) and Toyota's development of a new fuel cell hybrid (June 6, 2008).
For 80-kWe transportation fuel cell stacks, the 2015 stack power density status was 3,000 W/L, targeting 2,250 W/L by 2020 and 2,500 W/L ultimately.
Stack specific power remained at 2,000 W/kg across 2015, 2020, and ultimate phases.
Performance at 0.8 V targets 300 mA/cm2 for both 2020 and ultimate phases.
Stack cost targets were $26/kWnet in 2015, reduced to $20/kWnet by 2020 and $15/kWnet ultimately, projected to high-volume production of 500,000 stacks per year.
Stack durability in an automotive drive cycle mirrors system targets: 3,900 hours in 2015, 5,000 by 2020, and 8,000 ultimately.
Stack start-up/shutdown durability also targets 5,000 cycles for 2020 and ultimate phases.
The Q/ΔTi metric (heat transfer efficiency) was 1.9 kW/°C in 2015, targeting 1.45 kW/°C for 2020 and ultimate phases.
Robustness metrics for cold operation, hot operation, and cold transient all target a ratio of 0.7 for 2020 and ultimate phases.
Stack targets exclude hydrogen storage, power electronics, electric drive, and ancillary systems (thermal, water, and air management).
Stacks operate on direct hydrogen and air at up to 150 kPa absolute inlet pressure.
Power density refers to net power minus projected balance of plant power, with volume measured as box volume including dead space.
Stack power density status references Toyota's environmental technology development press release (September 24, 2012).
Stack specific power status references Nissan doubling power density with a new fuel cell stack (October 13, 2011).

Q/ΔTi calculations assume 90 kW gross stack power for 80 kW net power, measured using polarization curve protocols with up to 40% relative humidity inlet humidification and maximum operating coolant outlet temperature.
Robustness metrics are calculated as voltage ratios at specific temperatures (30°C, 80°C, 90°C) and dew points using polarization curve protocols.
source_title: DOE Technical Targets for Fuel Cell Systems and Stacks for Transportation Applications | Department of Energy

facts: Georgia's November unemployment rate was 3.7 percent, up one tenth from a revised 3.6 percent in October.
The unemployment rate was five-tenths lower than the national unemployment rate.
GDOL Emergency Interim Successor Louis DeBroux stated that despite a slight uptick in unemployment this November, Georgia’s economy remains among the strongest in the nation.
Georgia added 85,000 workers to the labor force and created nearly 46,000 new job opportunities over the past 12 months.
The state is bringing its total jobs closer to the milestone of five million jobs.
Unemployment remains well below the national average, further establishing Georgia as the premier state for businesses to thrive and families of hardworking Georgians to prosper.
Total jobs were down by 2,000 over the month and up by 45,900 over the year to 4,983,400.

Over-the-month job gains included Health Care and Social Assistance (2,300), Finance and Insurance (2,000), Arts Entertainment and Recreation (1,400), Transportation and Warehousing (1,300), and Local Government (1,200).

Over-the-month job losses included Accommodation and Food Services (-3,000), Administrative and Support Services (-2,600), Retail Trade (-1,100), Durable Goods Manufacturing (-900), and Wholesale Trade (-800).
Jobs were at an all-time high in Government (721,800) and Private Education and Health Services (698,100).

Over-the-year job gains included Health Care and Social Assistance (22,900), Accommodation and Food Services (11,800), Local Government (11,700), Professional Scientific and Technical Services (5,200), and State Government (3,900).

Over-the-year job losses included Administrative and Support Services (-13,300), Wholesale Trade (-4,200), Information which includes Motion Picture and Sound Recording Industries (-3,700), Non-Durable Goods Manufacturing (-1,400), and Transportation and Warehousing (-500).
The labor force was down 3,000 to 5,413,644, marking the first drop in the labor force since November 2023.
The labor force was up 84,664 over the past 12 months.
The number of employed was down 5,217 to 5,215,239 and was up 57,184 over the past 12 months.
The number of unemployed was up 2,217 to 198,405, the highest level since July 2021, and was up 27,480 over the past 12 months.
Initial claims were down 9,323 over the month to 19,319 in November.
Initial claims were down 2,997 over the year.

entity_name: GREET (Greenhouse gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy use in Technologies) life cycle analysis
canonical_name: GREET (Greenhouse gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy use in Technologies)
facts: The Department of Energy developed the GREET life cycle analysis to assess environmental impacts associated with technologies, fuels, products, and energy systems across various stages of the supply chain.

For any given energy or transportation system, GREET calculates total energy consumption (non-renewable and renewable), fossil fuel energy use, greenhouse gas emissions, air pollutant emissions, and water consumption.

The Argonne National Laboratory R&D GREET Model evaluates energy use and emissions output of transportation and energy sector technologies to assess research and development progress and inform RD&D direction.
R&D GREET is available both as a Microsoft Excel file and as a .NET tool.

40BSAF-GREET is the model adopted by the U.S. Department of the Treasury to calculate emissions reduction percentages under the Sustainable Aviation Fuel Credit, § 40B(e)(2) of the Inflation Reduction Act.
The latest version of 40BSAF-GREET was released in October 2024.

45VH2-GREET is the model adopted by the U.S. Department of the Treasury to determine emissions rates for purposes of the Clean Hydrogen Production Tax Credit under Title 26 of the U.S. Code, Section 45V.
The latest version of 45VH2-GREET was released in December 2025.
45ZCF-GREET is the model adopted by the U.S. Department of the Treasury to determine emissions rates under the Clean Fuel Production Credit, Section 45Z of the Internal Revenue Code.
The latest version of 45ZCF-GREET was released in June 2026.
CA-GREET4.0 is a modified version of the Argonne National Laboratory GREET1 2022 model designed to reflect California-specific fuel pathways and generate carbon intensities for those pathways.
The ICAO-GREET version estimates and verifies default core life cycle assessment values of CORSIA-approved safe aviation fuel pathways.
The GREET suite of models was developed by Argonne National Laboratory in 1994, with the first version released in 1995.

The development and applications of GREET have been an integral part of DOE’s research, development & demonstration efforts to pursue energy and material technologies for energy efficiency and affordability.
GREET models are developed for specific use cases aiming to guide decision-making, research and development, and regulations related to the transportation and energy sectors.
Users seeking a GREET model for regulatory compliance must review guidance specific to that program to ascertain the appropriate version.
source_title: GREET | Department of Energy

canonical_name: Photovoltaic Research & Development (PVRD) Funding Opportunity Announcement
facts: The purpose of the Photovoltaic Research & Development (PVRD) funding opportunity is to support continued innovation in photovoltaic cell and module technologies.
It is anticipated that the PVRD funding opportunity will award $20,000,000 in Federal funding.
The funded projects will advance the limits of photovoltaic cell and module performance toward and beyond the $0.06/kWh 2020 SunShot goal.

Successful applicants must demonstrate a convincing ability to improve power conversion efficiency, fielded energy output, service lifetime, or manufacturability of commercial and emerging PV technologies.
The program includes multiyear research projects in Topic 2 and Topic 3.
Topic 1 is dedicated to small, single-year projects intended to demonstrate the potential for expanded work in a given area, which will be reviewed and selected on an accelerated schedule.
This funding program represents one aspect of the SunShot Initiative’s efforts to encourage the widespread deployment of PV systems by achieving cost competitiveness with traditional energy sources.
Applicants are encouraged to form teams that leverage the strengths of individual members and demonstrate the timeliness and relevance of the project to the present status of the industry.
A second upcoming PVRD webinar will cover Full Applications, the award negotiation process, and DOE's active program management processes.
The webinar is scheduled for Tuesday December 8 at 2:00 PM ET.
source_title: DOE: CMEI eXCHANGE: Funding Opportunities

The establishment represented one of the biggest single foreign investments in the history of Georgia.
Kia and its suppliers have helped create more than 14,000 jobs since the facility's establishment.
The facility has produced more than 4.8 million vehicles to date.

The facility produces the all-electric EV9 and EV6 SUVs.
The advanced manufacturing facility represents a $3.2 billion investment.

The facility operates 24 hours a day.
Kia Georgia's goal is to be the best and most innovative automotive company in the world.
The company is driven by the belief that customer satisfaction is rooted in quality.

Companies such as Fieldale Farms, Northeast Georgia Health System, Häring Precision, and Diana Food all call this area home.
The Gainesville Inland Port in Hall County provides direct rail access between the Port of Savannah and Northeast Georgia.
Dan Raatjes, SVP & Chief Operations Officer at King’s Hawaiian, stated that “Georgia and Hall County had all the ingredients for success for King’s Hawaiian.”

entity_name: Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Trends from the Alternative Fueling Station Locator: Third Quarter 2022
canonical_name: Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Trends from the Alternative Fueling Station Locator: Third Quarter 2022
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy's Alternative Fueling Station Locator contains information on public and private nonresidential alternative fueling stations in the United States and Canada.
The locator currently tracks ethanol (E85), biodiesel, compressed natural gas, electric vehicle (EV) charging, hydrogen, liquefied natural gas, and propane stations.
EV charging continues to experience rapidly changing technology and growing infrastructure.
This report provides a snapshot of the state of EV charging infrastructure in the United States in the third calendar quarter of 2022 (Q3).
Using data from the Station Locator, the report breaks down the growth of public and private charging infrastructure by charging level, network, and location.
The report measures the current state of charging infrastructure compared with two different 2030 infrastructure requirement scenarios.
This information is intended to help transportation planners, policymakers, researchers, infrastructure developers, and others understand the rapidly changing landscape of EV charging infrastructure.
This is the eleventh report in a series on electric vehicle charging infrastructure trends.
The report was published on March 9, 2023.
Authors include A. Brown, J. Cappellucci, E. White, A. Heinrich, and E. Cost.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Publications

entity_name: Grid Resilience and Innovation Partnerships (GRIP) Program
canonical_name: Grid Resilience and Innovation Partnerships (GRIP) Program
facts: The Office of Electricity is administering a $10.5 billion Grid Resilience and Innovation Partnerships (GRIP) Program to enhance grid flexibility and improve the resilience of the power system against growing threats of extreme weather.

These programs will accelerate the deployment of transformative projects that will help to ensure the reliability of the power sector’s infrastructure, so all American communities have access to affordable, reliable electricity.
The content presented on the page reflects information available as of December 2024.

On October 13, 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced more than $600 million to bolster grid resilience and reliability in the face of extreme weather and increased electricity demand across the states affected by Hurricanes Helene and Milton.
The funding will help harden the electric grid across the Southeastern U.S.
The funding will install advanced conductors and controls to help local power companies better face increasing extreme weather.
The funding will deploy self-healing devices and enable more efficient and precise dispatching of field teams during outages.
The funding will upgrade lines to meet critical capacity and flexibility for projected load growth and renewable integration.
The funding will build, rebuild, or reconductor transmission infrastructure.

This investment will help reduce the likelihood of outages, speed up restoration times, and increase grid operational resilience for millions of customers in communities most susceptible to prolonged outages in one of the highest storm-risk regions of the country.
Randolph Electric Membership Corporation is receiving up to $11.4 million in North Carolina for projects within communities affected by Hurricane Helene.

Tennessee Valley Authority is receiving up to $250 million in Alabama, Georgia, Kentucky, Missouri, Mississippi, North Carolina, Tennessee, and Virginia for projects within communities affected by Hurricane Helene.
Gainesville Regional Utilities is receiving up to $47 million in Florida for projects within communities affected by Hurricane Helene.
Georgia Power Company is receiving up to $160 million in Georgia for projects within communities affected by Hurricane Helene.

Georgia Transmission Corporation is receiving up to $97 million in Georgia, Arkansas, Arizona, Kentucky, Maine, Minnesota, North Carolina, Nebraska, New Mexico, South Dakota, and Wisconsin for projects within communities affected by Hurricane Helene.
Switched Source is receiving up to $47 million in Florida and Illinois for projects within communities affected by Hurricane Helene.

As of October 18, 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy has announced about $4.2 billion in federal investments through the second round of GRIP funding for 46 projects in 47 states plus the District of Columbia.

The Round 2 funding aims to protect the U.S. power grid against growing threats of extreme weather, lower costs for communities, and enable additional grid capacity to meet load growth stemming from an increase in manufacturing, data centers, and electrification.
Through the first and second rounds of GRIP funding, OE has announced a total of $7.6 billion in funding for 105 selected projects in all 50 states and the District of Columbia.
DOE does not endorse, recommend, or favor any specific commercial product, process, or service by trade name, trademark, or manufacturer.
source_title: Grid Resilience and Innovation Partnerships (GRIP) Program Projects | Department of Energy

Georgia has emerged as a leader in the e-mobility sector, with more than $27.3 billion in investments since 2018.
The state's e-mobility investments have created over 32,200 new jobs.
Major Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) making significant investments in Georgia include Kia, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA), Rivian, and Archer Aviation.
Battery manufacturers at the forefront of battery innovation in Georgia include SK Battery America and LG Energy Solution.
Recyclers and fuel cell manufacturers advancing recycling and hydrogen fuel technologies in Georgia include Aurubis AG, Ascend Elements, and Plug Power.
The state’s traditional automotive sector continues to thrive, with exports reaching $4.1 billion in 2023.

The Georgia Ports Authority’s Brunswick facility is the second-busiest automotive Roll-on/Roll-off port in the U.S., reinforcing Georgia’s strategic position in the automotive and e-mobility supply chains.
Georgia’s strong foundations in mobility are anchored by its aerospace and automotive sectors.
For over a decade, Georgia has established itself as a prime location for global business growth, offering diverse opportunities across key industries.
The state’s e-mobility sector has attracted over $27.3 billion in investments and created 32,200 jobs.

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy's Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) is committed to financing American energy and manufacturing projects that meaningfully contribute to U.S. energy security, grid reliability, and lowering costs for all Americans.
Under the Trump Administration, EDF empowers the private sector to invest in the future, help win the AI race, strengthen American industry, and restore American Energy Dominance.
EDF is working on behalf of the American people to secure and strengthen American energy assets.
EDF is working on behalf of the American people to boost American energy innovation and technologies.
EDF is working on behalf of the American people to expand affordable, reliable, and secure energy for all Americans.
EDF is working on behalf of the American people to create American jobs.
EDF is working on behalf of the American people to bolster national security.

In response to the nation’s growing energy demands and the loss of hundreds of baseload plants, EDF stands ready to partner with the private sector to strengthen America’s energy foundation and support U.S. leadership in emerging artificial intelligence technologies.

Through its financing programs, EDF supports the Trump Administration and Secretary Wright’s efforts to enhance energy security, maintain technological leadership, and ensure an affordable, reliable, and secure energy supply for all Americans.
EDF issued a conditional loan commitment to finance the purchase of long-lead time items needed to rebuild America’s commercial nuclear supply chain.
Updated guidance focuses on the Energy Dominance Financing Program (EDFP) and its role in advancing critical grid and baseload energy generation infrastructure projects across the United States.

EDF closed a historic $26.5 billion loan package to support two wholly owned subsidiaries of Southern Company, delivering over $7 billion in electricity cost savings to millions of customers in Georgia and Alabama.
DOE senior advisor Greg Beard was appointed Director of EDF, taking the helm of the office's deep portfolio and active pipeline to unleash American energy dominance.

President Trump and Secretary Wright have made commitments to the American people to unleash American energy dominance, and the Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) is on the front lines of delivering affordable, reliable, secure energy.

EDF’s Energy Dominance Financing Program (EDFP) powered by the Working Families Tax Cut is a core pillar to the Administration’s strategy to help win the global AI race by increasing the nation’s energy supply through new eligibility for clean coal and oil and gas power-generated projects, securing critical mineral supply chains, and reinvigorating the nuclear industry.
The Energy Dominance Financing Program (Section 1706 or EDF Program) guarantees loans to projects that add energy to the grid or enhance reliability.
The program finances projects that retool, repower, repurpose, or replace energy infrastructure that have ceased operations.
The program finances projects that enable operating infrastructure to increase capacity and output.

The program finances projects that support or enable the provision of known or forecastable electric supply at time intervals necessary to maintain or enhance grid reliability or other system adequacy needs.

The Energy Dominance Financing Program can also finance critical materials projects and secure America’s critical minerals supply chain, reflecting the important applications of critical minerals and materials across the energy sector.
The Energy Dominance Financing Program can support upgrading or uprating energy infrastructure so it can restart or operate at higher output.
The Energy Dominance Financing Program can support replacing retired energy infrastructure with new energy infrastructure to increase power available to the grid.
The Energy Dominance Financing Program can support building new dispatchable or baseload power generation facilities.
The Energy Dominance Financing Program can support maintaining, enhancing, or replacing electric grid and transmission infrastructure.
Under the Trump Administration, EDF offers financing for energy generation and grid reliability projects.
Under the Trump Administration, EDF offers financing for innovative energy technology projects.
Under the Trump Administration, EDF offers financing for manufacturing and advanced transportation projects.
Under the Trump Administration, EDF offers financing for tribal investment in energy-related projects.
EDF has standard application processes to determine each project’s eligibility, readiness to proceed, and reasonable prospect of repayment.
Following the application process, EDF will commence a due diligence phase that is similar to that of commercial lenders.
The duration of this process varies based on the applicant’s level of preparation and project complexity, but it typically takes a minimum of six months to more than a year.
Prospective applicants may request a no-cost pre-application consultation with the EDF team.
The Loan Programs Office (LPO) operates as the Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) and performs the duties assigned to LPO through the Energy Policy Act of 2005, as amended.
EDF possesses all authorities, receives all appropriations, and performs all requirements assigned to LPO.

facts: TI Automotive will invest $30 million to build a new manufacturing facility in Hart County, Georgia.
The plant will initially manufacture blow-molded plastic fuel tanks and filler pipes for vehicles built in nearby southern states by BMW and an Asia-based automaker.
Groundbreaking ceremonies for the 145,000-square-foot plant took place on July 1, 2004.
The facility is located in the Gateway High Tech Industrial Park off Interstate 85 at Exit 177 in northeast Georgia.
Site preparation, including grading and installation of water and sewer lines, began last month at the 25-acre plot.
Construction was scheduled to begin within several weeks of the July 1, 2004 groundbreaking.
The TI Automotive plant will initially employ 100 people.
The facility will produce 350,000 automotive fuel-tank systems annually upon initial operation.
Hiring will begin in March 2005 with the start of training and prototype assembly.
A majority of the new employees will be hired locally.
Training for high-tech operators will be provided by the state’s Quick Start program through North Georgia Technical College.
Brian Lindsay, TI Automotive managing commercial director for Global Fuel Systems, expects full production of fuel-system components for BMW and the Asia-based automaker in 2006.
The site was developed through the Joint Development Authority of Hart, Franklin, and Stephens counties.
Development was funded in part by a State of Georgia Edge Grant of $650,000 provided through the One Georgia agency.

Officials at the groundbreaking included Craig Lesser (Georgia Department of Economic Development), Mike Beatty (Georgia Department of Community Affairs), Nancy Cobb (OneGeorgia Authority executive director), Mark Lytle and Kevin Langston (Georgia Department of Economic Development), Buddy Glazner (Georgia Power), and Jack Edmunds (chairman of the Joint Development Authority).

Local officials in attendance included State Representatives Jeanette Jamieson and Alan Powell, Dan Reyen (Hart County Board chairman), Sammy Elrod (Franklin County Board chairman), Hartwell Mayor Matt Beasley, Lavonia Mayor Ralph Owens, and Hart County Administrator John Caime.
Brian Lindsay noted the warm reception and strong spirit of cooperation for the site selection in Hart, Franklin, and Stephens counties.
Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue stated that TI Automotive’s investment means more jobs for citizens and demonstrates Georgia is a great place to do business.
The facility was designed and engineered by O’Neal Constructors of Atlanta, which also serves as the general contractor.

Paul Darden, O’Neal Constructors vice president, stated the building will use significant glass for natural light, be fully air-conditioned, and feature flexible space with minimal interior partitions.
TI Automotive will lease the Gateway Park site from the tri-county Joint Development Authority.
Incentives include state job tax credits totaling $3,000 per employee per year during the first five years of operation, which can be spread over a 10-year period.
Manouchehr Kambaksh, TI Automotive vice president of Global Advanced Engineering, stated the facility will have two blow-molding press lines with an annual capacity to produce one million fuel tanks.
The plant will use an innovative blow-molded filler-pipe process, which is a first for a North American automotive supplier.
This Hart County plant is the first TI Automotive facility to incorporate the assembly of fuel tank components, filler pipes, and tanks under one roof.
TI Automotive is the world’s leading supplier of fluid storage, transfer, and delivery systems for brake, fuel, and air conditioning applications.
The company is based in Warren, Michigan, employs over 20,000 people at more than 130 facilities across 29 countries on six continents, and reported nearly $2.5 billion in sales in 2003.
