entity_name: Massachusetts Electric School Bus Pilot Project
canonical_name: Massachusetts Electric School Bus Pilot Project
facts: A $2 million pilot project funded through the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative allowed three school districts to purchase Type C electric school buses and Level 2 bidirectional vehicle-to-grid (V2G) charging stations to test the technology in cold weather environments.
Massachusetts Department of Energy Resources (DOER), which houses the Massachusetts Clean Cities (MCC) coalition, administered the project.

The project included $400,000 grants for Amherst Regional Public School District, Cambridge Public School District, and Concord Public School District to invest in the vehicles and electric vehicle supply equipment (EVSE).
Combined, the three buses traveled approximately 14,000 miles and provided transportation for 279 days.
Though the pilot has ended, the school districts still operate the electric buses and DOER continues to evaluate their impact.
During the pilot, life cycle air pollution was about half of what pollution from diesel buses would have been.
As the Massachusetts grid mix incorporates larger sources of alternative energy sources, those pollution benefits will increase.
Bus efficiency ranged from 1.3 to 1.4 kilowatt-hours (kWh) per mile, providing a range of 70 to 80 miles.
The implementation team noted that districts would have each benefitted from having more than one bus for redundancy.

Stephen Russell, former MCC director, stated one bus per district gave a good flavor but recommended piloting a few buses in the same location to make it easier to manage issues, trained staff, and parts.
Once each district selected an equipment manufacturer, it took approximately three months to build the buses.
Charging infrastructure installation lagged behind bus arrival because each school had to bid out installation separately after purchasing stations from a state contract.
Brian Foulds, an electric technology champion for Concord, noted a bus sat for approximately five months while charging station contracts were pulled together.
Fleets benefit from coordinating with their local utility to understand electrical upgrades, rate structures, and demand charges.
Demand charges apply to commercial electricity usage customers based on the highest usage recorded at any time in a month and accounted for a significant portion of operating costs.
One way to avoid demand charge costs is to charge the buses outside of peak demand times.
The buses consumed excess energy from fans and heaters used to cool and heat the batteries during charging.
Fueling costs were anticipated to be lower, but unmanaged charging shifted the scales; energy costs were estimated that 63% higher than necessary.
A lesson learned is that buses should be charged at night rather than during the day when electricity use is already high.
When the electric fleet grows, scheduled charging and V2G will be addressed with software to manage state of charge based on bus duty cycles and reduce peak load at the substation level.

The pilot sought to determine whether the technology was market ready, real-world pollution reductions, fuel efficiency benefits, operational costs compared to diesel buses, and V2G capabilities for a school district.
Concord's mission was to explore a fuel that can reduce the health and environmental impact of busing students.
For Concord, the school bus fleet accounts for approximately 8% of all municipal sector pollution.
To maximize pollution benefits, Concord deployed the bus along a route for students using the wheelchair lift because there are no fumes when using the lift and diesel buses have to idle to lift.
While the purchase price is high, electric buses can help lower maintenance and fueling costs.
Concord's long-term goal is to reduce electrical utility peak load while buses aren't in use by pairing with V2G capabilities in the future.

Amherst Regional Public School District operates its own vehicles with a relatively small, 10-vehicle fleet supported by a single technician; their electric bus traveled an average of 34 miles per day.
Cambridge Public School District is privately operated and transports 2,500 students; their electric bus was incorporated into regular operations and covered roughly 45 miles a day.

Concord Public School District buses operate in up to three-hour intervals to transport over 3,400 students from Concord, Carlisle, and two local private schools; their electric bus traveled approximately 45 miles per route.

Vermont Energy Investment Corporation (VEIC) assisted DOER with the project by evaluating available technology, developing procurement materials, providing ongoing support, tracking vehicle reliability, energy efficiency, and energy consumption, and providing an evaluation of the experience.
Each fleet used a well thought out request for proposal developed by VEIC covering battery size, range, safety regulations, and warranty terms and conditions.
The Lion Electric Company was the only original equipment manufacturer with electric school buses in 2015 and was awarded the contract for all three demonstration sites.
As of 2020, four manufacturers are at the table including Lion, Blue Bird Corporation, IC Bus, and Thomas Built Buses.
The Lion buses used in the pilot included four batteries that provide 104 kWh of energy storage with an estimated range of 75 miles.
The batteries are warrantied for 160,000 kWh or eight years.
Amherst, Cambridge, and Concord all had reliability or mechanical issues ranging from noisy cooling fans to charger failures to bus breakdowns while in service.

One of the biggest challenges was working with a company that did not have a local presence; because Lion is based in Canada, parts took three days to two weeks to arrive and mechanics had to be sent from Canada extending downtime.
Even over the course of the pilot the technology advanced as the buses received were early models different from the Lion buses in production at the pilot's conclusion.
Lion learned from the issues the school districts experienced and worked to improve their technology.
Almost all the technical issues stemmed from being one of the first electric school bus projects in the United States.
After two years of operation Lion took each bus back to the factory for up to two months and rewired them to fix the issues reported by the districts.
In the year since the buses were redelivered there were no operational issues.
Lion recently opened a parts and maintenance facility in Albany New York which will allow for easier servicing.
Russell advised fleets interested in deploying electric school buses to ask the manufacturer to provide an experienced electric bus technician and driver for training at the start.
Following the rebuild each bus was equipped with telematics which transmit use data to DOER.
Amherst Cambridge and Concord continue to operate the electric school buses and report data weekly to DOER including kWh consumed and miles travelled per week.
MCC is working to analyze the data and provide an update on pollution impacts and maintenance costs.
Through a vehicle lifecycle cost comparison Concord is developing a plan to switch 80% of its bus fleet to electric.
The second bus is expected to arrive in 2020 for Concord.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Massachusetts School Fleets Get Answers through Electric Bus Testing

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE) Office of Electricity (OE) released two Notices of Intent (NOIs) to issue funding for grid modernization research and development.
OE intends to issue two Notices of Funding Opportunity (NOFOs) totaling $13 million later this year to promote a more reliable, resilient, secure, and affordable electric grid.
Upgrading critical systems and controls will extend the life of existing grid components, increase electricity transmission capacity, and improve communications to predict and prevent failures.

Gene Rodrigues, Assistant Secretary for Electricity, stated the funding opportunities will advance innovative hardware and software solutions to increase reliability and lower costs of America’s electricity transmission system.
The first opportunity is an $8 million Renewable Integration Management with Innovative High Voltage Direct Current Power Circuit Breakers (REIMAGINE BREAKERS) NOFO.
REIMAGINE BREAKERS supports R&D for high voltage direct current (HVDC) power circuit breakers to reduce technology costs and support widespread HVDC adoption.
DOE anticipates announcing the $8 million NOFO ($7 million from OE, $1 million from WETO) by the end of 2024.
The number of awards for REIMAGINE BREAKERS depends on applications submitted and fund availability.
The second opportunity is a $5 million Human-Centric Analytics for Resilient & Modernized Power sYstems (HARMONY) NOFO.
HARMONY aims to improve risk assessment and communication for grid operators amid big data trends, including the integration of renewable energy and EV charging.
Grid transformation trends involving renewable energy and EV charging are changing long-standing assumptions about power system operations and causing gaps in assessing systematic uncertainties.
Advanced physics- and human behavior-aware analytics are critical to help grid operators predict, prevent, and mitigate cascading failures in power grids.
Approximately $5 million is expected to be available for three-year R&D cooperative agreements under HARMONY.
OE expects to announce the HARMONY NOFO by the end of 2024.
Both NOFOs will be posted at FedConnect if released.
source_title: U.S. Department of Energy Announces Intent to Issue $13M in Two New Grid Modernization Funding Opportunities | Department of Energy

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) hosted its Grand Opening celebration on March 26, 2025.
The ceremony marks the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history, occurring two and a half years after breaking ground.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that HMGMA represents advanced manufacturing capabilities, commitment to innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.

Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the grand opening ceremony alongside U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Hyundai Motor Group Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz, Kia Corporation President and CEO Ho Sung Song, company officials, state and local leaders, HMGMA Meta Pro employees, and other distinguished guests.
Governor Kemp called HMGMA a once-in-a-generation opportunity made possible by working with job creators to build fruitful partnerships for decades.

Kemp highlighted collaborations between HMGMA and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges, including Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility, which are preparing Georgians for high-quality jobs.
HMG entered into a landmark agreement with the State of Georgia in May 2022 to establish an electric and hybrid vehicle production and battery manufacturing facility in Ellabell, Georgia.
The facility is located 20 miles outside of historic Savannah on a 2,906-acre Megasite.
HMGMA was officially formed by June 2022, with groundbreaking occurring within four months of formation.
On October 3, 2024, less than two years after breaking ground, the first vehicle rolled off the assembly line at HMGMA.
The first vehicle produced was a Hyundai IONIQ 5.
Hyundai’s IONIQ 9 three-row electric SUV is also now in production at HMGMA.
The first Kia model to be built at the facility will join production in 2026.
The plant manufactures electric vehicles for Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia, and is equipped to produce hybrid vehicles.
HMGMA initially has the capacity to produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
The Group's investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion.
This represents the largest single investment in Georgia's history and includes a total of 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.

The Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs and generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
As of July 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment and approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers across 12 counties in Georgia.
This supplier investment more than doubles the additional $1 billion investment originally anticipated.
To date, Hyundai Motor Group has invested more than $20.5 billion in the U.S., creating or supporting over 570,000 jobs nationwide.
The Group announced a significant investment of $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028.
Of this $21 billion commitment, $9 billion will be invested to establish an annual production capacity in the U.S. of 1.2 million vehicles.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA's annual production capacity to a total of 500,000 units, an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia will also see improvements in production facilities as part of this expansion.

A total of $6 billion will be allocated to enhance the Group’s parts and logistics business and establish an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill in the U.S. through its affiliate, Hyundai Steel.
The new steel mill will have an annual production capacity of 2.7 million tons and will supply high-quality automotive steel plates to HMGMA when commercial production begins in 2029.

An additional $6 billion will be invested to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence (AI), and advanced air mobility (AAM).
Through the $21 billion commitment, the Group anticipates creating 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028.
The overall economic impact of this commitment is expected to generate more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.
HMGMA’s architecture blends advanced technology with design elements reflecting Hyundai’s brand identity and the natural beauty of the Savannah area.
HMGMA integrated proof-of-concept manufacturing technologies and systems from Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS), scaling them to accommodate larger production volume.
All HMGMA processes, including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production, are optimized using AI and real-time data.
Cutting-edge robotics and vision systems ensure high-quality vehicle production at the facility.

HMGMA expands its mixed-model production capability with various robot processes to enhance automation, including automated guided vehicles for logistics, Boston Dynamic’s Spot for exterior quality inspection in the weld shop, and parking robots in the assembly shop.
Automation in HMGMA’s assembly shop is nearly double that of the Group’s existing facilities.
Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated that creating a comfortable and welcoming work environment for Meta Pros is as important as building an energy-efficient and technologically advanced plant.
All work zones from office to production areas feature open layouts with ample daylight, indoor landscaping, and team centers with skylights to encourage communication and collaboration.
The 3,050-foot-long front of the HMGMA building’s exterior is lit in two parallel lines inspired by a stream, allowing interstate travelers to view vehicles moving from paint to general assembly.
A solar parking lot at the front of the site will generate 5.2 megawatts of energy, accounting for roughly five percent of the Metaplant’s total power consumption.
The solar panels are installed above 1,878 parking spaces, providing shade while producing electricity.
A 41-acre ecological park featuring trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas is under construction in front of the Metaplant for employee use.
HMGMA has deployed Hyundai Motor XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations.
A total of 21 XCIENT trucks are currently in operation, representing more than a third of the Glovis America truck fleet at HMGMA.
These trucks transport vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the Megasite daily.
Since 2022, Hyundai and Genesis, along with various social responsibility initiatives and nonprofit organizations, have donated nearly $4.5 million to local organizations in Georgia.

Kia's Autoland Georgia factory in West Point has provided over $22 million in support of Georgia communities since 2009, including more than $6 million for educational programs like the THINC College & Career Academy and SAE’s “A World in Motion.”
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Celebrates Grand Opening, Powering US Economic Growth | Press Releases & Insights | Worldwide | HTWO

facts: In January 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office announced the closing of a $289.7 million loan guarantee to Sunwealth Holdco 18 LLC for Project Polo.
The loan guarantee will finance the deployment of up to 1,000 solar photovoltaic (PV) systems and battery energy storage systems (BESS).
The PV and BESS systems are located primarily at commercial and industrial facilities and integrated across up to 27 states.
Sunwealth has partnered with SYSO Technologies of Boston, Massachusetts, to provide its distributed energy management software platform.
The software platform will allow the project to function as a virtual power plant (VPP).
VPPs help facilitate the clean energy transition by optimizing resource utilization and coordinating demand response.

VPPs aggregate electrified, grid-connected devices such as solar-plus-storage systems, grid-interactive efficient buildings, air conditioners, and electric vehicles and their associated charging infrastructure.
Sunwealth’s distributed energy resources in concert with SYSO’s software platform will store and generate significant amounts of energy.
VPPs often provide energy at a lower levelized cost than traditional options, doing so more cleanly while offering consumers greater resilience during grid disruptions.
The Sunwealth VPP offers advanced management of PV and BESS, enhanced forecasting of PV production and coincident peaks, and aggregation and dispatch of DERs.
This project is expected to create approximately 3,700 jobs.
The job creation includes over 1,900 solar and storage installation jobs and over 1,700 operations and maintenance jobs.
Sunwealth estimates the project will lead to the avoidance of up to 4.07 million metric tons of carbon over the project’s lifetime.
Sunwealth provides employees salary and benefits that target 120% of the living wage in each office location.
Sunwealth has created “Sunwealth University,” a four-tier internal training program for employees to promote advancement through the company.
Sunwealth University provides job-specific training, courses on project finance and development, and more.
Sunwealth is committed to building relationships with the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers (IBEW) and the National Electrical Contractors Association.
Sunwealth is committed to integrating local solar nonprofits and trade groups into its developer workforce.
The company hosts events with local trades and provides grants to local nonprofit training organizations, such as Green City Force, Emerald Cities, and Browning the Green Space.
Project Polo is owned by Sunwealth Holdco 18 LLC.
The owner location is Cambridge, Massachusetts.
The loan guarantee falls under the Title 17 Loan Program.
The guaranteed loan amount is $289.7 million, which includes principal and any capitalized interest.
Permanent U.S. jobs supported by the project are estimated at 1,700 at the time of closing.
The project is expected to avoid up to 4.07 million metric tonnes of CO2 over its lifetime.
source_title: Project Polo | Department of Energy

entity_name: Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certification Program
canonical_name: Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certification Program
facts: Communities and property owners can seek state-approved certification through the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certification Program to ensure sites are prepared for quick development and attract company interest.

The GRAD program provides business and site selectors a portfolio of pre-qualified industrial sites for which due diligence has been completed, making those sites more marketable in an on-demand world.

In early April, Tier 1 Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) supplier Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation selected Tradeport East Business Center in Liberty County, Georgia, for its $72 million operation.

Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation joined fellow HMGMA Tier 1 suppliers in selecting GRAD Certified sites for their urgent needs, including Hyundai Mobis at Belfast Commerce Park in Bryan County and Seoyon E-HWA at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
These companies recognized quickly the benefits of a truly site-ready operation when meeting critical production deadlines in the race for consumer adoption.
The Fitzgerald and Ben Hill County Development Authority worked together to ensure the 54-acre Forward Fitzgerald site at 351 Jacksonville Highway is primed for development.

The new “GRAD – Select” certified site in Fitzgerald fronts U.S. Route 319, is situated less than 10 minutes from the Fitzgerald Municipal Airport, just 30 minutes from Interstate 75, and roughly two hours from both the Ports of Savannah and Brunswick.
The Forward Fitzgerald site will receive nearly $6 million in infrastructure enhancements, giving it some of the most modern infrastructure in South Georgia.
This certified location joins more than 65 locations, which are easy to find via Georgia Site Selector.
source_title: Georgia’s Latest Pre-Qualified Industrial Site Ideally Situated | Georgia

facts: Transportation sector accounts for ~30% of total U.S. energy needs and 70% of U.S. petroleum consumption.
Average U.S. household spends >15% of its total family expenditures on transportation, making it the most expensive spending category after housing.

Energy Efficient Mobility Systems (EEMS) Program supports the Transportation Technologies Office's (TTO) mission to improve transportation energy efficiency through low-cost and secure energy technologies.

EEMS conducts early-stage research and development (R&D) at the vehicle, traveler, and system levels to create knowledge, insights, tools, and technology solutions that increase mobility energy productivity for individuals and businesses.
EEMS develops tools and capabilities to evaluate the energy impacts of new mobility solutions and create new technologies that provide economic benefits through enhanced mobility.
SMART Mobility Laboratory Consortium is a multi-year, multi-laboratory collaborative dedicated to further understanding the energy implications and opportunities of advanced mobility solutions.

In FY2020, the EEMS Program concluded the first phase of research under “SMART Mobility 1.0,” a 3-year effort consisting of 5 national laboratories and 30+ projects spanning 5 coordinated research pillars: Advanced Fueling Infrastructure, Connected and Automated Vehicles, Mobility Decision Science, Multi-Modal Freight, and Urban Science.
At the conclusion of the first phase of SMART Mobility, CMEI published six SMART Mobility Capstone reports summarizing the Consortium’s research methods, results, and insights.
Findings from SMART Mobility were highlighted in a monthly webinar series.
In FY2020, EEMS launched “SMART Mobility 2.0,” which is comprised of 6 National Laboratories.
SMART Mobility 2.0 supports continuing research from SMART 1.0 as well as new research projects.

The Consortium is the EEMS Program’s primary effort to create tools and generate knowledge about how future mobility systems may evolve and identify R&D gaps that the EEMS Program may address through its research portfolio.
source_title: Energy Efficient Mobility Systems | Department of Energy

facts: Amprius is considering Georgia for a battery plant.
Amprius has plans to go public.
Experts say Georgia’s growing number of manufacturers acts as a magnet for more companies in the industry.

facts: GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson was named the 2023 “Georgian of the Year” by Georgia Trend.
The award recognizes Commissioner Wilson as the top salesperson for the state and commends his work promoting the advantages of Georgia to attract investments and create jobs.
Wilson was appointed as economic development Commissioner by former Governor Nathan Deal six years prior to the article's publication.
Under Wilson's tenure, Georgia maintained its place as the No. 1 state in the nation to do business for the ninth consecutive year in 2022.

Former Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue credited Wilson with much of the credit for Georgia being one of the best places to grow a business by communicating the achievements of the governor and legislature.
In August 2022, GDEcD announced that investments in expansions and new locations during FY22 totaled more than $21.2 billion.
The FY22 investments were projected to create 51,132 direct new jobs in the state, setting new records for Georgia.
GDEcD's provision of solutions for companies and open communication helped secure two of its largest-ever investments in FY22.
Wilson expressed that he gets emotional hearing how job creation has changed people's lives and is grateful to the governors who appointed him.

GDEcD teammates Lee Thomas (Director of the Georgia Film Office) and David Nuckolls (Executive Director of the Center of Innovation) were also recognized by Georgia Trend for their state contributions.
GDEcD describes itself as the “quarterback” that brings partners across the state together to facilitate business development.

links.context: Learn more about why business wants what Georgia has

source_title: GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson Named ‘Georgian of the Year’ by Georgia Trend - Valdosta CEO

entity_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle (EV) Supply Chain & Industry
facts: The University of Georgia operates the largest fleet of electric buses on any college campus in the country.
UGA’s fleet consists of 33 Proterra E2 buses managed by Transportation & Parking Services.
The electric buses are constructed from corrosion-resistant fiber and have a potential life expectancy of 30 years, which is more than twice that of diesel buses.
Don Walter serves as the Director of Campus Transportation at UGA.
Federal and state grants totaling nearly $18 million aided the acquisition of the Proterra E2 buses.
The electric buses now make up one-third of the University’s student bus portfolio.
The EV buses consume approximately $10 worth of electricity per day, costing about one-tenth as much as diesel.
Yearly maintenance costs for the buses dropped from $22,000 to roughly $7,500 per bus.
Proterra reports that over a 12-year span, its electric buses will displace more than 2.5 million gallons of diesel.
The buses eliminate over 4.5 million pounds of carbon emissions annually.
The buses charge overnight at twelve charging stations installed at the campus transit facility.
A team of UGA engineering students is redesigning the transit facility to improve efficiency and eventually integrate solar panels.
Students are exploring additional uses for bus batteries to potentially provide power to other parts of campus.
Jason Perry works as a specialist with UGA’s Office of Sustainability.
One of the world’s largest EV battery installations is expected to launch production in 2022, located just 20 miles north of Athens.
SK Battery America is constructing a $1.67 billion facility near Commerce in Jackson County.
SK Battery America plans to invest an additional $940 million to accommodate exploding demand.
SK Battery America is contracted to produce lithium-ion battery cells for the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen.
Volkswagen is expanding its production site in nearby Chattanooga to assemble all-electric SUVs beginning in 2022.
Since 2017, at least ten companies aside from SK Innovation have made investments in EV-related production in Georgia.
In November 2020, Georgia announced that TEKLAS would invest in opening its first North American facility and headquarters in Dalton.
TEKLAS is a Turkish advanced research and development, manufacturer, and supplier of EV parts.
GEDIA Automotive Group announced plans to build an advanced manufacturing facility in Calhoun, Georgia.
GEDIA specializes in making state-of-the-art automotive parts and will focus on lightweight structural automotive parts for electric vehicles.
In May 2021, Gov. Brian Kemp announced that Duckyang would invest $10 million to open its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
Duckyang is an electric mobility parts supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems.
The new Duckyang facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton, located not far from SK Battery.
Dong-in-Son is the CEO of Duckyang.
Enchem, a Korean manufacturer specializing in electrolytes for rechargeable batteries and electrostatic double-layer capacitors, chose Jackson County to supply SK Battery.
Enchem will invest $61.35 million in two plants expected to create more than 300 jobs.
John Scott serves as Jackson County vice president and director of economic development.
Within about a 45-minute drive from the center of Jackson County, there is a population of at least 1.8 million people available to support manufacturing workers.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia invested more than $1 billion on its first North American assembly plant in West Point, West Georgia.
The Kia plant hired 3,000 Georgians and created work for countless suppliers in a network that followed it.
Access to Atlanta’s Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and the Port of Savannah were major factors in Kia’s decision to locate in West Point.
The Georgia Quickstart training program offers free, customized training to manufacturers through the state’s extensive network of community and technical colleges.
Stewart Countess is senior vice president and chief operations officer at Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia.
In August, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced a statewide initiative to strengthen Georgia’s position as a national leader in the electric mobility industry.
The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) is driven by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).

EMIA includes partners from government, industry, electric utilities, education, nonprofits, and other stakeholders focused on advancing Georgia’s role in the electric mobility industry and its supply chain.
Governor Kemp stated that Georgia has a proven record of investing early in resources and infrastructure needed to connect to the world and develop jobs of the future.
The five EMIA committees will develop objectives for Supply Chain, Infrastructure, Workforce, Innovation, and Policy/Initiatives.
The Carl Vinson Institute at the University of Georgia will host EMIA’s committee meetings and integrated research forums.
According to a report by the Alliance for Automotive Innovation, automakers are planning to invest $250 billion in electrification by 2023.
IHS Markit predicts there will be 130 EV models available in the U.S. by 2026.
Georgia is positioned as the center of the southeast automotive corridor with close proximity to the region’s automotive original equipment manufacturers.
source_title: A QUIET REVOLUTION – Site Selection Magazine

facts: Duckyang Industrial Co. will invest $10 million in a new facility in Braselton, Georgia.
The company plans to hire 285 people for the Braselton plant.
The facility is located northeast of Atlanta in an industrial park near an Amazon warehouse.
The Braselton plant will manufacture battery modules and energy storage systems for SK Innovation's nearby Commerce, Georgia plant.
SK Innovation's Commerce plant has a total investment of $2.6 billion and will employ 2,600 workers.
Duckyang did not disclose a production start date for the Braselton facility.
The new building will occupy 230,000 square feet (21,000 square meters).
Duckyang currently supplies cockpit modules to automakers including Kia and Hyundai.
The supplied cockpit modules are dashboard assemblies containing more than 130 parts, including steering wheels, instruments, and electronics.
Kia operates a manufacturing plant in West Point, Georgia.
Hyundai operates a manufacturing plant in Montgomery, Alabama.

Duckyang CEO Dong-in Son stated the company is entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market through SKBA to cooperate with other automotive companies like Hyundai and Kia Motors that have U.S. manufacturing bases.
The company hopes to supply interior parts and electrical vehicle parts from the Braselton facility.
Duckyang may claim an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $1,250 per job from state income taxes, capped at 50% of its tax liability.
The tax breaks could save the company up to $1.8 million over five years.
To qualify for the tax savings, workers must earn at least $28,000 per year.
The state of Georgia will provide job training for the facility's workers but will not provide any discretionary incentives.
The city of Braselton and Jackson County may provide property tax breaks to Duckyang.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that flagship companies like SK Innovation attract supplier follow-on investment.
Georgia is focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain, according to Commissioner Wilson.
SK Innovation announced a joint venture with Ford Motor Co. to manufacture batteries and related equipment starting in 2025.
The joint venture is named BlueOval SK.
BlueOval SK's production volume will require approximately two jointly owned plants.
Ford Motor Co. anticipates needing battery capacity equivalent to 10 factories worldwide by 2030.
SK Innovation will spend approximately $5.3 billion to build the BlueOval SK plants, separate from its $2.6 billion Commerce plant investment.

The U.S. International Trade Commission ruled in February that SK Innovation stole 22 trade secrets from LG Energy and initially barred it from importing, making, or selling batteries in the U.S. for 10 years.
The dispute was settled in April with SK Innovation agreeing to pay $1.8 billion and an undisclosed royalty, allowing production at the Commerce plant to proceed.
source_title: Korean firm to invest $10M, hire 285, to make battery parts | AP News

facts: Enchem operates as Korea's leading electrolyte solution supplier to global top-tier customers.
The company provides high-functional electrolyte additives and optimal electrolyte development and manufacturing technology for each customer.
Enchem is establishing a one-stop solution for electrolyte by completing vertical integration through the internalization of raw materials.
The company has established local supply systems in Korea, China, Europe, and the United States.
Enchem is securing four global production bases located in the USA, Europe, China, and Korea.
Production capacity targets for 2025 include 5,000 tons, 30,000 tons, 150,000 tons, 70,000 tons, 155,000 tons, and 78,000 tons.
Expected production capacity targets for 2026 include 7,000 tons, 30,000 tons, 200,000 tons, 100,000 tons, 300,000 tons, 120,000 tons, 40,000 tons, and 200,000 tons.
Expected production capacity targets for 2027 include 250,000 tons.
A US production facility is located in Commerce, Georgia.

facts: Enchem America, Inc., a South Korean battery component maker, planned to build an electrolyte manufacturing facility at the I-40 Advantage Industrial Park in Brownsville, Tenn.
The proposed facility was valued at $152.5 million.
The project would have brought 190 jobs to Haywood County.
Enchem America announced its plan to build on the 'shovel ready' industrial park in 2023.
The site is located roughly 16 miles from BlueOval City, a massive electric vehicle manufacturing campus.
BlueOval City is slated to produce Ford Motor Company's new all-electric pickup truck and electric vehicle batteries in partnership with SK On.
The BlueOval City campus is expected to serve as manufacturing space for BlueOval SK, Ford, and electric vehicle component suppliers.
The campus is intended to inject jobs and residents into the surrounding rural West Tennessee communities.
The Enchem facility would have produced electrolytes used for electric vehicle batteries.
Production at the Enchem facility was expected to magnify the company's ability to supply and support its electric vehicle partners in the United States.
Enchem America decided to withdraw from the project in mid-September 2025 after careful consideration and an extensive review of its North American business strategy.
The withdrawal decision was primarily driven by a recent shift in demand from the company's key North American customers.

Brownsville-Haywood County Economic Development Corporation Executive Director Aaron Stewart informed the corporation's board members and West Tennessee mayors of Enchem America's withdrawal via email on Sept. 26.
Enchem America did not respond to a request for comment regarding the withdrawal.
The project cancellation occurs amid continuing shifts in the electric vehicle market.
Ford Motor Company announced it will delay mass production of its next-generation pickup truck at BlueOval City until 2028.
Ford still plans to start producing prototypes of the truck at BlueOval City in 2027.
The Brownsville-Haywood County Economic Development Corporation is working to terminate the state incentives Enchem America was set to receive for the project.
Enchem America received a $2 million recruitment grant in 2023, according to a state database.
The Brownsville-Haywood County Economic Development Corporation will work with the state and the Tennessee Valley Authority to put the site back on the market.
The Tennessee Valley Authority has contributed grant funds to prepare the industrial park for future development.
Enchem America completed preliminary site grading but had not yet begun construction.
Enchem America is not abandoning a facility, as all site preparation was completed as part of the state's Select Tennessee Certified Sites program.
The site always generated interest due to its proximity to Memphis and Jackson.

With BlueOval City nearby and production beginning in 2027 alongside mass production in 2028, the Brownsville-Haywood County Economic Development Corporation suspects a new tenant will draw interest similar to before.
More details about the site's future will be discussed at the Brownsville-Haywood Economic Development Corporation board meeting on Oct. 8.
The Brownsville-Haywood County Economic Development Corporation executive expressed no concern regarding uncertainty in the evolving electric vehicle market.

Navigating Washington policies, international supply chains for rare earth metals and aluminum production, tariffs, and trade agreements makes it difficult for automakers to chart a course for the next 12 months, let alone five to 10 years.

facts: Global secondary battery electrolyte specialist Enchem announced on the 12th that the expansion plan for its key production facility in Georgia, USA, is progressing smoothly.
Enchem aims to achieve annual sales of approximately KRW 3.6 trillion by 2025, when major bases in the US, China, and Europe will be expanded.
The projected 2025 sales represent about a 30-fold increase compared to total sales in 2020 and about seven times higher than the previous year.
To achieve its sales targets, Enchem plans to supply products directly not only to global cell makers but also to global original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Enchem currently owns its own electrolyte production facilities in the US (Georgia), China (Huzhou and Zaozhuang), and Europe (Poland).
Enchem is actively pursuing expansion work in line with local customer needs and production increase plans.
The company intends to build stronger partnerships with global top-tier customers through its expansion efforts.

Enchem is focusing on expanding production capacity in the North American market because the US is expected to lead the external growth of the global secondary battery materials market over the next decade.
Due to the US Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), the secondary battery material supply chain, previously dominated by China, is rapidly being reorganized around the US.
Enchem plans to expand its business rapidly with the US as the center.
Enchem plans to expand its Georgia plant's production capacity from 20,000 tons to 40,000 tons within this year.
Through additional expansions, Enchem aims to increase its Georgia plant's production capacity to a total of 200,000 tons by the end of 2024.
In 2025, Enchem plans to establish a 100,000-ton production facility in Texas.
By 2025, Enchem will have a total electrolyte production capacity of 300,000 tons in the US alone.
With proactive expansion and market share growth, Enchem expects to generate approximately KRW 1.8 trillion in sales in North America alone by 2025.
An Enchem official stated that the company is focusing all efforts on expanding global production capacity as well as expanding its customer base.

An Enchem official stated that the company is actively engaged in discussions not only with top-tier cell makers but also with the world's number one electric vehicle company and global OEMs such as General Motors (GM).
An Enchem official stated that from 2025, when the use of Chinese minerals will be virtually banned in North America, Enchem will become the biggest beneficiary of the IRA.

Based on an annual production capacity of 300,000 tons, Enchem expects to supply about 10 out of approximately 11 cell makers in the North American market, including K-battery companies, demonstrating an overwhelming market share.
Enchem expects a significant restructuring of its sales structure.
Previously, more than 50% of Enchem's sales were generated from China, which drove the company's quantitative growth.
From 2025, the US and Europe are expected to account for 65% of Enchem's total sales, amounting to approximately KRW 2.25 trillion.
Enchem estimates that sales of about KRW 1.2 trillion will be generated from 2025 when its expansion plan underway in the Chinese market is completed.
Enchem currently has a production capacity of about 120,000 tons, including a third plant in Zhangjiagang, China, which will be completed within this year.
source_title: Enchem "Targeting North American Market to Achieve 3.6 Trillion KRW Sales by 2025" - The Asia Business Daily

facts: Michael Kofoed, an assistant professor of economics at The University of Tennessee Knoxville, stated that the relationship between South Korea and the U.S. will never be the same following recent events.

South Korea has opened an investigation into whether human rights violations were committed during a Sept. 4 raid at LG Energy Solution's joint battery plant with Hyundai Motor in Georgia that detained 475 workers, more than 300 of whom were South Korean nationals.
Following the raid, Korean employees at LG Energy Solution's Spring Hill, Tenn., operation left the U.S., citing growing concerns over visa status and legal uncertainty.
LG Energy Solution told the Tennessean that its plant is still operating as usual despite reports of departing employees.
An LG Energy Solutions spokesperson stated the company remains committed to its U.S. projects, will continue necessary investments and business, and noted the U.S. is an important market.
LG Energy Solution plans to locate half of its total global battery cell-making capacity in North America in the mid- to long-term.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung stated at a Sept. 11 news conference that as things stand, Korean businesses will hesitate to make direct investments in the United States.

Many Georgia detainees held Electronic System for Travel Authorization visa waivers or B-1 temporary business traveler visas, which prohibit foreign nationals from working in construction or equipment installation in the U.S.
More than 20 South Korean companies have collectively invested nearly $6 billion in Tennessee as of Oct. 2024, according to the Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development.

Michael Kofoed noted that injecting fear and uncertainty into the business environment effectively raises the cost of doing business in the United States, similar to raising taxes on companies like LG.
U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem acknowledged the trade policy impact but stated the deportations signal policy consistency that could ultimately bolster investor confidence.
Much of the South Korean investment in Tennessee revolves around auto industry second-tier suppliers supporting electric vehicle production for local OEMs like Ford and General Motors.

Wisarut Suwanprasert, an Associate Professor of Economics at Middle Tennessee State University, stated that while Korean investment might not be noticed by household consumers, it has created thousands of jobs.
Wisarut Suwanprasert believes the current diplomatic issue is minor and suggested the Trump administration should provide a formal apology to make things smoother.
Michael Kofoed does not anticipate a U.S. apology nor suggest South Korea would try to restore the bond between partners given the current trajectory.
Kofoed expects less willingness from foreign partners to do foreign direct investment in the U.S. due to the current trajectory.

Amid shifting global dynamics and U.S. trade and immigration policies under President Donald Trump, countries like South Korea and Japan have increasingly explored economic ties with other major powers including China.
Kofoed stated that because the U.S. is receding as a business partner, countries like South Korea are being drawn into the Chinese sphere of influence.

Kristi Noem argued that tough actions mean there is no uncertainty about the Trump administration's policies and claimed investors prefer a stable, rule-bound environment where immigration laws are enforced evenly to protect American jobs long-term.
Kofoed expects long-term trust problems, describing the situation as a conflict between trusting a partner and having that trust betrayed regarding investment plans.
Ultium Cells is a joint venture between Korean-owned LG Energy Solutions and General Motors that manufactures low-cost electric vehicle battery cells known as lithium iron phosphate (LFP).
Ultium Cells announced a $2.3 billion investment in July, located onsite at the GM manufacturing plant in Spring Hill.

LG Chem is a global leading chemical company specializing in petrochemicals, advanced materials and life sciences, and is the parent company of LG Energy Solutions following a diversification in December 2020.
In Nov. 2022, LG Chem announced it would invest roughly $3.2 billion to build a cathode materials plant for electric vehicle batteries in Clarksville.
Dongwha Electrolyte USA is a subsidiary of Dongwha Enterprise Company that specializes in the production of electrolytes used in lithium batteries.
In 2023, Dongwha Electrolyte USA announced a $70 million investment into creating a Clarksville production facility.
Enchem America invested $152.2 million in June 2023 to create an electrolyte manufacturing site for electric batteries in Brownsville.
Enchem America is a subsidiary of Enchem Company, and its Tennessee operations support its U.S. headquarters located in Commerce, Georgia.
Hanon Systems specializes in ventilation and air conditioning and announced a $170 million expansion in August 2023 to create a production facility in Huntington Park.
Hanon Systems' Tennessee operations supply thermal solutions for automakers across North America.
In January 2024, Unitech announced it would invest roughly $30 million to debut its first U.S. facility in Tennessee.
Unitech specializes in thermal adhesive and battery compression pad production for electric vehicle manufacturers across the Southeast, and also manufactures industrial adhesives and sealers.
To support operations at Ford's BlueOval City, the Aluko Group announced a $36.3 million facility to produce aluminum battery casings for the automaker in December 2024.
The Aluko Group consists of Aluko, Hyundai Aluminum, Gogang Aluminum, Alutec, KPTU, Hyundai Aluminum Vina and Alutec Vina.
source_title: Tennessee-South Korea trade ties strained by auto plant deportations

facts: Enchem America, Inc. is a South Korean battery component maker.
Enchem America backed out of plans to build a $152.5 million electrolyte manufacturing facility in Haywood County.
The withdrawn project would have brought 190 jobs to Haywood County.
Enchem America announced its plan to build at the “shovel ready” I-40 Advantage Industrial Park in Brownsville in 2023.
The site is roughly 16 miles from BlueOval City, an electric vehicle manufacturing campus.
BlueOval City is slated to produce Ford Motor Company’s new all-electric pickup truck and electric vehicle batteries.
BlueOval City will be produced in partnership with SK On.
The BlueOval City campus is expected to serve as manufacturing space for BlueOval SK, Ford, and electric vehicle component suppliers.
BlueOval City is expected to inject jobs and residents into the surrounding rural West Tennessee communities.
The Enchem facility would have produced electrolytes used for electric vehicle batteries.
The facility was expected to magnify Enchem’s ability to supply and support its electric vehicle partners in the United States.
Enchem decided to withdraw from the project in mid-September 2025.

The withdrawal followed “careful consideration and an extensive review of (their) North American business strategy,” according to Brownsville-Haywood County Economic Development Corporation Executive Director Aaron Stewart.
Stewart communicated the withdrawal to the corporation’s board members and West Tennessee mayors on Sept. 26 via email.
The decision was “primarily driven by a recent shift in demand from (their) key North American customers,” according to the company.
Enchem America did not respond to a request for comment.
Ford Motor Company announced it will delay mass production of the next-generation pickup truck at BlueOval City until 2028.
Ford still plans to start producing prototypes of the truck at BlueOval City in 2027.
The Brownsville-Haywood Economic Development Corporation is working to terminate the state incentives Enchem was set to receive for the project.
Enchem received a $2 million recruitment grant in 2023, according to a state database.
Stewart will work with the state and the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) to put the site back on the market.
The TVA has contributed grant funds to prepare the industrial park for future development.
Enchem completed preliminary site grading but had not yet begun construction.
Aaron Stewart stated that Enchem is not abandoning a facility.
The site will take time to get back on the market, but it “always generated interest because of its proximity to Memphis and Jackson.”
All site preparation was completed as part of the state’s Select Tennessee Certified Sites program.
Stewart suspects a new tenant will draw interest due to BlueOval City’s production beginning in 2027 and mass production in 2028.
More details about the site’s future will be discussed at the Brownsville-Haywood Economic Development Corporation board meeting on Oct. 8.
Stewart is not concerned by uncertainty in the electric vehicle market, which is still evolving.
Stewart compared navigating the automotive industry to looking into a crystal ball.

Navigating policies from Washington, international supply chains for rare earth metals and aluminum production, tariffs, and trade agreements makes it difficult for automakers to chart a course for the next 12 months, let alone five to 10 years.
Stewart highlighted a $5.6 billion investment in the BlueOval City campus.
The BlueOval City campus has nearly 11 million square feet under a roof.
Machinery and equipment at BlueOval City is still being installed and calibrated, albeit at a slower pace.
source_title: EV Battery Component Company Backs Out Of Plans For $152M Haywood County Facility | Tennessee Conservative

facts: Lithium-ion batteries control more than 90% of the global grid battery storage market according to some estimates.
The lithium-ion supply chain is becoming constrained due to insufficient raw material production to meet imminent demand from EVs and renewable energy storage installations.
A comparison between adoption projections and miner investments shows that lithium-ion’s supply chain will soon be very constrained.
This shortage will become more severe as governments around the world pass legislation accelerating the transition to EVs and renewables.
Lithium is unlikely to be replaced for EVs because its position on the periodic table guarantees it will remain the king of energy density.
Renewable energy storage primarily requires a low lifetime cost rather than high energy density.
Lithium-ion’s safety risks make it a poor fit for markets seeking to place massive battery packs in people’s homes and businesses.
Non-lithium batteries are far more likely to succeed in renewable energy storage markets.
Many companies have tried building new energy storage batteries over the past decades, but none have really succeeded.
Technologies that made it out of the lab were eroded by rapidly decreasing lithium-ion manufacturing costs before they could scale up.
Any alternative to lithium-ion batteries needs to adopt standard manufacturing processes to allow for rapid and low-cost scale-up.
Zinc-ion batteries are the only non-lithium technology that can adopt lithium-ion’s manufacturing process to make an attractive solution for renewable energy storage.
Salient Energy’s zinc-ion battery cell functions using intercalation, with zinc ions reacting at both electrodes and traveling through a water-based electrolyte.
During discharge, zinc metal at the anode dissolves into the electrolyte as zinc ions while zinc ions are absorbed into the cathode; this process reverses during charge.
Lithium-ion batteries use intercalation where lithium reacts at both the anode and cathode through a liquid electrolyte.
In lithium-ion batteries, the electrolyte only serves as a conduit between electrodes rather than storing large amounts of ions, allowing for compact designs.
Most other battery chemistries do not use intercalation and depend on each electrode reacting with the electrolyte, requiring large quantities of electrolyte to store reactants.
Lithium-ion electrodes can be built with relatively thin coatings of active material totaling less than 0.1 millimeters in thickness.
Lead-acid batteries have electrodes multiple millimeters thick, whereas lithium-ion's thin coatings allow for higher energy efficiency and better performance in high-power applications.

Lithium-ion manufacturing involves applying thin coatings to metal substrates, placing a thin separator between electrodes, winding them together, injecting electrolyte, sealing the cell, and performing formation cycling.
Formation cycling causes reactions within the cell that protect its longevity.
A novel battery chemistry must store large amounts of energy in a small amount of active material at both electrodes to be compatible with lithium-ion manufacturing processes.
Non-active components like metal substrates and separators represent about one-third of a lithium-ion cell’s material costs.
Using excess electrolyte requires thicker separators and limits the amount of energy that can be stored in a cell container of a given size.
Very few battery chemistries meet the requirements for thin electrodes and minimal electrolyte while also meeting market cost and performance demands.
Zinc-ion batteries meet all requirements for lithium-ion compatibility by using intercalation to limit the electrolyte's function to an ion conduit.
Active materials in zinc-ion batteries are very energy dense, allowing for sufficiently high energy storage even in thin electrodes.
Zinc-ion batteries can improve on lithium-ion manufacturing because they do not require the highly controlled atmosphere needed for lithium’s violent reactivity with water.
As a water-based battery, zinc-ion avoids the cost and complexity constraints associated with lithium's production steps.
Zinc-ion batteries do not require formation cycling at the end of life, allowing them to move more quickly from manufacturing lines to customers.
The ability to use lithium-ion manufacturing enables the rapid and inexpensive scale-up of zinc-ion battery production.
Zinc-ion’s key short-term differentiators from lithium-ion are safety and supply chain security.
Zinc-ion’s intrinsic safety comes from using water as an electrolyte, enabling it to gain traction in markets where lithium-ion adoption has been limited due to safety concerns.
Dense urban centers with fire regulations preventing lithium-ion adoption are an example market for zinc-ion batteries.
Zinc-ion can be built with materials that are produced in abundance in North America.
Customers will seek to develop domestic supply chains as energy storage plays an increasingly important role in critical infrastructure.
The need for domestic supply chains will become more acute if U.S.-China relations worsen or if government subsidies enforce strong “Buy American” requirements.
As zinc-ion production ramps up and takes advantage of economies of scale, zinc-ion batteries will become a lower-cost alternative to lithium-ion.
Paired with long service life, zinc-ion batteries will offer a far lower cost of storage than can be achieved with lithium-ion today.
Zinc-ion has demonstrated substantial improvements in supply chain security and safety compared to lithium-ion.
Salient Energy is a battery energy storage technology company based in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada.
Ryan Brown serves as the co-founder and CEO of Salient Energy.
source_title: Zinc-ion Batteries Are a Scalable Alternative to Lithium-ion
