facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to purchase tens of thousands of Boston Dynamics robots.
Hyundai Motor Group will build a dedicated robotics factory capable of producing 30,000 humanoid Atlas units annually by 2028.
The initiative is backed by a $26 billion U.S. investment plan that includes the new robotics factory.
This commitment represents the largest known order for humanoid robots by any single manufacturer.
Boston Dynamics unveiled the production-ready version of its electric Atlas humanoid at CES 2026 in January.
The final enterprise Atlas model can reach up to 7.5 feet tall, lift 110 pounds, and operate in temperatures ranging from minus 4 to 104 degrees Fahrenheit.
Boston Dynamics CEO Robert Playter called the Atlas robot “the best robot we have ever built.”
All Atlas deployments for 2026 are fully committed, with initial fleets shipping to Hyundai’s Robotics Metaplant Application Center and to Google DeepMind.
Google DeepMind is integrating its Gemini Robotics AI foundation models into the Atlas platform.
Hyundai plans to begin using Atlas robots in its automotive plants by 2028, starting with parts sequencing tasks at its $7.6 billion Metaplant America facility outside Savannah, Georgia.
By 2030, the Atlas robots are expected to handle component assembly, repetitive motions, heavy loads, and other complex operations.
Hyundai Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang stated that “physical AI and humanoid robots will transform our business landscape to the next level.”
Robert Playter noted that Hyundai Motor Group will become Boston Dynamics’ biggest customer.
Boston Dynamics’ Spot quadruped robots are already deployed for industrial inspection and predictive maintenance at Hyundai facilities.
Spot units are currently performing exterior quality inspections in the Metaplant America’s weld shop.
Hyundai Mobis announced a strategic collaboration framework with Boston Dynamics to supply actuators for the Atlas robot.
Actuators represent more than 60 percent of a humanoid robot’s material cost, making them the single most expensive subsystem.
Hyundai Mobis plans to build a mass-production system for actuators and gradually expand its robotics portfolio to include hand grippers, sensors, controllers, and battery packs.
Boston Dynamics’ Zack Jackowski said the collaboration “allows us to access the well-established cost structures and scale potential of the automotive industry.”
Hyundai acquired Boston Dynamics from SoftBank in 2021 for $1.1 billion.
Competitors such as Figure AI, Apptronik, and Tesla’s Optimus program have not disclosed production capacity targets on a similar scale.
Boston Dynamics’ existing commercial customers include Gap Inc., InBev, and South Korean steelmaker POSCO.
Goldman Sachs projects the humanoid robotics market will exceed $38 billion by 2035.
Atlas pricing has not been disclosed, while Mercedes-Benz’s Apollo humanoid robots from Apptronik cost less than $100,000 each.
The first large-scale Atlas production lines could begin operating by 2028.
source_title: Hyundai Targets 30,000 Atlas Robots Per Year as Boston Dynamics Shifts From Prototype to Mass Production — The Machine Herald

facts: Honda is preparing to create four new plants over the next four years to establish a complete electric vehicle (EV) supply chain at its Alliston location.
This expansion builds upon manufacturing first established at the site in 1986, which has developed into one of the area’s largest private-sector employers.
Honda intends to have the capacity to build 240,000 electric vehicles every year in Alliston by 2028.
Currently, Honda of Canada Manufacturing’s capacity is about 400,000 vehicles per year at its two existing manufacturing facilities combined.
The multibillion-dollar investment is being described as the largest in Canada’s manufacturing history.
Honda anticipates boosting its workforce by about 1,000 people to a total of 5,200.
Parts manufacturers are expected to seek supply agreements with Honda, creating a ripple effect reliant upon goods, technology, and automation services and equipment from other sources.
Simcoe County has a thriving manufacturing sector, which is buoyed by the automotive sector.
According to the 2021 census, 29,610 people in Simcoe County (including Barrie and Orillia) worked in manufacturing, representing 10.7 percent of the area’s total workforce of 275,620 in 2021.
About one-third (or 10,000) of those manufacturing jobs are related to the automotive sector, according to the County of Simcoe.
Honda will build four plants in Ontario including a new Honda EV plant and a new stand-alone EV battery plant in Alliston.
Honda’s “EV value chain” is to also include the creation of a cathode active material and precursor (CAM/pCAM) processing plant and a separator plant through two joint venture partnerships.
Current production of combustion vehicles is expected to continue in Alliston with intentions to train the current workforce to meet the needs of EV manufacturing.

Honda Canada spokesperson Ken Chiu explained that the company plans to up-skill associates for new technologies and processes related to roles such as IPU (Intelligent Power Unit) sub-assembly and handling the installation of the IPU in the vehicle.

Chiu added that the transition to producing EVs will create demand for new skills, and Honda is planning training needed for BEV development and manufacturing roles to ensure associates are supported and prepared.
Adam Kallio, manager of economic development for the County of Simcoe, expects a long-term impact on the area’s economy and job creation.

Kallio stated that the announcement is the biggest and most impactful automotive announcement in Canadian history and will have strong positive economic and job impacts across Simcoe County for decades to come.
Kallio noted that many of the new jobs will still be in motor vehicle and parts manufacturing, with future opportunities and trends progressing in electric vehicles across Canada and globally.

New in-demand positions will include engineering disciplines such as electrical, chemical, industrial, and software development related to battery management, braking systems, acceleration, and entertainment systems.
Ongoing work will involve robotics, artificial intelligence (A.I.), self-driving, and other automated functions.
Honda Canada may expand upon its long-standing relationship with Georgian College for education and training support during electrification and future EV development.
Lakehead University, which has a campus in Orillia, is also anticipated to be involved in potential collaborations.
Georgian College electrical engineering professor John McCluskey described the college’s relationship with Honda as “complex,” involving many of the local school’s programs.
McCluskey stated that the investment reflects confidence in the area’s skilled labor and access to infrastructure and education facilities.

McCluskey said Georgian College’s immediate benefit will be being the No. 1 supplier of their workforce and educating their workforce, citing already set-up relationships, technology, and new buildings and infrastructure to accommodate growth.

McCluskey pointed out that much of the required knowledge still needs to be developed for lithium-ion batteries, related assembly processes, battery management systems involving hardware and software, and testing.
Georgian College regularly reviews its programs and courses to ensure they reflect community needs, with anticipation of Honda’s EV transition being part of program advisory committee discussions.
The college will also take into consideration the needs of Honda’s suppliers when updating programs.

McCluskey emphasized that Georgian will benefit across the board not just with Honda but also with tier one and suppliers, covering safety, compliance, logistics, operations, project management, business, and environmental aspects as Honda strives to become zero emissions.
source_title: Major local investment at Honda will jolt area's EV manufacturing - Barrie News

facts: Honda's first modern electric vehicle is the 2024 Prologue midsize SUV, arriving in dealerships in spring 2024.
The Prologue shares many systems with the Chevrolet Blazer EV, including batteries, motors, and platform.
General Motors engineered the shared batteries, motors, and platform systems for the Prologue and Blazer.

GM has struggled to build vehicles using these systems; the Blazer EV was withdrawn from sale in early January due to owner complaints about charging issues alongside navigation, entertainment, and other features.
Blazer EV sales remain on hold as of February 16.
Honda engineers monitored quality for the Prologue during development.
The Acura ZDX EV will be built by GM alongside the Cadillac Lyriq in another plant.
Chief engineer John Hwang confirmed Honda's standard quality gate system is in place for Prologue development and production.
Honda engineers and designers were involved from the start of the three-year program that led to the Prologue and Blazer.
The Prologue exterior shares no body panels with the Blazer, with visual differentiation created by varying proportions within an inch in most dimensions.
Honda calls the Prologue design "neo-rugged," evoking a sporty wagon rather than traditional Honda SUV styling.
The Prologue interior features unique controls, displays, colors, materials, and brand-correct narrow horizontal air vents instead of Chevrolet's circular vents.
Competitors include the Chevrolet Blazer EV, Ford Mustang Mach-E, Hyundai Ioniq 5, Kia EV6, Nissan Ariya, Polestar 3, Tesla Y, Toyota BZ4X, Volvo XC40 Recharge, and VW ID4.
The Prologue is 192 inches long (2.9 inches longer than the Honda Passport), has a roof 64.7 inches off the ground (7.5 inches lower than the Passport), and is 0.5 inches narrower.
The Elite model features 21-inch tires and an unrevealed curb weight.
Multilink front and rear suspensions absorb bumps and maintain stability in quick maneuvers.

Controls include an 11.3-inch touch screen, dials for temperature and volume, physical switches for climate controls, and buttons for parking brake, lane keeping, and sport mode on the lower instrument panel.
Honda engineers tuned the suspension and steering for quick, direct steering and a smooth ride.
The Elite model includes a hotel-style key card and a sport mode that tweaks steering, pedal response, and ambient lighting.
The Prologue will offer either front-wheel drive (212 hp, 236 lb-ft torque from a single front motor) or all-wheel drive (288 hp, 333 lb-ft torque from dual motors).
Honda hopes both FWD and AWD models will be available when sales begin, though only AWD models were available for testing at the time of writing.
The EPA rates the standard 85 kWh battery at 296 miles range for FWD models and 281 or 273 miles for AWD models depending on trim.
Honda did not provide charging speed details but noted Level 2 240-volt home chargers will be included for overnight charging.
The battery supports up to 150 kW DC fast charging, with Honda citing the ability to add 65 miles of range in 10 minutes under ideal circumstances.
Honda will offer adapters to use Tesla chargers sometime this year.
Honda is participating in a seven-automaker program to add thousands of new DC fast chargers along major highway routes.

The Prologue will initially be sold in 18 states following California emission standards: California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Maryland, Maine, Massachusetts, Minnesota, Nevada, New Mexico, New York, New Jersey, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Vermont, Virginia, and Washington.
Prices start at $47,400 for the FWD EX trim and top out at $57,900 for the AWD Elite trim, excluding a $1,395 destination charge.

The Prologue offers features including a panoramic opening sunroof, heated front seats, Bose audio, wireless device charging, head-up display, wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto, power tailgate, four USB-C ports, Google built-in assistant, and key fob/card access.
Honda expects the Prologue to qualify for the $7,500 federal tax credit along with state EV incentives.
Trim levels and prices include: EX FWD ($47,400), EX AWD ($50,400), Touring FWD ($51,700), Touring AWD ($54,700), and Elite AWD ($57,900).
The Prologue includes standard Apple CarPlay and Android Auto with voice recognition and a Google-powered app store.
GM offers only Google built-in in its EVs that share the same hardware as the Prologue.
Google Maps calculates optimal charging stops in real-time, and the system preconditions the battery for quickest and most efficient charging stops.
The Prologue is assembled in Ramos Arizpe, Mexico.
source_title: 2024 Honda Prologue EV combines Honda look and feel with GM Ultium batteries and motors - AOL

facts: Georgia sustained economic development momentum during the first half of Fiscal Year 2023 (July 1 through December 31, 2022).
GDEcD’s Global Commerce division supported 218 locations or expansions expected to create approximately 17,500 new jobs and more than $13 billion in investment for the state.
Both the number of locations and total investment amount increased over the same period last year.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the sustained momentum through economic development projects in key industries.
Areas outside the 10-county metro Atlanta region accounted for 85 percent of jobs created and 92 percent of investments from economic development projects during the period.
Advanced manufacturing, automotive, aerospace, and food processing accounted for nearly 75 percent of new jobs and 84 percent of investments.
Planned investments resulting from these projects increased by more than 38 percent compared to the same time last year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted Georgia Quick Start job training and GRAD certified sites as proven solutions for accelerated project timelines.
The automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment, after advanced manufacturing.
In October, state and local officials joined company representatives for the groundbreaking of the new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) EV facility.
Korean Ambassador to the U.S. Tae-yong Cho attended the HMGMA groundbreaking.
The HMGMA EV facility will support more than 8,100 direct jobs in Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham Counties.
Three off-site HMGMA suppliers announced locations following HMGMA’s groundbreaking.
These supplier additions swiftly surpassed the initial $1 billion in investment predicted to come from such additions.
International companies have been valued partners in creating a closed-loop electric mobility supply chain and accounted for 23 percent of state projects since July 1, 2022.
SK On and Hyundai Motor Group formed a partnership in Bartow County.
Next-generation developer FREYR Battery announced a Coweta County facility.
Both the SK On/Hyundai and FREYR Battery projects are in the top five largest projects by investment in the state’s history.
Battery recyclers Sung-Il Hitech and Denkai America joined Georgia’s growing electric mobility ecosystem.
The aerospace and defense industries created more than 1,500 new jobs.
Archer Aviation Inc. announced a facility near airports to advance sustainable urban air mobility.
Anduril Industries announced a facility near airports for defense technology.
Both Archer Aviation and Anduril will benefit from nearby top-ranked technical colleges and four-year universities.
Advanced manufacturing is a cornerstone industry sector in Georgia’s middle and coastal regions.
YKK AP announced a major investment or expansion in Macon.
Beretta Holding S.A. announced a new facility in Bryan County through its subsidiary Norma Precision Inc.
The advanced manufacturing sector has announced more than 7,700 new jobs in the state since July 1, 2022.
Germany-based Haering Precision celebrated the grand opening of its Lavonia facility in November.
Haering Precision's opening event included a “Future Day” for approximately 400 local high school students and community members.
Companies in the food processing industry announced more than $695 million in investments, largely through expansions.
King’s Hawaiian announced another expansion of its Oakwood facility, marking its second expansion in less than a year.
GDEcD Deputy Commissioner for Global Commerce Kristi Brigman emphasized workforce challenges and the Georgia Quick Start program for upskilling Georgians.
Georgia Tech hosted a groundbreaking for its Science Square purpose-built district in August.
The Rowen Foundation’s 2,000-acre science and learning campus broke ground in early December.
Athena Studios announced it will add an additional 350,000 square feet to its Athens facility.
Athena Studios' expansion includes a structure dedicated to training the local workforce through a partnership with the University of Georgia and Georgia Film Academy.
BlueStar Studios announced more than 600,000 square feet of new stage space at the former Fort Gillem outside of Atlanta.
BlueStar Studios will also add an additional 45,000 square feet dedicated to technical resources and amenities, including refurbished buildings preserving historic architecture.

links.context: Details of state-announced economic development projects

source_title: GA sustains momentum for economic development

facts: Right-to-work laws are clustered mainly in the conservative Southeast and up through the nation’s midsection, used for decades to lure automakers and manufacturers with cheap, compliant labor.

The same right-to-work labor pool is now attracting electric vehicle stakeholders, putting red state officials in the awkward position of leading the decarbonization movement while continuing to rail against it.
Georgia was among the first wave of states to adopt anti-union, right-to-work legislation shortly after World War II.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) continues to pitch its anti-union status to the auto industry.
Only 4.7% of workers in the private sector in Georgia are unionized, which is well below the national average according to GDEcD.
GDEcD positions Georgia as a hub for the electric transportation industry under an elaborately detailed section titled “Electric Mobility Manufacturing.”
Since 2018, 35 EV-related projects have contributed $23 billion in investments in Georgia, with GDEcD highlighting Hyundai Motor Group’s recent investments.
A new $4.3 billion joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and electric vehicle battery maker LG Energy Solution is the latest clean tech investment in Georgia.

GDEcD leads the Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA), which is dedicated to supporting the growth of the entire electric mobility industry, fostering innovation, creating a business-friendly environment, and promoting favorable public policy.

Georgia is among 25 or Republican-dominated states that pitched a lawsuit last January against new federal Department of Labor rules supporting pension fund managers who ascribe to ESG (environment, social, governance) investing principles.

The anti-ESG movement focuses on protecting fossil energy stakeholders from having to compete in a low-carbon economy and uses stirring up public opinion against the “woke mob” as part of its strategy.
Georgia State Attorney General Christopher M. Carr joined the anti-ESG lawsuit on behalf of Georgia.
Republican Georgia Governor Brian Kemp signed onto a joint anti-ESG statement with 18 other governors dated March 16, railing against ESG-friendly rules.

The joint letter states that the proliferation of ESG is a direct threat to the American economy, individual economic freedom, and our way of life, putting investment decisions in the hands of the woke mob to bypass the ballot box and inject political ideology into investment decisions, corporate governance, and the everyday economy.
GM is pumping $1 billion or more into Phase II of its “Ultium CAM” joint venture with Korean firm POSCO Future M to onshore the battery materials part of its EV supply chain.
The Ultium CAM joint venture is majority owned by POSCO and aims to support GM’s Ultium lithium-ion EV battery platform.

CAM stands for Cathode Active Materials, a combination of lithium and other raw materials that go into lithium-ion EV batteries; Phase II integrates pCAM, referring to the engineering of precursor materials for producing CAM.

With CAM production concentrated mainly in Asia, GM and other US automakers are on the prowl for opportunities to shore up their North American supply chains, which the Phase II POSCO hookup will accomplish.
The Ultium CAM joint venture will support production of approximately 360,000 Chevrolet, Cadillac, GMC, Buick and BrightDrop vehicles annually in the 2025-2030 timeframe in North America.

The announcement left out the location of the new integrated CAM/pCAM processing facility; GM has dozens of manufacturing facilities around the US including two EV battery cell factories in Michigan, but Georgia has come up empty-handed so far.
GM also has battery cell plants in Ohio and Tennessee.

The POSCO partnership angle hints that GM is eyeballing solid-state EV battery technology; GM announced plans for a joint venture with POSCO Chemical in 2021 to launch Phase I of the Ultium CAM project and tapped POSCO as one of its six strategic supply chain partners last fall.
GM China Science Lab in Shanghai received the 2022 R&D 100 Award for its new solid-state battery technology, which GM has described as “game-changing.”
The high-power bipolar solid-state battery technology offers excellent thermal stability and abuse tolerance compared with conventional liquid electrolyte.
Its unique bipolar stacking and proprietary interfacial ionic booster greatly enhance all-climate serviceability and give it a simplified configuration.

Dr. Helen Liu, manager of the Battery Lab Group at GM China Science Lab, stated that the highly safe battery innovation offers huge potential applications for promoting electrification inside and outside the automotive industry.

Governor Kemp launched the Electrification and Innovation Alliance in 2021; the alliance notes that advancements in battery technology, flying vehicles, autonomous driving and unmanned aircraft systems are driving the electric mobility industry.

Governor Kemp agreed in a 2021 press release announcing the launch of EMIA that the alliance will ensure Georgia is positioned to continue leading the nation in the rapidly growing electric mobility industry.
source_title: Be Careful What You Wish For, Electric Vehicle Edition

facts: Georgia-headquartered Flock Safety is opening a new manufacturing facility in Smyrna.
The new facility will be 97,000 square feet.
The project represents an investment of approximately $10 million.
The facility will ultimately create an estimated 210 new jobs over the next three years.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced the new Flock Safety manufacturing facility.
Over 300 law enforcement agencies and 100 businesses in Georgia have deployed Flock Safety’s technology to solve and reduce crime.
Flock Safety launched its Drone as First Responder system last year upon acquiring Aerodome, a leader in rapid response drones for public safety.
The new Smyrna facility is dedicated to the production of advanced public safety tools, including Drone as First Responder technology.
The facility will also refurbish damaged devices and assemble solar panels.
Flock Safety’s products can read license plates at up to 100 miles per hour and in heavy traffic.
Flock Safety’s products can be paired with gunshot detection technology.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated, “In Georgia, we foster a business environment where innovative companies can get off the ground and grow into leaders in their industry while operating in safe communities.”

Flock Safety Founder and CEO Garrett Langley stated, “We feel fortunate to have our roots in Georgia, the state that is undisputedly the leader in the American aerospace industry, and are proud to invest further by creating hundreds of advanced manufacturing and aviation jobs locally.”
The drones produced will be made-in-America and NDAA-compliant.
source_title: Georgia Governor announces new Flock facility in Atlanta area - TheTrucker.com

facts: Honda Transmission Plant-Georgia (TMP-G) began production of five-speed automatic transmissions in 2006.
The plant added six-speed automatic transmissions to its production line in 2012.
The facility produced its 5-millionth automatic transmission, marking a major milestone.
The milestone was announced via a company press release on April 11.
source_title: Honda’s Georgia Transmission Plant rolls out 5-millionth transmission - Automotive Technology Insight | Forecasts | Industry News | Supply Chain

entity_name: General Motors and Honda Motor Co.
canonical_name: General Motors and Honda Motor Co.
facts: General Motors and Honda Motor Co. plan to co-develop a line of affordable electric vehicles.
The co-developed electric vehicles will focus on compact crossovers.
The co-development initiative is scheduled to start in 2027.
source_title: GM, Honda to jointly develop 'millions' of affordable EVs by 2027 - Automotive News

facts: Norfolk Southern serves as a trusted rail partner for Georgia manufacturers, delivering raw materials in and shipping finished goods out to markets worldwide.

The company connects with major Georgia manufacturing companies including Coca-Cola, Georgia-Pacific, Smurfit/WestRock, Fieldale Farms, Wayne-Sanderson Farms, Argos USA, Southern Chips, ADM, and Cargill.
NS rail operations move enough fertilizer to treat more than 15 million farms in Georgia.
NS moves enough tires to equip over 1.4 million vehicles, supporting Georgia drivers and auto manufacturers.
NS transports enough pulpboard to produce 38 billion egg cartons for Georgia's poultry industry.
NS ships enough cement to build 44,000 swimming pools, fueling construction projects from Atlanta to Augusta.
Atlanta serves as a key rail hub linking Georgia manufacturers to key Southeastern markets.
Savannah and Brunswick ports provide direct access to global trade, with NS rail lines ensuring goods flow seamlessly to overseas buyers.
NS operates over 1,000 miles of track statewide, allowing both multinational corporations and local businesses to scale.
NS rail operations in Georgia avoid over 930,400 metric tons of CO2 emissions annually.
This annual CO2 reduction is equivalent to taking more than 3.9 million trucks off the highway each year.
NS supports workforce development and economic growth initiatives for communities where it operates.
source_title: Atlanta Business Chronicle: Powering Georgia’s manufacturing from the ground up | Norfolk Southern

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Co. and LG Energy Solution Ltd. Joint Venture Battery Facility
facts: Several hundred workers at the electric vehicle battery joint venture between Hyundai Motor Co. and LG Energy Solution Ltd., currently under construction in Georgia, were detained in a major US authority raid.
The Thursday raid dealt a setback to the project, which is part of what would be the biggest investments in Georgia.
The incident illustrates the increasing crackdown by the Trump administration on immigrants and its impact on businesses.

Analysts and corporate executives in Seoul fear the US crackdown could disrupt other multibillion-dollar semiconductor and battery manufacturing projects, potentially hurting US ambitions to tighten its grip on global supply chains.
Nearly 500 employees, including LG Energy staff, subcontractors, and engineers from Hyundai Engineering, were taken into custody on Thursday morning by ICE and HSI agents.
The raid also involved the DEA and Georgia state troopers, with armed officers sealing off the site in Ellabell, Bryan County, west of Savannah, using helicopters and armored vehicles.
One eyewitness described the scene as "like a military operation."
The 50-50 joint venture between Hyundai and LG is considered one of Georgia's largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites.
The detained employees are alleged to have worked on the site without proper work permits, having entered the US under ESTA or short-term B1 business visas.
The $4.3 billion joint venture battery facility is among the largest projects in Georgia and is expected to create 8,500 jobs.
The facility is scheduled for completion later this year to supply battery cells to Hyundai’s nearby EV factory, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC (HMGMA).
To prepare the site for commercial operation, LG and its partners dispatched hundreds of engineers on short rotations, many using 90-day ESTA allowances.
Industry executives warned that the custody of the workers could disrupt the plant's commissioning and affect other Korean projects in the US.
Construction work on the battery joint venture facility was suspended following the raid.
Korean conglomerates including Samsung Electronics Co., SK Hynix Inc., Samsung SDI Co., and SK On Co. are heavily investing in US chip and battery facilities with partners like GM and Ford.
Several of these Korean-invested US manufacturing sites rely on rotating teams of Korean engineers.
An industry executive stated, "This puts Korean workers in a precarious situation."
Washington wants rapid onshoring of advanced manufacturing facilities but refuses to provide necessary visa allocations that would allow Korean specialists to transfer their know-how.
Industry officials said Seoul needs to secure a dedicated visa quota with Washington, similar to arrangements the US has with Australia and Singapore.
A corporate executive in Seoul stated, "We are investing trillions of won in the US, but getting no visa support."
The Korean government raised concerns over the detentions, calling the incident "regrettable."
Seoul's Ministry of Foreign Affairs stated that the economic activities of Korean companies investing in the US and the rights and interests of their nationals must not be unfairly violated.
A Hyundai official said the company is cooperating with US law enforcement and is committed to abiding by all labor and immigration regulations.
An executive at the Hyundai-LG battery joint venture said the company is cooperating fully with authorities regarding activity at the construction site and has paused construction to assist them.
Sources said the Korean consulate in Atlanta is forming a legal team to handle the matter.

links.context: Contact email for article authors Jin-Won Kim, Woo-Sub Kim, and Eui-Myung Park

source_title: S.Korea rattled by detention of hundreds of workers at Hyundai-LG battery JV in sweeping US raid - KED Global

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America & Hanwha Qcells Georgia Facilities
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Hanwha Qcells Georgia Plants
facts: Two workers were killed this week in separate workplace accidents at two of Georgia’s most high-profile manufacturing sites: the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America battery plant near Savannah and the Hanwha Qcells solar panel plant in Cartersville.
The deaths occurred within six weeks of the death of Ronald Adams, Sr. at the Stellantis Dundee Engine Complex in Michigan.
Adams, a 63-year-old worker, was killed in an accident on April 7 when an overhead gantry engaged, pinning him to a conveyor and causing fatal crushing injuries to his upper torso.
On Tuesday, May 20, a construction worker was killed at the HL-GA Battery facility, part of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America complex in Ellabell, Georgia.
The worker was fatally injured when an unsecured load fell from a forklift during loading operations.
The incident occurred shortly before 11:00 a.m.
As of the article's writing, the identity of the worker, who was employed by a subcontractor involved in the construction of the battery plant, has not been released.
The HL-GA Battery plant is not yet operational and is a key part of Hyundai’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
The HL-GA Battery plant is slated to supply batteries for Hyundai’s new electric vehicle assembly plant.
This is not the first fatality at the Hyundai Metaplant construction site; in March of this year, Sunbok You, a 67-year-old worker, was struck and dragged by a forklift, resulting in fatal injuries.
In April 2023, Victor Gamboa, a 43-year-old contractor, died after falling 60 feet when his safety line failed.
According to an OSHA investigation, which cited the contractors, the workers had not been provided with adequate safety gear.
On Monday, May 19, a fatality occurred at the Hanwha Qcells solar panel plant in Cartersville, Georgia.
Firefighters responded to a call at 7:15 p.m. and found Marion Jose Rugama, a 33-year-old resident of Norcross, dead on top of a tank.
It has not yet been established if Rugama was a direct employee of Hanwha Qcells or a contractor, but the use of contract labor is widespread at the facility.
According to local authorities, the oxygen level at the top of the tank was only 15 percent, far below the minimum required to sustain life.
A nitrogen leak is suspected as the cause, though an autopsy and toxicology screening are pending.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Ellabell, Georgia is the largest single economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Backed by Hyundai and its partners, including LG Energy Solution and SK On, the sprawling complex is expected to employ 8,500 workers by 2031.
The complex includes both vehicle assembly and battery manufacturing operations.
The facility is being constructed at breakneck speed, with the goal of making Georgia a hub for electric vehicle and battery production.
The Hanwha Qcells plant in Cartersville is part of a $2.5 billion investment by the South Korean clean energy giant.
Hanwha Qcells also operates a major facility in Dalton, Georgia.
Together, the two plants form the largest solar manufacturing operation in the Western Hemisphere.
There are plans to expand the Cartersville facility even further in 2025.
Neither the workforce at Hyundai Metaplant nor the Hanwha Qcells Cartersville facility are unionized.
A common arrangement in large-scale industrial construction, the use of contract labor allows companies to cut costs, evade responsibility and fragment the workforce.
Contract workers are often paid less, receive fewer benefits and have less job security than direct employees.
They are also less likely to receive adequate safety training.
According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, 5,486 workers died on the job nationwide in 2022, with transportation, construction and manufacturing consistently ranking among the deadliest industries.
In February 2024, OSHA reported an investigation of the Columbus, Georgia facility of Aludyne Columbus LLC, a prominent global manufacturer of auto parts with 30 production facilities worldwide.
OSHA’s findings were followed by proposed penalties of $182,344 and uncovered 22 serious violations.
OSHA found that Aludyne Columbus LLC failed to provide protective gear and enforce safety protocols where employees worked dangerously close to energized power lines without insulated tools.
Employees at the facility were exposed to airborne silica at levels up to 15 times higher than the permissible limit.
The workers lacked proper respiratory equipment, and the company failed to conduct fit testing for those exposed to silicosis hazards.

OSHA Atlanta-West Area Office Director Jeffery Stawowy said in a statement that Aludyne Columbus LLC’s failure to prioritize employee safety and health nearly cost a worker their life and allowed employees to be overexposed to silica well above the permissible exposure limit.
Workers at StarPlus Energy, a joint venture between Stellantis and Samsung SDI in Kokomo, Indiana, took to social media to expose dangerous working conditions inside the facility.

The workers said that there was no medical emergency response, no safety data sheets and no workers’ comp doctors in the facility and that any worker who is injured must climb four flights of stairs to seek assistance.

The International Workers Alliance of Rank-and-File Committees (IWA-RFC) has launched an independent investigation, led by rank-and-file workers, into the death of Ronald Adams, the Dundee Engine worker.

The specific details of his death have been shrouded in secrecy, as Stellantis, the UAW appatus and the Michigan Occupational Safety and Health Administration (MIOSHA) have refused to release any information about the circumstances of the accident or the official investigation into the causes of the fatality.

The campaign for an independent investigation into Adams’s death has resonated with workers across the auto industry and other industries, who have seen firsthand the catastrophic conditions created by speedup, understaffing and the relentless drive for profit.

The deaths in Georgia further highlight the urgent need for such independent oversight and for workers to organize collectively to demand safe working conditions, regardless of whether they are direct employees or subcontracted laborers.
source_title: 2 workers killed in separate fatal workplace accidents in Georgia - World Socialist Web Site

facts: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications to support the future of sustainable mobility, including developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $12.6 billion in new dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia.
The investment includes the $7.59 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) site in Bryan County, Ga., that will create more than 8,500 direct jobs.
The investment includes the $5 billion battery manufacturing facility joint venture with SK On in Bartow County, Ga., that will employ approximately 3,500 people.
Proximity to Georgia Tech was one of the important considerations in deciding the locations for these new facilities.
Kia Georgia’s West Point assembly plant began vehicle production in 2009 under the leadership of Honorary Chairman Mong-Koo Chung and Executive Chair Euisun Chung.
University System of Georgia Chancellor Sonny Perdue participated in the HMG-Georgia Tech MOU ceremony and was the Governor of Georgia at the time of the Kia Georgia plant’s opening.
A recent study by the Center for Automotive Research reveals that Hyundai Motor’s automotive operations contributed $20.1 billion and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy in 2021 alone.
Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.

Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that the partnership with Georgia Tech includes research and applications to support sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy, workforce development, and smart cities, and noted it was one of the reasons for choosing Georgia for EV investments.

José Muñoz, President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, said Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
José Muñoz stated that proximity to institutions like Georgia Tech was one of the many reasons Hyundai selected Georgia for its new EV manufacturing facility.
source_title: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group Sign MoU for Future Mobility Collaboration | MOTOR

facts: Hyundai Motor Company unveiled its roadmap to 2030 plan which includes an expansion for the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Ellabell.
The announcement comes two weeks after Homeland Security raided the Hyundai megasite, which the agency called the largest single-site enforcement operation in its history.
Phase 2 of the expansion is set to add production capacity of 200,000 units by 2028.
The expansion will bring total production capacity to 500,000 cars a year.
The expansion includes a $2.7 billion investment.
The expansion will create 3,000 new jobs.
Hyundai’s original contract with the State of Georgia stipulates it must create over 8,000 full-time jobs in the state by 2031 to receive promised billions of dollars in tax breaks.
Hyundai Motor aims to produce more than 80% of cars sold in the U.S. domestically by 2030.
The company made the announcement at its Investor Day.
CEO José Muñoz addressed the immigration raid, which halted construction at the HL-GA Battery Company.
HL-GA is owned jointly by Hyundai and LG, and is located on the same megasite as the EV factory.
In the raid, 475 workers were detained by ICE.
Around 300 of those detained were South Korean nationals, all but one of whom have been repatriated.
Roughly 175 non-South Korean workers remain detained at an ICE facility in Folkston.

CEO José Muñoz stated: “I want to restate our support to the workers that were involved. We’ve provided all the possible support to our colleagues from LG and their suppliers. But having said that, we obviously continue to see the US as the most important market outside of Korea to continue with the growth.”
The production announcement is only for the EV plant, not HL-GA Battery Company, though the battery supplier exists to support the EV plant.
The jobs of the people detained by ICE do not qualify as full-time, permanent positions that are contractually obligated to be created.
The new jobs announced today do qualify as full-time, permanent positions for contractual obligations.
CEO José Muñoz stated: “We need more capacity to be able to increase our localization. Our localization in North America is one of the lowest that we have globally.”

CEO José Muñoz stated: “We’re very happy to have announced the additional 200,000 unit capacity, in total $2.7 billion of additional investment which is going to give more confidence to the State of Georgia and our partners while all this visa and immigration issues get resolved immediately.”

Savannah Mayor Van Johnson released a statement saying: “this project will bring new opportunities for our workforce, bolster our infrastructure, and strengthen Savannah’s position as a hub for advanced manufacturing.”
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp released a statement to X following the announcement.
source_title: Hyundai to invest $2.7 billion in Georgia plant expansion after immigration raid

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Institute of Technology announced an expansion of their collaboration on April 29, 2026, to advance hydrogen-powered transportation, deepen applied research and education, and accelerate zero-emissions vehicles in Georgia.
The multi-year partnership includes Hyundai Motor North America donating four NEXO fuel cell electric SUVs exclusively for campus use.
Hyundai Motor Group is donating a hydrogen electrolyzer project that will be installed at Georgia Tech’s North Avenue Research Area (NARA).
The donated vehicles and infrastructure will support campus operations and interdisciplinary research focusing on engineering, sustainability, energy systems, and public policy.
Faculty and students will study the real-world performance of hydrogen technology, infrastructure requirements for large-scale deployment, and environmental impacts of hydrogen energy systems.
Insights from the initiative aim to inform and accelerate the widespread use of hydrogen technology in campuses, fleets, cities, and freight corridors.
The partnership also aims to advance innovation in autonomous driving, electric vehicle batteries, charging infrastructure, materials science, and cybersecurity.

Ken Ramírez serves as Executive Vice President and Head of Global Energy and Hydrogen Business at Hyundai Motor Group, is a Georgia Tech alumnus (class of 1991), and currently sits on the Georgia Tech Advisory Board.
Randy Parker is the President and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America.
Ángel Cabrera is the President of the Georgia Institute of Technology.
Georgia serves as a hub for zero-emission transportation through HTWO Logistics, a clean logistics partnership operating Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell heavy-duty trucks near Savannah.
The collaboration builds on Hyundai Motor Group's regional presence, which includes the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Georgia.
The initiative supports Georgia Tech’s strategic plan goal of expanding the use of zero-emissions vehicles powered by sustainable energy sources.
Hyundai Motor Group aims to achieve carbon neutrality by 2045 through its long-term clean mobility and hydrogen initiatives.
Additional announcements regarding research projects, educational programs, and vehicle deployment are expected in the coming months.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech Sign MOU to Further Collaborate on Hydrogen Mobility Development_FuelCellChina, Your Gateway to China's Hydrogen Ecosystem

facts: Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on its first U.S. plant dedicated to making electric vehicles.
The groundbreaking ceremony took place in Ellabelle, Ga.
A robotic dog delivered a champagne toast at the ceremony.
Georgia's governor and junior U.S. senator shared a brief bipartisan celebration ahead of high-stakes elections.
Hyundai announced the $5.5 billion manufacturing plant in Bryan County west of Savannah five months prior to the groundbreaking.
Vast areas of the site have already been cleared of trees.
Euisun Chung, executive chairman of Hyundai Motor Group, said the plant will be the envy of the industry.
The South Korean company plans to open the factory in 2025.
The plant will employ at least 8,100 employees.
The plant will produce up to 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
Georgia officials say it's the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
Gov. Brian Kemp said: “This is transformational for the region, not just for people's children now, but for their grandchildren.”
Gov. Brian Kemp added: “This is going to be a project that will resonate for generations to come.”
The groundbreaking ceremony was held two weeks before the Nov. 8 election.
Both Gov. Brian Kemp and Sen. Raphael Warnock face formidable challengers at the top of the ballot in the upcoming election.
After the robotic dog delivered champagne flutes, officials joined other dignitaries in picking up shovels to turn ceremonial scoops of dirt.
Kemp and Warnock shared a brief handshake after the ceremony.
Sen. Raphael Warnock said: “I’d like to see more of this kind of collaboration, quite frankly.”
Warnock noted that at the end of the day, they want to see more jobs in Georgia.
His race with Republican Herschel Walker will help determine which party controls the U.S. Senate.
Kemp worked with state and local officials to lure Hyundai to Georgia with a package of $1.8 billion in tax breaks and incentives.
Kemp faces an Election Day rematch with Democrat Stacey Abrams.
President Joe Biden has embraced boosting U.S. production and sales of electric vehicles as a key part of his strategy for slowing climate change and adding manufacturing jobs.
The White House recently announced $2.8 billion in grants to ramp up production of EV batteries in 12 states.
A sweeping climate and health care law passed by congressional Democrats in August includes incentives for manufacturing and purchasing electric vehicles.
The law includes tax credits for EV buyers worth up to $7,500.
Changes made to the tax credit by Congress limit eligibility only to electric vehicles produced in North America.
Hyundai EVs will no longer qualify for the tax credit until the company starts production in Georgia.

South Korea's ambassador to the U.S. told dignitaries at the Tuesday event that his country believes the revised EV tax credit unfairly excludes Hyundai as it commits to expanding EV production in America.
Ambassador Tae-yong Cho said: “Korean companies are now at risk of being disadvantaged by the electric vehicle tax credit provision.”
Ambassador Cho added: “I believe it is not good for Korea’s partnership, for the state of Georgia, and not even for the common cause of climate change by limiting the consumer choices.”
Warnock voted in support of the Inflation Reduction Act that contained the revised tax credit.
Warnock defends the law overall, saying it shows the U.S. is serious about investing in EV manufacturing.
Warnock has introduced a bill that would push back the date the tax credit revisions take effect.
Warnock has asked the Treasury Department to use “maximum flexibility” in adopting regulations to carry out the new criteria.
Warnock said after the ceremony: “I will continue to work with Ambassador Cho as well as with other officials to do everything we can to perfect what is already clearly a win.”
Georgia's other big deal for a $5 billion EV plant east of Atlanta has run into deeper problems.
Electric truck maker Rivian announced last year plans to employ 7,500 workers in Georgia.
A Morgan County judge last month rejected local governments’ plan to exempt Rivian from an estimated $700 million in property taxes.
Rivian agreed to pay $300 million in lieu of taxes.
State and local officials are weighing an appeal of the judge’s decision.
The property tax break was a major piece of $1.2 billion in incentives offered to Rivian.
Rivian is a California automaker that has seen its stock value decline sharply this year.
Rivian recently announced a big recall to tighten a loose fastener that could affect drivers’ ability to steer.
When asked if he thinks Rivian's Georgia plant will still happen, Kemp said: “I’m very confident.”
Kemp added: “The vast majority of people want the Rivian project to come.”
Kemp stated: “We’re excited about the whole EV marketplace here in Georgia.”
source_title: Hyundai breaks ground on $5.5B electric car plant in Georgia | Taiwan News | Oct. 26, 2022 06:04

facts: Hyundai Motor Company unveiled its 2030 vision and product roadmap at the 2025 CEO Investor Day in New York.
The company aims for 5.55 million global vehicle sales by 2030.
Hyundai plans to achieve 3.3 million electrified vehicle sales by 2030.
The plan includes a comprehensive lineup of hybrid models and region-specific EVs, such as the IONIQ 3 for Europe and India’s first locally designed EV.

José Muñoz, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that Hyundai is positioned to win in the industry's transformation through compelling products, manufacturing flexibility, technology leadership, outstanding dealer partners, and global scale.
Hyundai's strategy includes launching Extended Range EVs (EREVs) by 2027.
These EREVs will offer more than 600 miles of driving range to bridge the transition to full electrification.
The company is investing $2.7 billion to expand production capacity.
This investment will create 3,000 new jobs in Georgia.
The job creation and investment are part of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America expansion.
Hyundai plans to produce more than 80 percent of vehicles sold in the United States domestically by 2030.
The company aims to increase its domestic supply chain content from 60 percent to 80 percent.
The Genesis luxury brand will introduce EREV, hybrid, and BEV powertrains across its lineup.
Genesis targets annual sales of 350,000 units by 2030.
Hyundai Motor’s CFO, Seung Jo (Scott) Lee, announced a KRW 77.3 trillion investment plan over five years from 2026 to 2030.
The investment aims to strengthen global competitiveness through the development of software talent and strategic investments.
Hyundai's comprehensive strategy positions it as a leader in the automotive industry’s transformation towards electrification and technological innovation.

facts: The full name of the sprawling complex is Hyundai Motors Group Metaplant America, or HMGMA for short.
The facility is located in Ellabell, Georgia, just outside of Savannah.
The site covers a 2,900-acre megasite.
The plant is six times larger than Disneyland, plus California Adventure and Downtown Disney.
A future nature preserve with a walking path for employees will be located on the campus.
A water tower on the campus features a livery designed by local art students.
HMGMA represents the single largest investment by any company in the history of Georgia.
In May 2022, Hyundai pledged a $7.6B expenditure for the project.
The investment quickly grew to $12.6B, which includes the addition of a separate battery facility joint venture with SK On located on the same megasite.
In September 2025, the company spent another $2.7B on the project.
The first Ioniq 5 rolled off the production line in October 2024.
The first Ioniq 5 was covered in the signatures of the "Meta Pros," which is HMGMA-speak for factory workers.
The company hopes the Metaplant will eventually have the capacity to build up to 500,000 BEVs and EREVs per year.
The eventual production capacity will span across the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
By 2030, Hyundai aims for 80 percent of its vehicles sold in the U.S. to be built in the U.S.
Hyundai is promising 36 new models by 2030 as part of a new product offensive.
More than 30 percent of Hyundai’s U.S. sales in 2025 were electrified in some capacity.
The company presumes at least 80 percent of the 36 new models promised by 2030 will be built in the United States.
A new body-on-frame midsize truck is being developed to compete with the Colorado, Ranger, and Tacoma.
The brand showed a production-intent-looking Bronco/Wrangler competitor called the Boulder at the New York Auto Show.
Hyundai bowed a conceptual off-roader called the Crater at last November’s L.A. Auto Show, which appears to be a tributary flowing off the Ioniq 5 platform.
Hyundai Motor Group managed to move 984,017 units in the U.S. in 2025.
Of those 2025 units, 772,712 were retail sales.
Q1 of 2026 represents an all-time sales record for the Hyundai brand.
Q1 2026 also marked 40 years since Hyundai sold its first car in the U.S., which occurred in February 1986.
Hyundai saw a nearly 40 percent increase in EV sales from February to March of 2026, attributed to the war in Iran spiking gas prices.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz is the 2025 MotorTrend Person of the Year.
source_title: Inside Hyundai’s Wild Georgia Metaplant: EV Production, Jobs, and Factory Tour Insights

facts: The ICE raid on Hyundai’s battery plant in Georgia was the largest single-site operation in the department’s history.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz said he only learned about the ICE raid from the news.
José Muñoz took the helm at the South Korean company in January.
Muñoz was working out of his California office when officials rounded up 475 workers at the Hyundai-LG factory on Sept. 4.
Muñoz told CNN during a media roundtable that he could not believe what he saw because he would have known normally before the news.
Muñoz likely wasn't notified immediately because the EV battery plant in Ellabell, Georgia, is operated by South Korea’s LG Energy Solution.
More than 300 South Korean workers were detained by ICE during the raid and deported back to South Korea last week via a charter plane.
Video footage released by US authorities showed some workers being shackled with chains around their hands, ankles and wrists.
The shackling has put strain on the country’s relationship with South Korea.
Muñoz said he has been in touch with the Trump administration since the massive deportation effort.
Muñoz told reporters that both governments, South Korea and the US, are working actively to try to ensure that situations like this don’t happen again.
When asked if he was worried about future raids, Muñoz added that nobody can say they are exempt from everything.
Muñoz opened the company’s first US investor conference in New York City on Thursday with a message to those detained workers and their families.
Muñoz expressed sincere empathy for the workers from supplier partner companies who were detained, acknowledging the stress and hardship caused to them and their families.
Hyundai has funneled billions of dollars into US infrastructure, including the EV battery plant in Georgia.
The EV battery plant in Georgia is one of the largest economic development projects in the state’s history.
Muñoz said earlier this month that the battery plant, which is still under construction, will face a two to three month startup delay as a result of the ICE raid.
During the investor conference on Thursday, Hyundai confirmed that it is still moving forward with plans to expand the factory.
Hyundai will spend $2.7 billion to increase production capacity at the Ellabell site by 200,00 over the next three years.
The expansion will bring total capacity to a total of 500,000 vehicles annually.
Hyundai has had to move workers from other plants to the Georgia site to make up for specialized workers flown back to South Korea after the raid.
Muñoz stated that activities in this particular battery factory require very specific expertise that is not available in the country.
Muñoz said foreign specialized workers should be able to receive a special visa that allows them to enter and exit the US five, six, or seven times.
Once the factory is finished, these specialized workers do not come back again.
Such visas are offered to workers from nations like Canada, Mexico, Singapore, and Chile.
This visa option is not available for workers from South Korea.
source_title: Hyundai CEO says he learned about ICE raid on Georgia plant from the news

facts: Hyundai is expanding its workforce and launching hybrid vehicle manufacturing at its electric vehicle factory near Savannah.
The facility is located in Ellabelle, near Savannah, Georgia.
The South Korean automaker will formally announce production of the Kia Sportage hybrid in the coming weeks.
An evening work shift will start in September.
Hiring for the second shift has reached 66%.
Hyundai expects to meet its hiring needs and have all second shift workers in training by July.
Brent Stubbs, the manufacturing facility’s chief administrative officer, stated the plant was designed for ultimate flexibility to build both electric vehicles and hybrids.
The facility aims to reach a production capacity of half-a-million plus cars per year.
Demand for hybrids is surging.
source_title: First Kia hybrid from South Georgia Hyundai EV factory announced

facts: Hyundai Motor is unveiling its first electric three-row SUV, IONIQ 7, at the Busan International Motor Show in June.
The IONIQ 7 is built on Hyundai Motor's unique E-GMP platform.
Hyundai's IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 6 models are favored by consumers for their excellent performance and design.
According to S&P Global, Hyundai ranked fourth in electric vehicle sales in the U.S. market last year, accounting for 4.5% of the market.
Unlike rivals such as Ford and General Motors, which have slowed down electric vehicle production, Hyundai has shown strong momentum.
Hyundai Motor will open its first large-scale electric vehicle and battery factory in Georgia this year to further consolidate its position in the electric vehicle field.
Hyundai Motor America CEO Jose Munoz said the company is working hard to qualify for electric vehicle tax credits to reduce costs and improve competitiveness.
The US$7.6 billion Georgia factory is expected to be put into operation in October this year.
The facility is expected to provide IONIQ tax credits for Class 7 models.
The IONIQ 7 will reportedly be paired with Kia's electric three-row SUV, the EV9, sharing some components and having 8 inches of ground clearance.
The IONIQ 7 is expected to offer multiple battery capacity options and support up to 350 kW charging capacity for fast charging in a short time.
Hyundai Motor plans to start producing the IONIQ 7 at its Asan plant in South Korea in July 2024.
Hyundai Motor plans to introduce the IONIQ 7 to its new plant GA (Georgia) for production next year.
The IONIQ 7 and Kia EV9 share the same E-GMP platform but have obvious differences in appearance and performance.
The front face design of the IONIQ 7 features a majestic look with a sloping A-pillar, giving it a smoother, more fashionable, and dynamic appearance compared to the EV9.
Hyundai Motor announced a groundbreaking ceremony for a new electric vehicle-specific factory in Ulsan, with an investment of 2 trillion won (approximately 11.08 billion yuan).
Full construction of the Ulsan factory will begin in the fourth quarter of 2023, with mass production starting in the first quarter of 2026.
The new Ulsan factory covers an area of 548,000 m2 and has an annual production capacity of approximately 200,000 vehicles.
The Genesis luxury electric SUV will be the first model produced in the new Ulsan factory.
source_title: Hyundai Motor accelerates its electrification layout, and IONIQ 7 three-row SUV leads the future trend-It Industry-php.cn

facts: The first Kia vehicle and the first hybrid vehicle ever built at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America were officially unveiled on Tuesday.
The facility is recognized as Georgia’s largest economic development project.
The unveiled vehicle is a 2027 Kia Sportage Hybrid SUV.
State and local leaders, including Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp, attended the unveiling at the Ellabell facility in Bryan County.
The Kia Sportage Hybrid joins two electric vehicle models already being assembled at the plant.
Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess stated that the launch demonstrates the facility’s continued growth and the economic opportunities it is creating throughout the region.
Stuart Countess noted that jobs at the facility are multiplied many times over by all the suppliers that come in to support it.
Governor Brian Kemp praised the metaplant’s impact on job creation, innovation, and economic development across Georgia.
Governor Kemp stated that the Kia Sportage Hybrid is the latest Georgia-made vehicle that state leaders proudly stand behind.
Governor Kemp added that the campus has become an integral part of the region’s economy and continues to evolve to meet consumer demand.
Plant leaders said the 2026 Kia Sportage Hybrid is already available to consumers.
The 2027 Kia Sportage Hybrid model is expected to arrive on dealership lots in the near future.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s manufacturing operations have become a major driver of economic activity in coastal Georgia since construction began.
source_title: First Kia, first hybrid vehicle built at Georgia Metaplant unveiled in Bryan County

facts: Hyundai Motor Group’s accumulated electric vehicle (EV) sales in the United States exceeded 100,000 units last month.
Hyundai Motor, its independent Genesis brand and Hyundai’s smaller affiliate Kia Corp. had sold a cumulative 104,326 EVs in the U.S. market as of the end of January.
The three brands achieved EV sales exceeding 100,000 units in the US in eight years after it advanced to the world’s most important automobile market with the Kia Soul EV in 2014.
Strong demand for the Hyundai IONIQ 5, the Kia EV6 and the Genesis GV60 drove the auto group’s EV sales in the U.S. to nearly triple to 58,028 units in 2022 from 19,590 the previous year.
The Hyundai IONIQ 5 and the Kia EV6 are equipped with the Korean automotive group’s EV-dedicated platform called E-GMP.
The three brands are targeting to sell a total of 131,000 EVs in 2023, more than doubling from their 2022 sales.
The group plans to launch the Hyundai IONIQ 6, the Kia EV9 and the Genesis GV70 all-electric model in the U.S. this year.
Last month, the group said it will accelerate a wider transition to EVs this year, as it aims to morph into a future mobility solutions provider.

The group’s Executive Chair Euisun Chung said the company will gain a bigger share in the global EV market and generate a new growth driver in autonomous driving, future mobility and robotics in 2023.
The ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) remain major worries for the group’s sales in the U.S. this year.
The IRA excludes EVs built outside North America from tax credits.
The new law is widely expected to deal a blow to Hyundai and Kia, as they produce their EVs at domestic plants for export to the US.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Electric Vehicle Sales Surpasses 1 Lakh Mark in US | Auto News - News18

facts: Plans were unveiled for a sprawling Hyundai Motor Group battery and EV components manufacturing facility near Savannah, GA.
The facility is estimated to occupy over 5,000 acres.
The site is located 30 minutes west of Savannah.
The metaplant will employ 4,000 workers.
The facility will boost company annual EV capacity to 300,000 a year.
The project supports Hyundai, Kia and Genesis electric vehicles.
The facilities will also house 6 or more major suppliers.
The estimated investment is $4.3 billion.
The investment is described as a boon to Georgia and the rest of the mid-south economies.
LG Solutions has recently taken a substantial partnership in this deal.
The partnership focuses on EV and onboard tech.
source_title: Hyundai Group Metaplant Under Construction Near Savannah, Georgia

facts: The partnership between Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group will focus on sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy, and workforce development.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing more than $12 billion in EV manufacturing and battery production in Georgia.
Hyundai’s economic impact in Georgia will be tens of billions of dollars in the years to come and will help create nearly 40,000 jobs.
The collaboration builds upon Hyundai Motor Group’s long history in Georgia.

Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding on September 19, 2023, to collaborate on research and applications to support the future of sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy, workforce development, smart cities, and other areas of cooperation.
The group is investing $12.6 billion in new dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia.
This includes the $7.59 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) site in Bryan County, Georgia that will create more than 8,500 direct jobs.
This also includes a $5 billion battery manufacturing facility joint venture with SK On in Bartow County, Georgia that will employ approximately 3,500 people.
The close proximity of these facilities to Georgia Tech was an important consideration in choosing the locations.
The Kia Georgia assembly plant began vehicle production in 2009 and was established under the leadership of Honorary Chairman Mong-Koo Chung and Executive Chair Euisun Chung.
University System of Georgia Chancellor Sonny Perdue was Governor of Georgia at the time of the Kia plant opening and also attended today’s ceremony.
A recent study by the Center for Automotive Research reveals that Hyundai Motor’s automotive operations contributed $20.1 billion and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy in 2021 alone.
According to the study, Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.

Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated that Hyundai is a global brand synonymous with quality, innovation, and technology advancement, and expressed gratitude for the transformative investments in the state.

President Cabrera noted that the Hyundai brand will feature prominently on campus and looks forward to working with Hyundai leaders to develop exceptional leaders and new ideas shaping the automotive industry and future mobility.
Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, said Georgia is like a second home to the company and emphasized their mission-driven commitment to a sustainable future.
Chang stated that the partnership with Georgia Tech will last for decades and was one of the reasons Hyundai chose Georgia for its EV investments.

José Muñoz, President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, said Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Muñoz noted that proximity to institutions like Georgia Tech was one of the many reasons Hyundai selected Georgia for its new EV manufacturing facility.

Muñoz envisioned zero emissions hydrogen-powered vehicles on campus, advanced air mobility shuttles to Hartsfield International Airport, and hands-free autonomous vehicles on I-75 and I-85 as part of the collaboration's potential impact.
The partnership will include opportunities for student professional development and cooperative work programs.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia stands at the forefront of an unprecedented transformation in the mobility sector toward sustainable transportation alternatives.
Wilson highlighted that Georgia’s strategic focus on building a sustainable mobility ecosystem continues to add to its industry-leading success and brings future jobs to Georgians statewide.
The partnership includes joint research and development programs focused on sustainable mobility, battery technology, and expanding the hydrogen economy.
Georgia Tech and Hyundai will create technical training and leadership development programming for Hyundai employees.
The collaboration will initiate engagement activities to stimulate interest in STEM degrees among students.
Hyundai will actively participate in career recruitment events at Georgia Tech and create learning programs for both undergraduate and graduate students.
Hyundai’s partnership includes field-naming recognition at Bobby Dodd Stadium, which is now known as Bobby Dodd Stadium at Hyundai Field.
The partnership will provide new revenue for Georgia Tech Athletics to support student-athletes and teams athletically and academically.
The collaboration provides a vital pathway for Georgia Tech to expand its stature as a leading research entity in automotive electrification through world-class hydrogen research.
The partnership offers Georgia Tech an opportunity to contribute more broadly to Georgia’s commitment as a hub for electric vehicle production.
source_title: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group Sign MoU for Future Mobility Collaboration - HMGMA

facts: The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
The plant represents the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Vehicles produced at the South Georgia plant will be shipped across the country and around the world.
The facility provides local community opportunities including new jobs, workforce training opportunities, and exposure to the growing electric mobility sector.
The project helps advance the clean energy transition in Georgia.
SACE was invited to tour the facility on behalf of former Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols.
Tim Echols created and previously managed the EV Braintrust.

The EV Braintrust is a collaborative initiative that brings together EV stakeholders, partners, and industry leaders to discuss advancements in EV technology, charging infrastructure, and workforce development.
The factory tour highlighted a highly coordinated balance between human workers ("meta pros") and advanced automation/robotics.
Human employees lead operations and manage different stations across the floor.
Robots handle heavy lifting, moving car parts, and repetitive labor-intensive tasks.
A Boston Dynamics robotic dog was used to check engineers' work before components moved to the next assembly stage.
Hyundai representatives emphasized that their investment in EV manufacturing is a long-term commitment.
The decision to build the plant was made in May 2022.
The Inflation Reduction Act was passed and signed into law in August 2022, after the decision to build the plant.
Hyundai announced it remains committed to electrification and maintaining its presence in Georgia regardless of political changes.
Prior to the plant's official opening, there was initial skepticism among local residents.
Three tragic workplace deaths occurred prior to the plant's official opening, creating concern and mistrust within the community.
The facility includes an on-site emergency medical center designed to respond quickly to workplace incidents.
SACE utilizes a Hyundai IONIQ 5 for EV outreach efforts.
The Hyundai IONIQ 5 is used at ride-and-drive events and community showcases where people can experience electric vehicles firsthand.
The tour provided visibility into the assembly process of the Hyundai IONIQ 5.
source_title: Hyundai Makes a Historic Investment in Georgia’s Clean Transportation Future - Southern Alliance for Clean Energy (SACE)

facts: Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson visited South Korea in March before the announcement of Hyundai Motor Group's $5.54 billion investment in the state.
Hyundai and its sister company Kia have operated plants along Interstate 85 straddling the Georgia-Alabama border for more than a decade.
Georgia has maintained an investment and trade office in South Korea since the 1980s.
The relationship between Georgia and Hyundai Motor Group spans more than 15 years.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp took his first economic development mission to South Korea in 2019.
The state declined to renew its investment recruiter contract in China prior to the 2019 trip.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated it was time to dedicate more resources to the Korean market after declining the China contract renewal.
Georgia previously secured an SK Innovation EV battery plant valued at $1.7 billion, which later expanded to a planned $5.2 billion investment.
Hyundai Motor Group will build its first dedicated EV and battery plant outside Korea on a 2,284-acre mega-site in Bryan County, approximately 30 miles from Savannah.
In addition to the $5.5 billion plant investment, an additional $1 billion is expected from suppliers.
The project is expected to create a total of 8,100 jobs for Georgians.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that shifting focus from China to South Korea was a strategic decision based on advanced activity in battery tech, electric vehicles, and renewable energy.
A U.S. policy consensus emerged favoring collaboration with allies over adversaries in sensitive economic sectors as Georgia deepened its focus on South Korea.
The Biden administration signaled a strong commitment to electric vehicles to curb climate change upon entering office in 2021.
Korean firms in semiconductors, medical items, and EVs announced plans to localize U.S. production during President Moon Jae-in's summit with President Biden.
Former South Korean President Moon Jae-in visited Georgia’s SK Battery plant after his summit with President Biden.
Hyundai previously announced a $7.4 billion U.S. investment for EV manufacturing without specifying the location before this announcement.
President Biden traveled to South Korea in May to shore up alliances through commercial diplomacy.
President Biden met with newly elected South Korean President Yoon Suk-yeol on May 20.
Governor Brian Kemp and President Biden share support for EV initiatives despite differing political backgrounds and motivations.
Governor Kemp marked his second largest-ever state investment in an EV project this fiscal year.
Rivian Automotive previously announced a $5 billion investment and 7,500 jobs in Covington, Georgia, before facing local opposition.
Governor Brian Kemp stated at the announcement that the automotive industry will undergo more change in the next 10 years than in the prior 100.
The Georgia state government purchased the site for Hyundai's plant one year prior to the announcement.
The Savannah-Interstate 16 Joint Development Authority, comprising Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, and Effingham counties, provided input on preparing the site.
Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis captured over 10% U.S. market share last year, surpassing Honda.
Kia is targeting 14 battery-electric models by 2027 as part of a transition away from internal combustion engines.
Hyundai and Genesis are set to offer a combined 17 electric models by 2030.
The group plans to produce 3.23 million EVs in the United States by the end of the decade.
Governor Kemp cited visiting major companies in South Korea as the reason for choosing it as his first economic development trip.
Governor Kemp met with Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung during his 2019 mission to South Korea.
Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated the plant's smart factory will incorporate artificial intelligence, connected manufacturing, and renewable energy sources to accelerate carbon neutrality goals.
The investment will contribute to a stable supply chain for U.S. EV manufacturing and create a wide range of EVs for American customers.
Hyundai Motor Group considers the U.S. an important part of its global strategy and aims to achieve electrified mobility and sustainability goals with Georgia.
Hyundai Motor has not specified which exact models will be produced at the new Georgia plant.
Construction on the Georgia plant is set to begin in January.
Production at the new facility will start in the first half of 2025.
The plant will have an annual capacity of 300,000 vehicles.
A separate battery plant will be built later through a strategic partnership, though details were not provided.
SK Group, which manufactures batteries in Commerce, Georgia, has supplied batteries for Hyundai and Kia electric models globally.
Hyundai committed $300 million in April to upgrade its Montgomery, Alabama plant to produce hybrid Santa Fe SUVs and an electrified Genesis G70.
The first electrified Genesis G70 will roll off the production line in December.
The Genesis G60 crossover will be introduced to the U.S. market this year.
Genesis plans to produce only electric models after 2025.
Hyundai Motor CEO Jaehoon "Jay" Chang stated the U.S. was chosen for its embrace of change and innovation.
Hyundai Group has invested over $13.5 billion and created over 100,000 jobs in the U.S. since 1985.
Hyundai's U.S. business grew by 20% last year despite global chip shortages limiting vehicle supplies.
Kia is one of the Georgia Ports Authority's largest customers, using the Garden City Terminal in Savannah for containerized parts.
Both Kia and Hyundai ship parts in and out of the automotive port in Brunswick, Georgia.
Atlanta supports Hyundai's operations through its large Korean diaspora and strong nonstop air links to Seoul.
Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Hyundai is very comfortable with Georgia's offerings.
CEO Jaehoon "Jay" Chang expressed pride in calling Bryan County home and emphasized Hyundai's global enterprise approach.
Hyundai Motor America President and COO José Muñoz welcomed dealer advisory board members from Hyundai and Genesis at the event.
Commissioner Pat Wilson praised the Kia EV6 all-electric crossover for its advanced ride comfort and consumer-friendly interior technology.
Commissioner Pat Wilson used a Hyundai Ioniq 5 electric crossover during a recent economic development mission to London.
Georgia previously lost out on attracting foreign automaker Volvo, which ultimately built in South Carolina after considering a Georgia megasite.
Commissioner Pat Wilson praised the four-county joint development authority for preparing the Bryan County site just a year after its formation.
Savannah Economic Development Authority President Trip Tollison expressed strong support for the Hyundai project at the event's conclusion.
