facts: Aurubis is a global leader in non-ferrous metals and the world’s largest copper recycler.
On September 24, 2025, Aurubis fired up the largest and most technologically advanced secondary smelter ever built in the US.
The hot commissioning marks a milestone for Aurubis’ $800 million investment at the US site.
The facility keeps valuable metals for the US market in the loop, including copper, nickel, tin, and zinc.
AI and data centers, energy infrastructure, electrification, and defense fuel the growing demand for critical metals supplied by the facility.
The project strengthens supply security by making US supply chains more resilient, enhancing domestic security, and reducing America’s dependence on foreign critical metals.
Aurubis Richmond is the nation’s largest and most advanced secondary smelter, specialized in multimetal recycling.
Approximately $800 million has been invested in the US site.
The facility creates around 240 high-quality and well-paid industrial jobs.
Aurubis buys a broad range of recyclable materials, including copper scrap, e-scrap, and complex industrial residues.
Aurubis experts review customer material specs to provide recovery solutions for the recycled materials.

facts: The Supervisory Board of Aurubis AG, a European-based global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide, announced a significant and accelerated investment in its U.S. operations.
The company plans to spend an additional €250 million to fast-track the expansion of its Richmond plant.
The Richmond plant is currently under construction in Augusta, Georgia.
This investment is projected to double throughput volume from the previously planned 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials to 180,000 tons per year.
Once both phases are complete, Aurubis anticipates about 200 employees at the facility.
Aurubis broke ground on its Richmond plant in June 2022.

Taking into account a capital expenditure adjustment for infrastructure requirements and inflation of €90 million for the current construction project, Aurubis plans to invest a total of €640 million in the U.S. state of Georgia.
Aurubis expects to generate operative earnings (EBITDA) of around €170 million starting in fiscal year 2026/27.
CEO Roland Harings commented that the world needs metals such as copper, which can be recycled without loss of quality or performance, for cars, mobile phones, batteries, cables, and computers.
CEO Roland Harings stated that today's high recycling demand cannot be met by existing facilities.

Consistent U.S. market growth of complex recycling creates excellent conditions for Aurubis' rapid expansion by efficiently repurposing hundreds of thousands of tons of recyclable materials that otherwise would be shipped out of the U.S. or sent to local landfills.

facts: Aurubis is spending an additional $276 million to double the metals recovery capacity of its secondary smelter in Georgia.

facts: Aurubis operates two hot-dip tinning and two electroplating lines in Europe and the US.
Aurubis offers additional coating types including TN, TQ, silver, and gold, with selective procedures available via service partners.
The three main coating processes used are hot-dip tinning, electroplating, and electroplating with a subsequent reflow treatment.
Automotive, electrical, and electronics industries place high demands on connectors and stamped grids, often requiring surface-treated copper and copper alloys.

Hot-dip tinning strip thickness ranges from 0.10 to 1.50 mm, with tin layer thicknesses from 0.8 to 12 µm providing properties like low insertion force, corrosion protection, good solderability, and coverage for special applications.

Electroplated strip thickness ranges from 0.15 to 4.0 mm, offering processes such as Matte/Bright/Brushed Tin, Reflowed TN (available in the US), TQ (available in the US), Advanced Reflow Tin, Super Thin Advanced Reflow Tin (STAR), Nickel Diffusion barrier, and Silver.
Coating properties include super low insertion forces, long-term solderability, whisker formation avoidance, high corrosion protection, and higher temperature resistance.
Other alloys and selective plating are available upon request.
Commercial services include copper market competence, long-term copper supply, market information, and hedging advice.

Technical services feature support from experienced engineers, joint production chain optimization projects, and a laboratory with physical/chemical analysis facilities including scanning electron microscopy.
Logistics and service offerings include customer recycling concepts, an integrated supply chain from concentrate to strip, and worldwide availability through a global sales network.
Aurubis maintains a global operational presence described as operating globally while acting locally.

facts: On June 17, 2022, 150-year-old German-based Aurubis AG broke ground on the United States' first large secondary smelter for multimetal recycling in Augusta, Georgia.
Total investment for the new facility in Richmond County, Georgia, is estimated at €300 million (approx. $320 million).
The plant is expected to be fully operational by mid-2024 or the first half of 2024.
Once fully on-line, Aurubis Richmond will have the capacity to process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually.
The processed materials include printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other recycling materials containing metals such as copper, nickel, tin, and other industrial and precious metals.
The facility will create over 120 new jobs directly in the greater Augusta area.

Aurubis AG CEO Roland Harings stated that the US currently produces about 6 million tons of recycling materials containing valuable metals, which the company aims to capture for local reuse instead of shipping them to Asia and Europe or trucking them to landfills.

Roland Harings noted that current metal supply cannot meet demand, particularly for copper needed for cars, mobile phones, batteries, cables, and computers, making Aurubis' Augusta facility a game changer for manufacturers and the transition to a renewable economy.
The new plant will reduce waste and direct key metals to industries including solar, wind, electric vehicle, and battery manufacturers.

Aurubis is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals that processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues into high-quality metals.
Globally, Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually, along with other metals including precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin, and zinc.
The facility's state-of-the-art environmental technology meets or exceeds the highest State of Georgia and federal government environmental standards.
Aurubis Richmond will have a very low impact on regional air quality, accounting for less than 0.2 percent of dust emissions in Richmond county.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp called the project one of the biggest international investments in state history and the largest single investment made by a German company in Georgia history.
Aurubis AG employs approximately 7,200 people worldwide and operates production sites in Europe and the US.

facts: Companies looking to lower emissions and reduce carbon footprint have invested over $11.5 billion in Georgia since 2018.
Ascend Elements, formerly known as Battery Resourcers, is investing $43 million to open a commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling plant in Georgia.
The Ascend Elements facility will extract critically scarce materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel from batteries.
The Ascend Elements plant will become the largest of its kind in North America when fully operational later this year.

Roger Lin, Ascend Elements’ Vice President of Marketing and Government Relations, stated that Georgia was selected primarily because of the growing electric vehicle and battery ecosystem in the state.
Proximity to partners and customers is essential in the sustainable battery materials industry, according to Lin.
The Southeast is a mecca for the electric vehicle and battery industry, and in Georgia, Ascend Elements is close to factories like SK Battery America and others like it.
Operating in Georgia minimizes transportation costs, provides proximity to top talent from nearby universities, and offers access to renewable energy, notes Lin.
Korea’s SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two facilities near Atlanta to supply batteries to Ford and Volkswagen as well as other electric vehicle makers.
SK Battery America made its first announcement regarding the Georgia facilities in 2018.
Plug Power recently announced a new $84 million, zero-emission hydrogen fuel production facility near I-95 and Georgia’s deepwater ports.
Plug Power provides fuel cell systems for e-mobility that deliver efficiency, fast fueling, and low cost to customers.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp launched the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance, a statewide initiative between government, industries, electric utilities, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
The alliance focuses on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.

In 2021, EV manufacturer Rivian announced an investment of $5 billion to build a carbon-conscious facility to make vertically connected electric platforms for trucks, sport/utility vehicles, and delivery vans.
Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia, announcing a $5.54 billion investment.

Since 2009, Georgia has increased net electricity generation from renewable sources by more than 93%.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the installation of renewable energy capacity in Georgia has been driven organically by companies and consumers, accelerated through economic attractiveness and removing barriers rather than mandate.

Smart policies have allowed Georgia to promote renewable power development and offer a comprehensive portfolio of clean energy options that give the state a competitive edge in attracting leading businesses, according to Wilson.
Solar PV is the state’s fastest-growing source of renewable energy.

Recent sustainability-related announcements in Georgia include German copper recycler Aurubis, electronic waste recycler Igneo, Danimer Scientific (manufacturer and developer of biodegradable materials), and recycled furniture maker SoPoly.
EF Block turns everyday recycled Styrofoam into earth-friendly, stronger, sustainable walls that replace traditional building materials.

Companies leading the charge in mobility research & development, battery manufacturing and recycling, and electric vehicle production all call Georgia home.

facts: Amazon.com said on Tuesday that its cloud computing division, Amazon Web Services (AWS), plans to invest about $11 billion to expand its infrastructure in Georgia.
The expansion aims to support cloud computing and AI technologies.

Big Tech companies are pouring billions into artificial intelligence, highlighting the growing need for robust data centers and advanced infrastructure to support AI-driven innovations and cloud services.
Microsoft announced plans last week to invest about $80 billion in fiscal 2025 to develop data centers for training AI models and deploying AI and cloud-based applications.
AI applications, from machine learning to generative models, require vast computing resources.
This drives demand for specialized data centers that enable tech companies to link thousands of chips in clusters.
The investments in Butts and Douglas counties are expected to create hundreds of jobs.
Amazon stated the investments are expected to enhance Georgia's position as a hub for cutting-edge digital innovation.
The project is expected to create at least 550 new high-skilled jobs.
This surge in AI and cloud computing has also driven a significant increase in U.S. electricity consumption, as AI data centers consume vast amounts of energy.

Amazon has secured multiple agreements with US utilities to supply power to its data centers nationwide.
Partnerships include Talen Energy in Pennsylvania and Entergy in Mississippi.

facts: Rivian now expects to produce up to 300,000 vehicles at its Georgia manufacturing plant, a 50% increase from its initial estimate.
The company is increasing initial production capacity to 300,000 vehicles annually, a 50% increase from 200,000 to support its new midsize platform.
Rivian said the change will be more cost-effective while also leaving room to expand output in the future.
As part of the changes, the company worked with the Department of Energy to update its DOE loan.
The up to $4.5 billion loan is now in line with Rivian’s updated facility design.
With vertical construction expected to begin this spring, Rivian said, it expects to first draw on the loan by early 2027.
The company remains on track to begin vehicle production at the facility in late 2028.
Preparations are also underway for the stamping press area, one of the most costly projects.
Rivian will accelerate progress on the plant this summer as the primary buildings begin to take shape.

CEO RJ Scaringe said that “R2 dramatically expands our market opportunity,” adding that “the thousands of dedicated people who will soon work in our Georgia plant will be instrumental to Rivian’s growth as we scale American manufacturing.”
Rivian recently announced a partnership with Uber to build up to 50,000 robotaxis at its Georgia plant, starting in late 2028.
source_title: Rivian (RIVN) boosts production capacity for Georgia plant by 50%

facts: The Middle Corridor is a multimodal land and sea freight transport route from China to Europe that was first mooted in the 1990s.
The route transits through Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and Turkmenistan, crosses the Caspian Sea through Azerbaijan and Georgia to the Black Sea.
The corridor consists of around 4,250 kilometers of rail lines and 500 kilometers of seaway.
Two paths lead from the corridor to Europe via Turkey or the Black Sea.
In 2021, there was a 29% increase in container trains along the Middle Corridor.
The corridor still carries just a small percentage of total China-EU trade.
An efficient Middle Corridor could offer transport time between China and Europe of 13 to 21 days compared to 35 to 45 days for the sea route, according to the German Economic Research Team.
The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) suggested in a report for the European Commission that around €19 billion is needed to make necessary improvements to the route.

Financing is not the main issue; officials cite soft issues like aligning national visions, coordinating planning, harmonizing standards, enhancing the PPP environment, liberalizing markets, and integrating border management as key hurdles.
Infrastructure deficiencies require addressing bottlenecks including a lack of capacity in shipping, goods-handling infrastructure, and IT at ports.
There is a shortage of maritime services at sea.
On land, there is insufficient modern rolling stock for rails, uncoordinated border crossing points, and unstandardized information technology.
A World Bank report released in November 2023 forecasts that by 2030, if the Middle Corridor is operational, Georgia’s export tonnage could rise by over 3%.
The same World Bank report projects an increase in the value of exported goods for Georgia of as much as $107 a ton overall.
Georgia stands to gain notably from increased exports of prepared foodstuff, especially beverages, mineral waters, and wines, particularly as exports to China via the Middle Corridor.
Georgia’s mineral exports are projected to grow, notably for construction materials and non-ferrous ores.
The World Bank forecasts a 44% rise in the regional cargo base between 2021 and 2030.
This growth will foster the development of logistic clusters and concentration of specialist activities.
Despite a 33% rise in 2022 container traffic as China rerouted EU-bound shipments, high transport delays drove shippers back to alternative corridors in 2023.
The first eight months of 2023 saw a decrease of 37% in Middle Corridor container traffic according to the World Bank.
International logistic giants Maersk, Rail Bridge Cargo, and CEVA Logistics initially rushed to the Middle Corridor after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine but have since held back.
Bottlenecks make the Middle Corridor take far longer than the Northern Corridor, mostly due to inadequate Caspian and Black Sea shipping and port capacity.
Tariffs on the corridor are uncompetitive and costs fluctuate.
In October 2023, 2,000 international delegates attended the Silk Road Forum in Tbilisi to discuss challenges and opportunities for the corridor.
Georgia, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, and Turkey signed a roadmap in 2022 listing priority investments and actions needed.
In June 2023, an agreement was reached between Georgia, Azerbaijan, and Kazakhstan on the creation of a joint logistics operator.
The World Bank, EU, ADB, and other international organizations confirmed their support and interest in providing technical assistance and investments.
Forecasts predict a 30% rise in EU-China trade by 2030.
Inter-regional trade between Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Kazakhstan is predicted to increase by 37% by 2030.
Trade between these countries and the EU is anticipated to grow by 28%.
The UK-US think tank IISS lists an upcoming tender to construct the Anaklia deep sea port as a hard investment for Georgia.
The IISS also lists the reconstruction of sections of the Baku-Akhalkalaki-Kars railway as a priority.
Plans exist for the creation of a logistics and industry park near Poti.
A logistics center is planned in Akhalkalaki.
APM Terminals has proposed capacity expansion at Poti Port to help ease congestion.
Georgia has intensified work to finish a new east-west road highway connecting its ports to the Caspian Sea.
Georgia is reforming the Georgian Railway by raising money for new rolling stock and bringing in a new ownership structure.
For a 1,500 TEU vessel, a sole ship-call in Poti costs $38,800.
This equates to around $97 per 40-foot box on average.
Crossing the whole port costs $275 in Poti and $430 in Batumi for a 40-foot box.
These port costs are significantly higher than in Aktau or Baku ($148), or European or Asian ports.
The U.S. Foreign Policy Research Institute expects it will be at least five years before the new Road Map eliminates red tape problems.
Russia has long sought to be the dominant influence in countries traversed by the Middle Corridor and control trade.
If Russia wins decisively in Ukraine, it is likely to direct resources towards disrupting the corridor to protect the Northern Corridor’s role in transcontinental trade.
source_title: Gateway to development – assessing Georgia’s Middle Corridor opportunity – Investor.ge

facts: The facility is located in Cordele, GA, and operates in the Transportation Equipment sector under NAICS code 336212.
The facility is operated under parent company Bcd Parent Inc.
Across 5 years of records spanning 2019–2023, the site has disclosed 253 lbs in cumulative toxic releases across 4 distinct chemicals.
Two of the reported chemicals (50% of the chemical profile) carry a carcinogenic classification.
Air releases are the dominant pathway at 194 lbs (76.7% of total volume), followed by off-site transfers at 23.3%.
Reported toxic release totals decreased by 100% from 61 lbs in 2019 to 0 lbs in 2023.
Yearly reported toxic releases were 61 lbs in 2019, 60 lbs in 2020, 90 lbs in 2021, 42 lbs in 2022, and 0 lbs in 2023.
The facility files annual Form R disclosures to the EPA Toxics Release Inventory under EPCRA Section 313.
TRI release totals are self-reported estimates, not measured stack emissions, and treat every pound of reported chemical equally regardless of toxicity.
Inclusion in the TRI database does not establish a health risk to nearby residents; it documents the annual reporting record filed with the EPA under federal community right-to-know requirements.

facts: The US economy is adjusting to ever-changing conditions, with tariffs and protectionism creating serious headwinds for companies that outsource parts and materials due to rising costs and volatile supply chains.
Energy scarcity is a looming concern for some parts of the country as sustainability initiatives and climate change concerns drive business outlooks.
Mid-level businesses are considering private equity as a source of capital but must navigate distinctions between local private equity firms and bigger players along the east coast.
Atlanta Business Chronicle brought together five leading minds in Georgia manufacturing and infrastructure for a roundtable discussion on where the manufacturing industry is headed statewide.
Topics covered in the roundtable included marketing in the era of protectionism, understanding private equity, and the unique benefits of the frozen base rate for energy.
Manufacturing is one of the most energy-intensive industries anywhere, and Georgia’s success as a manufacturing hub is attributed to its robust and affordable energy supply.

David Wagner, Vice President of Supply and Origination for Gas South, stated that Georgia is well situated to provide cost-effective and reliable natural gas and power due to its proximity to major interstate energy infrastructure.
Georgia's position between natural gas producing regions in the Appalachians and along the Gulf Coast, connected by extensive pipeline infrastructure, provides a robust supply solution.

Arthur Tripp, vice president of economic development for Georgia Power, noted that Georgia’s forward-thinking energy policies allow local utilities to capitalize on the state’s position and offer multiple years of frozen base energy rates.

Tripp stated he is not aware of any other utility in the country freezing base rates and emphasized that this decision creates expanded opportunities for reliability and sustainability because everything is interconnected.

Georgia operates as a vertically integrated energy market, which provides opportunities for strategic investments in reliability as the power grid expands and efficient new generation sources are added.
Tripp highlighted that Georgia's market structure is a major reason why utility rates have averaged 15% below the national average since 1990.
Manufacturing is a talent-driven industry, and Georgia is considered a great place to live and work for attracting and retaining the best talent.

Annette Sally, executive vice president for marketing agency Brunner, described a renaissance moment happening for manufacturing and noted an opportunity to romance jobs in manufacturing to make Georgia a top destination.

Brad Beard, senior director of middle market banking at Synovus, stated that business and political climate are huge drivers, but location is vital due to proximity to a major port and significant international airport.

The state has invested heavily in infrastructure and understands the importance of attracting more diverse industries than it had 40 years ago, with emerging industries now coming to Georgia from California and other parts of the country.

Jason Hodell, partner and industrial manufacturing & consumer goods leader with Cherry Bekaert Advisory, praised Georgia officials for being very forward-looking in enticing out-of-state and foreign businesses into the state.
Hodell noted that efforts to help middle-market companies optimize and negotiate with the state, port, or county are extremely lucrative and bring jobs, people, and workforce into the economy.
Quality of life is a big incentive for employers considering Georgia, including access to the Beltline, beaches, mountains, and the excitement created by Atlanta.
Sally emphasized that momentum underpins these factors, noting that forward momentum and growth create positive energy that drives both people and companies to want to be in Georgia.
Natural gas is a foundational fuel here for the long run, supplying power plants for electricity and direct heat, both of which manufacturers need for many processes.
Gas South is working to partner to enhance local gas facilities and work with upstream infrastructure providers so manufacturers can experience success and avoid disruptions.

Tripp stated that in 2025, having a reliable energy source is a necessity, not just a commodity, and highlighted how smart investments and infrastructure planning worked wonders after Hurricane Helene knocked out power throughout many parts of the state.
Following Hurricane Helene, Georgia Power coordinated generators for nursing homes in South Georgia to restore power.
Tripp noted that across the country providers are raising rates and struggling to meet demand, while Georgia froze base rates and still meets growing energy demands as a rare benefit.

Hodell considered the immense opportunity facing manufacturers in Georgia, stating this could be the best manufacturing climate ever since World War II, allowing companies to run off an entire project in the first year of deployment.
Manufacturers can immediately expense R&D and get tax credits under current policies.
Middle-market companies have access to historic government funding like the roughly $300 billion of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act federal allocation that is still available.
Tariffs and Made-in-America mandates force nearshorers to open up American distribution and manufacturing to avoid tariffs and reduce logistics costs.

Beard stated there are significant programs such as the USDA Financing program that can create attractive financing arrangements for companies to expand and bring economic development to areas of the state that may not benefit otherwise.
Synovus, the largest bank based in Georgia, advises family-owned manufacturers on succession planning routes including strategic sales to competitors, private equity transactions, or forming an ESOP.

Hodell explained that businesses can protect themselves from tariffs by setting up a foreign trade zone to delay paying tariffs until selling the product in America, providing nine months of cash flow.

Companies can avoid tariffs altogether if they re-export or materials are scrapped, and many misunderstand how much they can earn in R&D tax credits from the state and federal government for simple process improvements, shop floor investments, and software investments.
Sixty percent of companies are either rebranding or preparing to rebrand for new protectionist policies, which is now table stakes.
Sally advised that since very few products are 100% made in America, companies should play offense with transparency on what they make domestically rather than hiding imports.
Smart companies understand marketing is a long game influenced by every part of the company, and building brand reputation consistently is foundational for success in the turn to protectionism.
Georgia manufacturers who compete against international players love the new market because protectionism creates winners alongside headwinds.

Tripp stressed that utilities must be strategic partners growing Georgia deliberately through day-to-day engagement with local development authorities, chambers of commerce, and site selectors worldwide.
Advanced manufacturing is still leading the way over data centers in attracting businesses to Georgia, and knowing what business fits each community is key.

Wagner noted that while local infrastructure may provide physical interconnection for manufacturers, expanding upstream natural gas transportation capacity can take months or years, making power availability a key factor in bringing new business to the state.
Gas South has partnered in multiple circumstances to leverage its portfolio of gas supply deliverability to bridge the gap between short-term needs and long-term solutions for business leaders.
Georgia’s deregulated gas market puts it in a unique position to find collaborative solutions that bring lower-cost, more reliable energy to manufacturers.

Wagner stated that infrastructure upgrades throughout Georgia are a step toward keeping everyone’s energy needs filled affordably, with cutting-edge infrastructure being the central focus for continuing to lead in manufacturing.

Tripp emphasized that affordability is the next priority for manufacturing and that freezing base rates for three years brings predictability, which is critical for businesses and residential customers who make up the workforce.
Sally noted that building a positive and reliable reputation behind Georgia's benefits is what will get the state on the map, keep it there, and help it reach the number one spot in manufacturing.
source_title: Table of Experts: The future of manufacturing in Georgia - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: Georgia Ports Authority links importers and exporters to global markets via 37 weekly container ship calls.
Georgia’s ports are a key factor in the decision for automakers, their suppliers, other manufacturers, and logistics operations to bring jobs to Georgia.
Griff Lynch serves as President and CEO of the Georgia Ports Authority.
In partnership with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), the state announced 350 projects from potential port users in Fiscal Year 2023.
The Fiscal Year 2023 announcements will bring more than 33,000 manufacturing and logistics jobs to 96 counties across Georgia.
The Fiscal Year 2023 announcements will bring $21 billion in investment to 96 counties across Georgia.
Griff Lynch stated that driving the success of the entire state is a mission to deliver greater economic opportunity for Georgians in large and small communities.
GPA, GDEcD, and the Georgia Department of Transportation are working together to foster responsible growth that delivers good jobs while preserving quality of life.
Georgia Ports is investing $29 million in an overpass that will route trucks leaving the Ocean Terminal in Savannah directly onto U.S. 17, avoiding community streets.
Announcements associated with the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America have located in more than 10 counties across Georgia.
Pat Wilson is a Commissioner at the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Pat Wilson stated that companies, especially in e-mobility, are prioritizing speed-to-market solutions.
Pat Wilson noted that investing in site development and “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” certification are ways communities can prepare for new opportunities.
Anovion Technologies, a synthetic graphite manufacturer, is creating 400 jobs for Southwest Georgia in Decatur County.
Chip Dunn serves as Executive Chairman of Anovion Technologies.

Chip Dunn described the state’s recruitment effort that swayed Anovion’s decision to locate in rural Georgia, including meetings with Gov. Brian Kemp and factors such as GPA’s inland port system and access to waterway and land transport.
The GPA inland port system includes the Appalachian Regional Port in Crandall, Ga., and the Blue Ridge Connector rail yard now under construction near Gainesville, Ga.
The inland port system supports the movement of large, heavy products from centrally located communities to the state’s deepwater ports.
The inventory of industrial property in the Savannah area totals more than 124 million square feet.
More than 14 million square feet of industrial property in the Savannah area was under construction at the close of Calendar Year 2023.
Stephen Ezelle is a broker for the industrial real estate firm Cushman & Wakefield.
Stephen Ezelle stated that the Savannah regional economy continues to experience expansion and strong absorption in the industrial sector despite a slowing economy across most U.S. markets.
Projections indicate Savannah will eclipse 8 million TEUs of throughput by 2030.
The operational efficiency offered by GPA and the shift in import cargo from the West Coast and NY/NJ are key drivers in the growth of Savannah’s industrial market.
Savannah is expected to continue a balanced expansion thanks to the Hyundai announcement and GPA’s growing national market share.
Stephen Ezelle predicts continued absorption of well-located light industrial warehouse space.
Savannah’s industrial sector ended 2023 with an overall vacancy rate of 7 percent.
Stacy Watson serves as director of economic and industrial development at GPA.
Stacy Watson stated that being within 30 miles of the port is great but not required to be competitive in drayage, lease rates, and overall cost of doing business.
Locations 40 or 50 miles away from Savannah are an easy drive due to the port’s location upriver of the Savannah population center and Georgia DOT efforts reducing truck route congestion.
Direct truck routes from the Port of Savannah connect to Interstates 95 and 16.
On-terminal service at the marine terminal is provided by two Class I railroads to expedite cargo movement.
Available industrial properties in Bryan, Liberty, and McIntosh counties as well as points west offer interstate and rail links to the port and thriving inland markets.
Augusta is a short drive from Savannah, and a majority of manufacturers in Augusta are port users.
Cal Wray serves as president of the Development Authority of Augusta, Ga.

Cal Wray stated that Augusta-Richmond County provides an ideal location for port users due to available, affordable industrial land, over 350 existing manufacturers, and easy rail and highway connections to the port.
Aurubis Richmond, a metal smelting operation in Augusta, is bringing 250 jobs and $600 million in investment.
Denkai America, a copper film producer in Augusta, is providing 250 jobs and $440 million in investment.
Purecycle Technologies, which recycles plastic into like-new pellets for reuse in Augusta, is creating 100 jobs and $400 million in investment.
An electric vehicle manufacturing ecosystem exists in Augusta with EZGo Textron and Club Car and their related suppliers located in Richmond and Columbia counties.
Metals and chemical manufacturing are two other important industries in the Augusta region.
GPA supports the efficient operation of existing industries and local economic development efforts, serving as a key partner in ongoing recruitment of new industry.
GPA is a resource to existing manufacturers who have invested billions in the region and many of which have announced significant further investments in the last five years.
source_title: Georgia Ports Highlights Site Selection Opportunities - Columbus CEO

facts: Bosch is expanding its semiconductor business with silicon carbide chips.
The technology company plans to acquire assets of the U.S. chipmaker TSI Semiconductors, based in Roseville, California.
TSI Semiconductors has a workforce of 250 and operates as a foundry for application-specific integrated circuits, or ASICs.
Currently, TSI mainly develops and produces large volumes of chips on 200-millimeter silicon wafers for applications in the mobility, telecommunications, energy, and life sciences industries.
Over the next years, Bosch intends to invest more than 1.5 billion USD in the Roseville site.
Bosch plans to convert the TSI Semiconductors manufacturing facilities to state-of-the-art processes.
Starting in 2026, the first chips will be produced on 200-millimeter wafers based on the innovative material silicon carbide (SiC).
As of Dec. 31, 2022, the Bosch Group employs nearly 37,000 associates in more than 100 locations in North America.
According to preliminary 2022 figures, Bosch generated consolidated sales of $15 billion in the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.
The Bosch Group employs roughly 420,000 associates worldwide as of Dec. 31, 2022.
The company generated sales of $93.1 billion in 2022.
Bosch's operations are divided into four business sectors: Mobility Solutions, Industrial Technology, Consumer Goods, and Energy and Building Technology.
At 128 locations across the globe, Bosch employs roughly 84,800 associates in research and development, of which more than 44,000 are software engineers.
In North America, Bosch employs approximately 3,500 associates in research and development.
source_title: Rising demand for SiC chips: Bosch plans to acquire U.S. chipmaker TSI Semiconductors - Bosch Media Service US

facts: In 2023, Bosch announced a $130 million investment in Lincolnton, North Carolina to support manufacturing of power tools accessories.
In 2023, Bosch announced and closed an acquisition of a wafer fab in Roseville, California.
Bosch intends to invest $1.5 billion at the site to produce silicon carbide chips by 2026.
The silicon carbide (SiC) chips produced by Bosch are used in the power electronics of electric cars and can increase driving range.
Starting in 2026, Bosch plans to manufacture the first vertically structured SiC chips on cost-effective 200-millimeter wafers.
Bosch closed its acquisition of U.S.-based specialist hydraulic company Hydraforce in 2023, strengthening its global hydraulics business.
Bosch plans to grow its integrator business in North America, evidenced by the acquisition of Paladin Technologies in 2023.

Mike Mansuetti, president of Bosch in North America, stated that Bosch continues to register profitable growth in North America and is investing to further grow and diversify its business as an integral part of the global portfolio.
The integrated Mobility business sector has been in place since the beginning of 2024, with horizontal responsibility for software, semiconductors, and vehicle computers.
Bosch is aiming for 10 percent annual growth in demand for new redundant braking systems, which support automated and electrified driving, up through 2030.

Dr. Stefan Hartung, chairman of the board of management of Robert Bosch GmbH, stated that current climate policies are destabilizing the business environment, citing Germany’s sudden abolition of electric-car subsidies as an example of erratic policy structure impeding long-term purchasing decisions.
Bosch expects production levels in the automotive industry for 2024 to be similar to those of the previous year.
Bosch plans to spend around 4 billion euros by 2030 on training and reskilling associates to move them from shrinking areas to growing ones.
As of December 31, 2023, the Bosch Group employed some 427,600 people worldwide.
source_title: The 2023 business year: Bosch increases sales and result despite headwind  - Bosch Media Service US

facts: The auto division of Robert Bosch GmbH is the world’s largest auto supplier.
The company also manufactures power tools, household appliances and products and components for energy and building technology.
Following massive job cuts and various other cost-cutting programmes over the past two years, the Bosch company employed just under 420,000 workers in 60 countries worldwide at the end of 2024.
Last year alone, 11,500 employees lost their jobs worldwide.
This included 4,400 of the company’s 129,000-strong workforce in Germany.
The preliminary business result for 2024 suggests sales of 90.5 billion euros.
This corresponds to a maximum profit of 3.5 billion euros and a return of 3.5 percent.
In 2023, Bosch profits stood at 4.6 billion euros.
The return in 2023 was 5 percent.
Bosch executive Stefan Hartung expects 2025 to be a challenging year.
Hartung announced “painful decisions” for the workforce but not for company owners.
The company plans to at least double its profits from 2024 by 2026, according to Hartung.
Hartung stated there will be further savings programmes because of another major shift in the auto manufacturing complex by 2030.
This structural change is expected to continue over the next five or six years.
Hartung refused to disclose the number of redundancies the company is planning.
In March 2024, Hartung announced the wiping out of 3,500 jobs in Germany.
He stated there would be no end to the current attacks on jobs and wages.
At the end of 2024, Bosch announced plans to cut a further 12,230 jobs worldwide by 2026.
This includes 7,000 cuts at its German plants.
Hartung emphasized that job cuts could only be achieved in close cooperation with the IG Metall union.
Bosch intends to follow a similar path to Volkswagen regarding workforce reductions.
At VW, IG Metall’s alliance with the company is leading to the destruction of at least 35,000 jobs.
This includes a reduction in real wages of up to 20 percent.
It will also lead to plant closures sooner or later.
Bosch is cutting wages and salaries as well as jobs.
The working hours of more than 10,000 employees are being forcibly reduced to 35 hours.
These reductions are without wage compensation.
Most of those affected currently work 38 to 40 hours a week.
They may lose up to 15 percent of their gross salary due to the reduction.
Bosch Mobility works council chairman Frank Sell is also deputy chairman of the Supervisory Board of Robert Bosch GmbH.
Sell complained that the works council was “only aware of the planned course of action in advance in hints and has now been presented with a fait accompli.”
Less than two years ago, in July 2023, Sell declared employees at Mobility sites could look forward to job security until 2027.
Sell stated works councils will be involved in strategic and economic planning at an early stage regarding future products and target plans beyond 2027.
Bosch was founded by Robert Bosch in 1886.
In 1913, the Bosch plant in Stuttgart was temporarily closed due to a strike for better wages and working conditions.
Hartung instructed works councils in each individual division to negotiate separately with board members and managers.
He also instructed works councils at each plant to negotiate separately.
This is intended to prevent a joint response from approximately 130,000 workers in Germany and almost 420,000 workers worldwide.
IG Metall is postponing its nationwide day of protest until 15 March.
Demonstrations for the protest have been registered in Hanover, Stuttgart, Cologne, Frankfurt and Leipzig.
The postponement occurs after the federal election.

facts: Sales to third parties in North America increased to $18.8 billion in 2025, up from $17.3 billion in 2024.
Bosch pushes ahead with technologies for automation and robotics at BCW 2026, providing comprehensive expertise and added value from sensors to systems.
Bosch sets a new benchmark for electric propulsion with a next-generation synchronous motor featuring world-record efficiency and series-ready performance.
The next-generation synchronous motor helps enable automakers to reduce battery size and cost while maintaining range.
Bosch introduces Rapid Catalyst Heating to reduce cold-start emissions from gasoline engines.
source_title: Bosch Media Service - Bosch Media Service US

facts: Bosch announced it will slash 13,000 jobs primarily in its automotive division.
The layoffs are driven by faltering demand, fierce Chinese competition, and a sluggish transition to electric vehicles (EVs).
The announcement was made on Thursday, Sept. 25.
Bosch cited a need to save €2.5 billion ($2.92 billion) annually in its mobility division.
The company framed the move as a painful but necessary adaptation to shifting demand.
Stefan Grosch serves as Bosch's labor director.
Labor director Stefan Grosch stated, "We need to orient ourselves to where our markets and customers are."
The announcement acknowledges the accelerating exodus of business from Europe.
All layoffs are located in Germany and represent 10 percent of Bosch's domestic workforce.
Competitors Schaeffler and Continental have already shed thousands of jobs amid similar industry struggles.
Automakers Volkswagen and Porsche source parts from Bosch.
Volkswagen and Porsche are scaling back EV investments due to weak consumer uptake.
Volkswagen plans to eliminate up to 30,000 positions alongside a massive restructuring.
The job cuts highlight the deepening crisis facing German manufacturers as global trade barriers rise and key markets pivot toward local suppliers.
China now favors domestic brands like BYD, squeezing profit margins in a brutal price war.
The EV transition has stalled in Europe, leaving manufacturers burdened with excess capacity.

Brighteon.AI's Enoch engine noted that "EVs have not seen widespread adoption due to their heavy, inefficient conversions; limited range and speed; and high maintenance costs from frequent battery replacements. Additionally, the globalist push for forced electrification ignores these practical drawbacks while suppressing superior alternatives like hydrogen or clean combustion engines."
Marco Zehe serves as Bosch's electrification lead.
Electrification lead Marco Zehe admitted, "Electromobility has not taken off as quickly as forecast," underscoring miscalculations in corporate and government green energy mandates.
U.S. tariffs and a global push for localized supply chains have further eroded Germany's export-driven model.
Workers' representatives condemned the cuts as "historically unprecedented."
Frank Sell is head of the works council for Bosch Mobility.
Works council head Frank Sell warned, "Bosch is leaving behind social devastation in many regions," and vowed to resist closures.
Markus Heyn serves as head of Bosch Mobility.
Head of Bosch Mobility Markus Heyn conceded, "The days when Germany could produce a great deal for the rest of the world are over."
Germany's economy is stagnating, with manufacturing output shrinking for over a year.
Analysts warn that without drastic cost reductions, even industry titans risk being outpaced by leaner global rivals.
For decades, Germany's automotive prowess symbolized precision and reliability.
Intensifying trade wars and accelerating technological disruptions now pose an existential test to Germany's automotive dominance.
source_title: Germany’s Bosch announces JOB CUTS impacting 13,000 positions as the country’s auto industry faces serious crisis

facts: Bridgestone Americas has officially broken ground on a 50,000-square-foot addition to its 200,000-square-foot Bridgestone Bandag plant in Abilene, Texas.
The US$60-million project will support six- and seven-day production schedules and add new mixing operations by January 2025.
Bridgestone says the investment and additional operating days will increase the plant’s output by 16%.

Chris Daniel, executive director – Bandag manufacturing, Bridgestone America, stated: “Today we celebrate the growth and commitment of this plant and this community, with new investment and a physical expansion driven by growing demand for our products and, just as fundamentally, by this team’s commitment to delivering innovation and superior quality for our customers here and around the world.”

Chris Daniel also stated: “Our company and our Abilene team have a vital role to play in the transformation of mobility brought forth by a desire of building greener retreads, while helping maximize fuel efficiency for fleets.”
source_title: Bridgestone Americas breaks ground on Bandag plant expansion - Truck News

facts: Bandag has signed an agreement with Tegeta Motors to build a state-of-the-art Tegeta Bandag Factory in Rustavi, Georgia.
The new facility is supported by an initial investment of USD 2 million.
The plant aims to produce up to 5,000 retreaded tyres annually.
Production capacity is expected to double within five years.
The new facility will be the first Bandag retreading factory in the Caucasus region.
Construction is scheduled to begin this year.
Completion of the facility is targeted for late 2026.
The project marks a significant milestone in Bandag’s commitment to advancing circular mobility and promoting environmental responsibility.
The initiative strengthens Tegeta Motors’ position as a leading provider of advanced automotive services in the region.
Jacques Fourie, President of Bridgestone Africa, Middle East Region, stated that the collaboration combines technical expertise, shared vision, and a deep commitment to sustainable mobility.
The facility will initially retread tyres for buses and trucks.
Every product produced will meet international safety and performance standards.
Representatives from Bridgestone and Bandag will provide construction oversight to ensure the facility adheres to global quality standards.
The plant will be equipped with state-of-the-art Bandag machinery sourced from Europe and the United States.
Retreaded tyres cost 30-40% less than new tyres.
Retreaded tyres deliver comparable performance to new tyres.
Retreaded tyres provide a service life of up to five years.
Bandag’s retreading technology requires less raw material and energy compared to manufacturing new tyres.
The process extends a tyre’s life through multiple cycles, reduces waste generation, minimizes carbon emissions, and supports more efficient use of natural resources.
Ekaterine Kavtaradze, CEO of Tegeta Motors, stated that the launch advances fleet solutions, promotes sustainable mobility, and strengthens their presence in the Caucasus region.
Tegeta Motors will integrate the retreading operations into its existing ecosystem, which includes tyre services, oil and battery operations, and recycling activities.
Bridgestone MEA continues to provide technical expertise and innovation support throughout the factory's development as Bandag’s supporting entity in the Caucasus.

facts: Bridgestone Americas announced a USD 60 million investment to expand its Bridgestone Bandag, LLC manufacturing plant in Abilene, Texas.
The expansion adds 50,000 square feet of space to the Abilene plant.
The expansion addresses growing demand for the company’s tread rubber products driven by rapid growth in its retread business.
Operational changes include an immediate increase to six and seven-day production schedules.
New mixing operations will be constructed at the facility.
The new mixing operations are expected to be completed and operational by January 2025.
The investment and additional operating days will increase the plant’s output by 16 percent.
Barry Owens, senior vice president of Bridgestone Americas Manufacturing Group, stated that the company celebrates the growth and commitment of the plant and community.
Owens noted the physical expansion is driven by growing demand for products and the team’s commitment to delivering innovation and superior quality for customers globally.
Owens highlighted the company's vital role in the transformation of mobility through building greener retreads to maximize fuel efficiency for fleets.

facts: Bridgestone Americas announced Bridget Neal has been named president, off-the-road (OTR), Bridgestone Americas Tire Operations, effective Aug. 14.
Neal succeeds Taylor Cole, who recently was named president of Firestone Building Products, LLC.
Kurt Danielson, president, commercial group, U.S. and Canada, BATO, stated that Bridget has developed extensive knowledge of the commercial tire business across her 15 year career with Bridgestone.
Neal has held a number of leadership roles in critical business areas, including supply and demand planning, logistics, marketing and more.
As president, Neal will lead all aspects of the Bridgestone OTR tire business in North America, including strategic planning, product and technology introductions, sales and engineering.
She will be responsible for aligning the company’s go-to-market strategies with the broader commercial portfolio, while also helping drive coordination for the Bridgestone OTR business globally.

Additionally, Neal will continue to advance the company’s efforts to develop industry-leading technology solutions that track tire performance and help OTR customers improve their productivity and profitability.
Neal joined Bridgestone in August 2002 as a supply planning and sourcing engineer.

During her tenure, she has held roles of increasing responsibility within the company’s commercial tire business, including commercial supply chain manager; manager of distribution programs for Bridgestone Bandag retread systems; account executive for original equipment commercial truck and bus tires; and general manager of original equipment and government sales for the truck and bus business.
She was promoted to her current position as director, OTR tire sales, in 2015.
Neal earned a bachelor’s degree in Industrial and Systems Engineering from the Georgia Institute of Technology.
source_title: Bridgestone Americas names new President for OTR Business

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced he will lead an international mission to Germany and Poland to strengthen existing economic and cultural ties while developing new relationships for Georgians.
Over the last six years, international companies have invested over $38 billion in Georgia, creating more than 58,000 new opportunities for Georgians.
Communities outside of metro Atlanta attracted over 80% of the jobs from international investments in Georgia.
Germany is one of Georgia's largest trade partners and a major contributor to job creation and investment in the state.
The mission aims to reinforce collaboration with Germany while opening dialogues with Poland, an emerging market.
Representatives from the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) will accompany the Governor on the mission.
The mission itinerary includes meetings with firms already operating or under construction in Georgia.
Notable German companies on the itinerary include BASF, Gerresheimer, Carcoustics, KIRCHHOFF Automotive, and GEDIA Automotive Group.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that German companies excel in industries like life sciences and automotive manufacturing.
Georgia serves as a strategic gateway for German and other European businesses entering the U.S. market.
Over the past two decades, German automotive firms alone have contributed approximately 2,200 jobs and $830 million in investments to Georgia communities.
The growth of life sciences companies like Gerresheimer demonstrates how international businesses become integral to Georgia's economy.
Exploring emerging markets such as Poland allows Georgia to stay ahead of global trends, build new partnerships, and support local companies seeking to expand internationally.
The State of Georgia has had continuous representation in Europe since 1973.
Germany is the fourth largest export and import market for the State of Georgia.
More than $12.8 billion in total trade moved between Georgia’s global gateways and Germany in 2023.
German companies have invested more than $3.9 billion in Georgia since 2010 through projects with state involvement.
An estimated 650 German-owned facilities support approximately 42,000 jobs for Georgians across the state.

Georgia-based companies such as Alimera Sciences, Bridgestone Golf, Delta Air Lines, Eversheds Sutherland, Graphic Packaging Holding, Novelis, Porex Corporation, and WestRock maintain operations in Germany.
BASF’s manufacturing presence in Georgia includes operations in Attapulgus, Edgar, and Savannah.
Gerresheimer first established a production facility in Peachtree City in 1993 and expanded in 2009 with the establishment of a Technical Competence Center (TCC).
Gerresheimer announced additional expansions in Georgia in December 2023 and May 2024.
In 2017, Carcoustics established Carcoustics USA South Inc. in Buford, Georgia, including a central research center and a manufacturing presence.
KIRCHHOFF Automotive opened an assembly facility in Lawrenceville in 2023.
In 2020, GEDIA Automotive Group announced an advanced manufacturing facility that will begin production with a focus on parts for electric vehicles in the greater Dalton area.
source_title: Kemp to lead mission to reinforce economic partnerships in Europe

facts: Club Car is a global leader in small-wheel, zero-emissions vehicle production.
Club Car announced the grand opening of its new Appling Manufacturing Facility in Georgia.

The facility opening event featured a ribbon-cutting ceremony and tours attended by Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, First Lady Marty Kemp, and other state and local elected officials and business leaders.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Club Car is bringing jobs into Georgia within the growing e-mobility sector.
Governor Kemp noted that the company is leading the way in compact utility EV manufacturing.
Governor Kemp lauded the company's significant contributions to Georgia's economy and its commitment to maintaining the state as a prime business destination.
The new Appling facility spans 105,000 square feet of flexible production and warehouse space.
Club Car committed an $8 million capital investment into the facility over a two-year period.
The facility's state-of-the-art production line is capable of producing up to 20 vehicles daily.
The expansion aims to meet growing U.S. demand for Club Car's vehicles through a customer-focused approach.
Club Car President and CEO Mark Wagner highlighted the company's impact on commercial fleet electrification, mini-mobility, and last-mile delivery.
Club Car has been based in Georgia for more than sixty years.
The company emphasizes quality, safety, and reliability as core components of its product manufacturing.
source_title: Club Car Unveils New Manufacturing Facility in Georgia

facts: Platinum Equity acquired Club Car from industrial conglomerate Ingersoll Rand in 2021 for approximately $1.7 billion.
The acquisition moved Club Car from a division inside a publicly traded corporation to a standalone business under private ownership.
Tom Gores founded Platinum Equity in 1995, building it into a firm managing roughly $48 billion in assets across about 60 operationally complex businesses.
Club Car had been a subsidiary of Ingersoll Rand since 1995, operating within its broader portfolio for over 25 years before the 2021 sale.
Under Platinum Equity ownership, the company's entire budget and strategic focus revolve around the small-vehicle market rather than competing for capital with other business units.
In April 2022, Club Car announced a deal to acquire Garia A/S, a Denmark-based electric low-speed vehicle manufacturer, from Lars Larsen Group.
The April 2022 acquisition also included Melex, a Polish manufacturer of lightweight utility vehicles that Garia had acquired months earlier.
Mark Wagner, who led Club Car as President and CEO through the transition from Ingersoll Rand to Platinum Equity, retired effective August 1, 2025.
Craig Scanlon replaced Mark Wagner as President and CEO on June 9, 2025.
Craig Scanlon spent 18 years at Polaris, rising to Chief Marketing Officer, and most recently served as CEO of K&N Engineering before joining Club Car.
Scanlon actually started his career in 1998 selling Club Car Carryall vehicles when the company was still part of Ingersoll Rand.
Club Car traces its origins to 1958 when Landreth Machine was founded in Houston, Texas.
In 1962, Bill Stevens Sr. acquired the company and relocated operations to Augusta, Georgia, officially launching Club Car LLC.
Club Car's global headquarters and primary manufacturing operations remain in Augusta, Georgia, where the company has been based for over 60 years.
The Augusta facility handles assembly and engineering for the company's full vehicle lineup, from golf cars to commercial utility vehicles.
Despite the change to private equity ownership, Club Car's manufacturing footprint stayed in Augusta, and the plant remains a significant employer in the area.
With the Garia and Melex acquisitions, Club Car now has production capacity in Denmark and Poland, giving it a three-continent manufacturing presence.
The Augusta site remains the center of gravity for engineering, design, and the bulk of production volume.

Club Car's product line spans three broad categories: personal vehicles (Onward series in standard and street-legal LSV configurations, CRU, XRT utility model), golf course operators (Tempo fleet integrating with Visage GPS and connectivity platform), and commercial side (Carryall utility and transport vehicles for campuses, resorts, airports, and industrial facilities).
The company has been pushing hard into lithium-ion battery technology across its lineup.
Club Car's lithium-ion platform is available on Tempo, Carryall, and Onward vehicles, featuring a steel, watertight battery enclosure that meets IP67 waterproofing standards.
The lithium-ion system includes smart charging that allows off-peak programming and extended storage without monthly state-of-charge checks.
For fleet operators, the reduced maintenance and longer lifespan of lithium batteries compared to traditional lead-acid packs can meaningfully lower total cost of ownership over time.

Private equity firms typically hold portfolio companies for five to seven years before selling, focusing on growing revenue, expanding margins, and building value for an eventual exit through a sale or IPO.
source_title: Who Owns Club Car? Platinum Equity’s $1.7B Acquisition - LegalClarity

facts: Club Car is a global leader in innovation and design for golf cars, low-speed utility vehicles, and personal-use transportation.
Mark Rickell serves as Vice President of Sales at Club Car and was featured in The Means Report news broadcast on April 16, 2024.

In 2024, American manufacturers in the low-speed personal transport vehicle (LSPTV) industry petitioned the International Trade Commission to address illegal dumping and subsidies on LSPTVs imported from China.
Club Car joined a petition to the ITC and Department of Commerce targeting illegally subsidized products dumped into the U.S. market in violation of commerce and trade laws.
Rickell stated that while the petition saw success, the company must remain vigilant as competing companies try to evade tariffs.
Club Car manufactures its vehicles to automotive standards and utilizes road load simulators to test every aspect of product safety and reliability.
The company recently previewed the Onward LSV and new Tempo upgrades as latest additions to its product line for recreational and commercial users.
Club Car's product portfolio has expanded from golf fleet operations to include vehicles for commercial, personal, and street-legal low-speed use.
The Club Car Vehicle Group includes the luxury electric vehicle brand Garia and the lightweight electric utility vehicle brand Melex.

The facility is dedicated to the production of advanced aluminium automotive structural components and crash management systems.

The facility is strategically located to supply automakers in the Southeast U.S.
Constellium may expand the facility to 220,000 sq. ft. in the future to meet customer supply needs.
Constellium expects to have 150 employees at the White, Georgia location by 2019.

Paul Warton, President of Constellium’s Automotive Structures & Industry business unit, stated that being closer to customers’ assembly plants will allow better service to automakers in making vehicles lighter and safer and responding to growing demand for aluminium structural parts.

Eric Krepps, Vice President and General Manager of Constellium Automotive Structures North America, extended gratitude to the State of Georgia, Bartow County, local partners, and employees for their strong support in establishing the White, Georgia plant.
