facts: Barnesville-Lamar County will lose a major employer in late 2026 when Continental Tires shuts down its Aldora Mills operation.
Aldora celebrated 136 years of local operations in May prior to the shutdown announcement.
A meeting between company officials and local leaders was scheduled for November 14, 2025, to provide more information on the shutdown.
Continental announced its decision to discontinue operations at the Aldora Mills textile manufacturing plant in Barnesville, Georgia, by the end of 2026.
The Aldora Mills facility employs approximately 230 people.
The facility produces textile reinforcement materials, including tire cord fabric, hose yarn, and knitted fabric.
The produced materials are used exclusively within Continental’s Tires and ContiTech group sectors.
Continental will support affected employees during the transition by offering career counseling and access to employment resources in collaboration with local partners.
Continental acquired the Aldora Mills facility in 1988.
The decision to shut down the plant follows a comprehensive business review aimed at safeguarding profitability, competitiveness, and business performance in the Americas region.
The plant has not been cost-competitive for many years despite continuous turnaround efforts.
Ongoing changes in global market dynamics and supply chains necessitated the shutdown decision.
Continental reaffirms its commitment to customers and partners in the United States.
Continental operates tire manufacturing facilities in Plymouth (Indiana), Clinton (Mississippi), Sumter (South Carolina), and Mount Vernon (Illinois).
Continental’s U.S. tire plants have an annual production capacity of more than 16 million tires.
Continental employs more than 8,800 people in the U.S. across its Tires and ContiTech group sectors.
In the last decade alone, Continental has invested around 1.5 billion USD in its tire manufacturing operations in the United States.
In October (2025), Continental announced a more than 85 million USD investment to expand manufacturing operations at its ContiTech plant in Mount Pleasant, Iowa.
In September 2024, Continental broke ground on its first fully owned tire distribution center in the Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex, scheduled to open in early 2026.
The new Dallas–Fort Worth distribution center will complement seven partner-owned distribution centers currently serving the U.S. market.
source_title: ALDORA MILLS SHUTTING DOWN - Barnesville.com

facts: Continental has operated a brake parts manufacturing facility on Lovers Lane in Culpeper since 1977.
The Germany-based parent company announced it would close the local facility in 2024.
The closure is part of a major global restructuring in response to dramatic decreases in vehicle production made worse by the pandemic.
The plant closing will eliminate 225 jobs.
The Culpeper County plant manufactures a valve block and its housing unit.
The valve block and housing unit are used by carmakers in a range of vehicles.
source_title: Continental to close Culpeper plant in 2024, eliminating 225 jobs

facts: Continental AG improved its adjusted EBIT margin by 260 basis points year over year, demonstrating resilience in a challenging market.
The company successfully concluded most of its pricing negotiations in the automotive sector, contributing to improved financial performance.
Continental AG reduced headcount by over 3,000 employees and is targeting EUR200 million in cost savings for 2024.
The tires segment showed strong performance with a 3.5% organic sales growth, driven by solid passenger vehicle replacement market sales.
The company is making strides in R&D efficiency, aiming for a high single-digit R&D to sales percentage by 2027, with a 30 basis point reduction achieved in Q3 2024.
Continental AG faces persistent weakness in industrial markets, particularly affecting its ContiTech segment.
The company adjusted its guidance for ContiTech, reducing the sales outlook by EUR400 million and the EBIT margin by 70 basis points.
The automotive sector continues to be burdened by weak global production volumes and high customer inventory levels.
Continental AG is dealing with tax investigations by Italian authorities, leading to provisions in the low to mid double-digit million EUR range.
The company anticipates further raw material and labor cost headwinds in Q4 2024 and into 2025, which could impact profitability.
90% of pricing negotiations have been successfully closed with a significant portion being sustainable.
Uncertainties remain, and further rounds of pricing negotiations will be needed in 2025.
Continental AG achieved EUR100 million in cost savings year-to-date and is targeting EUR200 million by the end of 2024.
The EUR400 million savings target is focused on administrative functions, with additional savings expected in 2025.
A portion of Continental's US tire sales is covered by European production, with additional sourcing from South America.
The US cannot supply itself exclusively via the domestic market due to insufficient capacity.
Regarding a braking issue with BMW, vehicles can be delivered with the brakes and the company is clarifying the situation with BMW.
Continental has reviewed and adjusted the provisions built up in previous quarters related to the BMW braking issue.
Strong replacement tire business in Europe and APAC is expected to continue, with a focus on increasing premium business in the ultra-high-performance segment.
Continental has invested around EUR3 billion over the last decade in the US and plans to continue at that level.
The US tire market requires imports due to insufficient domestic capacity.
The tire pricing environment is influenced by raw material and labor cost increases, with independent pricing decisions made as needed.
It is too early to discuss 2025 semiconductor pricing as negotiations are ongoing.
There is no automatic pass-through for semiconductor costs in contracts, and adjustments depend on negotiations.
source_title: Continental AG (CTTAF) Q3 2024 Earnings Call Highlights: Resilience Amid Market Challenges

facts: Continental is a Germany-based tiremaker.
Continental has announced its decision to cease operations at the Aldora Mills textile manufacturing plant in Barnesville, Georgia.
The closure of the Aldora Mills textile plant will occur by the end of 2026.
The decision to close the plant is part of Continental's strategy to enhance competitiveness amid global market changes.
source_title: Continental to close Aldora Mills textile plant in US by 2026 - Automotive Technology Insight | Forecasts | Industry News | Supply Chain

entity_name: HL-GA Battery Co. / Hyundai-LG Battery Complex
facts: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp's pro-investment stance collided with his tough-on-immigration rhetoric after immigration authorities raided a battery plant construction site and detained foreign workers.
Approximately 475 people were held in the raid, including more than 300 South Korean nationals.
The raid targeted the construction site for a battery plant to be operated by HL-GA Battery Co., a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution.
The battery plant is slated to open next year (2026).
The facility is part of a $7.6 billion complex planned to eventually employ 8,500 people.
The complex was initially announced and later expanded following Kemp's trips to South Korea to court Hyundai officials.
The detentions sparked questions in South Korea regarding U.S.-South Korea relations, particularly concerning Hyundai and its partners.
Hyundai stated it is investing $26 billion in the United States from 2025 to 2028.
This $26 billion investment includes the Ellabell plant, located about 50 miles (80 kilometers) west of Savannah, where Hyundai Motor Group manufactures electric vehicles.
The investment also covers a separate $5 billion battery joint venture in Cartersville, Georgia.
Additional investments include a steel mill in Donaldsonville, Louisiana, a robotics hub, an expansion of the Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama, and the Kia Corp. plant in West Point, Georgia.

Kemp's office issued a written statement on Friday asserting: "In Georgia, we will always enforce the law, including all state and federal immigration laws. All companies operating within the state must follow the laws of Georgia and our nation."
Kemp cannot run for governor again in 2026 and passed up a U.S. Senate race, but is entertaining discussions about a potential 2028 presidential run.
Kemp's office did not immediately respond to questions regarding whether the raid would have a long-term impact on Hyundai's investments.
The state Department of Public Safety coordinated with ICE to support the raid.
This is Kemp's second term as governor, during which he has declared a goal of making Georgia the "electric mobility capital of America."

Kemp has a history of tough-on-immigration rhetoric, including a 2018 ad where he said he would use his pickup truck to round up "criminal illegals," and he backed a state law requiring local jails to check prisoners' immigration status.

Kemp is aligning more closely with President Donald Trump's administration on immigration enforcement, having last month announced the mobilization of 75 National Guard soldiers to support ICE operations in Georgia.
Kemp also announced sending more than 300 National Guard members to participate in Trump's law enforcement takeover in Washington, D.C.
Kemp has traveled twice to South Korea to meet with Hyundai and LG Group executives.

Kemp disputes claims that tax dollars subsidized the jobs of detained workers; his administration states none of the $2.1 billion in projected state and local incentives for the Hyundai complex directly benefits subcontractors employing detainees.
Georgia's Department of Economic Development stated: "Without exception, the Department expects anyone doing business in Georgia to follow federal and state laws."
Kemp has clashed with Democratic U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff over who deserves credit for Georgia's green energy boom.

Kemp disputes that incentives signed by President Joe Biden spurred investment, claiming industries were already moving to Georgia before the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act, though he acknowledges the rush accelerated after Biden signed the climate law.
According to E2, an environmental business group, 33 additional green energy projects announced by the end of 2024 were the most nationwide.
Green energy projects in Georgia top $20 billion in value, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
Hyundai has continued its investment commitments even as President Trump and congressional Republicans have rolled back electric vehicle incentives.
source_title: Georgia Governor's Electric Mobility Dream for Georgia Tested After South Koreans Detained in Raid

facts: Continental Tire broke ground on a new building in Sumter, South Carolina on August 29, 2022.
Over 126,000 square feet will be added to the existing two million-square-foot tire manufacturing facility.
The new expansion area will house additional production equipment for current production and future capacity needs.

Benny Harmse, Continental plant manager and vice president of Manufacturing, stated that the new production building is an investment in the future of the Sumter facility and exemplifies the trust Continental has in dedicated employees who continue to deliver quality products to customers.
Continental will begin the installation of a new extruder in May 2023.
The new extruder will be operational by November 2023.
The extruder installation is intended to support the current production capacity.
Approximately 10 new jobs will be created by 2023 to support tire production at the facility.
The Continental Tire Sumter plant produces high-quality, premium lines of passenger and light truck tires for original equipment and replacement markets.
The Sumter Plant employs a growing team of approximately 1,300 employees.
The facility holds IATF 16949:2016 certification.
The facility holds ISO 14001 certification.
The Sumter Plant operates as a zero-landfill facility.
Continental Tire the Americas, LLC is the parent organization of the Sumter plant.
Continental was founded in 1871 and develops technologies and services for sustainable and connected mobility.
In 2021, Continental generated sales of €33.8 billion and employed more than 190,000 people across 58 countries and markets.
On October 8, 2021, Continental celebrated its 150th anniversary.
The Tires group sector operates 24 production and development locations worldwide.
Continental is a leading tire manufacturer with more than 57,000 employees in the tire sector and posted €11.8 billion in sales for the sector in 2021.
The company's tire business portfolio includes services for the tire trade and fleet applications, as well as digital management systems for tires.
source_title: Continental Tire Breaks Ground on New Sumter Building

facts: Continental Tire the Americas is a major subsidiary of Continental AG.
The company manufactures and distributes a complete premium line of passenger, light truck, and commercial tires for original equipment and replacement markets.
Founded in 1871, the technology company offers safe, efficient, intelligent, and affordable solutions for vehicles, machines, traffic, and transportation.
Continental Tire the Americas is listed as a participating contractor under the NASPO ValuePoint portfolio program for the State of Georgia (along with other states).
The statewide contract was awarded on 04/01/2019, with an expiration date of 06/30/2024 and a renewal limit of 06/30/2024.
Marshall Prewitt is listed as the contractor contact for this agreement.
Available contract documents include Continental Model PA, Contract Amendment 2, Continental Amendment 1 Jan 2019, and the Continental Technical Proposal.
source_title: Portfolio Contractor - NASPO ValuePoint

facts: Daechang Seat Corp. USA is a supplier of automotive seat frames.
The company will invest $72.5 million in Georgia’s Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The facility is located near Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The project will create more than 500 jobs.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA is a Korea-based company that employs more than 5,000 people in seven countries.
Jinsuk Lee, CEO of Daechang Seat Savannah Corp., stated the primary objective is to drive the growth of DSC through facility expansions, ultimately transforming DSC into a global entity.
DSC currently holds a prominent partnership with Hyundai Transys and Hyundai Mobis at HMGMA.
DSC expressed hope to collaborate with even more projects in the future.
source_title: Daechang Seat Corp. USA to create 500 jobs in Georgia

facts: Daechang Seat Corp. USA is a supplier specializing in automotive seat frames.
The company will invest $72.5 million in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The new facility will be located in close proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The investment will create over 500 jobs.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the news of another HMGMA supplier coming to Georgia highlights the impact projects like the Hyundai Metaplant have beyond their initial investment.
To date, suppliers for the Hyundai Metaplant project alone reach at least nine counties.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed excitement about adding Daechang Seat Corp. USA to Georgia's growing manufacturing base centered around Chatham County and the Savannah region.
Daechang Seat Corp. (DSC) was established in 1979 in Kyungju, South Korea.
DSC has become one of the largest seat frame producers in the world.
Globally, DSC employs more than 5,000 people in seven countries.
The company’s production process includes fully automatic lines with arc welding and spot-welding machines.
Jinsuk Lee is the CEO of Daechang Seat Savannah Corp.

Jinsuk Lee expressed appreciation to the State of Georgia, local communities, and the City of Savannah for DSC's role in creating improved opportunities and employment prospects within Chatham County and the Savannah area.
Jinsuk Lee stated DSC's primary objective is to drive growth through facility expansions to transform DSC into a global entity.
DSC currently holds a prominent partnership with Hyundai Transys and Hyundai Mobis at HMGMA.
DSC hopes to collaborate with even more projects in the future.
DSC’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The company will be hiring for assembly operators, equipment technicians, mechanical engineers, and electrical engineers.
Hiring will occur through local job fairs and website postings in the future.
Griff Lynch is the Georgia Ports Authority President and CEO.
Griff Lynch stated that DSC is only minutes from the Port of Savannah, ideally located to take advantage of Georgia’s world-class connectivity.
The Georgia Ports Authority aims to provide the best global gateway for U.S. supply chains and welcomed DSC as a job creator in Georgia’s automaking sector.
Trip Tollison is the Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO.
Trip Tollison announced that Daechang Seat Corp. USA will be the third Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America supplier to locate in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The project brings 500 new jobs and $72.5 million in capital investment to Chatham County.
Trip Tollison described Daechang Seat Corp. USA as a well-established global company and expressed anticipation for them growing and becoming part of the community.
Alyce Thornhill is the Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team.
Thornhill represented GDEcD on this competitive project in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Pat Wilson is the GDEcD Commissioner.
Pat Wilson stated that countless partners are helping transform Georgia into a center of the automotive and mobility industry and welcomed DSC in Savannah.
Pat Wilson noted that Savannah is an established global gateway and Chatham County has leveraged that advantage to create a bustling community.
As the market transitions to electric vehicles, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain.
Georgia has created more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
DSC has recently ventured into the electric vehicle sector and continues to expand its scope.
DSC anticipates greater opportunities for growth with the completion of new manufacturing facilities, providing larger installation spaces.
source_title: Automotive Supplier Daechang Seat Corp. USA to Invest $72.5 Million in Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center - AllOnGeorgia

facts: A South Korean company that makes automotive seat frames will open a factory in Georgia to supply a new Hyundai Motor Group plant.
Daechang Seat Corp., based in Gyeongju, South Korea, announced plans to invest $72.5 million in a plant in Savannah and hire more than 500 people.
The company is the latest to announce a plant to supply Hyundai's $7.6 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, near Savannah.
Hyundai's Ellabell plant was announced last year and could grow to 8,500 employees and begin producing vehicles as early as 2024.
Suppliers to the Hyundai plant have pledged to invest nearly $2.3 billion and hire nearly 5,800 people.
Daechang Seat opened a factory in Phenix City, Alabama, in 2019.
The company announced a $23.4 million expansion in Phenix City last year, which includes building a new facility and hiring an additional 300 people.
Daechang Seat currently employs more than 5,000 people across seven countries.
Jinsuk Lee, CEO of Daechang Seat Savannah Corp., stated that the company's primary objective is to drive growth through facility expansions to transform DSC into a global entity.
Workers at the new Georgia plant will earn an average wage of more than $18 per hour, excluding bonuses and benefits.
Angela Hendrix serves as a spokesperson for the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
The state of Georgia will pay to train workers for the new facility.
Daechang Seat could qualify for $10 million in state income tax credits, valued at $4,000 per job over five years, provided workers earn at least $31,300 annually.
Local officials have granted a 15-year property tax reduction projected to save the company $5.8 million.
Local officials are selling land for the plant at a reduced rate.
source_title: South Korean auto parts maker plans $72.5M plant near new Hyundai facility in Georgia, hiring 500 - Statesboro Herald

facts: A South Korean company plans to build a $72 million factory in Georgia to make parts for electric vehicles, hiring more than 140 workers.
Daesol Ausys announced its plans to build a factory in West Point, Georgia, to manufacture luggage boards and covers for compartments in electric vehicles.
The company supplies parts to Hyundai, Kia, and General Motors.
This facility will be the third factory in the same West Point industrial park for members of the company's corporate family.
Daehan Solution Co. opened a million factory there in 2008 to make headliners and other sound insulation parts for the nearby Kia plant.
Daesol Material opened a $35 million factory there in 2017 to make automotive fiber padding.
Daehan Solution currently employs 350 full-time workers, while Daesol Material employs 50 workers.
Daehan Solution also operates a plant near Montgomery, Alabama, that supplies the Hyundai assembly plant there.

The project follows a wave of electric vehicle suppliers announcing plants in Georgia after Hyundai said in 2022 that it would build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The Hyundai Ellabell site could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
EV suppliers have pledged to invest nearly $2.2 billion and hire 5,000 people in Georgia.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that the state’s automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for the jobs of the future.
Daesol Ausys is scheduled to begin production in December 2024.
The company will pay workers $15 to $20 an hour.
The state of Georgia will pay to train the factory's workers.
The state provided $500,000 last year to help Harris County clear and grade the site and build a road, with matching funds coming from county sales tax collections.
The city of West Point and Harris County are granting eight years of property tax breaks worth a projected $5.7 million.
Daesol Ausys could qualify for $875,000 in state income tax credits, at $1,250 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
source_title: South Korean firm to construct $72M EV parts factory in Georgia | Fox News

facts: The company operates in the automotive business sector.
The facility address is 791 Progress Pkwy West Point GA 31833.
The company is currently hiring for a Quality Specialist position.
The Quality Specialist role requires a Bachelor's Degree and is designated as an entry-level career path.
The Quality Specialist job posting has a closing date of until filled.

The Accounting Specialist role requires a Bachelor's Degree and is designated as an entry-level career path.
The Accounting Specialist job posting has a closing date of until filled.
Both the Quality Specialist and Accounting Specialist positions are located in West Point, Georgia.

facts: A $72 million auto parts plant project will be built in Harris County.
The project will create more than 140 new jobs in the Columbus area.
The manufacturing facility is located in West Point, Georgia.
Daesol Ausys Georgia will join the local automotive industry alongside Kia Motors, Adient, Daehan Solutions, Daesol Materials, Kopla, and Hyundai Glovis.
Kia Motors opened its only U.S. manufacturing plant in the area in 2010.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that West Point has become an automotive capital in its own right.
Georgia's automotive industry currently employs over 50,000 Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose locations across the state.
Daesol Ausys is a South Korean company established in 2017.
Daesol Ausys designs and manufactures motor and electric vehicle interior components and accessories for carmakers such as Kia, Hyundai, and General Motors.
CEO Min Ho Kwon stated the company is committed to delivering world-class services that exceed client requirements.
The company expressed excitement about the project in the Northwest Harris Business Park and looks forward to continuing its partnership with the State of Georgia and Harris County.
The new facility is expected to open in December 2024.
At the new facility, Daesol Ausys will produce luggage boards and luggage covers.
The company plans to hire personnel for roles in production, purchasing, quality control, maintenance, human resources, and management.
Robert Grant, chairman of the Harris County Commission, stated that Daesol Ausys' decision to locate in the Northwest Harris Business Park represents a commitment to Harris County.
West Point and Harris County have a strong relationship with Daesol Ausys' sister companies Daehan Solutions and Daesol Materials.
Local officials have been working closely with company officials to establish this new venture in West Point.
Craig Greenhaw, chairman of the Harris County Development Authority, welcomed Daesol Ausys as a new corporate citizen and noted the venture will bring jobs and capital investment into the region.
source_title: Daesol Ausys Georgia to open new facility in West Point, GA | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

facts: Three Hyundai suppliers plan to add manufacturing plants in Georgia for a combined investment of nearly $180 million.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the series of investments from Hyundai suppliers.
Automotive parts manufacturer NVH Korea will invest over $72 million in a new manufacturing facility in Locust Grove, Georgia.
NVH Korea will produce EV battery system components that protect, connect, and monitor battery cells' performance.
The NVH Korea facility is expected to create 160 jobs.
Operations at the NVH Korea plant are anticipated to begin in Q2 of 2024.
The Locust Grove plant will be NVH Korea's second Georgia facility.
NVH Korea's subsidiary, AFS America, has a facility in Columbus that produces floor and carbo mats and cargo trays.
South Korea-based Daesol Ausys will establish a $72 million facility in West Point.
The Daesol Ausys plant will create 140 jobs in production, quality control, maintenance, human resources, and management.
Production at the Daesol Ausys plant is expected to begin in December 2024.
Daesol Ausys will produce luggage boards and covers.
Daesol Ausys is a supplier for General Motors and Kia.
Kia has a manufacturing facility in West Point.
In July, Kia announced it was investing $200 million to expand the plant to produce its new electric SUV model beginning in Q2 of next year.
Car seat maker Das Corp will establish a $35 million plant in Metter.
The Das Corp facility will produce automotive seat structures when operations begin in the second half of 2024.
The Das Corp investment will create 300 jobs.
These three factories follow Hyundai to Georgia after the automaker announced a $5.5 billion manufacturing campus in Ellabell last year.
The Hyundai Ellabell campus is the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated that suppliers for the Hyundai Metaplant resulted in over $2 billion in investment last fiscal year alone, helping Georgia achieve a third straight year of record-breaking economic growth.
In late May, Hyundai and LG Energy Solution struck a joint venture deal to build a $4.3 billion battery cell plant adjacent to Hyundai's EV campus.
At the beginning of September, Hyundai and LG Energy Solution announced an additional $2 billion investment toward the upcoming plant.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution will each take a 50% stake in the venture and facility.
Hyundai will assemble battery packs using cells from the joint venture plant to be sent to its U.S. manufacturing sites.
The total $6.3 billion investment will create 8,500 jobs.
source_title: Hyundai’s Georgia EV supplier network adds 3 more manufacturers | WardsAuto

facts: Pratt Industries Inc. is building a new production facility in Warner Robins.
The Warner Robins facility will be the 13th site in Georgia for the Atlanta-based recycled paper and packaging company.
Pratt Industries is the fifth-largest corrugated packaging company in the U.S.
The 496,000-square-foot facility will open in 2024.
The Warner Robins facility will bring Pratt Industries' total investment in Georgia to more than $800 million.
DAS Corp., a key supplier for both Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia and Hyundai Motor Group, is opening a new manufacturing facility in Metter.
The DAS Corp. facility represents a $35 million investment.
The Metter facility will mainly produce auto seat structures.
The Metter facility will create 300 new jobs.
Production at the Metter facility is scheduled to begin in late 2024.
CJ Foodville Corp., a subsidiary of Korean company CJ Group, is opening a $47 million bakery and food processing facility in Hall County.
The Hall County facility supports the expansion of the TOUS les JOURS bakery café brand.
At full capacity in 2025, the Hall County facility will produce more than 19,000 tons of products a year.
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions (LGES) are investing an additional $2 billion into their battery cell manufacturing joint venture at the Metaplant in Bryan County.
This expansion raises the total investment value of the Hyundai-LGES joint venture to more than $4.3 billion.
The Metaplant expansion will create another 400 jobs.
Korean auto interior manufacturer Daesol Ausys Georgia is opening a new facility in Harris County.
The Daesol Ausys Georgia facility represents a $72 million investment.
The Harris County facility will create more than 140 jobs.
Daesol Ausys Georgia is a key supplier for Kia Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group, and General Motors.
Operations at the Daesol Ausys Georgia facility are scheduled to begin in late 2024.
Fouts Bros. Inc. is expanding its operations in Milledgeville.
The Fouts Bros. expansion represents a $4 million investment.
As part of the expansion, the fire/rescue truck manufacturer is adding nearly 100 jobs this year.
An additional 70 jobs are planned to follow at the Fouts Bros. facility in 2024.
source_title: Economic Development Around the State - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: South Korean auto parts company Daesol Ausys Georgia is investing $72 million in a new manufacturing facility in West Point, Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the investment on Tuesday.
Daesol Ausys specializes in designing and manufacturing motor and electric vehicle (EV) interior components and accessories.
Daesol Ausys is a supplier to Kia Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group, and General Motors.
The new plant will manufacture luggage boards and luggage covers.
The facility will be located at the Harris County industrial park factory.
The new plant is expected to become operational in late 2024.
The new plant is expected to create 140 new jobs.
Min Ho Kwon serves as the CEO of Daesol Ausys.
The project is located in the Northwest Harris Business Park.

Min Ho Kwon stated that the company is committed to delivering world-class services that exceed client requirements and expectations, and expressed excitement about continuing its partnership with the State of Georgia and Harris County regarding this project.
Georgia has seen significant investments in automaker and auto parts supplier plants in recent years, particularly in the EV market.
Hyundai Motor Group is involved in two Georgia EV-related projects with an expected combined investment of approximately $11 billion.
One Hyundai Motor Group project involves an estimated $5.5 billion investment for an EV plant and EV battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County, Georgia.
The second Hyundai Motor Group project is valued at another $5.5 billion.
The second project is a joint venture between Hyundai and SK On.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis currently operates a plant in West Point, Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis supplies Kia and other automakers with cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies from its West Point location.
Hyundai Mobis plans to spend $926 million on an EV power electric system plant in Richmond Hill, Georgia.
The Richmond Hill plant will provide the Bryan County Hyundai plant with an anticipated 900,000 power electric systems annually.
The Richmond Hill plant will also provide the Bryan County Hyundai plant with an anticipated 450,000 integrated charging control units annually.
In May, Chicago-based Anovion Technologies announced an $800 million investment.
Anovion Technologies plans to build a 1.5-million-square-foot synthetic graphite anode materials manufacturing plant in Decatur County, Georgia.
The synthetic graphite produced at the Anovion facility will be used in the production of lithium-ion batteries.
source_title: Daesol Ausys entering Georgia EV market with $72M plant

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the state's automotive industry employs over 50,000 Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers expand across the state.
Daesol Ausys is scheduled to begin production at the new West Point factory in December 2024.
The company will pay workers $15 to $20 an hour, according to economic developer Meghan Richardson.
The state of Georgia will cover the cost of worker training.
The state provided $500,000 last year to help Harris County clear and grade the factory site and build a road.
Harris County matched the state's contribution using sales tax collections.
The city of West Point and Harris County are granting eight years of property tax breaks worth a projected $5.7 million.
Daesol Ausys could qualify for $875,000 in state income tax credits, calculated at $1,250 per job over five years.
To qualify for the state income tax credits, workers must earn at least $31,300 annually.

entity_name: Columbus State University & UGA Terry College of Business 2024 Economic Forecast Event
canonical_name: Columbus State University & UGA Terry College of Business 2024 Economic Forecast Event
facts: Business deans from Columbus State University and the University of Georgia hosted more than 200 local business leaders for an analysis of Georgia’s 2024 economic outlook on Tuesday in January 2024.

The event was part of the UGA Terry College of Business’ annual economic outlook statewide tour designed to share insights into the national, state and local factors shaping the economy for the coming year.

Dr. Deborah Kidder, dean of the D. Abbott Turner College of Business & Technology, and Dr. Benjamin Ayers, dean of the University of Georgia’s Terry College of Business, headlined the lunchtime event.

Dr. Benjamin Ayers declared the state’s economy is heading for a “soft landing” in 2024 as it fully recovers from some COVID-era challenges, while also outpacing the nation in several key performance indicators.

Ayers stated Georgia is well positioned to weather an economic slowdown and its economy will outperform the U.S. economy due to the build-out of many large projects in the economic development pipeline and favorable demographics.
Georgia’s post-COVID economic expansion is expected to slow but continue, with its gross domestic product (GDP) increasing by 1.1% in 2024.
The state's GDP growth rate will be smaller than the 3% growth rate Georgia saw in 2023 but will continue to outpace the national GDP growth rate, which forecasters project to be 0.8% in 2024.
The probability that Georgia will slip into recession is 33%, compared to around 50% for the U.S.
The number of jobs in Georgia will rise by 0.7% in 2024, which is slower compared to the 2.3% gain estimated for Georgia in 2023 but higher than the national job growth rate of 0.3%.
Ayers stated the forecast depends on the resilient labor market and the strong financial positions of households to sustain the post-pandemic economic expansion.
Pratt & Whitney’s current $206 million expansion of its Columbus Engine Center was noted as boosting both workforce development and economic projections in the Chattahoochee Valley region.
Daesol Ausys’ $72 million investment in a Harris County-based manufacturing facility was highlighted as a key industry boosting regional workforce development and economic projections.
The recently announced relocation of the Atlanta Braves Double-A franchise from Pearl, Mississippi, to Columbus’ Golden Park was mentioned alongside other local developments.
The community’s leisure, hospitality, healthcare, military and financial sectors were credited with bolstering the region’s overall economic stability.

Of greatest concern to civic and business leaders is Columbus’ slow population growth, despite Selig Center research pointing to annual statewide population growth of 0.7% in 2024 (more than double the 0.3% national average).
Outmigration concerns were underscored as Columbus residents relocate to Atlanta and Auburn, Alabama, especially in search of higher paying and high-tech jobs.
The region’s low cost of living and single-family home price gains remaining below the statewide average were cited as positives for attracting new residents and retaining current ones.

Survey data collected by the Turner College’s Butler Center for Research & Economic Development showed area residents list rising prices as their top concern, buttressed by related financial concerns involving income, affordable healthcare and debt, with crime following as a close second.
Business owners were cautious regarding their sales revenue projections for 2024, considering 36% reported that sales revenue declined in 2023.
Finding qualified employees to fill vacancies was cited as equally concerning for business owners.
62% of business owners reported hiring in the last year, and only 10% reported layoffs.
Over half of business owners expect to hire additional employees during the next 12 months.
Columbus State President Stuart Rayfield emphasized that the university must be lockstepped and aligned with the economic development going on in the community.
A new partnership initiative was launched with local business and human resource leaders to explore how Columbus State can better align its educational mission with what’s next for the region.
Employers need deeper experience in “career-ready competencies” such as adaptability, communication, problem-solving and critical thinking for the post-graduate workforce.
Columbus State is working to redesign its general education core curriculum to truly meet those career-ready competencies.

Rayfield stated Columbus State will provide the workforce for whatever the economic forecast holds and emphasized the imperative of sitting at the table with industry partners to understand and meet their needs.
source_title: Columbus State, UGA Partner to Share 2024 Economic Forecast - Columbus CEO

facts: Two pioneering companies, DAS Corp. and 4 Fungi's Regenerative, took the first steps toward a more sustainable tomorrow with a double groundbreaking ceremony set to create economic opportunities and foster growth in Candler County.
The two companies officially broke ground on Wednesday, November 15, 2023, in the I-16 Industrial Park in Metter.
Combined, the two companies will invest $62 million into Candler County.
Combined, the two companies will create 340 local jobs.
DAS Corp. (DAS) is a leading automotive parts company with a global presence that has chosen Metter as the location for its newest manufacturing facility.
The new DAS facility is state-of-the-art, will bolster production capacity, and stimulate economic growth in the region.
DAS specializes in designing and manufacturing seating systems, safety seating components, and seating structures.
DAS is a crucial supplier for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) and Hyundai Motor Group.
The new DAS manufacturing facility is projected to create 300 new local jobs.
The new DAS manufacturing facility will contribute over $35 million in investments to Candler County.

Sean Kim, Chief Operating Officer at DAS, stated that the strategic location near prominent cities like Savannah and Macon, facilitating convenient access, played a pivotal role in their decision-making process.
Operations at the new DAS facility are expected to commence in the latter half of 2024.
4 Fungi's Regenerative is a sustainably controlled environment agriculture (CEA) and food production company making waves in Metter with its new greenhouse project.
The company's unique approach to growing mushrooms and various crops aligns with the region's sustainable agricultural practices.
4 Fungi's Regenerative utilizes agricultural waste, circular economy principles, and reconstituted byproducts to enhance soil quality.
The new 4 Fungi's greenhouse is located at the Metter-Candler 1-16 Industrial Park.
The 4 Fungi's greenhouse project represents a $27 million investment.
The 4 Fungi's greenhouse project will create nearly 50 local jobs.

Grant Anderson, CEO of 4 Fungi's Regenerative, stated that their focus is on providing regional food resilient to global market volatility, nurturing community and soil health, and growing the local economy.
source_title: DAS Corp. and 4 Fungi's Regenerative break ground in Metter, GA  - Grice Connect

facts: A Japanese company announced plans for a $150 million plant to make copper foil for electric vehicle batteries in Augusta, Georgia.
The project location was moved from a previously announced site near an existing Camden, South Carolina, facility.
Nippon Denkai's American subsidiary said it would hire more than 100 people for the Georgia site.
The company could eventually triple production at the Georgia site from an initial 9,500 metric tons (10,500 tons) to 28,500 metric tons (31,500 tons).
Tripling production would raise investment to $430 million.
The expansion would create 250 jobs over the next five years.
Denkai America spokesperson Michael Coll stated, “Our goal is to triple the capacity in Augusta; market conditions will determine timeline.”
The company said it would raise its initial investment by $10 million from what it had announced in December.

It told Japanese investors that the 115-acre (47-hectare) Augusta site is better than the South Carolina location because there is room for expansion, because it can host an “advanced” equipment layout and because of a “key cost competitive edge in electricity price.”
State and local officials will give the company what could be more than $76 million in tax breaks and incentives.
Nippon Denkai said it will start construction in September.
The company hopes to be shipping samples to manufacturers by the summer of 2024.
The company said it sees a big opportunity to make copper foil for electric vehicle battery makers, saying many battery plants are being built in the United States, but few copper foil plants are.
Nippon Denkai said that companies will want domestic sources to avoid supply chain problems including risks of politically driven supply disruptions.
The company projects an annual 21.4% growth in copper foil demand through 2035.
Nippon Denkai said the ability to triple production in Augusta will allow it to pursue that growth.
Combined with the 30-year-old Camden plant, the company would have an American production capacity of more than 35,000 metric tons.
In June, Denkai announced a smaller $14 million expansion at the 80-worker Camden plant, also related to expansion in the electric battery and vehicle market.
Augusta-Richmond County will give property tax breaks valued at a projected $68.7 million over 25 years.
The company will pay $30 million in taxes, according to the Augusta Economic Development Authority.
The company gets free land valued at $2.25 million, said Cal Wray, the authority's president.
Georgia officials said they will spend an undisclosed amount of money to train workers.

Nippon Denkai could claim various state tax breaks, including an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $4,000 per job from state income taxes, up to $5 million over five years, as long as workers make at least $28,000 a year.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson pointed to the announcement as more fruit of the state's focus on what he calls a “generational opportunity” in electric vehicles, including the $5 billion Rivian Automotive plant east of Atlanta and the $5.5 billion Hyundai Motor Group plant near Savannah.
Wray said a $347 million, 125-job copper smelter announced by Aurubis AG in Augusta is another development driven by the growth in electric vehicles.
source_title: New Copper Foil Plant Moves from SC to Georgia | Industrial Equipment News

facts: Denkai America is the only North American manufacturer of high-quality electrodeposited copper foil used in printed electronics.
Denkai America will locate its new manufacturing facility and North American Headquarters in Richmond County.
The company’s initial investment will be $150 million to establish a copper foil production facility, with a focus on electric vehicle battery foil.
The facility will be located on a 115-acre site.
The company has a goal to invest a total of $430 million and create 250 new jobs over the next five years.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that selecting Denkai America strengthens Georgia's status as a leader in the emerging electric mobility industry.
Gov. Brian Kemp emphasized the state has been laser focused on bringing quality jobs to highly-trained Georgians and highlighted Georgia’s top-ranking pro-business environment.
Denkai America’s materials have traditionally been used in printed circuit board applications, primarily for the aerospace and defense industries.
The company's materials are also now used in the lithium-ion batteries powering electric vehicles.
Denkai has operated for more than 60 years with team members worldwide and operations in Japan and North America.
Denkai Group President and CEO Heday Nakajima, Ph.D., stated the investment highlights their commitment to leading ED battery foil manufacturing in North America.
Heday Nakajima expressed gratitude to the teams in Richmond County and the State of Georgia for making this project possible.
Construction is expected to begin in September 2022 as the first part of a three-phase investment.
The facility will be fully operational by December 2024.
The company will be hiring for skilled industrial technicians, production managers, and engineers.
Denkai America President and CEO Nobu Masuda stated the company is thrilled to expand its manufacturing presence into Augusta, which has the skilled and talented work force needed for growth.
EV-related projects have contributed more than $12.6 billion in investments to Georgia since 2020.
EV-related projects have contributed more than 17,800 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.
The Development Authority of Augusta, Georgia, welcomed Denkai America and its North American Headquarters to the Augusta Corporate Park.
Augusta Economic Development Authority Chairman Steven Kendrick stated the company will bring high-skilled jobs to Augusta and the Augusta Corporate Park.
Steven Kendrick thanked the Augusta Commission and the Augusta Utilities Department for their support and hard work to make this happen.
Augusta Corporate Park is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development Certified park.
To earn a GRAD program certification, a property must undergo due diligence and complete specific certification requirements prior to a visit by a prospective business.

Completing the certification program with the Georgia Department of Economic Development is a proactive way both community planners and landowners can help catalyze economic growth and industrial development.

facts: Japanese firm Nippon Denkai’s American subsidiary announced plans for a new copper foil facility and establishment of its North American headquarters at Augusta Corporate Park.
In a joint announcement on July 20 with Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp’s office, Denkai America committed an initial $150 million to the project, with the total investment growing to $430 million.
The project will create 250 jobs within five years.
Parent company Nippon Denkai envisions the new Augusta facility’s manufacturing output tripling within those 5 years, from 10,500 tons to 31,500 tons.
Denkai America is the only North American manufacturer of high-quality electrodeposited (ED) copper foil used in printed electronics.
Denkai’s materials have been used in both the aerospace and defense industries but are now used in lithium-ion batteries powering the electric vehicle market.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated the investment would further strengthen the state's status as a leader in emerging electric mobility and existing aerospace, defense, and technology industries.
Gov. Kemp emphasized a focus on bringing quality jobs to Georgians and praised Georgia’s top-ranking pro-business environment.
Construction on the 150-acre site is expected to begin in September, with the project completed and ready for operations by December 2024.

Denkai originally announced in December (prior to this article) plans to build a facility adjacent to its existing plant in Camden, S.C., but chose the Augusta site due to future expandability and cost competitiveness.
Nippon Denkai cited a key cost-competitive edge in electricity prices, local quality team members, and business-friendly local government support as reasons for choosing Augusta over Camden.
Through the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Denkai secured a $1.85 million EDGE grant from the Georgia Department of Community Affairs.
EDGE grants are designed for cross-state expansion and relocation projects to help Georgia compete for economic investment that might otherwise go to neighboring states.
Pat Wilson, commissioner for Georgia’s Department of Economic Development, stated Denkai America plays a vital role in the EV ecosystem.
Wilson noted Georgia is committed to harnessing electric mobility adoption to provide future jobs for Georgians currently employed in the automotive industry.
Gov. Kemp announced that Georgia is cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain intended to cut inefficiencies and reduce reliance on imported materials.
Georgia is actively incentivizing EV market investment, including Hyundai’s May announcement for a $5.5 billion EV plant in Bryan County, located about 30 minutes west of Savannah.
Steven Kendrick, chairman of the Augusta Economic Development Authority, called Denkai America a "great corporate citizen" that will bring high-skilled jobs to Augusta and the Augusta Corporate Park.

Denkai's settlement in Augusta Corporate Park follows less than a month after groundbreaking for German company Aurubis’ copper smelting and metals recycling plant at the same industrial park on Valencia Way.

Aurubis’ plant is expected to generate 125 jobs and received local financial backing via a Richmond County judge's May authorization for up to $760 million in revenue bonds for its construction on 150 acres.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and GDEcD announced Georgia sustained economic development momentum through key industries during H1 FY2023 (July 1 through December 31, 2022).
The 218 locations or expansions supported by GDEcD’s Global Commerce division are expected to create approximately 17,500 new jobs and more than $13 billion in investment for the state.
Both the number of locations and total investment amount increased over the same period last year.
Areas outside the 10-county metro Atlanta region accounted for 85 percent of jobs created and 92 percent of investments from economic development projects during the period.
Four of Georgia’s largest industries – advanced manufacturing, automotive, aerospace, and food processing – accounted for nearly 75 percent of new jobs and 84 percent of investments.
Planned investments resulting from these projects increased by more than 38 percent compared to the same time last year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson cited proven solutions such as the Georgia Quick Start job training program and GRAD certified sites that support accelerated project timelines.
The automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment, after advanced manufacturing.

In October, state and local officials joined company representatives including Korean Ambassador to the U.S. Tae-yong Cho for the groundbreaking of the new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) EV facility.
The HMGMA EV facility will support more than 8,100 direct jobs in Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham Counties.
Three off-site HMGMA suppliers announced locations following the groundbreaking, swiftly surpassing the initial $1 billion in investment predicted to come from such additions.
International companies have been valued partners in creating a closed-loop electric mobility supply chain and accounted for 23 percent of state projects since July 1, 2022.
The partnership between SK On and Hyundai Motor Group in Bartow County is a historic project example of international investment.
Next-generation developer FREYR Battery announced a facility in Coweta County as another historic project example.
Both the SK On/Hyundai and FREYR Battery projects are in the top five largest projects by investment in the state’s history.
Battery recyclers Sung-Il Hitech and Denkai America joined Georgia’s growing electric mobility ecosystem.
The aerospace and defense industries continued to benefit from Georgia’s logistics infrastructure and engineering talent pool, creating more than 1,500 new jobs.
Aerospace company Archer Aviation Inc. advancing sustainable urban air mobility announced facilities near airports.
Defense technology company Anduril Industries also announced facilities near airports.
Both Archer Aviation and Anduril Industries will benefit from nearby top-ranked technical colleges and four-year universities.
Advanced manufacturing is a cornerstone industry sector in Georgia’s middle and coastal regions.
YKK AP announced a major investment or expansion in Macon during FY 2023.
Beretta Holding S.A. announced a new facility in Bryan County through its subsidiary Norma Precision Inc. during FY 2023.
The advanced manufacturing sector has announced more than 7,700 new jobs in the state since July 1, 2022.

Germany-based Haering Precision celebrated the grand opening of its Lavonia facility in November with a two-day event including a “Future Day” for approximately 400 local high school students and community members.
Supported by agricultural strength and logistics capabilities, the food processing industry continued to choose Georgia for growth.
Companies in the food processing field announced more than $695 million in investments, largely through expansions.
King’s Hawaiian® announced another expansion of its Oakwood facility, marking its second expansion in less than a year.

GDEcD Deputy Commissioner for Global Commerce Kristi Brigman noted that companies connect with community partners to solve workforce challenges and utilize the Georgia Quick Start program to build talent from the ground up.
These programs help Georgians upskill, including those who are part of Georgia’s strong inbound domestic migration trends.
Life sciences and film industries welcomed additional facilities that will support industry breakthroughs and learning opportunities for students.
Georgia Tech hosted a groundbreaking for its Science Square purpose-built district in August.
The Rowen Foundation’s 2,000-acre science and learning campus broke ground in early December.

The Athena Studios expansion includes a structure dedicated to training the local workforce through a partnership with the University of Georgia and Georgia Film Academy.
BlueStar Studios at the former Fort Gillem outside Atlanta announced more than 600,000 square feet of new stage space.

BlueStar Studios will also add an additional 45,000 square feet dedicated to technical resources and amenities, which will include refurbished buildings that preserve the base’s historic architecture.

Following an extensive multi-state search including more than 50 site visits, Dinex selected Dublin due to logistics, supplier access, operating costs, workforce, and quality of life.

facts: Eaton Corporation established its eMobility business unit in 2018 to integrate electrical and vehicle segment expertise for vehicle electrification demands.
The initial commitment for the eMobility initiative was $500 million to develop intelligent power electronics, advanced power systems, and circuit protection technologies.
Eaton's eMobility offerings target battery electric vehicles (BEVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), and broader EV applications.

Components produced include onboard chargers, inverters, DC-DC converters, power distribution units, high-voltage fuses, stamped battery terminals, and automated transmissions optimized for electrified powertrains.
Eaton developed Breaktor circuit protection technology specifically designed for EV architectures to enhance safety and reliability under high-voltage conditions.
In the early 2020s, Eaton intensified its strategic pivot toward electrification and digital power management.
A 2023 initiative was launched to enhance European manufacturing and design capabilities for EV components to meet growing demand for electrified commercial and passenger vehicles.
By 2024, Eaton's ePowertrain offerings encompassed EV transmissions, gearing, and differentials to capitalize on the shift from internal combustion engines to battery-electric systems.
In February 2025, Eaton announced advanced testing facilities in Europe for ICE and EV powertrains to accelerate development amid varying electrification adoption rates.
In October 2025, Eaton unveiled a next-generation 800 VDC architecture aimed at supporting higher-power data centers and EV infrastructure through modular, scalable power distribution.

Eaton offers AC and DC chargers, power management systems, and software for residential, commercial, and fleet applications, incorporating bidirectional capabilities and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) integration.
A partnership with ChargePoint was established to provide ultrafast DC V2G chargers delivering up to 600 kW.
In May 2023, Eaton expanded its European eMobility operations to bolster design, technology, and manufacturing capacity amid rising EV adoption.
The Vehicle and Mobility Sector was established in August 2023 to unify the Vehicle Group and eMobility businesses for internal combustion engine, hybrid, and fully electrified vehicles.
In 2023, the Vehicle and Mobility group generated sales of $2.3 billion and employed over 14,000 people globally.
The sector's headquarters is in Southfield, Michigan, with key offices in Turin, Italy; Shanghai, China; and São Paulo, Brazil.
Eaton acquired Royal Power Solutions in 2022 to enhance high-voltage capabilities for electrified powertrains.
In March 2026, Eaton completed the acquisition of Boyd Thermal (the thermal management business of Boyd Corporation) for $9.5 billion.

The Boyd Thermal acquisition adds advanced cooling solutions including liquid cooling systems, cold plates, heat pipes, and thermal interface materials tailored for high-density applications like AI data centers.
These thermal capabilities integrate with Eaton's existing power management systems and air-based cooling innovations to provide end-to-end grid-to-chip thermal and power management solutions.
Eaton's Electrical Sector achieved global sales of $17.7 billion in 2024, representing a significant portion of the company's total revenue of $24.9 billion.
The Electrical Sector is targeting over $31 billion in sales by 2030 through approximately 10% organic compound annual growth rate and margin expansion to around 30%.
Eaton has invested over $1 billion in capacity expansions across more than 20 sites, adding over 2 million square feet of manufacturing space.
Eaton collaborates with suppliers, universities, and research institutions to advance emerging areas like next-generation materials and system designs for eMobility.
Dedicated eMobility labs are used to simulate real-world operational stresses on components before deployment.
In 2024, the Electrical Sector captured an estimated 27.6% of U.S. industry revenue in power conversion equipment manufacturing.
Eaton's overall revenue grew to $24.9 billion in 2024, marking a 7.3 percent increase from $23.2 billion in 2023.
For 2026, the company guided for organic growth of 7 to 9 percent and adjusted earnings per share of $13.00 to $13.50.
source_title: Eaton Corporation — Grokipedia

facts: Is leading, investing and executing for growth—driving revenue growth, margin expansion and returns for shareholders.

facts: Eaton Corporation announced they are closing their Toccoa manufacturing plant in Stephens County.
The targeted closing date for the Toccoa plant is during the first quarter of 2021.
According to Stephens County Development Authority Executive Director Tim Martin, the closure will displace 128 employees.
Displaced workers have options available to them, including finding work in other industries in Toccoa and surrounding counties that are actively hiring.
The Georgia Department of Labor is in the process of mobilizing its resources to assist those displaced workers.
According to Eaton Plant Manager Richard Haislip, the company has put together a robust severance package for its employees, which includes out-placement and counseling assistance.
Haislip stated it is important that they hold on to their local workforce until after the first of the year as the company has contracts to fulfill.
Once the plant is closed, plans are to market the land and building.
Eaton is a power management company made up of over 92,000 employees, doing business in more than 175 countries.
The company manufactures energy-efficient products and services to help customers effectively manage electrical, hydraulic and mechanical power more reliably, efficiently, safely and sustainably.

facts: Republican Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced that a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County will create 456 new jobs and invest about $205 million.
The facility will be opened by Ecoplastic Corporation, an automotive parts manufacturing company.
Governor Kemp stated the company will serve the Hyundai Metaplant well from Statesboro due to its close proximity to Georgia's ports system.
Ecoplastic was established in 1984 and is a Korea-based company that manufactures plastic automobile parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and other suppliers.
Ecoplastic is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and Kia.
The company engages in the manufacturing of automotive interior and exterior plastic parts, including bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
Ecoplastic’s new facility will be located at 4822 Highway 301 South Register near Statesboro.
The new plant is capable of injection molding and painting.
The facility will produce exterior parts, bumpers and garnishes, interior parts, consoles, and trims for electric vehicles.
The company expects to begin operations by October 2024.
The company will be hiring for HR, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection, and paint production positions.
Shang Han serves as the President and CEO of Ecoplastic.
Shang Han stated that Ecoplastic is honored to establish a production facility for electric vehicle parts in Bulloch County, Georgia.

Shang Han noted that by increasing new jobs within the area and providing a stable supply of high quality products to client HMGMA, the company looks forward to contributing to the economic development of the State of Georgia and Bulloch County.
Shang Han expressed appreciation for the support of the State and County governments and stated Ecoplastic will do its best to preserve a continuous and mutually beneficial relationship.
source_title: Georgia Governor Announces New Manufacturing Facility Will Bring 456 New Jobs And Invest $205 Million | Doc and the Block

facts: Hyundai is spearheading its new $7.59 billion “Metaplant” in Bryan County, Georgia, described as the largest economic development initiative in Georgia history.
The Metaplant site is located about an hour west of Savannah on Interstate 16 and spans nearly 3,000 acres.
The facility is scheduled to come online in early 2025.
Upon completion, the Metaplant will produce up to 300,000 electric vehicles each year.
Upon completion, the Metaplant will also produce the batteries that power the vehicles.

Chris Smith, chief project implementation officer for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, describes the project as a huge undertaking involving vehicle manufacturing, battery facilities, affiliate suppliers, construction teams, utilities, and government entities.
The project is anticipated to create an estimated 8,500 new jobs for the region.

During the site vetting process, Hyundai conducted an in-depth analysis of workforce demographics, education levels, and local population trends to confirm Bryan County and the Savannah area could support the facility and its jobs.
Eight additional suppliers plan to build their own sites in Georgia.
These supplier sites account for an additional $2 billion investment and another estimated 5,000 jobs.
South Korea-based supplier Ecoplastic has construction underway on a facility in nearby Statesboro.
The Ecoplastic facility will manufacture bumpers, consoles, and trim for EVs produced at the Metaplant.
The Ecoplastic facility will create around 450 jobs when completed next year.
The Ecoplastic facility could staff hundreds more as it increases capacity over time.
Charlie Kim, president and CEO of Ecoplastic America, states this is the company's first U.S. facility opening and aims to drive job creation and economic impact in Georgia alongside Hyundai.
Hyundai recently announced a joint $5 billion venture with SK On to establish an EV battery cell manufacturing facility in northern Georgia’s Bartow County.
The Hyundai-SK On joint venture plant will produce batteries for the EVs being built at the Metaplant and add an additional 3,500 jobs to the area.
According to the Georgia Department of Economic Development, the Hyundai Metaplant and supplier sites will have an estimated $4 billion annual economic impact.
Over the next 27 years, Hyundai is estimated to pay over $523 million in property tax payments.
A local health care provider will open a 7,700-square-foot on-site medical center for Hyundai employees next year as part of the community benefits.

Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority, expects the project to elevate nearby counties and communities, driving growth in housing development, schools, restaurant openings, and commercial activity built on quality jobs.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, notes that Hyundai has supported U.S. economic and environmental goals for nearly four decades through manufacturing investments and is leading the EV transition in the U.S.
source_title: Paid Program: The Ripple Effect of Investing in American Jobs

facts: Fouts Fire is an American fire apparatus manufacturer located in Milledgeville, Georgia.
The company was formerly known as Fouts Bros. Fire Equipment or Fouts Brothers.
Fouts Fire has been family-owned and operated since 1952.

Fouts Fire is a subsidiary of Fouts Bros Inc., a collection of commercial automotive equipment brands including Fouts Commercial Vehicles Inc., Smyrna Truck, Fouts Defense, Big Deal Parts, and Electric Commercial Vehicles Inc.
In 2019, Fouts moved their headquarters and primary production facility from Smyrna, GA, to Milledgeville, GA.
Fouts Bros was founded in 1952 by CJ and Jerry Fouts as a single service station in Smyrna, GA.
In 1989, the company opened its fire division with the introduction of the 3000 Gallon Tanker.
The company eventually expanded into a standardized line of fire tankers, pumpers, light rescues, and brush trucks.
In 2024, the fire division was rebranded as Fouts Fire.
Currently under third-generation family management, Fouts Fire is led by Scott Edens.
The company operates out of a 700,000 square foot production facility and headquarters in Milledgeville, GA.
Fouts Fire has a second factory located in La Grange, TX.
Fouts Fire builds pumpers, tankers, rescues, and quick attacks on both custom and commercial chassis.
The company builds a limited, standardized line of apparatus to streamline production processes and costs.
This business model supports keeping stock units of all models readily available for fast delivery nationwide.
Product models include the FB-94 pumper and the Fouts FOUR mini-pumper.

facts: Fouts Bros Inc. is a family-owned American manufacturer of commercial automotive equipment, including a wide range of trucks for various public and private sectors.
The company was founded in 1952 by brothers CJ and Jerry Fouts, who initially purchased a Sinclair service station in Smyrna, Georgia.
Over the years, the company expanded its business interests into auto and truck dealerships, commercial body manufacturing, and real estate.
The company entered the fire apparatus market in 1989 with the introduction of a 3000-gallon tanker.
The Fouts Fire division produces a standardized line of fire tankers, pumpers, light rescues, and brush trucks.
The company's business model focuses on streamlining production and costs by building a limited, standardized line of apparatus to keep stock units readily available for fast delivery.
A typical lead time from purchase order to delivery is four to six months.
The company is now in its third generation of family management.
The company is led by President and CEO Scott Edens, grandson of co-founder C.J. Fouts.
Heather Edens serves as the Executive Vice President.
In 2019, Fouts Bros moved its headquarters and primary production from Smyrna to a 700,000-square-foot facility in Milledgeville, Georgia.
The company operates a second factory in La Grange, Texas.
This move allowed for a significant expansion of their manufacturing capabilities.
The Fouts Bros Inc. family of brands includes Fouts Fire, Fouts Commercial Vehicles Inc., Smyrna Truck, Fouts Defense, Big Deal Parts, and Electric Commercial Vehicles Inc.
The Fouts Defense division is led by industry veteran George Bergamini.
The Fouts Defense division specializes in equipping U.S. military and government agencies with commercial and fire apparatus.
The company's product lineup includes tankers of various sizes (2000, 3000, and 4000 gallons).
The product lineup includes commercial pumpers like the FB-94.
The product lineup includes the versatile Fouts FOUR (First Out Utility Rescue) vehicle.

A Fire Production Manager role was located at Smyrna Truck in Milledgeville, Georgia.
The Fire Production Manager position oversees the development of fire vehicles.
The role requires a detail-oriented professional with strong leadership skills and a profound understanding of fire vehicle development processes.
The Production Manager ensures all projects are delivered on time, within budget, and to the highest quality standards.
Key responsibilities include leading, mentoring, and managing the production team to ensure efficient workflow, high morale, and adherence to company policies.
The manager conducts regular performance reviews and provides ongoing training and development opportunities.
Responsibilities involve overseeing and optimizing manufacturing processes to ensure the highest efficiency and quality.
The role requires implementing lean manufacturing principles to reduce waste, improve workflow, and increase productivity.
Data-driven decision-making is utilized to enhance manufacturing operations.
The manager ensures all units are built within quoted hours while maintaining high standards of quality and efficiency.
Workflows are monitored and adjusted to meet production timelines and reduce overtime costs.
Strategies are implemented to drive labor efficiency, including optimizing staff schedules, enhancing training programs, and utilizing productivity tools.
The goal is to maximize output while minimizing labor costs.

Project management duties include planning, coordinating, and overseeing all aspects of the upfitting process, including resource allocation, scheduling, and tracking progress against project milestones.
The manager collaborates with engineering and design teams to ensure project specifications are met.
Stringent quality control procedures are implemented and maintained to ensure all fire units meet or exceed company and industry standards.
Regular inspections and audits are conducted to identify and rectify any issues.
The role involves developing and managing the production budget to ensure all projects are completed within financial constraints.
Expenses are tracked and cost-saving measures are implemented without compromising quality.
Technical guidance and support are provided to the production team on upfitting techniques, tools, and best practices.
The manager stays updated on industry trends and advancements to continuously improve production processes.
Areas for improvement in the production process are identified, and strategies are implemented to enhance efficiency, productivity, and quality.
A culture of continuous improvement and innovation is fostered within the team.
All production activities must comply with safety regulations and company policies.
Regular safety training sessions and audits are conducted to maintain a safe working environment.
Inventory levels are monitored, material requirements are forecasted, and coordination with suppliers ensures timely availability of parts and materials.

facts: Hyundai's Georgia Metaplant, where it makes the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 electric vehicles, was raided by law enforcement officers including the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.
About 475 people were detained during the raid, most of them South Korean nationals.
Hyundai stated that the detainments will not impact car production at the factory.
The Savannah, Georgia Metaplant is a 16 million square-foot factory.
Hyundai Motor America officials confirmed that none of the detained workers were directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
The detained workers appear to have been working for LG Energy Solution, which co-owns the plant with Hyundai and operates its nearby battery facility.
Hyundai officials stated they are closely monitoring the situation while working to understand the specific circumstances.
Hyundai emphasized its commitment to full compliance with all laws and regulations in every market where it operates, including employment verification requirements and immigration laws.
Hyundai expects the same commitment to legal compliance from all partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
A Hyundai spokesperson confirmed that vehicle assembly at the plant will not be affected by today's arrests.

Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure all parties working on its projects maintain high standards of legal compliance, including thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
It was not immediately clear why the EV and battery manufacturing site was targeted by the Trump administration.
The raid comes as the Trump administration continues to dramatically step up immigration-related detentions, arrests, and deportations across the country.
According to a New York Times report, an attorney for at least two detained suspects said they were in the United States on a visa waiver program that allows stays of 90 days or less for business.
A New York Times report indicated that Korean companies often bring their own technicians on-site to help set up new operations because they have the training to do such work.
One immigration lawyer said U.S. visa laws are not properly set up to sponsor trained workers to run those factories.
ICE officials stated the raid was part of an active, ongoing criminal investigation.
ICE officials said the arrested individuals were found working illegally, in violation of the terms of their visas and/or statuses.
People on short-term or recreational visas are not authorized to work in the U.S., according to ICE.
A green card holder from Mexico was arrested after being determined removable from the U.S. based on multiple criminal convictions.
Hyundai's Metaplant is perhaps the automaker's most important effort to bolster manufacturing in the U.S., its largest and most important market.
The plant is also the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.
The project has a planned capacity of 14,000 manufacturing jobs.
Hyundai announced this year that the plant can make hybrid cars, amid potential concerns about electric demand following the end of the federal EV tax credits later in the month.
Hyundai officials stated that the safety and well-being of everyone working at their sites is a top priority.

Hyundai takes its responsibility as a corporate citizen seriously, noting incidents like this remind them of the importance of robust oversight throughout the entire supply chain and contractor network.
source_title: Hyundai Responds To Massive ICE Raid On Georgia EV Metaplant - Autojunction

facts: Freudenberg-NOK Sealing Technologies announced it will increase its testing capabilities with the addition of a new materials laboratory at its Findlay, Ohio, manufacturing plant.
The new laboratory is part of an overall plant expansion taking place in 2015.
The new laboratory will provide the company’s Fluid Power Division with new material characterization, failure analysis and mechanical property test capabilities.
It also will consolidate and expand an existing engineering test laboratory at the facility.
Findlay’s expansion, announced last month, will add 43,000 square feet to the existing factory.
The new lab will account for approximately 2,000 square feet of the total.
The new lab represents an investment of approximately $450,000 in equipment and building expenses.
The entire project will create 25 new jobs at the Findlay plant.
The entire project represents an investment of approximately $8.6 million in construction and equipment upgrades.
It will be complete by winter 2015.
The new laboratory will perform analysis and testing of parts the plant is making from high-performance plastics.
These include thrust washers, seals and other components used in the automotive industry.
In addition, the company is installing a new engineering test rig in the lab that will measure torque loss.
Freudenberg-NOK says the data will help further improve custom engineered sealing solutions that are reducing CO2 emissions and improving fuel economy.
The Findlay expansion will result in creation of 25 new jobs including chemist, chemical engineer and quality technician positions.
The company worked with the state of Ohio, including the Findlay-Hancock County Economic Development authority, Jobs Ohio and the Regional Growth Partnership on workforce incentives.
