facts: Freudenberg-NOK Santa Ana produces gaskets and O-rings that fit between two surfaces to form seals under radically different circumstances with serious consequences for failure.
The company's gaskets are used in jet engines, landing gear, hydraulic systems, aircraft fuel controls, power transmissions, gas pumps, construction and agriculture equipment, and soda dispensers.
Freudenberg-NOK Santa Ana operates on a low-volume high-stakes production model that requires impeccable quality standards.
The plant won the 2025 IndustryWeek Best Plants Award.
The plant received the 2022 and 2024 Raytheon/RTX Supplier Platinum Award, as well as a Best Lean Award and Six Sigma Award.
Freudenberg-NOK Sealing Technologies Santa Ana is located in Santa Ana, California.
The facility employs 175 people.
The plant occupies 39,000 square feet of space.
Primary products include O-rings and injection/compression molded gaskets.
The site started up in 1975 as International Seal Co. before being purchased by Freudenberg-NOK in 1998.
Eugen Amann serves as the Application Center Manager at Freudenberg-NOK Santa Ana.
Gaskets used aboard aircraft must withstand high-energy systems, temperature variance, and chemical interactions.
Landing gear gaskets maintain an aircraft's tire pressure under landing pressure while withstanding temperature differentials between negative 45 degrees Fahrenheit and 158 degrees Fahrenheit.
O-rings inside jet engines handle long-term continuous operating temperatures close to 400 degrees Fahrenheit as well as chemical compatibility with jet engine turbine lubricants.
Gaskets for heat exchanger systems handle high-pressure, high-temperature air from the engine and low-pressure, low-temperature air in the wings and cabin.
The plant produces O-rings and other gasket shapes for about 250 customers using 90 different compounds.
Uncured rubber is closely inspected before entering inventory and then prepared for molding via compression or injection molds.
Molded parts are sometimes trimmed by hand or de-flashed cryogenically, where excess material is snap-frozen and cleanly removed.
Finished rings are inspected, packaged, and shipped after the manufacturing process.
The plant uses visual cues, labels, and poka-yoke error prevention methods throughout the factory floor, testing labs, quality control area, offices, bathrooms, and cafeteria.
Raw material for gaskets is stored in blue bins, newly-formed gaskets go in yellow boxes, scrap goes into red boxes, and finished gaskets go into green bins.
To prevent mixing up similar parts like 10-mm and 11-mm rubber O-rings, the plant uses a to-scale watermark of each O-ring on digital or paper orders for quick visual comparison.

Employee work teams have autonomy over production scheduling, skills certification, safety and environmental compliance, quality assurance, daily job assignments, performance reviews, training, hiring, and materials management.
Vacation spending is handled by work teams on a first-come, first-served basis, and team leads weigh in on disciplinary actions and terminations.
The plant offers gain-sharing rewards for individual performance, rewards for team performance, profit-sharing, and paid knowledge and skills training including lean management system training.
Leadership at the plant is expected to come from all levels of the organization rather than just the management team.
source_title: 2025 IndustryWeek Best Plants: Freudenberg-NOK Santa Ana Leads with Lean | IndustryWeek

facts: About 150 workers at an auto parts plant in Findlay, Ohio launched a strike on March 24 for better wages and health benefits.
The strike began at Freudenberg-NOK after talks with United Auto Workers (UAW) Local 1327 broke down.
Workers at the company produce seals, O-rings, gaskets and other components critical for engines, transmissions, drivetrains and hydraulic systems.
In addition to Findlay, Freudenberg-NOK has operations in Sandusky, Ohio, as well as in Michigan, Wisconsin, New Hampshire and Georgia.
The company also operates in Canada, Mexico and Brazil.
According to online listings, starting pay ranges from $18 to $22 an hour, with top rates reaching only $25 to $30 an hour.
The company maintains a small workforce and meets quotas by requiring overtime, which workers rely on due to low base pay.

Located about 60 miles south of Toledo, parts from the Findlay plant make their way into the final assembly of engines, cars and light trucks produced by General Motors, Ford and Stellantis throughout the Great Lakes region.
Workers are in a powerful position, as their strike has the potential to halt production at the Big Three automakers.
The strike could attract significant support from assembly workers, who are dealing with massive layoffs as the automakers attempt to offload the costs of lower-than-expected electric vehicle sales.

Earlier this week, GM announced it would lay off the only remaining shift at its Factory Zero plant in Detroit, which only a few years ago the company had trumpeted as the flagship of its EV transformation.
The UAW has not spread the strike to other Freudenberg-NOK facilities, allowing the company to shift critical manufacturing to its other plants to meet orders.

By shifting large portions of production to independent parts manufacturers, automakers reduce direct labor costs and transfer wage pressures onto subcontractors, while eliminating tens of thousands of jobs.
The terms of the 2009 auto industry bailout included massive concessions, including wage freezes, cuts to retiree healthcare and the introduction of second-tier wage structures for new hires.

According to research from the Economic Policy Institute, average real hourly earnings for motor vehicle workers—including both Detroit automakers and parts suppliers—have fallen significantly since the 2008 crisis, with wages failing to keep pace with rising prices for housing, healthcare and other living expenses.
source_title: Workers strike auto parts manufacturer in Findlay, Ohio - World Socialist Web Site

facts: The U.S. state of Georgia is actively assessing the growing needs of the electric vehicle (EV) and mobility sector and building on existing assets.

Led by Governor Brian Kemp, Georgia believes it is well on its way to completely revamping its supply chain, infrastructure, workforce training, government policies, and overall value proposition to better meet the shifting needs of the EV and mobility sector.
The U.S. only produces 10% of the world’s lithium-ion batteries.
EV cars are designed around the battery, and many components needed to make these cars operational are currently being made overseas, creating a need to bring them to the U.S.
There is a need for new facilities to accommodate battery recycling across the full life-cycle of EVs.
Drivers need access to recharging stations to make EVs a viable part of their lifestyles.
Georgia offers more charging stations per capita than anywhere else in the United States, except one state on the West Coast.
Georgia is actively identifying ways to expand its charging infrastructure to cover both metro and rural regions.
In 2018, SK Innovation announced plans to build a massive lithium-ion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia.
SK Innovation has shared plans to bring its total investment to nearly $2.6 billion.
SK Innovation has already started production at its Georgia facility.
SK Innovation will supply batteries for VW as well as a new all-electric Ford F150 Lightning.
The SK Innovation investment has created a ripple effect in the region.
Other global e-mobility suppliers and leaders like Enchem Ltd., TEKLAS, GEDIA Automotive Group, and Heliox have announced significant, job-stimulating investments in Georgia.
Governor Kemp has been personally talking to these companies since taking office in 2019 and visiting them overseas in Korea and Germany to ensure Georgia supports their moves toward electrification.
Georgia is a hotbed for startups and home to a rapidly growing number of innovative companies moving into the electric mobility space.
There is serious growth in renewable energies in Georgia, including Plug Power, a major provider of hydrogen solutions that broke ground in August on its manufacturing plant in Georgia.

The Curiosity Lab at Peachtree Corners is a city-owned and operated living laboratory designed specifically to test technology that has graduated from a closed laboratory environment in a safe, real-world setting.

The Curiosity Lab features a 5G-enabled three-mile autonomous vehicle test track and smart city infrastructure located in a 500-acre technology park where thousands of people and vehicles interact daily.
The City of Peachtree Corners is serving as a model for other cities with its EV initiatives.
The city launched a new solar roadway system that produces energy for an EV charging station at city hall.
The city established a new EV fast-charging plaza capable of serving up to 16 vehicles.
Georgia and its wide network of partners are financing these programs because they see opportunity in solving EV infrastructure shortfalls that are holding up wider industry growth.
Georgia has developed a very competitive environment with a powerful and positive business climate.

Georgia's extensive infrastructure offers easy access to market, including its ports which are already a major player for automotive imports and have expanded inland connectivity to improve material transport efficiencies.
Georgia's geographic location puts it within hours, or even minutes, to OEMs across the Southeast’s “Automotive Alley.”
Access to a skilled and educated workforce is a deciding factor for attracting companies to Georgia.
Over the past year, Georgia’s university system awarded a record number of degrees.
Georgia’s technical college system allows technical schools to partner with their communities to design certification programs that meet the workforce needs of local companies.
Georgia was the first state to create a workforce training program custom-designed for qualifying companies.
The Quick Start workforce training program has been ranked the number one training program in the nation for 14 years.

In July 2021, Governor Kemp’s administration established the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA), a coalition of government, private industry, electric utilities, nonprofits, and other relevant stakeholders focused on continued growth of the electric mobility ecosystem throughout the state.

EMIA committees have been formed to set objectives for supply chain, innovation, infrastructure, workforce, and policy recommendations to further enhance the state’s attractiveness to the electric mobility industry.
source_title: Georgia is ready for what’s next - AI Online -

Shipments utilize 40ft containers transported via Pier to Pier service.
source_title: HYUNDAI GLOVIS GEORGIA. | U.S. Import Activity

facts: The company is inaugurating a new production site in Lavonia.
Inauguration events kick off Friday, November 04 with a Future Day for local high school students.
Events culminate on Saturday, November 05 with an official inauguration ceremony and a major community event from 11am to 6pm.
The company was founded over 60 years ago by Anton Häring in Bubsheim, Germany, initially operating with just 2 automatic cut-off machines in a barn.
The company currently employs approximately 4,400 people across 5 locations on 4 continents.
The American location in Lavonia is considered a major milestone for the Häring Group.
President Dr.-Ing. Jürgen Häring expressed excitement about the Grand Opening ceremony.
Approximately 400 regional high school students will attend the Future Day event as part of a school trip.
Students will participate in team challenges competing for a trip to Six Flags Theme Park, answering company questions, and collecting points at stations.
The company aims to position itself as an attractive employer for young recruits through these events.
The company cooperates with schools and universities, providing dual training and apprenticeships where students split time between classrooms and the company.
A highlight of their training offerings is a Global Rotational Training Program with headquarters in Germany for international experience.
The facility features modern workplace design and state-of-the-art machinery to attract employees.
Miriam Häring serves as President of the Häring Group.
Events provide students and the community access to tour the manufacturing facility.
Numerous activities and information points are distributed across the company premises to showcase operations.
The public community event on Saturday includes attractions like a human foosball table and a go-kart course.
Guests can win prizes by visiting information points, completing quiz challenges, or participating in social media photo challenges.
An international food market featuring products from all five continents where the company operates will be available at the event.
The opening ceremony aims to demonstrate the company's identity and employment opportunities to the local region.
The company's ambition is to transition the production plant to independent American management personnel.
The company is seeking local and regional talent with roots in the area to fill roles at the new plant.
source_title: News | Anton Häring KG

facts: Haering Precision USA LP partnered with Hart County College and Career Academy to expand hands-on training for high school students.
Precision machining equipment from Hart County High School was placed directly within Haering’s Lavonia facility.
The strategic collaboration enhances local workforce development by giving high school students access to real-world training in a high-tech manufacturing environment.
The machines are located in the Haering Academy for Apprenticeship and Further Training, a dedicated on-site training space at Haering Precision.
The academy serves as a hub for technical education that combines classroom instruction with practical, industry-relevant experience.
The equipment installation is part of a broader initiative to enhance Work-Based Learning (WBL) opportunities for students in Hart County.
Students can train on familiar school-owned machines in an industry setting at Haering while learning directly from experienced professionals.
Nathan Lindler, Technical Plant Manager at Haering Precision, stated that this is often the students’ very first exposure to a real manufacturing facility.
The program aims to help students explore their interests, build confidence, and understand career possibilities in manufacturing.

The integration supports growing demand for skilled machinists and industrial technicians by accelerating career readiness and reinforcing ties between technical education and real-world applications.
Students gain hands-on experience, mentorship, and the opportunity to be part of a global company known for high-precision components in the automotive industry.
Steve Burton, CEO of Hart County College and Career Academy, expressed pride in partnering with Haering Precision to provide student access and support community growth.
The Work-Based Learning program is currently active and welcoming students.
source_title: Haering Precision and Hart County College and Career Academy Partner to Expand Hands-On Training for High School Students - WNEG

facts: Haering Precision USA LP partnered with Hart County College and Career Academy to place precision machining equipment from Hart County High School directly within Haering’s Lavonia facility.
The strategic collaboration enhances local workforce development by giving high school students access to real-world training in a high-tech manufacturing environment.
The machines are now located in the Haering Academy for Apprenticeship and Further Training, a dedicated training space on site at Haering Precision.
The academy serves as a hub for technical education, combining classroom instruction with practical, industry-relevant experience.
The equipment installation is part of a broader initiative to enhance Work-Based Learning (WBL) opportunities for students in Hart County.
Through the program, students can train on familiar school-owned machines in an industry setting at Haering while learning directly from experienced professionals.
Nathan Lindler, Technical Plant Manager at Haering Precision, stated that this is often the students’ very first exposure to a real manufacturing facility.
Lindler noted that giving students access to industry tools and professionals early helps them explore interests, build confidence, and understand career possibilities in manufacturing.

The integration supports a growing demand for skilled machinists and industrial technicians by accelerating career readiness and reinforcing the connection between technical education and real-world applications.
Students in the program gain hands-on experience, mentorship, and the opportunity to be part of a global company known for high-precision components in the automotive industry.

Steve Burton, CEO of Hart County College and Career Academy, expressed pride in working with Haering Precision to provide students with access and opportunity, calling it a strong example of education and industry supporting student success and community growth.
The program is currently active and welcoming Work-Based Learning (WBL) students.
source_title: Haering Precision, school system expand partnership | Hartwell Sun, Hartwell, Georgia

facts: Haering Precision actively welcomes students for exclusive plant tours as part of its commitment to inspiring the next generation of manufacturing professionals.
Students are given opportunities to explore cutting-edge production processes, interact with skilled professionals, and see the technology behind automotive component manufacturing.
Plant tours aim to expose students to career possibilities in manufacturing and the role of STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) in shaping the industry's future.

Dustin Chambers, Commercial Plant Manager at Haering Precision, stated that opening doors to bright minds provides hands-on experience of modern manufacturing and that engaging with students is critical to building a strong pipeline of future talent.

Haering Precision participated in Camp STEMPowerment, an empowering program hosted by North Georgia Technical College specifically designed for middle school girls to explore STEM fields through interactive workshops and real-world applications.

The company's involvement in Camp STEMPowerment underscores its dedication to fostering diversity and inclusion in STEM-related careers by inspiring young girls to pursue interests in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.

Nathan Lindler, Technical Plant Manager at Haering Precision, stated that seeing students dive into STEM with enthusiasm is a reminder of why they invest in education outreach and expressed excitement about being part of their journey.
Haering Precision participated in Career Fairs at North Georgia Technical College's Clarkesville and Currahee campuses.

The company is dedicated to continued involvement in educational and industry events, regularly hosting and participating in programs that promote STEM education, workforce development, and community engagement.
Haering Precision looks forward to further strengthening its ties with local schools, technical colleges, and industry leaders in the years to come.
source_title: News | Anton Häring KG

facts: Haering Precision recently announced the successful conclusion of its first annual Skills Invitational.
The event was held in partnership with North Georgia Technical College.
The Skills Invitational took place on April 24 at North Georgia Tech’s Clarkesville campus.
Participants were drawn from Franklin, Hart, Stephens, and Union counties.
The event aimed to ignite interest in machining and metals jobs among high school students.
It provided a platform for students to explore diverse career opportunities within the manufacturing industry.
Participants from local high schools teamed up with senior precision machining students from North Georgia Tech in a pro-am style contest.
The contest featured challenges designed to test technical skills, creativity, and teamwork.
Students faced hands-on challenges simulating real-world scenarios in precision machining and manufacturing.
Tasks included interpreting technical drawings and programming CNC machines.
Judges evaluated teams based on accuracy, efficiency, innovation, and collaborative problem-solving.
Dustin Chambers serves as the Commercial Plant Manager at Haering Precision.
Dustin Chambers stated the Skills Invitational was a platform for students to apply knowledge, showcase skills, and explore interest in machining, reaffirming belief in manufacturing's future.
Winners received prizes including tools and paid internship opportunities at Haering Precision.
The paid internships provide exposure to real-world manufacturing processes and foster professional growth.
Franklin County High School senior Logan Turner took home first place in the contest.
Tim Bennett serves as the Senior Technical Recruiter at Haering Precision.
Tim Bennett emphasized that the event empowered young talent and encouraged them to explore possibilities within the manufacturing industry.
Haering Precision and North Georgia Technical College plan to expand opportunities for students to pursue careers in precision machining and manufacturing.
source_title: Haering Precision Terms Innaugural Skills Invitational a Success; Logan Turner Takes Home Win - WNEG

facts: Haering Precision USA LP hosted high-level diplomatic representatives for an exclusive visit.
The event was organized in partnership with the Hart County Industrial Building Authority (IBA).
The visit provided guests with insight into the region's economic strength, workforce initiatives, and business-friendly environment.

Delegates from various countries exchanged views with Haering Precision's plant management on state-of-the-art manufacturing technologies, innovative strength, and the company's economic importance for the region.
Guests toured the plant and gained direct insights into Häring's high-precision production processes and advanced technologies.
The event was attended by Hart County leaders including Amanda Brown, President & CEO of the Hart County Chamber of Commerce.
Steve Burton, CEO of the Hart County College and Career Academy, also attended the event.
Representatives from the City of Hartwell and Hart County government were present at the event.
Local leaders provided an overview of the region's economic development.
Leaders emphasized close collaboration between local government, industry, and the education sector to promote skills training and attract new businesses.
Dustin Chambers, Plant Manager of Haering Precision USA LP, stated that as a global company, they are pleased to welcome the Georgia Consular Corps to Lavonia and showcase their work.

Chambers noted the visit demonstrates the company's commitment to innovation and workforce development, as well as the strength of Hart County's industrial sector and opportunities for international partnerships.
The Hart County Industrial Building Authority held discussions about expanding industrial opportunities, attracting new investment, and expanding infrastructure.
A particular focus of the visit was on skills development.
Haering Precision's education and training programs serve as a model for close collaboration between industry and educational institutions.
Partnerships with institutions such as the Hart County College and Career Academy ensure a qualified skilled workforce.
These partnerships increase the attractiveness of the region for international companies.
Haering Precision USA remains a leader in manufacturing excellence and workforce development, strengthening Hart County's position as a leading industrial center in Georgia.
source_title: 新闻 | Anton Häring KG

facts: HEXPOL Tallapoosa is dedicated to technological leadership in the development and production of high quality rubber compounds.
HEXPOL Tallapoosa's approach involves integration of materials, recipe, mixing, customer specifications and customer processes to arrive at high quality solutions at the best cost.
HEXPOL Tallapoosa produces a wide range of synthetic compounds for the Industrial & Transportation markets.
The compounds are produced on highly automated and fully process-controlled mixing lines, based on the latest technology.
source_title: Hexpol  - Greater Haralson Chamber

facts: HEXPOL is involved in advanced polymer compounding, specializing in the development and manufacture of custom polymer compounds for various industries.
The company provides a variety of compounding solutions including rubber, thermoplastic elastomer (TPE), and thermoplastic compounds, as well as engineered products like gaskets, seals, and wheels.
HEXPOL serves sectors such as automotive, construction, healthcare, energy, industrial, and oil and gas.
It was founded in 1893 and is based in Malmo, Sweden.
On November 1, 2024, Hexpol acquired an 80 percent stake in nylon compounder Piedmont Resin Supply LLC in a deal valued at US$86 million.
Piedmont Resin Supply LLC is headquartered in Cartersville, Georgia.
Piedmont was founded in 2013 and has grown to become one of the largest independent nylon compounders in the U.S., with approximately 60 employees.
Piedmont services a variety of customers within automotive, transportation, and the furniture industries.

Jan Wikström, president of Hexpol Thermoplastic Compounding, stated that the acquisition adds new capabilities, application knowhow, and a new customer base to Hexpol Thermoplastic Compounding in the U.S.
Hexpol is headquartered in Malmo, Sweden, and is organized into two business areas: Hexpol Compounding and Hexpol Engineered Products.
Hexpol has acquired 19 companies total, with its latest acquisition being Piedmont Resin Supply on October 31, 2024.
On March 3, 2023, Hexpol partnered with IFS for an upgrade implementation project overseen by IFS's partner Addovation.
source_title: Hexpol Portfolio Investments, Hexpol Funds, Hexpol Exits

facts: HEXPOL has signed an agreement to acquire 80 percent of the shares in Piedmont Resin Supply, LLC from the founder Mr. Matt Griffith and Mr. Paul Daniel.
Piedmont was founded in 2013 and has grown to become one of the largest independent nylon compounders in the US.
The company is an industry leader in technical nylon compounds on the US market.
Piedmont services a variety of customers within automotive, transportation and the furniture industries.
Piedmont operates a well invested manufacturing facility in Cartersville, GA.
The Cartersville, GA facility employs some 60 people.
The facility has further capacity to grow.
The company has a turnover of some 60 MUSD.
The company's profitability is on a similar level to the HEXPOL Group.
Matt Griffith and Paul Daniel see the merger with HEXPOL as the perfect fit between two successful companies with similar cultures and future ambitions.

Piedmont brings a portfolio of superior nylon compounds and strong market presence, which together with HEXPOL’s complimentary product portfolio and financial strength will enable further growth prospects.
The acquisition of Piedmont adds new capabilities, application knowhow and a new customer base to HEXPOL Thermoplastic Compounding in the US.

Klas Dahlberg stated that Piedmont has a diverse product portfolio and is a leading independent nylon compounder, and with this acquisition they broaden the capabilities of the HEXPOL Group on the US market.
The acquisition price amounts to 86 MUSD on a cash and debt free basis.
The acquisition is funded by a combination of bank facilities and cash.
The closing took place on October 31, 2024.
Pursuant to the agreement, HEXPOL has an option to acquire the remaining shares.
The founders have an option to sell their remaining shares to HEXPOL.
This information is such that HEXPOL AB (publ) is obliged to publish in accordance with the EU market abuse regulation.
The information was submitted for publication, through the agency of the contact persons set out above, at 10:50 p.m. CET on October 31, 2024.
This is a non-official translation of the Swedish original wording. In case of discrepancies between the English translation and the Swedish original, the Swedish text shall prevail.

HEXPOL is a world-leading polymers group with strong global positions in advanced polymer compounds, gaskets for plate heat exchangers, and wheels made of polymer materials for truck and castor wheel applications.

HEXPOL's customers are primarily system suppliers to the global automotive and engineering industry, building and construction industry, transportation, energy, consumer and cable industry, and manufacturers of medical equipment, plate heat exchangers and forklifts.
The HEXPOL Group is organized in two business areas: HEXPOL Compounding and HEXPOL Engineered Products.
The HEXPOL Group’s sales in 2023 amounted to 22,046 MSEK.
The HEXPOL Group has approximately 5,000 employees in fourteen countries.

facts: Polypropylene (PP) compounds are the products produced by blending Polypropylene with different components such as fillers and various additives.
This process grants PP compounds diverse characteristics and they are widely used in producing automotive, interior / exterior materials and electrical & electronics components etc.
With long-term experiences and technical know-how, HDC HYUNDAI EP is producing high-quality PP compounds with outstanding processibility, widening the range of its usage.
SUPOL is a highly functional composite PP resin that has superior processibility and dimensional stability.
HDC HYUNDAI EP's exclusive technology also granted SUPOL various colors, shock resistance and environmental resistance.

facts: HEXPOL Compounding Americas (HCA) announced key changes to its Executive Leadership Team under President and CEO Gary Moore.
Bruce Wynd was promoted from Director of Automotive to Chief Commercial Officer.
Wynd will be supported by four Commercial Directors responsible for the Automotive, Specialty, Industrial, and Silicone/Performance Additives markets.
Saul Reyes replaced Wynd as the Director of Automotive.
Chris Airhart continues as the Director of Industrial.
Chris Poirier continues to direct the Silicone and Additives business.
Brad Donat was newly hired as the Director of Specialty.
Gary Moore stated that Bruce Wynd has unparalleled industry knowledge at HEXPOL Compounding Americas and will lead the Commercial Teams.
Brad Donat brings over 30 years of industry experience, having served in various leadership roles within the Chemical Marketplace.
Donat most recently served as the Senior Account Manager, Carbon Black, for the Cabot Corporation based in Boston, MA.
Bruce Wynd stated that Donat's background of commercial success and market knowledge will benefit HEXPOL’s customers and drive growth in the Specialty marketplace.
Ellen Clunk was promoted from Purchasing and Supply Chain Director to Chief Procurement Officer to manage all aspects of the HCA supply chain.
Clunk has been with the organization for over 13 years, starting as a development chemist before shifting into various purchasing roles.
Andrew Chan was promoted from Director of Operations of the South and West regions to the newly formed position of Chief Operations Officer.
Chan will be supported by two Operations Directors: Ernesto Gutierrez overseeing HCA’s three Mexico sites, and Kevin Brown overseeing HCA’s five Northern sites along with its two West Coast sites.
Chan will also lead the Business Systems, Continuous Improvement, and Engineering/Maintenance teams.
Kevin Brown joined HCA from Morgan Truck Body, where he served as the Sr. Regional Director of Operations.
Brown has over 25 years of industry experience including leadership positions in Plant Management, Quality Control, and Operations.

Andrew Chan stated that Brown’s exceptional leadership and organizational skills will benefit the Operations Team and noted he is a hands-on manufacturing professional who understands continuous improvement, culture development, and problem-solving.
Gary Moore noted that these organizational changes will allow HEXPOL Compounding Americas to have clear visibility on the current and future needs of its customers.
source_title: HEXPOL Compounding Americas Announces Leadership Changes - Modern Plastics

Customers are primarily system suppliers to the global automotive and engineering industry, building and construction industry, transportation, energy, consumer and cable industries, and manufacturers of medical equipment, plate heat exchangers, and forklifts.

facts: On Nov. 1, Hyundai Industrial Co. purchased a building at 83 Amlajack Way in Coweta County.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced that morning that the auto parts supplier for Kia and Hyundai plants will create 100 new jobs in the county.
Kemp stated in a written statement that partners like Kia and Hyundai are attracting a large network of suppliers, creating jobs and opportunity for Georgians across the state.
The building on Amlajack Way will be retrofitted to produce arm and head rests for manufacturers including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County, Georgia.
Woosuck Kim serves as Team Leader at HDI America Inc.
Kim stated Hyundai Industrial has more than four decades of experience providing comfortable and safe experiences for drivers and passengers with some of the world’s biggest automotive brands.
Kim expressed excitement about being part of the e-mobility transition in Georgia and working with Coweta County to create mutually beneficial growth for the community and future employees.
Sarah Jacobs, president of the Coweta County Development Authority, relayed the announcement to authority members at their meeting Thursday morning.
The Coweta County Development Authority began working with the company in 2021.
Jacobs noted that by 2021, the company had already picked out the Amlajack property.
Jacobs stated the Georgia Department of Economic Development brought the project to them and they pitched for Coweta.
Jacobs was unsure how many other communities were in the running for the factory.
John Reidelbach, Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman, welcomed Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd. to Coweta County in a written statement.
Reidelbach noted that Hyundai joins thriving manufacturing industries and will have close proximity to I-85, making transport to nearby automobile plants effortless.
Reidelbach expressed excitement for the workforce opportunities Hyundai will bring to citizens as they continue to increase within the community.
source_title: Hyundai Industrial purchases building to settle in Coweta | News | times-herald.com

facts: A raid on Thursday at a battery factory under construction at Hyundai’s sprawling auto plant west of Savannah resulted in the detainment of 475 workers, more than 300 of whom were South Koreans.
Many detained South Korean nationals were engineers and equipment installers brought to Georgia for the highly specialized work of getting an electric battery plant online.
Some detained workers were involved in after-sales service and installation work.
Attorney Charles Kuck stated that many of the detained workers were doing work authorized under the B-1 business visitor visa program.
The detained South Korean workers had planned to stay in the U.S. for just a couple of weeks and never longer than 75 days.
Video released by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement showed some detained workers shackled with chains around their hands, ankles, and waists.
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem stated that the Hyundai plant raid sends a clear signal.

President Donald Trump said the workers were here illegally and that the U.S. needs to arrange with other countries to have experts train U.S. citizens for specialized work such as battery and computer manufacturing.
Attorney Charles Kuck said no company in the U.S. makes the machines used in the Georgia battery plant, so they had to come from abroad to install or repair equipment on-site.
Kuck stated that training someone in the U.S. to perform this specialized equipment installation work would take about three to five years.
Rosemary Coates, executive director of the Reshoring Institute, said it is not unusual for foreign companies to send workers from abroad to set up U.S. factories and then train U.S. workers.
Coates noted that the same practice occurred in the 1980s with Japanese carmakers setting up U.S. factories and in the 1990s with German carmakers.
A B-1 visitor for business visa allows foreign workers to stay for up to six months while getting reimbursed for expenses and collecting a paycheck back home.

Under B-1 rules, foreign workers can supervise construction projects but cannot build anything themselves unless equipment installation is spelled out in a contract, according to Los Angeles immigration lawyer Angelo Paparelli.

South Korea is one of 41 countries whose citizens can use the U.S. Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA), which provides a visa waiver for up to 90 days if they provide a legitimate reason for their visit, effectively giving them B-1 visa status, according to immigration attorney Rita Sostrin.

Advocates called for the detained workers to be released during a news conference Monday at a church in Savannah, about 25 miles east of the site where Hyundai began producing electric vehicles a year ago.
Sarah Park, president of the Korean American Coalition of Atlanta, stated that many of the detained South Korean workers had special skills needed to get the battery plant running.
Daniela Rodriguez, executive director of Migrant Equity Southeast, said immigrants from Mexico, Guatemala, Colombia, Chile, Ecuador, and Venezuela were also detained in the raid.
Rodriguez reported that the group's Savannah office was flooded with calls from family members of workers who could not be reached and were presumed detained.
Workers described seeing armed agents and military-style vehicles during the raid, while drones and helicopters hovered overhead.
One woman with a work permit who was not detained told Rodriguez she felt like she was being followed as an animal while they were hunting for her.
Even some workers who were not detained feel unsafe about returning to their jobs at the site.

Savannah labor union leader Christi Hulme, president of the Savannah Regional Central Labor Council, said local unions complained that Hyundai and its contractors improperly used South Korean workers for basic construction falling outside visa waiver rules.
Hulme stated that Korean workers were pouring cement, erecting steel, performing carpentry, and fitting pipes.
Hulme claimed that basically local labor was being given to illegal immigrants.
Spokespersons for Hyundai’s Georgia EV factory and the adjacent battery plant did not immediately reply to an email message seeking comment.
South Korea’s foreign minister Cho Hyun called the raid a very serious matter during a legislative hearing before departing for the U.S. on Monday to secure his citizens’ return on a charter flight.

Lawmaker Cho Jeongsik from the liberal governing Democratic Party questioned how South Korean companies could continue investing in the U.S. if U.S. authorities detain hundreds of Koreans almost like a military operation.
Some South Korean lawmakers called for retaliatory investigations of Americans who allegedly work illegally in South Korea.
Experts say the raid is unlikely to prompt major tit-for-tat measures given South Korea's dependence on the U.S. for security and business ties.
The Homeland Security agency’s largest workplace raid yet targeted Georgia, a symbol of bilateral cooperation where many large South Korean businesses operate and plan future investments.
Only weeks ago, South Korea promised hundreds of billions in U.S. investments to reach a tariff deal.
President Trump and South Korean President Lee Jae Myung held their first summit in Washington on Aug. 25.

Kim Taewoo, former head of Seoul’s Korea Institute for National Unification, criticized the way Trump is pressuring the Korean government as very rough and unilateral, stating it won't be good for U.S. national interests in the long term.
source_title: Lawyer says South Korean engineers and installers were doing authorized work at Hyundai | AP News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is announcing a significant investment of USD 21 billion in the United States from 2025 to 2028.
This commitment reflects the Group’s strategic focus on expanding its manufacturing capabilities, advancing future technologies, and enhancing energy infrastructure in America.
This latest U.S. investment builds on the Group’s existing allocation of approximately USD 20.5 billion since entering the U.S. market in 1986.

Hyundai Motor Group stated: “Hyundai Motor Group is deepening its partnership with the United States, reinforcing our shared vision for American industrial leadership. The Group’s investment and efforts will further expand our operations in the U.S. and grow our American workforce. Thank you to our American partners, employees, and communities. We’re proud to stand with you, and proud to build the future together.”

To reinforce its production capabilities, the Group will invest a total of USD 9 billion to establish an annual production capacity in the U.S. of 1.2 million vehicles across its automotive brands, Hyundai Motor, Kia, and Genesis.

The Group plans to invest in improving its production facilities, including Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia, to further enhance its customer-centric approach in delivering high-quality automobiles.

A total of USD 6 billion will be allocated to increase the localization rate of automotive components – including core parts for electric vehicles (EVs), such as battery packs – to form an auto cluster following expansion of the Group’s production facilities, as well as strengthening Group logistics to ensure robust supply chains and investing in steel production in the U.S.
Hyundai Steel, the Group’s steel affiliate, will construct an Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) steel mill in the state of Louisiana, capable of producing 2.7 million tons of steel annually.

This facility will produce low-carbon steel sheets using the abundant supply of steel scrap in the U.S. with the aim of enhancing the Group’s agility and flexibility in response to external uncertainties.

The Group will invest USD 6 billion to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in areas including autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence (AI), and advanced air mobility (AAM).
The Group will collaborate with Boston Dynamics to expand the U.S. ecosystem for robotics components and establish a mass-production system.
The Group will partner with NVIDIA to accelerate the development of AI solutions for future mobility, including autonomous driving and robotics.
The Group will advance R&D with Supernal, its US affiliate for AAM business, to commercialize an eVTOL vehicle by 2028.
The Group will supply robotaxis to Waymo as part of its strategic partnership with Hyundai Motor Company, and co-develop autonomous driving services with Aptiv.
The Group will invest in potential startups through venture capital and other funding mechanisms to support U.S. startups specializing in mobility, robotics, and AI.

As part of its USD 6 billion commitment, the Group will also invest in energy infrastructure projects to secure new business opportunities and contribute to the development of sustainable energy generation.
The Group will pursue strategic cooperation between Hyundai Engineering & Construction Holtec International on Small Modular Reactor (SMR) technology.
The Group will establish infrastructure to bolster use of renewable energy.
The Group will invest in the IONNA EV charging alliance to expand infrastructure.
Through these investments, the Group anticipates it will create 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028.
The overall economic impact is expected to generate more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.

The Group plans to hold its Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Grand Opening celebration in Georgia, U.S., later this week, marking the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history, just two and a half years after breaking ground.

HMGMA exemplifies HMG’s dedication to driving economic growth, furthering technological advancements, and delivering sustainable solutions to the U.S. market, creating or supporting over 570,000 jobs nationwide.
source_title: Hyundai to Invest $21B in US, Build Steel Plant to Supply Georgia Auto Plants - Metro Atlanta CEO

facts: Hyundai Motor Company CEO Jose Munoz appeared Wednesday on 'The Claman Countdown' where he talked about Hyundai Motor Group’s newly-announced massive investment in the U.S.
His appearance came the same day the automaker marked the official grand opening for its Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) near Savannah, Georgia.
Hyundai revealed it will put $21 billion over the next few years toward boosting manufacturing in the U.S.
CEO Jose Munoz stated that HMGMA Metaplant America is comprised of $12.6 billion dollars.
The facility includes one assembly plant with a capacity of 300,000 that will get to as much as 500,000 and two battery plants.
Hyundai's executive chair, Mr. Chung, announced before President Trump that the company is planning to invest $21 billion between now and 2028 in a number of mega projects.
One mega project is the new Electric Arc Furnace plant in Louisiana.
The $21 billion investment will include $9 billion to bring annual U.S. production capacity to 1.2 million vehicles.
The investment includes $6 billion for 'enhancing parts localization and logistics'.
The investment includes $6 billion towards more 'collaboration in future industries' like autonomous driving, robotics, and energy infrastructure.
Munoz told Claman that the company 'took a big decision to invest in America' during the prior Trump administration.
Munoz noted that America is their largest market and they decided to make the largest investment in the world with this $12.6 billion.
Localizing production in America is intended to address potential tariffs and bring jobs.
Hyundai produces not only cars, but steel and many other things.
The new investments will bring in total about 670,000 jobs, direct and indirect.
Vehicles that Hyundai will manufacture at its Savannah, Georgia plant 'probably they will have to pay no tariffs.'
Hyundai is the latest among a growing number of companies announcing plans to increase investment in the U.S. under the Trump administration.
The Trump administration has been using tariffs to encourage companies to bring manufacturing back to American soil and reduce reliance on foreign goods.
Trump implemented 20% levies on goods imported into the U.S. from China.
Mexico and Canada were hit with tariffs, but Trump later introduced exemptions for those two countries on goods under the USMCA until April.
Imports of steel and aluminum have become subject to new tariffs.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Company CEO talks automaker's $21 billion US investment | Fox Business

facts: Hyundai is in talks to supply Waymo with 50,000 IONIQ 5 autonomous vehicles by 2028.
The potential contract for the vehicles is worth roughly $2.5 billion.
The deal implies an estimated price of $50,000 per vehicle.
Production of the vehicles would happen at Hyundai's Georgia plant.
Manufacturing in Georgia qualifies the vehicles for domestic manufacturing incentives.
If finalized, the order would represent one of the single largest vehicle supply deals in autonomous driving history.
The IONIQ 5 platform features a fast-charging architecture and spacious cabin ideal for a commercial fleet.
Waymo intends to add only 2,000 more vehicles in 2026.
Adding those vehicles would bring Waymo's total fleet to about 3,500 vehicles.
There is a clear gap between the massive infrastructure commitment and Waymo's current pace of fleet expansion.
The deal shows Waymo locking in its supply chain for the next several years.
Waymo secured a $16 billion funding round to fuel exponential scaling.
The funding round values Waymo at $126 billion post-money.
This valuation is more than double Waymo's last funding round, which occurred just over a year ago.
Lead investors in the funding round include Dragoneer Investment Group, DST Global, and Sequoia Capital.
Alphabet maintains its majority stake in Waymo and continues to provide sustained support.
The capital infusion is explicitly designated to fund global fleet expansion and manufacturing.
Funds will help Waymo scale operations into over 20 additional cities in 2026, including Tokyo and London.
The capital allows Waymo to lock in supply deals like the one with Hyundai.
The funding round transforms Waymo from a technology demonstrator into a capital-intensive infrastructure play.
Across 127 million miles of fully autonomous operation, Waymo has achieved a 90% reduction in serious injury crashes.
Waymo is executing a multi-source manufacturing strategy to secure its supply chain for exponential growth.
Waymo is building a new autonomous vehicle factory in Metro Phoenix with Magna to produce thousands of Jaguar I-PACEs.
Tesla has not yet launched a commercial robotaxi service and faces significant hurdles in technology and regulatory paths.
Waymo currently completes more than 250,000 paid trips each week across major U.S. cities.
The successful ramp of Hyundai IONIQ 5 production is a primary catalyst for Waymo's expansion.
Waymo must seamlessly integrate the new IONIQ 5 platform into its fleet by 2028 to meet expansion targets.
The production line at Hyundai's Georgia plant is critical for scaling service into over 20 new cities in 2026, including London and Tokyo.
The launch of the new Phoenix factory with Magna provides a domestic source for thousands of vehicles.
The Phoenix factory reduces Waymo's reliance on external partners.
Waymo is currently under NHTSA investigations into its operations due to rapid expansion.
Regulatory scrutiny could slow approvals or impose new requirements on Waymo.
Any incident or regulatory setback could damage the trust central to Waymo's safety moat and delay adoption.
Investors are advised to monitor the progress of the Hyundai production ramp and IONIQ 5 integration.
Investors should track the launch and performance of the new Phoenix factory.
Investors should watch for regulatory developments from NHTSA and other agencies.
The execution of Waymo's 2026 expansion into new cities will test whether infrastructure bets translate into scalable growth.

facts: Petr Fiala (Czech Prime Minister), Lukáš Vlček (Minister of Industry and Trade), and Yoshihiro Nakata (President of Toyota Motor Europe) announced the production of a new battery electric vehicle at Toyota Motor Manufacturing Czech Republic.
The new BEV will be the first produced in one of Toyota’s European plants.
TMMCZ’s plant in Kolin will be expanded from 152,000 to 173,000 square metres.
The expansion will accommodate production of the new vehicle and its battery, along with new paint and welding shops.
Toyota will fund the construction of the new BEV manufacturing activity through new capital expenditures of approximately €680 million.
The Czech Government will provide an investment of up to €64 million towards a dedicated facility for battery assembly.
This announcement supports Toyota’s multi-pathway strategy aimed at achieving carbon neutral operations in Europe by 2040.
Petr Fiala stated that the Czech Republic secured Toyota as a significant foreign investor despite interest from other countries.
The investment will expand current production in Kolín and represents an important step toward a higher technological level while keeping car manufacturing in the country.
The automotive industry accounts for around 10% of the Czech Republic's GDP.
The project will create 245 new job opportunities in the region.
Lukáš Vlček attributed the success to negotiations in Europe and Japan, citing a stable business environment, strong infrastructure, and skilled workforce as key factors.
The government supported the investment with €64 million, with estimated benefits for the state reaching €250 million.
Yoshihiro Nakata stated that the new BEV production demonstrates dedication to sustainable mobility and innovation and will enhance Toyota's presence in the European market.
Toyota currently produces Aygo X and Yaris Hybrid at TMMCZ at a capacity of approximately 220,000 vehicles per year.
The introduction of BEV production increases the potential of the plant's multi-pathway production capacity.
The project will lead to incremental direct employment with similar benefits for the supply chain, as the majority of parts volume will be sourced in the Czech Republic.
TMMCZ has maintained a manufacturing presence in the Czech Republic for over two decades and has produced over 4.5 million vehicles across that period.
Toyota first started producing Aygo at the plant in 2002 when it operated as a joint venture.
Toyota acquired full ownership of the plant in 2021.
TMMCZ is one of the largest employers in the Central Bohemia region, with a workforce of around 3,200 members.
Approximately 65% of TMMCZ's supply parts are sourced in the Czech Republic.

TMMCZ hosts Toyota’s first logistics European mega hub, established in 2024 to supply vehicles to Toyota and Lexus dealerships across six Central European markets including Germany, Austria, Poland, Slovakia, Hungary, and the Czech Republic.
Historical investments at TMMCZ from 2002-2005 for starting the new plant and production totaled €1,069 million.
Historical investments at TMMCZ from 2020-2024 for plant capacity extension for Yaris and Aygo X TNGA production and other investments totaled €251 million.
Total historical investments at TMMCZ from 2002-2024 amount to €1,320 million.
source_title: Toyota announces new BEV to be produced in Europe

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is now officially open.
The facility represents the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
The $12.6 billion facility is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
The plant marks a significant milestone for Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) as it expands its electric vehicle (EV) and battery production in the United States.
The new plant will manufacture up to 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
The facility has an initial production capacity of 300,000 vehicles per year.
The factory will produce a substantial number of batteries to support EV production.
This massive investment is expected to strengthen Hyundai’s presence in the U.S. market and ensure its vehicles benefit from government incentives designed to promote domestic EV production.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that the Metaplant represents the Group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities, commitment to innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.
The Inflation Reduction Act, passed in 2022, provided enhanced incentives for EVs, batteries, and battery components produced in the United States.
Hyundai’s investment in Georgia positions the company to leverage these benefits for its customers.
The future of these policies remains uncertain under the current administration, which could impact incentive structures for EV manufacturers.
Hyundai’s total investment in the United States has now reached $20.5 billion, with the Metaplant accounting for a significant portion of that amount.
The company estimates that its U.S. operations have created more than 570,000 direct and indirect jobs.
The HMGMA facility is expected to generate 8,500 jobs by 2031.
The plant will contribute to a broader economic impact of nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs in Georgia.
The Centre for Automotive Research projects that the plant will generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings annually.
The facility was developed at an unprecedented pace, with Hyundai agreeing with the state of Georgia in May 2022.
HMGMA was officially formed by June 2022.
Groundbreaking on the project occurred within four months of the initial agreement.
The speed of development underscores Hyundai’s commitment to rapidly expanding its EV production capabilities in the U.S.
Beyond manufacturing, the Metaplant includes a 41-acre ecological park featuring trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas for employees.
This employee amenity initiative aligns with Hyundai’s broader sustainability and workforce well-being strategy.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant represents a key step in the company’s global EV strategy, ensuring a strong foothold in the U.S. market.
The partnership with LG Energy Solution and SK On for battery production further strengthens Hyundai’s supply chain and manufacturing capabilities.
source_title: Hyundai Opens $12.6 Billion Metaplant in Georgia With Annual Production Capacity Of 500,000 Units

facts: Hyundai is building a $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia.

A federal agency plans to reassess its environmental permit for the plant after a conservation group complained that regulators failed to properly examine potential impacts on the area's water supply.

State and local economic development agencies that applied for the project's 2022 permit never mentioned Hyundai wanted to withdraw up to 6.6 million gallons (25 million liters) per day from the underground aquifer that is a major regional source of drinking water.
Details about the plant's water needs emerged earlier this year as the Georgia Environmental Protection Division considered a proposal for four new wells to supply water to the auto factory.
The Army Corps will revisit its prior finding that the project would have "negligible impacts."
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper conservation group gave notice in June that it planned to sue if the agency refused to revisit the permit issued for the Hyundai project in October 2022.

Ben Kirsch, legal director of the riverkeeper group, stated that the concentration of pumping will have local impacts on domestic and agricultural wells and raised questions about impacts on natural springs, wetlands, tributaries, and streams.
The Army Corps' reassessment letters do not order any delays or disruptions to construction at the plant site.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America stated it will assist as needed to ensure the Army Corps gets the information it needs.

Hyundai stated in a company statement that it has worked tirelessly with relevant authorities to ensure they are good neighbors and that operations do not negatively impact the community's water resources.

Sara Lips, spokesperson for the state Environmental Protection Division, said the extra scrutiny by the federal government is "unlikely to impact or delay" a final decision by Georgia regulators on whether to permit wells for the Hyundai project.

The Army Corps permit obtained by state and local economic developers authorized the filling or dredging of 221 acres (89 hectares) of wetlands at the plant site just a few months after Hyundai announced plans to build its EV factory in May 2022.
The Army Corps concluded at the time that the project would have "negligible impacts on municipal and private water supplies" and relied on information provided by economic developers.

Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Area Economic Development Authority, stated, "We never purposefully withheld anything," noting he worked with state officials to bring Hyundai to Georgia.
Tollison expects the updated information requested by the Army Corps to be submitted within 10 days and noted the federal agency would typically complete its review within a month.
Tollison expressed confidence that the reevaluation won't hold up the project, stating, "There's enough water for everyone," and "We feel really good about where we are."
Georgia's environmental agency issued draft permits in July for the four wells to supply Hyundai and is now evaluating public comments before reaching a final decision.
The wells would be drilled in neighboring Bulloch County, where some farmers and rural residents have expressed worry that the auto plant will siphon water away from their crops and homes.
State regulators concluded that water withdrawals by the Hyundai plant would lower water levels in the aquifer up to 19 feet (5.8 meters) for private wells within 5 miles (8 kilometers).
State regulators said most wells won't see any impacts because they reach deeper into the ground.
The state agency also stated that nearby rivers and streams won't be affected because dense layers of rock seal off the aquifer from surface water.
Ben Kirsch with Ogeechee Riverkeeper hopes a second look by the Army Corps will provide more details on how state regulators reached their conclusions.
Kirsch stated, "We definitely want to see the Corps independently evaluate this," and added, "This should have been all considered before the wetlands were filled and buildings went up."
source_title: U.S. agency to reexamine permit for Hyundai's $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia - The Sumter Item

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC
facts: New facilities represent U.S. investment of approx. USD 5.54 billion.
Commercial production to begin 1H 2025 with annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Groundbreaking officially occurred today on site at the Bryan County Mega site.
The celebration included a morning groundbreaking on site, followed by a future technology expo at Enmarket Arena in Savannah.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung and Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the groundbreaking alongside company officials and state/local leaders.
The $5.54 billion investment by the company and its affiliate suppliers was announced five months ago.
Plans include producing a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis and Kia electric vehicles (EVs) and a new battery manufacturing facility.
HMGMA will create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years.
The company aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
HMGMA’s new smart factory is expected to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated: "Today, our EVs are recognized as best in class, and with this partnership, we are determined to be the global leader in electrification, safety, quality, and sustainability."

Governor Brian Kemp stated: "Our partnership with Hyundai Motor Group and the groundbreaking of this innovative facility exemplifies that unprecedented success. With a long-term commitment to improving lives and livelihoods, we look forward to continuing this partnership and seeing its impact on both this community and our state as a whole."
Approximately $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
Hyundai Motor Group will be a leader in the U.S. auto market’s EV transition with the new facility.
The Group is accelerating its electrification efforts with the global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize this goal, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network that will ensure a stable supply of EVs around the globe.
With additional EV and battery production capabilities in the U.S., the Group aims to maintain its position as one of the top three EV providers in the U.S.
The Group plans to lead the EV market not just in sales, but also in terms of design, technologies, and mobility solutions optimized for the electric mobility era.
The investment is in line with the U.S. government’s roadmap to accelerate electrification, as global OEMs are announcing plans to expand their local EV production in the U.S.
Last year, the U.S. government announced a new initiative under which sales of zero-emission vehicles should account for at least half of OEM’s new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will boast a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system, which organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant where all processes of production—order collection, procurement, logistics and production—will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will also help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
As part of the Group’s commitment to sustainability, the plant will primarily rely on renewable energy sources to power the facility.
The plant will use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.

facts: Hyundai Motor chairman Chung Mong-Koo was arrested on embezzlement charges last week.
The formal contract with the Czech government will be signed in mid-May.
Vice-Chairman Kim Dong-Jin will likely sign the deal on behalf of Chung in a low-key manner.
A Czech Republic delegation, including industry Minister Milan Urban, will travel to Seoul for the signing.
Hyundai Motor postponed "indefinitely" a ground-breaking ceremony scheduled for May 17 for a one billion euro (1.26 billion dollar) plant at Nosovice in the northeast of the Czech Republic.
Hyundai suffered a serious setback in domestic sales last month due to the slush fund scandal, while exports continue robustly.
Chung, 68, was arrested and imprisoned Friday on charges of embezzlement and breach of trust following a month-long probe into a slush fund scandal.
He is charged with raising about 130 billion won (138 million dollars) in slush funds through group affiliates since 2001.
He is charged with causing 390 billion won in damages to the group through misconduct.
Prosecutors are investigating allegations that a substantial portion of the slush fund was used to bribe government and financial officials for business favors.
Chung is suspected of using part of the slush fund money to facilitate the transfer of corporate wealth and management control to his son, Chung Eui-Sun, president of Kia Motors.
The Hyundai Motor group is the world's seventh largest auto maker and controls more than 70 percent of the South Korean car market.
The planned Czech car plant should produce about 300,000 vehicles a year starting in 2008.
Kia Motors, a unit of Hyundai, operates a plant in neighboring Slovakia.
Citing the pending probe of its parent Hyundai Motor, Kia Motors last month put off a ground-breaking ceremony for a 1.2 billion dollar auto plant in West Point, Troup County, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor reported total sales rose 2.5 percent year-on-year to 215,037 vehicles in April, but were down 12.8 percent from March.
Domestic sales stood at 44,044 vehicles in April, the lowest level for the month since 2000, down 14.4 percent from March and 1.5 percent from a year earlier.
Exports totaled 170,993 vehicles in April, up 3.6 percent year-on-year but down 12.4 percent month-on-month.
Kia Motors sales were down 1.3 percent year-on-year to 113,406 vehicles in April, with domestic sales off 8.4 percent to 21,532 and exports posting a modest 0.5 percent rise to 91,874.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to deploy humanoid robots at its U.S. manufacturing plant in Georgia starting in 2028.
The deployment marks a step toward automating higher-risk and repetitive manufacturing tasks.
Hyundai unveiled the production version of the Atlas humanoid robot, developed by its unit Boston Dynamics, at the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas.
The company did not disclose the volume or cost of the robots.
Hyundai aims to roll out adoption across all of its manufacturing sites as part of a push into "physical AI".
The robots will initially carry out parts sequencing tasks from 2028.
Applications for the robots will expand gradually as safety and quality benefits are validated.
By 2030, Atlas robots were expected to move into component assembly.
A longer-term plan exists for Atlas robots to take on tasks involving heavy loads, repetitive motions, and complex operations across production sites.
The robots are designed to reduce physical strain on workers by taking on higher-risk and repetitive tasks.
This deployment lays the groundwork for broader commercial use in industrial settings.
At Hyundai Motor's affiliate Kia Corp, the labor union last year called to establish a body to address potential labour rights issues in preparation for the AI era.
Workers at Kia raised concerns about expanding automation.
Hyundai expects humanoid robots to become the largest segment of the physical artificial intelligence market.
Physical AI refers to AI systems embedded in hardware that collect real-world data and make autonomous decisions, spanning areas such as robotics, smart factories, and autonomous driving.
Atlas features human-scale hands with tactile sensing.
Atlas has the ability to lift up to 50 kg (110 pounds).
The robot can operate autonomously.
Atlas is designed to function in industrial environments ranging from minus 20 degrees Celsius to 40 degrees Celsius.
Hyundai is accelerating development through partnerships with global AI leaders, including a collaboration with Nvidia and Google.
The collaboration with Nvidia and Google aims at improving safety, efficiency, and real-world deployment.

Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around Georgia.

facts: Maxwell Technologies, Inc. is a leading developer and manufacturer of ultracapacitor-based energy storage and power delivery solutions.
Jörg Buchheim was appointed to Maxwell’s board of directors as a new independent director effective July 1, 2016.
Buchheim served as Maxwell’s senior vice president and chief sales officer since March 2016.
Buchheim resigned from Maxwell on June 30, 2016, to take a new position as the chief executive officer of INALFA Roof Systems B.V., a top 3 global supplier of vehicle roof systems located in Europe.
Buchheim is a seasoned global sales leader and an automotive industry veteran.
From 2002 through 2015 he worked at HELLA KGaA Hueck & Co. in a series of senior sales and management positions.
His most recent role at HELLA was president and chief executive officer of HELLA China and a member of the HELLA Group Management Board based out of Shanghai.
He previously served as HELLA’s Global Key Account Manager for Indian OEMs and Hyundai/Kia as well as Vice President Sales and Marketing China, Shanghai.
Prior to joining HELLA, Buchheim worked in European Key Account Sales for Mitsubishi Electric and in the Project Management for Spoerle / Arrow.

David Schlotterbeck, chairman of the board, stated that Jörg brings key automotive industry experience and relationships as well as an extensive background in operational and executive management to complement their already experienced Board of Directors.
Schlotterbeck noted that Jörg’s deep knowledge of Chinese business practices and his strong relationships with key customers and industry executives will be an invaluable resource on the board.
Maxwell's ultracapacitor products provide safe and reliable power solutions for applications in consumer and industrial electronics, transportation and telecommunications.

Maxwell's high-voltage grading and coupling capacitors help to ensure the safety and reliability of electric utility infrastructure and other applications involving transport, distribution and measurement of high-voltage electrical energy.
source_title: Exhibit

facts: Kautex Textron GmbH & Co. KG received an “A-” Climate Change rating from CDP.
The company is a subsidiary of Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT).
Kautex announced its sustainability roadmap in November 2021.
The roadmap commits to targeting an 80 percent reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions and a 30 percent reduction in Scope 3 emissions by 2030.
The company aspires to be net-zero by 2050 in all three scopes.
In 2022, Kautex challenged its 30-plus plants worldwide to reduce their Scope 1 and 2 emissions through practical steps like energy audits and strategic investments in renewable energy.
In 2022, all of the company’s facilities on mainland Europe, China, Mexico, and in Lavonia, GA are sourcing renewable energy.
The company joined CDP as a Supply Chain Corporate member.
Kautex is challenging its suppliers to join CDP to disclose their own environmental impact and take concrete steps to reduce their impact over time.
Sylvain Genesi serves as Kautex’s vice president, Global Supply Chain and Sustainability.
Kautex developed an application to assist customers in estimating the carbon impact of Kautex-designed products for product life cycle assessment.
Sophie Louis serves as global director, Energy and Sustainability at Kautex.
Kautex is a Tier One automotive supplier with more than 30 plants in 14 countries.

The company designs, develops, and manufactures traditional and hybrid fuel systems, advanced cleaning solutions for assisted and autonomous driving, engine camshafts, and plastic industrial packaging solutions.
Kautex is expanding its portfolio to offer smart products and data-driven services, including smart fuel systems, thermoplastic composite, and composite-metal hybrid battery systems.
source_title: Kautex Textron GmbH & Co. KG Receives A- Climate Change Score from CDP

facts: The SelectUSA Investment Summit in 2024 featured more than 5,000 participants representing 96 global markets.
Georgia has been ranked the No. 1 state for business for over a decade.
In fiscal year 2024, Georgia secured more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market.
Georgia maintains business development and trade representation in more than 12 global markets, which accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in fiscal year 2024.
Georgia has built a robust international presence over the past 50 years.
Georgia ranked as the sixth-largest state in total trade in 2024 with a value of $198.7 billion, reflecting a 6.4% year-over-year increase.
Over the past decade, companies from more than 40 countries have invested in Georgia, and over 70 international governments maintain a presence there.
Approximately 10% of Georgia’s population is foreign-born.
Gwinnett County ranks among the Top 10 most diverse counties in the U.S.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the Port of Savannah, and the Port of Brunswick provide critical logistical access to global markets for businesses in Georgia.
Georgia maintains a low corporate tax rate of 5.19%.
Georgia’s economic landscape includes e-mobility alongside aerospace, food processing, and agribusiness.
Georgia offers a blend of established industries, a skilled workforce with top training programs, and strategic investments in site development and infrastructure.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development supports existing industries, empowers vibrant arts and cultural communities, and encourages tourism to Georgia’s diverse destinations.
In 2023, Georgia’s international trade value surpassed $186.3 billion across 223 countries and territories, ranking the state seventh in the U.S.
source_title: From Global to Local: International Businesses Grow Globally in Georgi | Georgia

facts: Auto manufacturing is of increasing importance along Interstate Highway 85 to the east and west of Auburn.
Kia Motors has a large automobile factory in nearby West Point, Georgia.
Hyundai Motors has a large automobile factory in Montgomery, Alabama.
Mando America Corporation is listed as one of the largest employers in East Alabama near Auburn.

Decreases in automobile sales, including adverse changes due to interest rate increases, could adversely affect the Kia and Hyundai plants and their suppliers’ local spending and employment, and could adversely affect economic conditions in the markets we serve.
Overall cost of living remains below the national average.
Housing is more attainable in many communities across the state.

Georgia’s economy supports a wide range of careers and industries through a strong workforce ecosystem with universities, technical colleges, and training programs helping people build skills and advance their careers statewide.
Through the Georgia Center of Innovation, public and private partners collaborate to develop key industries including advanced manufacturing, logistics, aerospace, fintech, and AgTech.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the world’s busiest airport, places more than 80% of the U.S. within a two-hour flight.
A strong highway and rail network keeps people and goods moving statewide.
Through a statewide network of colleges and technical institutions, residents can pursue degrees, credentials, and hands-on training across urban and rural campuses.
Programs such as the HOPE and Zell Miller Scholarships make higher education more affordable.
Dual enrollment, apprenticeships, and workforce grants offer flexible pathways for students and adult learners to advance without leaving their communities.
source_title: Quality of Life in Georgia | Georgia
