facts: Sewon Company operates primarily on a Business-to-Business (B2B) model targeting major automotive Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs).
The company supplies high-quality components for vehicle assembly, including car body parts, chassis parts, interior parts, and molds.
Sewon is making strategic investments in an EV body parts plant in Georgia to align with the growing demand for EV components.
Sewon Precision Industry Co., Ltd. reported trailing 12-month revenue of $116 million as of March 31, 2025.
Sewon Corporation Company reported trailing 12-month revenue of $168 million as of March 31, 2025.
A new $300 million manufacturing plant is being built in Rincon, Georgia, dedicated to producing EV body parts.
The new Rincon facility is expected to begin operations in 2025.
The Rincon facility will supply major OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America.
The Georgia expansion is projected to create 740 new jobs.
The project is expected to support a total of 1,600 jobs in Georgia after completion.
Sewon America, a subsidiary of Sewon Precision Industry Co. Ltd., operates an existing manufacturing facility in LaGrange, Georgia that has expanded over time.
Sewon's strategic shift toward electric vehicle manufacturers is reflected in its investment in the Georgia EV body parts plant.
Automotive OEMs prioritize suppliers who consistently deliver high-quality components to ensure vehicle safety and performance.
Purchasing decisions by automotive manufacturers are often based on long-term contracts and supply chain stability.
Competitive pricing and the ability to integrate seamlessly into production processes are key factors in supplier selection.
The shift toward EVs demands suppliers capable of producing specialized components, including lightweight body parts and other EV-specific components.
Sewon addresses customer needs through continuous R&D and automated manufacturing sites to secure manufacturing competitiveness.
Sewon Co., Ltd. is headquartered in Daegu, South Korea, with international expansion into China and the United States.
Direct sales teams actively engage with potential automotive OEM clients to showcase capabilities and generate leads.
The company participates in industry-specific trade shows and conferences to network and expand its customer base.
Sewon prioritizes consistent quality and on-time delivery, providing dedicated customer service and technical support to address client needs.
The company offers customized solutions and product adaptations to meet specific OEM requirements.
Advanced manufacturing capabilities include automated manufacturing sites and real-time monitoring for quality control.
Investment in the Georgia EV plant serves as a retention strategy to meet future demands of key customers like Hyundai Motor Group.

facts: Sewon America operates an automotive production plant in LaGrange, Georgia.
Plaintiff Edgar Emmanuel Serrano Mondragon applied for a maintenance engineer position at the Sewon plant in January 2021 after attending a job fair in Mexico.
Serrano received a job offer on August 19, 2021, for a maintenance engineer role with an annual wage of $36,000.
Plaintiff Jimmy Ruiz Heredia applied for a mechanical engineer position via labor recruitment company TESS in February 2021.
Ruiz received an offer letter dated June 14, 2021, for a technical engineer position with an hourly wage of $10.70 for the first three months.
The June 14 offer letter stipulated that Ruiz's hourly rate would increase by $0.50 every six months until reaching a maximum of $16.80 per hour.
Sewon's chief financial officer and general manager, Nathan Jung, signed both the initial and subsequent offer letters on company letterhead.
Ruiz received a second offer letter dated June 21, 2021, which changed his title to Industrial Engineer and offered an annual salary instead of an hourly wage.

Sewon and TESS provided support letters on Sewon's letterhead to the U.S. Embassy in Mexico City stating the engineering positions were professional and specialized under NAFTA's permitted TN occupations.
Plaintiffs allege that upon starting work at the Sewon plant, they were assigned manual assembly line labor instead of the skilled engineering roles promised to secure their TN visas.
Plaintiffs filed a first amended complaint on July 21, 2024, alleging violations of the Georgia RICO Act, breach of contract, and the Fair Labor Standards Act against Sewon and TESS.
Ruiz alleges he was earning an annualized salary of $31,200 at the time of his termination, while Serrano earns $31,720 annually, both falling short of the $36,000 promised in their offer letters.
A U.S. District Court order dated February 12, 2025, denied motions to dismiss by Sewon and TESS regarding the plaintiffs' breach of contract and RICO claims.
source_title: JIMMY RUIZ HEREDIA EDGAR EMMANUEL SERRANO MONDRAGON v. SEWON AMERICA INC TOTAL EMPLOYEE SOLUTION SUPPORT LLC (2025) | FindLaw

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Sewon America will create 740 new jobs in Rincon.
Sewon America will invest more than $300 million in a new Effingham County manufacturing facility.
This project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon to-date.
The announcement fulfills the commitment to create jobs and investment in all four Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority (JDA) counties.
This fulfillment results from Hyundai's Metaplant location in Bryan County, which is the largest economic development project in state history.
Suppliers for the Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment, surpassing initial estimates by at least $800 million.
Governor Brian Kemp celebrated delivering on the promise to create jobs in all four JDA counties and the growth of a valued partner.
On his first international trip as governor to Korea, he congratulated Chairman Kim on the company's then-recent expansion and discussed continuing to work together.
Those discussions led to this announcement as Sewon America continues to grow its Georgia operations.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns represents part of Effingham County and played a role in securing the project and engaging Sewon America executives.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended Governor Brian Kemp, Sewon America, and stakeholders on the historic economic development announcement for Rincon.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones noted investments by companies like Sewon build on Georgia's commitment to remain the No. 1 state in which to do business while attracting a robust workforce.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Following completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
Vice Chairman Kim expressed happiness about building another Sewon facility in east Georgia and described the company as one family in two Georgia locations.
Growth is attributed to support from the Sewon team, Governor Kemp, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Effingham County.
Sewon’s new facility will be located at the Grande View industrial park in Rincon.
The new manufacturing location will produce electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2025.

Rincon Mayor Ken Lee welcomed Sewon America and commended the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority for bringing the largest employment and private investment project in the city’s history.
Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (IDA) Troy Smith stated Sewon selected Grande View industrial park as the best location for significant investment.
The IDA chairman noted the investment will allow current and future generations to have fulfilling, well-paying careers without leaving Effingham County.
Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority Griff Lynch expressed excitement to support Sewon’s growth in the new location.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted Sewon integrated into Georgia’s automotive community through local colleges, Georgia Quick Start, and statewide initiatives like Georgia Made.
Sewon is reinvesting in Georgia while supporting Hyundai Motor Group’s operations from Effingham County, which has a skilled workforce, infrastructure, and collaborative spirit to support growth.
On October 25, 2022, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry based on automotive industry assets.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
Sewon America was established in 2008 in LaGrange as a Georgia company and primarily operates in the Motor Vehicles and Car Bodies industry within the Transportation Equipment sector.
Sewon produces stamped body components and is celebrated for high-quality automotive parts.
source_title: Gov. Kemp:  Sewon America Announces 740 New Jobs in Effingham County - Effingham Herald

facts: The Company manufactures functional parts used in automotive interiors and car exterior products.
Main products include seat recliners, adjusters, window regulators, door locks (functional interior parts), door sashes and moldings (car exterior parts).
Aisin Seiki Co., Ltd. (currently Aisin) became the parent company in December 2015 via a stock transfer transaction that officially took effect in April 2016.
Shiroki was delisted from the stock market on March 29, 2016.
On April 1, 2023, the Company changed its name from Shiroki Kogyo to Aisin Shiroki Corporation.
As of March 31, 2025, Aisin Corporation holds a 100.00% investment ratio as the sole shareholder.
Main products include arm type window regulators (fan type and crescent gear type), wire-type window regulators, and guideless window regulators.
Main products include front and rear doorframes (TNGA and basic types) and aluminum front doorframes.
Main products include belt molding, arch molding, and frame molding.
Main products include high-tensile bumpers, rocker EA materials, and touch sensors.
Main products include power sliding doors and universal steps.
Main products include luggage compartment door closers, door locks, fully open-door stoppers, door hinges, door checks, engine hood locks, back door locks, rear seatback locks, and floor locks.
In March 1946, the company was established as Shiroki Kinzoku Kogyo Co., Ltd. with its head office and a factory in the Jujo Military Arsenal in Tokyo.
In October 1950, headquarters moved to Shinagawa-ward, Tokyo.
In July 1956, the Ex-Nagoya Plant started operations, later integrated into the Toyokawa Plant in June 1988.
In December 1959, the Osaka Plant started operations, originally in Ikeda City and moved to Ibaraki City in August 1977.
In November 1960, the Toyota-City Plant started operations in Toyota-City, Aichi Prefecture, renamed Toyota Plant in February 1985.
In February 1964, the Musashino Plant started operations in Musashimurayama City, Tokyo, renamed Tokyo Plant in October 1969 and integrated into the Fujisawa Plant in December 1986.
In January 1967, the Fujisawa plant started operations in Fujisawa City, Kanagawa Prefecture, where headquarters were transferred.
The company was listed on the 2nd Section of the Tokyo Stock Exchange in May 1970 and the Nagoya Stock Exchange in October 1972.
In August 1973, it was listed on the 1st Section of both the Tokyo and Nagoya Stock Exchange.
In October 1980, the Toyokawa branch plant started operation in Ichinomiya-cho, Hoi-gun, Aichi Prefecture, renamed Toyokawa plant in August 1981.
In September 1984, Shiroki Seiko Co., Ltd. was established.
In December 1985, Shiroki Shoji Co., Ltd. was established.
In May 1986, a two-headquarters system consisting of the Yokohama headquarters and the Toyokawa headquarters was launched.
In September 1986, the Toyokawa Technical Center was established.
In December 1986, Tokyo Plant operations were integrated into the Fujisawa Plant.
In March 1988, Shiroki-Wickes Corporation was established in Tennessee, U.S.A., later renamed SW Manufacturing, Inc. in December 1989 and Shiroki North America, Inc. in January 2007.
In June 1988, Nagoya Plant operations were integrated into the Toyokawa Plant.
In September 1988, the corporate name was changed to Shiroki Corporation.
In May 1989, Shiroki USA Corp. was established in Michigan, U.S.A.
In April 1991, the Nagoya Plant started operations in Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture.
In December 1993, Toyota Plant operations were integrated into the Nagoya Plant, establishing the Toyota Production Division.
In June 1995, SWM Georgia LLC was established in Georgia, U.S.A., which adopted a new name, Shiroki Georgia LLC, in January 2007.
In July 2000, the Yokohama headquarters was closed and operations shifted to a single headquarters at Toyokawa.
In August 2001, Kyusyu Shiroki Corp. was established.
In January 2002, Shiroki Thailand Co., Ltd. was established.
In February 2002, Shiroki Brose Corp. was established in Toyohashi City, Aichi Prefecture, later moved to Toyota City in 2005.
In October 2003, Guangzhou Shiroki Corp. was established in Guangzhou City, Guangdong Province, China.
In June 2005, SWM-GT LLC was established in Gordonsville, Tennessee, USA, later renamed Shiroki GT LLC in January 2007.
In December 2007, Production section 3 of the Nagoya Plant was integrated into the Toyokawa Plant as Production section 4.
In December 2009, Production Section 4 of the Toyokawa Plant was consolidated into Production Section 3, and the vacated plant was renamed Toyota Depot.
In March 2011, Matsumi Corporation's status was changed to a consolidated subsidiary.
In August 2011, P.T. Shiroki Indonesia was established in West Java, Indonesia.
In July 2012, Shiroki Asia Corporation Limited was established in Bangkok, Thailand.
In July 2013, Tohoku Shiroki Co., Ltd. was established in Japan.
In July 2014, Shiroki Technico India Pvt. Ltd. was established in Haryana, India.
In May 2015, a contract was signed to transfer the seat structure business with Toyota Boshoku and Aisin Seiki.
On April 1, 2016, the company became a wholly owned subsidiary of Aisin Seiki.
In December 2017, Shiroki Auto Components India Private Limited was established in Gujarat, India.
In August 2020, an additional 5% equity interest was acquired in the joint venture Shiroki Technico India Pvt. Ltd., making it a wholly owned subsidiary.
In March 2021, Shiroki Auto Components India Pvt. Ltd. merged with Shiroki Technico India Pvt. Ltd. and ceased to exist.
In June 2021, Shiroki Technico India Pvt. Ltd. changed its name to Shiroki Automotive India Pvt. Ltd.
In June 2022, TOYOTA BOSHOKU CORPORATION, AISIN CORPORATION and car seats Concluded a production.
source_title: AISIN SHIROKI CORPORATION (Formerly Shiroki Corporation) - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

The company was founded in 2010.

facts: The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced that German multinational liner shipping company Hapag-Lloyd will invest $18 million in its new North American headquarters.
The new North American headquarters will be located in Dunwoody, Georgia.
The investment will create an additional 250 jobs in the metro Atlanta region.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hapag-Lloyd has been moving people and cargo around the globe for over 150 years and is one of the most recognizable names in the shipping industry.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that logistics is an innate part of Georgia’s economic prowess, with the state's economy connected to the movement of goods and people via sea, rail, air, and road.
Hapag-Lloyd is based in Hamburg, Germany.
Hapag-Lloyd operates a fleet of over 250 modern container ships with a total transport capacity of 1.8 million TEUs.
Hapag-Lloyd currently employs more than 500 people across the state of Georgia.
Once the North American headquarters project is complete, Hapag-Lloyd will employ more than 750 people in Georgia.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson met with company leadership in Hamburg during a November economic development mission to Germany.

Uffe Ostergaard, President of Hapag-Lloyd’s North American region, stated that the new headquarters will bring more team members together under one roof to better serve customers and expand career pathways for staff.
The company will open its new 125,000-square-foot headquarters at Three Ravinia in Dunwoody by summer 2022.
Hapag-Lloyd will be hiring for careers in finance, human resources, IT services, and more.
Dunwoody Mayor Lynn Deutsch stated that the city is pleased to welcome Hapag-Lloyd and its team members to Dunwoody.

Decide DeKalb President Dorian DeBarr stated that Hapag-Lloyd’s announcement reinforces DeKalb County’s assets, including a skilled workforce, accessibility to major highways and transportation hubs, and a business-friendly community.
GDEcD Director of Corporate Solutions and Cybersecurity in Global Commerce Kristi Brigman represented the department on this project.
The project was executed in partnership with GDEcD’s Europe office, Decide DeKalb, the Dunwoody Development Authority, and Georgia Power.
source_title: Multinational Liner Shipping Company Hapag-Lloyd Selects Georgia for North American Headquarters - Savannah CEO

facts: SK Battery America was established by SK On as its production base in the United States in 2019.
SK Battery America is set to open an IT facility in Georgia.
The state governor’s office confirmed last week that the center’s launch is scheduled to take place later this month.
The opening of SK Battery America’s IT center is expected to create at least 200 tech jobs in Roswell, Georgia.
This project is part of the company’s major investment in the territory, which also includes the building of two new electric vehicle (EV) battery production plants.
Yonhap News Agency reported that the IT facility would operate as a management hub for SK On's battery manufacturing plants in the U.S., including the facilities in Georgia.
SK Battery America will invest about $19 million in the project over the next few years.
SK On is spending $2.6 billion for the construction of two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The products from these Jackson County manufacturing facilities are to be supplied for EVs such as the Volkswagen ID.4 sports utility vehicle and Ford F-150 Lightning pickup trucks.
SK On is also building two other EV battery factories in a joint venture with Ford Motor.
Their JV deal with Ford Motor is worth $11.4 billion.
The facilities for the SK On-Ford joint venture are set to be built in Kentucky and Tennessee.
The new IT center of SK Battery America will be an integrated IT management hub that will serve all of the company’s battery facilities in the country.
The IT center is located at 1110 Sanctuary Parkway in Roswell.
Hiring for IT experts in the Roswell area is expected soon.
Governor Brian Kemp of Georgia stated: “We are proud of SK Battery America’s continued growth here in the No. 1 state for business.”

Governor Brian Kemp added: “This innovative company continues to bring the jobs of the future that will benefit hardworking Georgians in manufacturing, and now it will also tap into the tech talent our top-ranking universities provide.”

SK Battery America’s head of information technology, Jason Choi, stated: “We decided to open our IT regional center in Roswell to strengthen SK’s position as a leader in the battery industry, and it will help us build and operate an advanced manufacturing system in accordance with further expansion of battery production bases in the United States.”
source_title: SK On’s SK Battery America to launch an IT center in Georgia - EconoTimes

facts: The SK Battery America plant is located in Commerce, Georgia.
The $1.6 billion project was expected to employ 2,000 people and was finalized in early January 2019.
The project required investment from state and local government to be built in Georgia.

SK Battery was looking at several other states, especially in the southeastern U.S., with final two sites between Commerce and somewhere in Tennessee, according to John Scott, director of economic development for Jackson County.
Georgia is regularly touted as being the No. 1 state for business in the country, although there is some disagreement on this ranking.
Beating out competitors for major industrial projects brings in potentially thousands of jobs at a time and can include significant investments from local governments.
Some experts say state and local developers in Georgia are careful to ensure these projects are net economic gains, while others raise concerns about transparency and democratic processes.
USA TODAY Network-Georgia examined six major economic development projects across Georgia using open records requests, state-provided data, and interviews with officials.
All of the examined projects created 500 new jobs or more and received government support ranging from tax breaks and leases to outright grants.

The examined projects included the SK Battery plant, the Hanwha Q Cells solar manufacturing facility in Whitfield County, Amazon facilities in Bibb County, Wayfair facilities in Athens and Savannah, and a warehouse development project still under development in Savannah.

Cal Wray, president of the Augusta Economic Development Authority, acknowledges there are not many 500-job projects locally but notes food, chemical, and advanced manufacturing companies draw national and international competition.
Each of these local projects might employ a few hundred people, alongside non-industrial cyber and office space projects.
Cyber is considered equally important to industrial development outside of the industrial piece, according to Wray.

Amazon came to Columbia County in 2020 with a fulfillment center that will employ 800 people, receiving state and local grants, infrastructure work, and some property tax breaks while still paying school taxes.
Earlier this year, Amazon announced a new sortation facility with no incentives that will create several hundred additional jobs.
Robbie Bennett, executive director of the Development Authority of Columbia County, stated the county's only input on the second facility was selling 47 acres for $37,000.
Bennett noted Amazon enjoyed doing business with Columbia County and liked the site access.
The original Amazon project brought 500 construction workers who stayed in Columbia County hotels during the COVID hospitality slumps.

Georgia competed against South Carolina, Alabama, Tennessee, and North Carolina for these projects, occasionally facing bids from cities as far away as Chicago, Dallas, and Denver, according to Scott McMurray, deputy commissioner for Global Commerce for the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

Michael Toma, Fuller E. Callaway Professor of Economics at Georgia Southern University, noted a new project creating 1,000 jobs can have a huge impact, while 500 jobs is significant for medium-sized cities.
Job quality remains a matter of debate, though state and local officials emphasize they look for jobs that pay well.

Christina Cummings, vice president of operations for the Partnership for Southern Equity, emphasized the need to dig deeper to determine what success looks like when bringing development to a community.

The SK Battery plant agreement between SK Battery, the city of Commerce, Jackson County, and Jackson County Industrial Development Authority requires the plant to employ 2,000 people and invest $1.6 billion by 2027.
Parties to the first agreement are working on an amendment to cover a second phase that will bring an additional $940 million investment, according to McMurray.
The SK Battery project was the reason behind a huge spike in state investment to the Athens area economic development region.

Common deal elements across Georgia projects include: local development authority owning land and leasing it to the company; PILOT agreements for reduced property tax liability; requirements that 80% of job and investment goals be met by a deadline; counting jobs at 110% and investment at 100% when calculating the 80% requirement; definitions of full-time jobs sometimes including minimum wage or salary; and sometimes one or more state grants for specific aspects.
To receive a grant, a project must be competitive, meaning it would have been located outside the state without the grants.
McMurray stated incentives can't make a bad deal good, they can only make a good deal better.
Toma noted Georgia is particularly careful about ensuring incentives are proportional to project benefits and tends to be more conservative in offering them compared to other states.

To land the SK Battery plant, the state provided a $5 million grant and a separate $18.75 million grant for rough grading of the 283-acre site, and the Industrial Development Authority issued a $1.6 billion bond to cover construction on authority-owned land.
Because of land ownership, SK Battery owed no property taxes but agreed to a PILOT schedule through 2039.
If SK Battery missed hiring and investing targets, it would owe penalties based on PILOT savings and proximity to targets.

Agreements with local industrial development authorities usually include side agreements like assistance with permitting or technical matters, but do not usually include statewide incentives like job tax credits.
McMurray stated governors are 100% behind efforts to develop the workforce and education.

Georgia has an unusually diverse economic base that helps bring in a wide range of industrial development projects, including growth in electric vehicle manufacturing, warehouses, and food processing.
McMurray highlighted Georgia's presence in the cybersecurity space with a cyber security center in Augusta doing fantastic work.
Major manufacturers like SK Battery bring their supply chain with them, creating further growth.
McMurray stated economic development efforts work across the entire state, all 159 counties, rather than just Atlanta.
source_title: Here's how Georgia is creating jobs, economic growth across the state

facts: Political leaders from Georgia are awaiting a decision in Washington this fall regarding the massive EV battery plant under construction in Jackson County and whether SK Innovation will be punished over cheating allegations.
In March 2019, SK Innovation broke ground on a $2.6 billion EV battery plant in Jackson County, Georgia.
SK Battery America promises to hire 2,600 people to manufacture batteries for the electric vehicle market at the Jackson County facility.
The factory is planned to open in 2022.
State and local governments provided $300 million in tax breaks and donated the land for the factory site.
Five weeks after the groundbreaking, rival company LG Chem accused SK Innovation of stealing trade secrets and attempting to cover it up, with allegations tied directly to the Jackson County plant.
Congressman Doug Collins attended the groundbreaking to welcome SK Battery America but stated he had no idea at the time that the company was suspected of stealing trade secrets from a competitor.
LG Chem filed lawsuits against SK Innovation in federal court and with the International Trade Commission (ITC), prompting concern among Georgia elected leaders.
The ITC complaint alleges that SK Innovation's alleged cheating intensified as it planned to enter the U.S. market and build the Jackson County factory.
Prior to the allegations, SK Innovation had already signed a deal to provide batteries to Volkswagen's factory in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
LG Chem accused SK Innovation of interviewing LG employees for jobs, extracting proprietary information, and deliberately deleting company emails to conceal the misconduct.

ITC investigators recovered numerous deleted emails containing directives such as “Please delete everything in the emails that discuss L-Company,” “You know why,” “Delete every material related to the rival company…,” and “Delete this email after completing this directive.”
An ITC law judge ruled against SK Innovation without holding a hearing, citing the blatant nature of the cover-up.

Congressman Doug Collins stated, “They need to be open and honest with their case and make sure they’re not doing something they shouldn’t be doing,” while acknowledging he was unaware of the trade secret theft complaints during the groundbreaking.
The ITC is scheduled to decide SK Innovation's fate by October, with a worst-case scenario being an exclusion order that would forbid the company from operating in the United States.
Local union leaders are meeting to determine how to secure more construction jobs for Americans at the SK Battery Georgia plant.

State and local leaders find themselves in an uncomfortable position, writing letters of support to the ITC for SK Innovation while attempting to avoid condoning its actions, with some letters citing the local construction jobs already generated by the project.

Union leader David Cagle dismissed claims about job creation as “a joke” and convinced Congressman Collins to ask Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to review who is actually being hired for construction work.
Cagle complained that American workers are being passed over for standard construction jobs in favor of Korean workers who may not be legally in the country.
A portion of a letter from the Jackson County Board of Commissioners to the ITC urges that SK Battery Georgia be allowed to remain open.
In May, U.S. Customs intercepted 33 Koreans at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport carrying “fraudulent employment letters” intended to secure jobs building the battery plant.
David Cagle remarked, “My personal opinion is there’s a lot of politicians that’s going to have egg on their face,” regarding the situation.
Business analysts in Korea predict LG Chem and SK Innovation will ultimately reach a financial settlement, allowing the Jackson County plant to open on schedule in 2022.
SK Battery America stated there is no change to the 2022 opening timetable and told FOX 5 I-Team that it “remains confident in the merits of our case.”

In a statement to FOX 5 I-Team, the company said, “SK Innovation will vigorously defend ourselves in this matter to ensure a robust, innovative EV battery industry in the U.S.,” with the statement notably omitting any mention of the deleted emails.
source_title: How will cheating allegations affect massive EV battery plant in Jackson County? | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: SK Battery America (SKBA), a U.S. subsidiary of SK On, is set to commence mass production of electric vehicle batteries for Hyundai Motor at its Georgia plant starting from October.
This strategic move marks a significant shift in SK On's production lines, which were initially dedicated to supplying batteries to U.S. automaker Ford.
During a second-quarter conference call, SK On highlighted the growing importance of U.S. production for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) due to the scale of subsidies and other factors.
SK On stated that "some line conversion work at the SKBA plant is underway" because the scale of subsidies makes U.S. production increasingly important for OEMs.
According to industry sources on September 22, the conversion of production lines at the SKBA plant is already in progress.
The line conversion is interpreted as a result of aligned interests between Hyundai Motor and SK On.
Hyundai Motor seeks to benefit from the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
SK On aims to improve the operational rate of its Georgia plant through this shift.

The IRA is a significant U.S. legislation designed to reduce inflation by investing in domestic energy production and manufacturing, including provisions that incentivize the production of EVs and their components within the U.S.
The operational rate of the Georgia plant is expected to increase significantly with the line conversion.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to operate a large-scale electric vehicle-only production plant named 'Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America' (HMGMA) in Savannah, Georgia.
This new facility underscores Hyundai's strategic focus on expanding its electric vehicle lineup and establishing a reliable battery supply chain.
The broader context of U.S. policies promoting domestic manufacturing, job creation, and technological innovation in the automotive sector further supports this development.

The conversion of production lines at the SKBA plant aligns with these U.S. policies and addresses complexities of the global supply chain for EV components, including challenges posed by geopolitical factors and trade policies.
source_title: SK On's Georgia Plant to Supply EV Batteries to Hyundai Motor Starting October - Businesskorea

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday.
The last working day for 958 plant employees marked about 37% of the workforce.
A Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice was filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The Commerce, Georgia plant will continue to employ approximately 1,600 workers.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The facility notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction was made to align operations with market conditions.
Collier noted SK remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
SK is pursuing future customers, including opportunities in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The City of Commerce and Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Ford scrapped its fully-electric F-150 version in December and opted for an extended-range version instead.
A Ford spokesperson declined to comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford previously invested $11.4 billion together in joint battery plants in the U.S. before ending the joint venture in December.
SK also supplies batteries to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff (Democrat) stated that the layoffs represent lost battery manufacturing jobs and criticized federal EV policy shifts.
SK had previously announced plans in June 2020 to invest $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time of the 2020 expansion announcement, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office projected it would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other major EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
Georgia led the nation with 33 additional green energy projects announced by the end of 2024, according to environmental business group E2.
Projects in Georgia topped $20 billion in investment, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, resumed normal production on Friday after temporarily reducing hours and pay due to U.S. customs detaining imported solar panel components.
A spokesperson for Gov. Kemp’s office highlighted Georgia’s robust job market, noting over 219,700 new jobs announced since 2019.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, matching the previous year's rate.
Automakers including Ford, General Motors, and Stellantis have reneged on factory, investment, and product plans due to mounting financial losses and shifting demand.
Many automakers across the EV supply chain are pivoting production toward hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles instead of fully electric models.
Consumer concerns over EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability are making hybrids and efficient gasoline vehicles more popular.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress eliminated federal tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers purchasing new or used EVs.
The Trump administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, removing federal incentives for cleaner vehicle fleets.
source_title: SK lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia plant amid cooling automaker EV plans - WTOP News

facts: SK On's North America president outlined the company's strategy for expanding its presence in the U.S. battery energy storage market during an interview with ESN Premium.
The executive emphasized that the firm's established manufacturing footprint in the United States provides a competitive advantage as it seeks to capture market share.
The South Korean technology company currently operates three manufacturing sites in the U.S.
SK Battery America in Commerce, Georgia, has a capacity of 22GWh and began production in 2022.
A joint venture plant with Hyundai, SK Battery Manufacturing America, located in Bartow County, Georgia, has a capacity of 35GWh.
A third facility, SK On Tennessee in Stanton, Tennessee, is rated at 45GWh and is fully constructed with most equipment already installed.
Production at that site is planned to start in 2028, though the president noted that the startup could be accelerated to align with market demand.
That factory originally began as an electric vehicle battery joint venture with Ford before SK On bought out its partner and took full control.
The company recently debuted its second-generation Grid On battery energy storage system at the American Clean Power Association CleanPower 2026 conference in Texas.
The 20-foot standard ISO container solution offers 5MWh of usable energy storage capacity and is available in 1-hour, 2-hour, and 4-hour configurations.
The product uses lithium iron phosphate cells built in SK On's U.S. plants, leveraging core technology from the company's electric vehicle battery business.
Energy density has increased by 20% compared to the previous generation due to incremental product improvements.
The system includes two safety features that SK On described as unique.

One is an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy early-detection solution linked to the battery management system, designed to detect abnormalities up to 30 minutes earlier than existing systems by using microcurrents to scan batteries and their thermal behavior.
The other is a coolant submersion system that activates to maintain battery pack stability in the event of a thermal event.

The president stated that the platform's flexibility, including discharge rates up to 1 degree Celsius, positions the company to address the data center segment as well as renewable and industrial applications.
The first-generation Grid On system, for which SK On is contracted to deliver 7.2GWh to U.S. developer Flatiron Energy for projects beginning this year, is built as DC blocks.
The second-generation product will incorporate AC elements as well, integrating in-house and third-party supplied components to cover the full AC/DC solution.
The president indicated that Grid On will be offered as a global product in markets expected to include Asia and Europe.

According to Solar Media Market Research analysis from August of last year, total U.S. battery cell manufacturing for BESS applications was expected to exceed 50GWh annual production capacity by the end of 2026.
BESS assembly capacity had already surpassed that figure at the time of the analysis.
LFP cell production is still a relatively new development for the U.S. market, with competitors including LG Energy Solution and Envision-owned AESC already in mass production.
Tesla and Canadian Solar had also made U.S. manufacturing announcements around that time.
The American Clean Power Association reported last month that U.S. domestic cell production is projected to reach nearly 180GWh per year by 2028, likely enough to cover market demand.
New entrants such as Ford Energy, the recently launched energy storage subsidiary of U.S. automaker Ford, are also entering the market.

Like LG Energy Solution and Samsung SDI, SK On is converting existing electric vehicle battery production lines to produce LFP cells as electric mobility demand slows in the U.S. amid policy support rollbacks.
The president noted that forecasting demand is somewhat uncertain, observing that projections for 2030 have shifted from a little over 100GW a year ago to around 150GW now.
He stated that several players have capability in the U.S. and are growing in the market, but that the company believes there is enough demand for it to compete and succeed.
SK On is rapidly expanding its energy storage team in the U.S. and plans to double the size of that team over the next six months to support customers.
The president said the company's approach is to cover the full range of needs across renewable, industrial, and data center applications.
source_title: SK On Expands U.S. Battery Storage Market Presence with Second-Generation Grid On System - News and Statistics - IndexBox

entity_name: Georgia EV and Battery Supply Chain
facts: China's move to curtail graphite exports could disrupt the supply chains of U.S. electric vehicle and battery makers that rely on highly processed graphite for plug-in automobiles.
China's graphite export restrictions follow U.S. export controls on semiconductors sold to China, which the U.S. cites as being driven by national security concerns.
The trade tit-for-tat demonstrates China's willingness to leverage its outsize influence over key minerals vital to battery production within the EV supply chain.
Starting in December, China will require export permits for specialized forms of synthetic graphite and natural flake graphite.
China dominates the global market for graphite, which is used in the anode of nearly every EV battery.
In 2021, the U.S. sourced roughly one-third of its graphite supply from China.
China is also a major player in other rare earth minerals used in EV production, including lithium.
A Congressional Resource Service report noted last year that the U.S does not have any active graphite mines and has few prospects to develop domestic reserves.
Kevin Ketels, an assistant professor of global supply chain management at Wayne State University, stated there is no easy way for the U.S. to decouple from China regarding graphite supplies.

Stephanie Valdez Streaty, director of industry insight at Cox Automotive, noted that while the immediate impact of China's actions is unclear, it highlights the importance of a robust and sustainable U.S. supply chain.
China's graphite restrictions have the potential to increase EV prices or place additional cost pressures on automakers, according to Valdez Streaty.
More than 870,000 EVs were sold in the U.S. in the first nine months of the year, with the industry on pace to surpass 1 million units annually for the first time in 2023.

U.S. companies and the federal government have prioritized researching new battery chemistries and finding alternative raw material sources to reduce reliance on China and build a domestic supply chain.
Since 2018, Georgia has recruited more than $25 billion in EV-related investments and commitments for over 30,000 jobs, according to Governor Brian Kemp's office.
Electric vehicles, which produce no tailpipe greenhouse gas emissions, are central to President Joe Biden's climate and economic agenda.

The Biden administration has passed sweeping legislation encouraging automakers and battery companies to manufacture in the U.S., aiming to create end-to-end domestic supply chains and tens of thousands of jobs.
American and foreign brands have responded by announcing new multi-billion-dollar EV and battery plants across the U.S.
SK Battery America built a massive factory in Jackson County, northeast of Atlanta, to supply Volkswagen and Ford.
Synthetic graphite maker Anovion has announced plans for a plant in Bainbridge.
Copper and mineral recycler Aurubis operates a factory in Augusta.
Ascend Elements recycles lithium-ion batteries at a facility in Covington.
Hyundai is constructing a $7.6 billion EV and battery assembly plant in Bryan County near the coast in partnership with LG Energy Solution.
Hyundai is also building a battery plant with an SK subsidiary in Bartow County.
Rivian expects to break ground early next year on its $5 billion factory located an hour east of Atlanta.
Cox Enterprises, which owns the publishing company behind this article, holds approximately a 4% stake in Rivian.
SK stated it does not expect China's graphite restrictions to immediately affect its supplies but is closely monitoring the long-term impact with partners.

Georgia Commissioner of Economic Development Pat Wilson stated that Governor Kemp tasked his agency with recruiting "the jobs of tomorrow," which has diversified the U.S. supply chain and benefited residents statewide.
Critical and rare earth minerals essential for supporting these jobs are controlled by a few countries with unstable, corrupt governments or those unfriendly to U.S. interests, according to Wilson.

When one country controls 90% of the processing of a key mineral like graphite, international recognition demands supply diversification and technology investment to alleviate potential system pinch points.
New battery technology development aimed at reducing reliance on imported minerals is occurring in Georgia.
Matthew McDowell, co-director of Georgia Tech’s Advanced Battery Center, identified silicon as a promising alternative to graphite that is cheaper, more abundant, and stores more energy.
Silicon is already used in smaller applications, with major automakers like GM, Mercedes-Benz, and Porsche banking on it for next-generation batteries.
Sila Nanotechnologies, co-founded by Georgia Tech professor Gleb Yushin, holds a $3.3 billion valuation and has a deal with Mercedes to produce silicon anodes for G-Class vehicles.
The silicon alternative technology still needs to mature, and in the interim, the U.S. must source graphite from more reliable trade partners.
There is currently no solution to fully replace graphite, despite rapid development efforts.
source_title: China curbs on graphite could roil EV supply chain in the U.S.

facts: SK Battery America, SK On’s US subsidiary, will furlough workers at its battery plant in Jackson County, Georgia.
The company is cutting down production amid a sluggish electric vehicle market.
The announcement regarding the furloughs and production cuts was made on Tuesday.
SK Battery America is temporarily adjusting its line operations and furloughing a portion of its production employees.
The decision is part of efforts to optimize line operations and workforce management with flexibility as the EV industry adjusts its growth pace.
The company did not disclose how many employees will be furloughed or the duration of their absence.
SK Battery America operates two battery factories in Jackson County, Georgia.
The combined annual production capacity of the two Jackson County facilities is 21.5 gigawatt-hours.
This capacity is sufficient to power approximately 180,000 EVs.
An SK On official stated that adjusting production up or down based on market status is standard industry practice.
SK Battery America remains committed to its Georgia site and optimistic about the long-term growth of the US EV market.
SK On believes its Georgia site will play a leading role for years to come in manufacturing batteries for American-built EVs.
The company previously announced layoffs in September.
The facility employs approximately 3,000 workers.
The exact number of employees laid off in September was not disclosed.
The factory currently has more employees than its originally targeted hiring goals.
The September layoffs were implemented to align production with current market demands.
SK On has invested a total of $2.6 billion to build the two Georgia battery manufacturing facilities since 2019.
The Georgia facilities supply batteries for EVs including the Ford F-150 Lightning and the Volkswagen ID.4.
SK On is pursuing a $5 billion joint venture with Hyundai Motor Group to build a battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
SK On stated that the Jackson County production adjustments are unconnected to the Bartow County joint venture.
The Hyundai-SK On joint venture plant is expected to begin mass production in the second half of 2025.
The joint venture plant will have an annual production capacity of 35 gigawatt-hours.
SK On is adjusting production lines amid concerns over short-term weakening EV demand driven by economic recessions and rising costs.
LG Energy Solution announced on Saturday that it is forgoing plans to build a battery plant in Turkey.
The abandoned Turkey project was a joint effort with Ford and Koc Holding.
Koc Holding withdrew from the Turkey battery plant project due to reviews regarding EV adoption rates and investment timing.
source_title: SK On to furlough workers, cut production at US plant - The Korea Herald

facts: Ford and South Korean EV battery manufacturer SK On have a long relationship that extends beyond a simple customer-supplier dynamic.
The joint venture between Ford and SK On, known as BlueOval SK, is preparing to begin production of EV batteries at the under-construction BlueOval SK Battery Park in Kentucky very soon.
SK On has signed a battery supply agreement with Nissan.
Under the agreement, SK On will supply Nissan with roughly 100 GWh of EV batteries between 2028 and 2033.
All batteries supplied under the agreement will be built in the U.S.
The batteries utilize high-nickel pouch cells.
The batteries will power Nissan's next-generation EVs.
Nissan's next-generation EVs will be built at the company's plant in Canton, Mississippi starting in 2028.
This agreement marks SK On's first partnership with a Japanese automaker.
SK On's other automotive partners include Ford, Hyundai, and Ferrari.
SK On currently operates two battery plants in the U.S.
SK On is building four additional battery plants with its partners in the U.S.
Once completed and fully operational, SK On's projected annual production capacity in the U.S. will be more than 180 GWh.
Seok-hee Lee, SK On President and CEO, stated that the agreement underscores the strength of their battery technology and growing presence in the North American market.
Seok-hee Lee added that leveraging their production footprint and expertise, they are committed to supporting Nissan’s electrification strategy and the broader transition to sustainable mobility.

Christian Meunier, Chairman of Nissan Americas, stated that the agreement is a significant milestone for Nissan’s electrification journey in North America and supports further investment in their U.S. production footprint.

Christian Meunier noted that through this partnership with SK On, Nissan can leverage their growing U.S. production capacity to deliver innovative, high-quality electric vehicles that meet customer needs.
source_title: Ford EV Partner SK On Signs Battery Supply Agreement With Nissan

entity_name: Georgia Economic Development Deal Evaluation Process
canonical_name: Georgia Economic Development Deal Evaluation Process
facts: Carl Campbell serves as executive director of the Dalton-Whitfield Joint Development Authority.
The Hanwha Q Cells solar manufacturing facility was pursued with a larger strategic goal beyond immediate financial return.
After the recession, the Dalton-Whitfield area lost 10,000 jobs in the carpet and flooring industry.
Campbell stated that bringing in Q Cells was intended to prove the area could attract manufacturers besides flooring.
Local authorities were willing to offer generous incentives for the Hanwha project to demonstrate economic diversification.
Carl Campbell described the Hanwha deal as "richer than we've done in the past" but believed it paid off.
Hanwha Q Cells moved into its building in December 2018.
Hanwha Q Cells hired 500 people within three months of moving in.
State and local governments calculate if money given to companies is worth it by running cost-benefit analyses.
These analyses look at economic impact from new jobs, wages, and potential economic impact from new residents, including school and public safety costs versus property and sales tax revenue.
Factors are summed over time to create a total economic impact known as the net present value (NPV) of the project.
The Hanwha plant received a payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) agreement guaranteeing no property taxes for 10 years and half property tax payments for the next five years.
Dalton-Whitfield did not run a standard cost-benefit analysis on the Hanwha project.
Campbell explained that most Hanwha workers lived outside the community, making standard software (LOCI) inaccurate for counting expected costs and benefits of expanded employment.
Alfie Meek is principal and chief economist at the Economic Impact Group, which licenses LOCI software.
Meek disagreed with skipping cost-benefit analysis, stating LOCI can use community-specific patterns and remains best practice.
Meek noted that communities often do not understand local financial costs related to economic development projects, especially regarding new households.
The SK Battery project in Jackson County had a state-estimated NPV of $5.7 million.
The SK Battery project was estimated to create up to 2,700 more jobs through increased economic activity.
The Wayfair facility in Athens had an estimated NPV of about $150,000.
Many Georgia municipalities use LOCI software for cost-benefit analyses using community-specific data.
Meek stated that anything over $100K NPV is probably quite positive when evaluating deals at the local level.
LOCI can analyze cost to specific entities, such as total county tax revenue impact, as well as overall economy.
Meek noted that a negative fiscal impact on a jurisdiction does not necessarily mean a project should be rejected if it meets larger strategic goals.
Local communities need to know the cost to achieve strategic goals before moving forward with projects.
Major industrial projects are part of Georgia's economic development and can bring thousands of jobs and significant local government investments.
The USA TODAY Network-Georgia examined six major economic development projects across Georgia using open records requests, state data, and official interviews.

The examined projects included the Hanwha Q Cell facility, SK Battery plant in Jackson County, Amazon facilities in Bibb County, Wayfair facilities in Athens and Savannah, and a warehouse development project under development in Savannah.
source_title: How Georgia evaluates economic development deals to bring in more jobs

entity_name: Battery Belt / Southern EV Manufacturing Boom
facts: Hyundai’s first U.S. plant for manufacturing electric vehicles is under construction in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
Construction is actively underway at the Toyota Battery mega-site in Liberty, North Carolina.
Production at the Toyota Battery site is slated to begin in 2025.
By 2030, when the 7 million-square-foot complex is fully operational, it will have 14 production lines.
The Toyota Battery complex will feature 10 production lines dedicated to batteries for electric vehicles and plug-in hybrid electrics.
The Toyota Battery complex will feature four production lines for hybrid electric vehicles.
The Toyota Battery complex will be operated by 5,100 workers.
The total population of Liberty, North Carolina is 2,655.
The Toyota Battery mega-site carries a staggering $13.9 billion price tag.
Federal and state governments are driving a manufacturing boom across nearly every aspect of the EV supply and production chain in the South.
In Kings Mountain, North Carolina, two companies backed by a $90 million grant from the Department of Defense plan to drain a former open-pit lithium mine lake and resume mining.
The Kings Mountain lithium mining project expects to yield enough lithium for 1.2 million electric vehicles per year.
This lithium will be refined at a soon-to-open $1.3 billion facility in nearby Chester County, South Carolina.
Much of the dramatic expansion in investment is attributed to the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) passed in 2022.
The IRA provided tremendous financial incentives, largely through grants and tax credits, to encourage cleaner energy production.
State governments are offering record-breaking incentive deals to EV manufacturers including tax breaks, infrastructure development, and workforce development funding.
Georgia will provide Hyundai up to $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other state assistance for its $7.9 billion capital investment in an EV and battery production complex outside Savannah.
The Hyundai complex in Georgia is forecast to employ up to 8,500 workers.
The IRA aims to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and slow climate change while competing with China, which currently produces the most EVs at the lowest prices.
Stephen Roach, former chair of Morgan Stanley Asia, stated in July that the U.S. wants to contain China even if it comes at the expense of addressing climate change.
The Biden administration recently imposed massive tariffs on imports from China related to various aspects of EV production.
These policies aim to create an entirely domestic supply and production chain for electric vehicles.
Since the passage of the IRA, companies have announced $110 billion in capital investments in new EV manufacturing.
Roughly half of that money and the corresponding jobs are destined for the U.S. South according to the EV Jobs Hub.
Eighty-five percent of the investments are concentrated in right-to-work states.
S&P Global Market Intelligence estimates that as many as two-thirds of EV jobs may eventually be located in the South.
Michigan tops the list in EV capital investments, followed closely by Georgia, North Carolina, and Tennessee with $23 billion, $20 billion, and $17 billion respectively.
In Tennessee, a joint venture between Ford and SK On is investing $5.6 billion to develop BlueOval City outside of Memphis.
BlueOval City is a six-square-mile complex where 6,000 workers will produce EVs and batteries.
The company boasts that BlueOval City will be among the largest auto manufacturing facilities in U.S. history.
The site will receive batteries from a BlueOval SK Battery Park in Kentucky.
The BlueOval SK Battery Park in Kentucky is a $5.8 billion project that will employ another 5,000 people.
Outside of Columbia, South Carolina, Scout Motors (owned and operated by Volkswagen) recently broke ground on a site it has invested $2 billion into.
The Scout Motors site will be operational in 2026.
Once operational, the Scout Motors site will employ 4,000 workers and produce 200,000 vehicles a year.
South Carolina is the country’s number one exporter of tires and home to BMW’s massive 11,000-worker plant in Spartanburg.
The BMW Spartanburg plant will soon begin receiving batteries from a nearby facility operated by AESC.
In April, AESC announced an expansion adding a second battery production facility for a total investment of $3.1 billion.

Auto and other industries have been steadily increasing their footprint in Southern states for several decades due to the region's pro-business climate, lowest union density, lowest wages, and stringent anti-labor laws.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson told the Financial Times last October that massive labor stoppages in traditional union states will benefit Southern states as companies look to grow.
In March, Georgia passed a law forbidding companies that receive state incentives from recognizing a union by any method other than secret ballot elections.
South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster pledged in his State of the State address this year to fight unions "to the gates of hell."

In a joint statement meant to deter Chattanooga Volkswagen workers from organizing, McMaster and five other Southern governors contended that the UAW drive threatens their values and would put their states' jobs in jeopardy.
Nearly every new foreign-owned auto plant that has opened in the U.S. since the 1990s has chosen the South for its base of operations.
The Midwest's share of automotive jobs has fallen from around 60 percent to around 45 percent, while the South’s share grew from 15 percent in 1990 to 30 percent today.
The South is home to 60 percent of Black workers and a growing population of immigrant workers.
Nearly two-thirds of plant construction investments in Southern states are for manufacturing in general, not just EVs.
The UAW has committed $40 million over the next two years to organize 13 non-union automakers largely concentrated in the South.
Workers at Ultium Cells in Lordstown, Ohio, which manufactures batteries for General Motors, recently won a strong agreement providing the UAW’s initial step into the EV industry.
Chattanooga Volkswagen workers won their union vote overwhelmingly in April.
Several weeks later, Daimler Truck North America workers in North Carolina, Georgia, and Tennessee won a record contract.
Workers at Mercedes in Alabama came up short in their May vote on unionizing.
Last year, workers in Georgia at Blue Bird, the country’s second-largest bus manufacturer which includes electric buses among its vehicles, won a victory to join the Steel Workers (USW).
Workers at New Flyer in Alabama, another manufacturer of electrified buses, voted yes this year for the Industrial Division of the Communications Workers (IUE-CWA).
The Southern Workers Assembly, a cross-sector rank-and-file network, has recently launched a program aimed at getting workers jobs in strategic sectors to organize.
source_title: New ‘Battery Belt’ Opens Organizing Front In The South - PopularResistance.Org

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that SK Battery America (SKBA) will open a regional IT hub facility in Roswell.
The new facility will create 200 high-tech jobs.
The investment for the IT center is approximately $19 million over the next few years.
Governor Brian Kemp stated: “We're proud of SK Battery America’s continued growth here in the No. 1 state for business.”

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “This innovative company continues to bring the jobs of the future that will benefit hardworking Georgians in manufacturing, and now it will also tap into the tech talent our top-ranking universities provide.”
SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The Jackson County facilities will supply electric vehicles such as the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen ID.4.
The SKBA facility is one of SK Group’s three major investments in the State of Georgia.
SK Group was founded in 1976.
SK Group is the second-largest conglomerate in South Korea.

Jason Choi, Head of Information Technology at SK Battery America, stated: “We decided to open our IT regional center in Roswell to strengthen SK’s position as a leader in the battery industry, and it will help us build and operate an advanced manufacturing system in accordance with further expansion of battery production bases in the United States.”
Jason Choi stated: “We are excited to expand our presence and engage with the community and surrounding region.”
SK Battery America’s new IT center will be an integrated IT management center serving the company’s battery manufacturing facilities across the United States, including its Georgia facilities.
The IT center is located at 1110 Sanctuary Parkway in Roswell.
Operations are expected to begin in January.
The company will be hiring IT experts for battery manufacturing execution system development and operations as well as data analysts.
City of Roswell Mayor Kurt Wilson stated: “We are looking forward to SK Battery America becoming part of our community and the opportunities it will bring to Roswell.”

Mayor Kurt Wilson stated: “Since taking office a year ago, our priority has been to recruit businesses such as SK Battery America — businesses that can provide high-quality job opportunities for Roswell’s talented, experienced workforce and enhance the vibrancy and economic strength of our city.”
Mayor Kurt Wilson stated: “Roswell is the perfect location for the IT Regional Center. We are proud SK Battery chose our city for the center and look forward to working closely with them.”
Robb Pitts, Chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners, stated: “We welcome SK Battery America to Fulton County and the many new job opportunities they will create for our residents.”
Robb Pitts stated: “Fulton County is a destination for the best and brightest, and today’s announcement reflects that.”
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was in partnership with City of Roswell, Fulton County, and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: “In 2019, SK On established SK Battery America as its U.S. production base, and the new IT regional center is a natural result of the company’s commitment to Georgia.”

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: “In economic development, we often talk about the ripple effects of a company’s initial investment. Suppliers, support centers, and expansions create new opportunities that go beyond the company’s original scope, location, and even industry.”

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: “We look forward to future opportunities to celebrate SK Battery America and SK On’s success in Georgia, and congratulations to the City of Roswell and Fulton County for this exciting addition to the region’s tech industry!”
Electric mobility related projects have contributed more than $21 billion in investments to Georgia since 2020.
Electric mobility related projects have contributed more than 26,700 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.
SK Battery America’s new IT regional center will support the company’s battery production operations.
The center will build on the state’s existing automotive assets and developing workforce.
The center will help to ensure a domestic supply chain for the electric mobility industry.
source_title: GA: SK Battery America to Open U.S. IT Center, Create 200 High-Tech Jobs in Roswell | Trade and Industry Development

entity_name: US Federal Electric Vehicle Tax Credit & Incentive Policy
canonical_name: United States Federal Electric Vehicle Tax Credit Program
facts: The new-for-2023 tax incentive requires the battery to be manufactured in the US and approximately 50% of all battery materials to be domestic.
A criticism of the new-for-2023 tax incentive was that no cars currently met the criteria for battery manufacturing and domestic material sourcing.
The federal EV tax credit is $7,500, structured as $3,750 plus $3,750.
Starting January 1st, the manufacturer sales cap for the federal tax credit goes away.
Illinois offers a $4,000 incentive on EVs.
The EV itself must be assembled domestically, not necessarily the battery pack.
Battery materials can qualify if they come from countries with free trade agreements (FTAs) with the US.
KORUS (Korea-US FTA) means SK batteries are acceptable for material sourcing requirements.
For 2023 unlimited cap credits, at least 50% of battery pack components must be sourced from the US, ramping up by 10% each year.
Battery pack components do not receive the same free trade agreement language as materials to spur domestic production.
The Bolt may become eligible for a $3,750 battery materials credit next year depending on sourcing, IRS final rules, and GM certification.
LG is likely sourcing its battery pack components mainly from SK, which probably disqualifies it from the component credit half.
Panasonic and Tesla produce battery pack components like cells and shells in Nevada.

The critical minerals requirement mandates that a specific percentage of the value of applicable critical minerals contained in the battery must be extracted or processed in the US, an FTA country, or recycled in North America.
For vehicles placed in service after proposed guidance is issued but before January 1, 2024, the applicable critical minerals percentage is 40 percent.
The battery components requirement mandates that a specific percentage of the value of components must be manufactured or assembled in North America.
GM is prioritizing battery replacements in customer cars over new vehicle sales.
Many 2022 EUVs have been sitting on dealer lots for a year waiting for battery replacements.
Dealer markups and market adjustments are driven by supply and demand, with some dealers adding thousands to MSRP.
Australia is starting to introduce incentives for EV buyers, but currently lacks incentives for manufacturers to sell them there.
Australia does not have fleet emissions standards or a cut-off date for ICE sales, which may delay adoption by 5-10 years compared to the rest of the world.
Used EV imports to Africa are currently small because only about 10% of EVs are old enough to enter the used import market, but this will accelerate as the EV market grows.

links.context: Breakdown of which electric vehicles still qualify for US federal tax credit
Official list of vehicles meeting federal tax credit requirements

source_title: The Electric Car Thread | Page 396 | Ars OpenForum

canonical_name: SK On-Hyundai Motor Group Joint Venture Battery Plant
facts: Incentives for SK-Hyundai EV battery plant could total $641M.

Two Korean conglomerates could receive more than $640 million in grants, tax breaks, free infrastructure and other incentives from state and local governments to build a $5 billion battery factory in Bartow County.
The incentive package offered to SK On was detailed in documents obtained by The Atlanta Journal-Constitution through the Georgia Open Records Act.
The company later brought on Hyundai Motor Group as an equal partner.
Plans for the factory near Cartersville were announced in December.
It is expected to employ at least 3,750 workers with an average wage of nearly $53,000.
The factory will be roughly the size of two Lenox Square malls.
The facility will join other multibillion-dollar endeavors by SK and Hyundai across Georgia, furthering the Peach State’s status as an EV hub.
The Bartow plant will supply batteries to the Kia factory in West Point and a Hyundai plant in Alabama that are incorporating EVs into their production lines.
Total incentives for the project amount to $641.4 million.
State tax breaks total $191.02 million.
Local job creation grants total $89.3 million.
Plant property tax abatement totals $64.0 million.
ABER tax credit amounts to $4.0 million.
Georgia Quality Jobs grant totals $11.0 million.
State and local infrastructure funding totals $6.0 million.
Local economic development incentives total $3.4 million.
Total value not included in the incentive package is $25.1 million.
Total value of incremental incentives received by the plant is not disclosed.
SK On staved off competition from Kia, Hyundai Genesis EV, and others to build a multibillion-dollar battery plant.
SK On was awarded the project after outcompeting other bidders for state and local incentives.
The Georgia Open Records Act allowed AJC to obtain the incentive documents.
The facility will be located in Cartersville, Bartow County.
SK On CEO Kim Jong-ho said, 'We are thrilled to bring this project to Georgia.'
Michael Moore, Secretary of Transportation, said that as a state and local government, they are committed to creating jobs and economic development for the future, calling it a win-win situation.
source_title: Georgia offered $641M in incentives to woo SK-Hyundai EV battery plant

facts: The Georgia Environmental Protection Division will hold a virtual hearing on Tuesday evening about Solvay Specialty Polymers USA LLC.
Solvay is a chemical plant seeking a permit to significantly expand its operations in Augusta, Ga.
The virtual hearing was requested by Savannah Riverkeeper and 17 local groups.
Southern Environmental Law Center and Savannah Riverkeeper are concerned about plans for more air emissions from the facility.
The groups state that Solvay’s current permit classifies it as a minor source of pollution that relies on weak and unenforceable limits.
In a news release, the groups stated that “Given the potential for significant pollution, Solvay should be classified as a major source and held to stricter emissions standards.”

facts: Stryten Energy LLC installed its advanced vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) system at Snapping Shoals EMC, marking the first VRFB energy storage system manufactured and installed in Georgia.
The installation was celebrated on August 2, 2023.
The VRFB system can store and provide clean energy to Snapping Shoals customers for up to 20 years without losing capacity.

Stryten Energy's partnership with Snapping Shoals EMC will focus on demonstrating the VRFB's energy storage capabilities and evaluating additional use cases for long-duration energy storage technology.

Shaun Mock, President and Chief Executive Officer of Snapping Shoals EMC, identified Stryten Energy as a natural partner in harnessing VRFB power for long-duration energy storage as the utility shifts to clean energy sources.
The VRFB system will support Snapping Shoals EMC's 100,000+ residential and business customers by reliably and affordably storing clean energy.

Mike Judd, Chief Executive Officer and President of Stryten Energy, stated that long-duration energy storage technologies are needed to support widescale renewable power generation and that VRFB is ideally suited for this application.
The demonstration project aims to prove VRFB capabilities in utility use cases including energy cost control, peak shaving, and avoiding curtailment.
Stryten Energy's VRFB offers industry-leading power density with a versatile, modular platform that is independently scalable in power and capacity.
With proper maintenance, the VRFB system has an unlimited cycle life and can operate for more than 20 years without the electrolyte losing energy storage capacity.
The technology is uniquely suited for medium- to long-duration energy storage applications requiring six or more hours to ensure grid stability and increase renewable utilization across the U.S.
The Department of Energy reports that Georgia has more than 3.6 gigawatts (GW) of solar, wind, and storage capacity.
Almost 1.1 GW of additional planned clean energy capacity is in development in Georgia, which will power more than 155,000 homes.
Snapping Shoals EMC serves approximately 100,000 homes and businesses across an eight-county area including Newton, Henry, Rockdale, DeKalb, Butts, Walton, Jasper, and Morgan counties.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company is introducing the M-Series Li710, a new line of Class I and II lithium batteries in a range of voltages, at ProMat 2025 in Chicago.
The proprietary module design for the M-Series Li710 is assembled in the USA.
The M-Series Li710 provides greater energy density in a maintenance-free package to power Class I and II lift trucks.
Stryten’s proprietary lithium module design utilizes nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) chemistry.
The NMC chemistry offers greater energy density for longer run times and a faster charge rate than lead and lithium iron phosphate (LFP) chemistries.
The batteries are ideal for 24x7 operations with limited charging opportunities.
Stryten will assemble the M-Series Li710 lithium batteries at its new lithium assembly plant in Cumming, Georgia.
Matt Gould serves as Vice President of Industrial Sales and Service at Stryten Energy.
The Li710 is field serviceable by a service network strategically located across North America.
The M-Series Li710 line features fault tolerance to maintain system availability.
The Li710 includes an automated hibernation/sleep mode after inactivity to prevent unintended discharge.
The Li710 products are compatible with the M-Series X-3 and X-7 chargers.
The Li710 products are designed to meet UL2580, and UL certification is in process.
The M-Series Li600 is based on lithium iron phosphate (LFP) chemistry.
The M-Series Li600 is available for Class I, Class II, and Class III motive power applications.
The M-Series Li600 features a fully configurable CANbus protocol, remote monitoring capability, comprehensive diagnostics via a color touch screen, and auto hibernation/sleep mode after inactivity.
The M-Series Li600 products are designed to meet UL2580, and UL certification is in process.
The M-Series T310 is a low maintenance tubular lead battery for medium to heavy duty lift truck applications.
The M-Series F110 features an extremely conductive and robust flat-plate grid design coupled with an industry-leading manufacturing process for superior performance in heavy-duty applications.

The M-Series X-7 charger allows configuration to multiple power levels, including 208-240V ideal for areas with lower power infrastructure such as the Northeast U.S., Florida, Mexico, and Latin America.
The new chargers offer IoT communication capabilities, including Wi-fi and Bluetooth.
The chargers can be configured for standard, opportunity, or fast charging to meet fleet demands.
The M-Series X-3 charger has an advanced 24/36/48V and 72/80V multi-voltage modular design available in 3 bay (3-9kW), 6 bay (3-18kW), and 10 bay (21-30kW) cabinet power levels.
The M-Series X-3 charger can be configured for either opportunity or fast charging.
