facts: On 23 Apr, Georgia Tech and Stryten Energy LLC announced the successful installation of Stryten Energy’s Lead Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at the Carbon Neutral Energy Solutions Laboratory (CNES).
The CNES building is located in the North Avenue Research Area of the Georgia Tech campus.

The CNES houses the Strategic Energy Institute (SEI), an interdisciplinary research institute focused on energy research, and multiple research groups dedicated to renewable energy and energy infrastructure-related topics.
The installation aims to create a living-learning lab on campus that supports research and real-world applications of medium-duration energy storage solutions.
Lead BESS was selected for this initial installation due to its cost-effectiveness, high discharge rates, and recyclability, backed by extensive research demonstrating its reliable performance.
The BESS is a dynamic storage system that integrates renewable energy sources into the existing power mix, providing stable and dependable backup power and reducing grid dependency during peak hours.

With additional components and software, the system is capable of bi-directional charging, allowing current to flow into the battery for charging and out of the battery to power the grid or microgrid.
At the CNES lab, Georgia Tech researchers can control charging and discharging cycles for the battery in coordination with the existing Solar PV array and a new EV charging test bed.
This research tool will allow the time-shifting of peak solar input by several hours to meet late afternoon building loads and store renewable energy for the overnight charging of campus vehicles.

The role of Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) in the broader energy landscape is a crucial area of research, particularly understanding their impact on the grid, their contribution to system reliability, and their effect on energy costs.

Christine Conwell, SEI’s interim executive director at Georgia Tech, stated that Georgia Tech’s strategic plan envisions the campus as a dynamic laboratory and experimental test bed where sustainable practices are seamlessly integrated into operations.

Conwell noted that through enduring partnerships with organizations like Stryten, they are creating mini ecosystems that yield valuable situational data to help chart a path for innovative energy research well beyond the campus.
Scott Childers, vice president of essential power at Stryten Energy, stated that as solar and other renewables hit the market years ago, large utility-scale implementations were clearly the focus.
Childers added that with the introduction of this BESS powered by lead batteries, behind-the-meter applications are getting their day in the sun.
Childers expressed particular excitement about deploying this unit in commercial and industrial microgrids and paired with EV charging stations to help the U.S. achieve its energy goals.
Childers praised Georgia Tech as a tremendous partner and highlighted the plan to demonstrate the advantages of lead BESS from cost savings, technology, environmental, and safety perspectives.
Richard Simmons, SEI’s director of research and studies at Georgia Tech, called the Stryten lead BESS system an enabling piece of the Distributed Energy Resources (DER) puzzle.
Simmons stated it is their hope that the lead BESS will be one of several living lab battery pilots at Georgia Tech.
Along with regional partners, Georgia Tech researchers are exploring similar R&D and testing projects involving flow batteries that can facilitate longer-duration storage.
Researchers are also exploring lithium-ion BESS that may integrate second-life EV battery modules for grid resilience, driving advancements in sustainable energy research.
The Georgia Institute of Technology is one of the top public research universities in the U.S., developing leaders who advance technology and improve the human condition.

Georgia Tech’s leading researchers work across the energy value chain in basic and applied science in EVs, photovoltaics, hydrogen, carbon capture, industrial decarbonization, grid security and resilience, and related social sciences.
Georgia Tech is consistently ranked among the top universities in the nation for graduating underrepresented minorities in engineering, physical sciences, and energy-related fields.
U.S. News & World Report recently ranked Georgia Tech as the No. 1 public university and No. 3 overall in energy and fuels research.

Serving as a regional resource to help communities understand how they can transition to a clean energy economy, Georgia Tech is the leader in achieving regional impact through education and contributions to communities.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC will launch the M-Series Li600, a new line of Class I, II and III lithium batteries, at MODEX 2024 in Atlanta, Georgia.
The Li600 batteries are available in Class I, II and III, in a full range of voltages with optional cold storage capability down to -40 C.
The Li600 for Class III features an onboard charger option.
The full line of Li600 batteries is compatible with the M-Series X-3 and X-7 chargers.
The Li600 is based on lithium iron phosphate (LFP) chemistry, which has a longer calendar life and a longer cycle life than other lithium chemistries.
The LFP chemistry used in the Li600 is comprised of readily available materials, making it a cost-competitive option and one of the safest lithium technologies.

Key features of the M-Series Li600 include fully configurable CANbus protocol, remote monitoring capability, comprehensive diagnostics via a color touch screen and auto hibernation/sleep mode after inactivity to prevent unintended discharge.
The M-Series Li600 products are designed to meet UL2580, with UL certification currently in process.

Stryten Energy is showcasing the M-Series T310 Lead Solution, a low maintenance tubular battery that offers optimized power, performance, cost and reduced maintenance for medium to heavy duty lift truck applications.
Stryten Energy’s round tube design for the T310 is more efficient and delivers longer run times than competitive square tube designs.
The M-Series X-7 4-bay charger delivers 30kW of power while handling lead and lithium battery types, and can be configured for standard, opportunity or fast charging.
The M-Series X-7 charger features a modular design that offers flexibility, reduced operating costs and accelerated charging times to meet the demands of the toughest fleets.
Stryten Energy inCOMMAND™ is an energy performance management software tool designed to make it easy to design and deploy web-connected battery and charger solutions.
The inCOMMAND software allows dealers and end users to monitor and manage their batteries and chargers right from their computers.

Matt Gould, Vice President of Industrial Sales and Service at Stryten Energy, stated that with the addition of the M-Series Li600 line, the company is a “one-stop-shop" to serve customers’ needs with a complete portfolio of battery technologies.
The Li600 line is field serviceable by Stryten Energy’s service network strategically located across the U.S., Canada and Mexico.

Trained stored energy experts provide planned maintenance, repair services, predictive service, installations, de-installation, recycling, application audits, power studies, training, and inspections for the batteries.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company provides energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military and Government sectors.
Stryten Energy’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers and energy performance management software.

facts: U.S.-based Stryten Energy announced the expansion of its battery production capacity to 24GWh across the U.S.
The strategic layout covers key areas such as military and grid energy storage.
Stryten Energy intends to comprehensively upgrade the production capacity of its 11 battery assembly plants located in Arkansas, Georgia and other places.
The company will lay out multi-technology routes including lead-acid, lithium-ion, and all-vanadium liquid current batteries.
The technology routes aim to satisfy the differentiated needs of the military in terms of high-reliability and long-duration energy storage on the grid.
Stryten Energy is investing in recycled plastic technology for used batteries.
The company will use recycled materials for new battery production.
Stryten Energy's new lithium battery assembly plant in Georgia has recently gone into production.
The new Georgia plant utilizes patented lithium module technology.
The new Georgia plant focuses on the military and industrial battery market.
Stryten Energy's capacity expansion plans align with U.S. government policy supporting local supply chains and clean energy.
Ravi Manghani, Senior Director of Strategic Sourcing at Anza Renewables, stated that local sourcing is still the least risky option for the U.S. energy storage industry at this stage.
There has been an observed acceleration in local energy storage manufacturing.
Companies that have traditionally focused on the electric vehicle market are beginning to shift to energy storage as the growth rate of electric vehicles has fallen short of expectations.
Battery manufacturers are aggressively expanding their customer base due to growing demand for energy storage and favorable market conditions.
This expansion is directly contributing to planning announcements for more local manufacturing facilities.
Stryten Energy is using technology partnerships and vertical integration to gradually eliminate its dependence on key materials.
The company's strategy aims to support the autonomous development of the U.S. energy storage industry.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced in August 2022 that SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC., will locate its first U.S. recycling facility in Georgia.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC. is a subsidiary of the Korea-based global industry leader SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd.
The company will invest more than $37 million in the new facility.
The project will create 104 jobs in Stephens County.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia recently announced record-breaking numbers for fiscal year 2022 and companies like SungEel are evidence that the rapidly-growing electric mobility ecosystem continues to generate new jobs for hardworking Georgians across the state.

Governor Brian Kemp noted that Korea has been a key partner in Georgia’s growing sustainable technology industry and expressed excitement that SungEel chose the Peach State for their first U.S. facility.
SungEel has more than 10 years of patented technology specializing in complete recycling technology using a 100% full circulatory system.

The recycling system is able to recycle five valuable metals, including nickel, cobalt, and lithium, from end of life batteries and scrap from battery manufacturing facilities with a metal recovery rate of more than 95%.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia’s new facility will be located at the Hayestone Brady Business Park in Toccoa.
The Hayestone Brady Business Park is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The GRAD Program is a proactive effort by Georgia’s economic development community to develop a portfolio of available sites ready for fast-track development.
Operations are expected to begin in early 2024.
Over the next few years, the company will be hiring for technicians, operators, administrative, and sales roles.

Brian Atkins, Chairman of the Stephens County Development Authority, stated it has been a pleasure working with SungEel HiTech over the past eight months and learning more about the company and its processes.
Brian Atkins noted this is an exciting time for Toccoa-Stephens County and a solid boost for the local job market.
Brian Atkins highlighted that investments in infrastructure, workforce development, and education were significant factors in the selection process.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on this competitive project in partnership with the Stephens County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated SungEel is a company at the cutting edge of sustainable technology and believes SungEel’s proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to amazing success.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson emphasized that creating the jobs of the future and protecting the opportunities of today means preparing key industries, such as the auto industry, for the next technological revolution.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed gratitude to partners in Stephens County for their investment in speed to market solutions like site preparation.

Georgia’s prime location, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and business-friendly climate has made it an attractive location for a diverse array of rapidly developing industries focused on creating a sustainable future.
Georgia is cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain that will help companies increase efficiencies by reducing the reliance on imported materials.
EV-related projects have contributed more than $13 billion in investments and more than 18,100 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.

SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd. is headquartered in Korea and has grown into a global leader in lithium ion battery recycling and raw material provider under the vision of Finite Resources to Infinite Resources.
SungEel HiTech currently operates facilities in South Korea, China, Malaysia, India, and Hungary, with new facilities underway in Poland and Germany.

The company’s objectives are to further improve the environment and to create value to customers, stockholders, and communities through innovation in recycling technology, material processing, and products.
source_title: Korean Lithium Ion Battery Recycler to Build First U.S. Recycling Facility in Georgia – Stephens County Development Authority

facts: SungEel HiTech Co. is a South Korean company that recycles metals and battery components.
The company plans to build its first U.S. facility in North Georgia, another addition to the state’s burgeoning electric vehicle sector.
SungEel HiTech will build a $37 million recycling center in Toccoa in Stephens County.
The facility will recycle lithium-ion batteries and battery manufacturing scrap material.
According to a news release Thursday from Gov. Brian Kemp’s office, the company plans to hire 104 people for the future recycling center.
The recycling center is expected to begin operations in early 2024.
EVs make up a tiny percentage of vehicles on American roads.
Automakers and the federal government are investing hundreds of billions of dollars, collectively, to promote electric vehicle manufacturing.

President Joe Biden has signed a bipartisan infrastructure bill and the Democrats’ climate and health care bill that includes funding to expand EV manufacturing and supply chain, charging and tax credits to buy the vehicles.
Gov. Brian Kemp’s office issued a news release announcing the project.
source_title: SungEel HiTech to open battery recycling facility in Georgia targeting EV sector

facts: Turkish hose maker Teklas is opening its first US factory in Georgia.
The new facility will serve as the company's US headquarters.
Teklas is spending $6.5 million (€5.3 million) to open the site in Gordon County, Georgia.
The company expects to hire 120 workers at the new facility.
Teklas makes a variety of fluid circulation systems for automotive applications, including hoses and pipes made from rubber, plastic and metal.
Production at the 200,000-sq.-ft. facility in Calhoun, Georgia, is expected to begin next spring.
Jobs will be available in a variety of skill levels, including advanced manufacturing, management and supervisory positions.
Teklas Regional Director Arcan Ergur stated the Calhoun location will help the company be closer to its main customers.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development highlighted Teklas' work with electric vehicles when discussing the project.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: "As the automotive industry continues to transform and move toward electric vehicles and e-mobility, we will work to foster an ecosystem of manufacturers and suppliers on the leading edge of the industry's transition."
The state indicated the Georgia location "will include assembly lines to serve the most prominent original equipment manufacturers in the industry."
Along with operations in Turkey, Teklas has plants in Bulgaria, Serbia, China and Mexico.
Sales offices are located in Germany and France.
The new US location will be the company's 10th manufacturing site and serve as its North American headquarters.
The company currently has about 5,600 workers.
Teklas' production of rubber-injected parts began in 1971, and rubber hose manufacturing started two years later.
Windshield wiper products were introduced in 1978.

The company has been particularly active regarding expansion in recent years, including a new investment for a production facility in Vratsa, Bulgaria, last year, in addition to another site in Kardzhali, Bulgaria, in 2017.

Teklas decided to move into Mexico with a production facility in Aguascalientes in 2016, just one year after establishing a location in Vladicin, Serbia, and two years after opening two production plants in Kardzhali.
The company's move into China took place in 2012 with a production site in Chang Zing.
Teklas' decision to expand internationally began in 2006 when the firm made its first overseas investment in its initial plant in Kardzhali.
Teklas exports 90% of its products and has customers in 35 countries, according to its website.
source_title: Turkish hose maker Teklas to open first US factory  | European Rubber Journal

canonical_name: City of Calhoun, Gordon County, Georgia
facts: Manufacturing is the primary economic sector in Gordon County, employing over 6,700 workers (about 25% of the total labor force) and centered on carpet, flooring, and textile production.
Major manufacturing employers in Calhoun include Shaw Industries Group and Mohawk Industries, both leveraging regional expertise in tufted carpet manufacturing.
Shaw Industries operates multiple facilities in Calhoun, including a production plant located at 355 South Industrial Boulevard.
Shaw Industries has invested in yarn processing capacity and maintains ongoing hiring for dozens of positions as of 2025.
Mohawk Industries relocated its headquarters to Georgia and maintains a significant manufacturing presence in Calhoun.
Mannington Mills made a 2020 investment in a 10.5-acre Calhoun site, creating 268 jobs in related flooring production.

Carpet and flooring operations account for a plurality of manufacturing jobs in Gordon County within the northwest Georgia cluster that produces approximately 85% of U.S. carpet within a 65-mile radius of Dalton.
Georgia's carpet mills are projected to employ about 16,473 workers statewide as of 2025.
Calhoun serves as a logistics hub for northwest Georgia's emerging industries, facilitated by its strategic location along Interstate 75.

Mid-20th-century construction of Interstate 75 enhanced connectivity to Atlanta and Chattanooga, enabling larger-scale manufacturing and attracting investment in heavy equipment and outboard motors alongside textiles.

The Gordon County Campus of Georgia Northwestern Technical College (GNTC), located at 1151 Highway 53 Spur SW, Calhoun, GA 30701, provides postsecondary education and workforce training tailored to regional industrial demands.
GNTC's core offerings emphasize practical skills in aviation and industrial technologies, including welding and precision machining, as well as business, cyber-related, and health technologies.
GNTC partners with local businesses for apprenticeships and customized training to address skill gaps in technical sectors.
Diversification efforts into logistics and non-textile manufacturing are complementing but not supplanting the traditional carpet and textile base in Calhoun.
Gordon County's gross domestic product totaled $3.142 billion in 2023, reflecting growth from $3.068 billion in 2022.
The county supported approximately 26,800 jobs in 2023, with employment expanding by 1.64% from the prior year amid an unemployment rate averaging around 3.3% through 2024.
source_title: Calhoun, Georgia — Grokipedia

facts: Augusta, Georgia-based Textron Specialized Vehicles is recalling certain Personal Transportation Vehicles or PTVs citing fall and injury risks.
The recall involves about 280 units of 2022 E-Z-GO Freedom RXV ELiTE, Freedom RXV Gas, and Freedom RXV 48V personal transportation vehicles with a rear-facing seat.
Recalled units have serial numbers within the range of 5633701 to 5651753.
These vehicles are commonly referred to as golf carts or golf cars and are used primarily to transport people.

The vehicles were manufactured in the United States and sold at E-Z-GO dealerships nationwide and online through E-Z-GO's Dealer to Driveway program, from August 2021 through December 2021 for between $9,900 and $17,450.
The rear seat structure of the PTV can fail, resulting in the rear seat tilting unexpectedly, posing fall and injury hazards to the riders.
The company has not received any reports of incidents or injuries related to the recalled vehicles to date.
Consumers are asked to stop using the rear facing seat of their recalled vehicle and contact Textron for a free repair.
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Recalls Personal Transportation Vehicles  | Nasdaq

facts: Textron is the umbrella company that manufactures E-Z-Go golf cars, which have been produced in Augusta since 1954.
Brandon Haddock serves as Textron Specialized Vehicles’ director of communications.
Textron employs between 1,400 and 1,500 people in Georgia, with approximately 1,200 working daily in Augusta.
Textron operates a plant in Kennesaw that manufactures ground support equipment for the aviation industry.
Textron manufactures hundreds of different models of vehicles and equipment in Augusta for the golf industry, commercial businesses, and consumers.
Textron acquired the former Proctor & Gamble facility located near its headquarters in South Augusta.
The acquired site contains about 600,000 square feet of manufacturing space on a 240-acre plot.
Textron has been building at the new site for over a year as of the interview date.
Textron announced it would integrate and relocate the manufacturing and operations of its sister company Jacobsen from Charlotte to Augusta.
The first Jacobsen equipment rolled off the new Augusta assembly line last month, with full relocation from Charlotte expected by the end of the year.
Jacobsen manufactures professional turf care equipment for golf courses, professional landscapers, and municipal and commercial turf care providers.
The expansion and Jacobsen relocation were originally announced in 2016.
Textron expects to create between 500 and 600 new jobs over the next five years in Augusta.
New positions will include assemblers, specialized skilled labor (welders, machinists), product engineers, and finance, HR, and administrative support staff.

Approximately one month before the interview, Textron partnered with the Georgia Department of Labor and the Georgia Department of Economic Development for a job fair that drew over 1,000 applicants for approximately 70 positions.
Textron primarily seeks employees with a high school degree, a strong work ethic, and a willingness to contribute ideas to improve operations.
Skilled roles like welders and engineers require additional training and experience, with Textron recruiting actively from regional universities and colleges.
Based on industry trends, Textron expects to add between 150 and 250 additional jobs within the next 18 to 24 months.
During The Masters Tournament week, Textron hosts plant tours and operates a hospitality house to entertain customers and partners.
source_title: The Masters Tournament Helps Highlight Augusta-Based Textron Specialized Vehicles | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Textron Inc. is an American industrial conglomerate based in Providence, Rhode Island.
Textron's subsidiaries include Bell Textron, Kautex, Textron Aviation (which itself includes the Beechcraft and Cessna brands), and Lycoming Engines.
In 1953, Textron purchased Burkart Manufacturing Company, which produced upholstery filling for the automotive industry.

E-Z-Go is a designer and manufacturer of light transportation vehicles for golf courses, planned communities, campuses, and other uses, including electric and internal combustion golf carts, low speed vehicles, and multipurpose utility vehicles under the E-Z-Go, Cushman, and Bad Boy Buggy brands.
Kautex Textron is a supplier to the automotive industry that produces plastic fuel systems, selective catalytic reduction systems, windshield and headlamp washer systems, and other products.
In August 2019, Textron Inc. was looking to spin off or sell the Kautex Textron division to focus on higher-margin parts of its business.
In March 2022, Textron agreed to acquire Pipistrel, an electrically powered aircraft manufacturer based in Italy and Slovenia.
Textron formed a new division called Textron eAviation for electric aircraft development following the Pipistrel acquisition.
The purchase of Pipistrel was completed in April 2022 for a price of US$235 million.
Textron will invest in the Textron eAviation division to hasten future aircraft development and production.
Pipistrel's founder and CEO, Ivo Boscarol, will stay on as a minority shareholder and Chairman Emeritus.
Pipistrel will continue as a brand and retain its existing headquarters and operations in Slovenia and Italy.
Arctic Cat is a manufacturer of snowmobiles, all-terrain vehicles, and side-by-sides, acquired by Textron on March 6, 2017 for US$247 million.
Textron sold the Arctic Cat division in April 2025 to former executive Brad Darling.
Textron announced it was halting manufacturing at its Thief River Falls and Saint Cloud, MN locations a few months before selling Arctic Cat.
Bell Textron is an American helicopter and rotorcraft manufacturer headquartered in Fort Worth, Texas.
On December 26, 2013, Textron agreed to purchase Beechcraft, including the discontinued Hawker jet line, for $1.4 billion, with the sale closing in March 2014.
The company formed a new company called Textron Aviation to market the products of Beechcraft, Cessna, and Hawker as individual brands.
From 2013 to 2016, R&D investments were 4.3%, 4.0%, 4.6%, and 4.2% of its revenues ($13.78 billion in 2016) and totaled more than $2.2 billion as it developed seven aircraft.

Textron developed the Bell 525 Relentless, Bell V-280 Valor tiltrotor, Cessna Citation Longitude, Cessna Citation Hemisphere, Beechcraft Denali single-engine turboprop, the Cessna SkyCourier twin cargo hauler, and the Textron Scorpion close support jet.
In October 2025, it was announced that Lisa Atherton will be President and CEO, effective January 4, 2026.
Textron employed over 45,000 people in 25 countries as of 2026.
The company ranked 265th on the 2021 Fortune 500 of the largest United States corporations by revenue.
In 1984, Textron took on more debt and bought Avco for $1.4 billion, which included the parent of Paul Revere Insurance Company.
Diminished demand for helicopters and airplanes led to layoffs at Cessna and Bell Textron following the economic downturn in March 2003.
Shares in Textron plummeted to as low as $10.09 per share in the aftermath of the 2008 economic downturn, driving its market capitalization down to just $3.17 billion.
On April 5, 2022, U.S. authorities announced criminal as well as civil charges against the former head of Xcalibur Aerospace Ltd. for making a fraudulent bid to buy Textron for $13.8 billion.
The Department of Justice and the SEC claimed that Xcalibur Aerospace was never in a position to complete a tender offer for Textron and lacked the finances to do so.
Able Aerospace Services is an international business and $70 million aerospace enterprise that is performing more than 2,800 proprietary FAA-approved repairs on aircraft and components.
ATAC is a government contractor based in Newport News, Virginia.
Greenlee Textron is an industrial and electrical tool company headquartered in Rockford, Illinois, acquired by Textron in 1986 that produces various wire and cable installation tools.
On April 18, 2018, Textron announced that it would sell its Greenlee brand to Emerson Electric.
Jacobsen Manufacturing was purchased by Textron in June 1978 and continued to produce garden tractors into the 1990s.
Today, Jacobsen sells various products used for turf care: maintenance equipment, vehicles, and other products.
Textron AirLand, LLC is a joint venture between Textron Inc. and AirLand Enterprises, LLC that is currently developing the Textron AirLand Scorpion aircraft as a private venture.
Beechcraft Corporation is an American manufacturer of general aviation and military aircraft, ranging from light, single-engined aircraft to twin-engined turboprop transports and military trainers.
The Cessna Aircraft Company is an American general aviation aircraft manufacturing corporation headquartered in Wichita, Kansas, merged into Textron Aviation in March 2014.

Textron Systems is an aerospace and defense development and manufacturing firm headquartered in Providence, Rhode Island, that reported 2012 annual sales in the Textron Systems segment as $1.7 billion.

Its operating units include Support Solutions, Electronic Solutions, Unmanned Systems (Hunt Valley, Maryland), Advanced Information Solutions (Austin, Texas), Geospatial Solutions (Sterling, Virginia), Lycoming Engines (Williamsport, Pennsylvania), Marine & Land Systems (Slidell, Louisiana), TRU Simulation & Training (Tampa, Florida), and Weapon & Sensor Systems (Wilmington, Massachusetts).
Weapon & Sensor Systems is a maker of weapons such as anti-vehicle mines and surveillance systems.
source_title: Textron - Wikipedia

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. company, announced the transition of its Bad Boy Off Road brand to Textron Off Road.
The announcement originated from Augusta, GA.
Side-by-side models in the current line remain the same; vehicles with the new Textron Off Road branding will begin to appear in authorized dealerships by April 2017.
The Textron Off Road brand features gasoline-powered Stampede and Stampede XTR side-by-sides.
The Textron Off Road brand features all-electric Recoil, Recoil iS, and Recoil iS Crew models.
The Textron Off Road brand features the hybrid Ambush iS model.

Textron Off Road pioneered the category of electrically powered 4x4 vehicles used for hunting and outdoor recreation due to their silent, odorless operation and environmentally friendly, emissions-free drivetrains.
The brand name change signifies the strong engineering and manufacturing legacy Textron has in industries such as aerospace, defense, and automotive.

John Collins, vice president of consumer for Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated that the new name reflects detailed design, precision engineering, manufacturing expertise, and high performance, demonstrating how serious Textron is about its future in the powersports market.
Changes to the company's website and social media platforms were complete and online as of the publication date.

Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. is a leading global manufacturer of golf cars, utility and personal transportation vehicles, snowmobiles, side-by-sides, all-terrain vehicles, professional turf-care equipment, and ground support equipment.
Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial, and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services.
source_title: New Textron Off Road Brand Reflects Heritage of High Performance

facts: The company operates in the Motor Vehicle Manufacturing industry.
The company is based in Augusta, Georgia, United States.
The company has 1001-5000 Employees.

Textron Specialized Vehicles designs and manufactures a broad range of vehicles and related equipment, including golf cars, recreational side-by-sides, industrial utility vehicles, and aviation ground support equipment.
The company emphasizes advances in technology to improve vehicle efficiency and the user experience.
It traces its origins to 1954 in Augusta, Georgia, where two brothers started in a small machine shop with the aim of building a better golf car.
Since 1960, TSV has operated as part of Textron Inc., a Providence, Rhode Island–based multinational with a broad footprint and thousands of employees worldwide.
It encompasses brands such as Cushman, E-Z-GO, Arctic Cat, Jacobsen, Ransomes, TUG, Douglas, Premier, and Safeaero.
The company serves customers across industrial, recreational, and aviation sectors with its vehicle and equipment offerings.
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Company Overview, Contact Details & Competitors | LeadIQ

facts: Textron announced its intent to separate the Industrial segment within 12 to 18 months through a potential sale or tax-free spin-off into a stand-alone company.
The Industrial segment is composed of Kautex and Textron Specialized Vehicles.
Kautex operates as a Tier 1 auto supplier with a primary product line of fuel systems for the automotive industry.
Kautex has built a meaningful position in hybrid fuel tanks which is a growing part of the industry.
Pentatonic is a battery and closure business within the Industrial segment that supports EV and hybrid platforms.
Pentatonic's platform support includes the Rivian R1 and a major European OEM start of production plan for 2027.
Allegro is an Industrial segment growth platform focused on solutions to clean autonomous vehicle cameras and sensors.

Textron Specialized Vehicles manufactures golf cars, off-road and turf vehicles, personal transportation vehicles, ransoms Jacobs and turf equipment, Cushman vehicles and tug ground support equipment.
Kautex secured its largest award to date for its hybrid plastic fuel tank offering.
Industrial segment revenues were $786 million, down $6 million on a reported basis but up 4% organically after accounting for the powersports divestiture.
Kautex revenues were $486 million, up $36 million or 8% from a year ago, primarily due to a favorable impact of $20 million from foreign exchange rate fluctuations and higher volume and mix.
Textron Specialized Vehicles revenue was $300 million, down $42 million, largely reflecting a $55 million impact from the divestiture of the powersports business in 2025.
Management plans to reallocate a portion of R&D investment into supply chains and factories to make them more effective and improve production output.
Textron expects enhanced supplier performance as part of operational efficiency improvements.
The Industrial segment had positive organic growth in the quarter.
Management stated that both Kautex and TSV are very well run and their end markets are in a stronger place.
source_title: Textron (TXT) Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript - AOL

facts: E-Z-GO® introduces the Liberty LSV and updates the Freedom RXV for use on golf courses and neighborhoods.
The new street-legal Liberty LSV has a top speed of 25 mph.
The Liberty LSV meets National Highway Traffic Safety Administration standards for an LSV.
The Liberty LSV can be operated on public roads with posted speed limits of 35 mph or less in most states.
The Liberty LSV features four forward-facing seats and a compact wheelbase.
The Liberty LSV is outfitted with a backup camera, operator protective structure, pedestrian warning system, seatbelts, and an optional infotainment system.
The Liberty LSV adheres to FMVSS 500 and SAE J2358 standards.
The 2024 Freedom RXV model features a new front end with integrated LED headlights.
The 2024 Freedom RXV includes a modernized ergonomic dash with space for personal items.
The Freedom RXV is available in two- and four-passenger models.
The four-passenger Freedom RXV features a new modular canopy equipped with a water management system.
Adam Harris, vice president of E-Z-GO for Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., stated that the new models address customer demand for vehicles suited for both golf courses and neighborhoods.
The Liberty LSV is exclusively powered by Samsung SDI lithium batteries.
Samsung SDI battery technology has been proven over many years in E-Z-GO’s industry-leading ELiTE series of lithium-powered vehicles.
Samsung SDI battery technology is more energy-efficient and better for the environment than traditional lead-acid batteries.
Samsung SDI batteries charge up to twice as fast as traditional batteries and require no maintenance.
Samsung SDI batteries are backed by an 8-year warranty, which is the industry’s longest.
The Freedom RXV is available with either Samsung SDI lithium battery technology (ELiTE model) or E-Z-GO’s exclusive EX1 gas powertrain.
The EX1 engine is purpose-built for golf-car applications to optimize fuel efficiency.
The EX1 engine features smart engine management that monitors the electrical system and turns off accessories when not in use.
Both the Liberty LSV and Freedom RXV are manufactured in Augusta, Ga., where E-Z-GO has operated throughout its 69-year history.
E-Z-GO was founded in Augusta, Ga. in 1954.
E-Z-GO became part of Textron Inc. in 1960 and operates as part of Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. subsidiary.
New models will be available for order starting June 6, 2023, through local dealers.
source_title: E-Z-GO® LAUNCHES NEW VEHICLES - FOR THE GOLF COURSE AND THE STREET

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles is located in Augusta, Georgia.
The recall involves 2024 Arctic Cat Mountain 600 Snowmobiles sold at Arctic Cat dealers nationwide from September 2023 through June 2024 for between $14,500 and $16,100.
About 600 units are affected by the recall.

The hazard is that the pin on the u-joint of the steering knuckle was not swagged correctly, which can allow the pin to come out of the u-joint and cause the rider to lose steering control, posing a crash hazard.
The firm has received six reports of cross pins that have become loose or have fallen out, with no injuries reported.

Consumers should immediately stop using the 2024 Arctic Cat Mountain 600 Snowmobile and contact their local authorized Arctic Cat dealer for a free inspection of the steering column u-joint and free repair if needed.
If the pin is properly swagged, there is no repair needed; if the steering post joint pin was not properly swagged, the entire steering post assembly will be replaced.
The model name is depicted as an “M” in the shape of a mountain and is printed on the side panels of the snowmobiles.
The 10th digit of the VIN is “R”.
The VIN number is stamped on the tunnel section or the decal on the top/rear of the tunnel.
The recall date is July 25, 2024, under recall number 24-317.
The product was manufactured in the United States.
Federal law prohibits any person from selling products subject to a Commission ordered recall or a voluntary recall undertaken in consultation with the CPSC.
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Recalls 2024 Arctic Cat Mountain 600 Snowmobiles Due to Crash Hazard - Motor Sports NewsWire

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. and Jacobsen will combine their operations by the close of 2017 in order to optimize efficiencies and better serve the companies’ shared customers and distributors.
Jacobsen is a leading manufacturer of professional and “prosumer” turf-care equipment under the Jacobsen®, Dixie Chopper® and Ransomes® brands.

Textron Specialized Vehicles designs and manufactures several vehicle and equipment lines for commercial and consumer use, including E-Z-GO® golf cars and personal transportation vehicles, Cushman® commercial vehicles, Bad Boy® Off Road side-by-sides and ATVs, and TUG™, Douglas™, Premier™ and Safeaero™ ground support equipment to serve the aviation industry.

The combination of the two companies will allow them to better serve their shared customers in golf and other industries, and operate more efficiently through a common supply chain and efficiencies of scale in manufacturing and back-office operations.
Kevin Holleran is president and CEO of Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc.

Jacobsen’s operations in Charlotte, NC will relocate to TSV facilities in Augusta, Ga., with the first phase of relocation expected to be completed by the end of March, and all operations planned to be relocated by the close of 2017.
Jacobsen’s field sales and service teams will remain in place, and its operations in Coatesville, Ind. and Ipswich, U.K. will continue.
Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. is a leading global manufacturer of golf cars, utility and personal transportation vehicles, professional turf-care equipment, and ground support equipment.
Textron Specialized Vehicles markets products under the E-Z-GO®, Cushman®, Bad Boy® Off Road, Jacobsen®, Dixie Chopper®, Ransomes®, TUG™, Douglas™, Premier™ and Safeaero™ brands.
Its vehicles are found in environments ranging from golf courses to factories, airports to planned communities, and theme parks to hunting preserves.

Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services.

facts: AJIN Georgia presented $40,000 in total support to 12 local non-profit, education, and public safety agencies at a luncheon.
The donations were presented by Jamie Calloway, AJIN General Manager of Human Resources.
Calloway stated the gifts reflect the generosity of Jung-Ho Sea, AJIN CEO and President, and his commitment to being a good corporate citizen.
AJIN had not yet manufactured any parts at the time of the donation but was already giving back to the community.
Earlier in the year, Jung-Ho Sea donated high-tech, Korean-made refrigerators to local agencies and Korean War Veterans.
Calloway noted that Sea has a special connection to Korean War Veterans due to their role in enabling him to start his companies.

Senior leadership attending the luncheon included Taechul Kim (COO), Jaeik Jang (CFO), Daewoong Kim (Sr. mgr/Finance), Seunghwan Kim (GM/Project), Junhyeon Sim (Sr. Specialist/Project), Chunwoo Shin (Sr. Mgr./Ajin USA HR), and Jaewook Jeong (Sr. Mgr./HR).
AJIN Georgia was the first Hyundai Tier 1 supplier to announce plans to build a plant in Bulloch County in November 2022.
The facility is located on I-16 near Hwy. 301 South in the Bruce Yawn Industrial Park.
The plant represents a $278 million investment and spans 853,000 square feet.
AJIN Georgia will manufacture a variety of car parts at the facility.
Production is planned to begin by October 2024.
Initial employment will be just under 300 employees, with a target of 600 employees as production demand increases.

Local dignitaries in attendance included Bulloch County Commissioner Chair Roy Thompson, Commissioners Jappy Stringer and Ray Mosley, Bulloch County Commission Chair and Bulloch County Development Authority Vice Chair Curt Deal, and Bulloch County Development Authority Chair Billy Allen.

The 12 recipient organizations were the Bulloch County Boys & Girls Club, Bulloch County Parks and Recreation Department, Statesboro Kiwanis Club, Statesboro Safe Haven, Statesboro Village Builders, Bulloch County Emergency Medical Services, Bulloch County Fire Department, Bulloch County Sheriff’s Office, Bulloch County Schools, Evans County Schools, Metter College and Career Academy, and Ogeechee Technical College.

facts: Ajin Industrial is a South Korean auto parts manufacturer that plans to develop a $317 million plant in eastern Georgia.
The facility will supply the nearby Hyundai electric vehicle factory (Hyundai Motor Group Metaplat America).
The company produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers.
The project is expected to create 317 jobs.
Ajin USA will produce electronics and body panels for Hyundai.
It operates as a tier 1 supplier of fabricated metal components for EVs.
On July 18, 2024, Ajin Georgia in Bulloch County’s Bruce Yawn Commerce Park celebrated the grand opening of their new automotive metal structural parts facility.
Joon Georgia Inc., d/b/a Ajin Georgia, is a subsidiary of Korea’s Ajin Industrial.
Local and state leaders celebrated the grand opening of Ajin Georgia, the first tier 1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplat America (HMGMA).
The company announced it would invest $317 million and hire 630 people in nearby Bulloch County, where Statesboro is the seat.
Kemp announced that Joon Georgia Inc., d/b/a Ajin Georgia will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
An automotive parts company moved into the facility in October.
Ajin Georgia gave $40,000 to the local community.
The massive facility spans 850,000 square feet.
Joon Georgia will build a parts factory at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park near Statesboro, about 30 miles west of the future Hyundai EV plant.
Ajin Georgia was the first Hyundai Tier 1 supplier to announce they were building a plant in Bulloch County in November 2022.
The facility is located at 301 South in the Bruce Yawn Industrial Park.
The site was a pad of dirt less than 14 months ago before opening.
It is now a regional employer that’s producing vehicle parts for the largest economic.
Ajin Industrial CEO Jung Ho Sea offered a traditional Korean greeting before welcoming guests at the grand opening celebration of their new manufacturing plant at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.

facts: Effingham and Tattnall counties are among two of the southeast Georgia school districts set to receive state funds aimed at meeting local industry needs.

The Georgia Department of Education (GaDOE) and Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) recently announced the award of Why Georgia Works Alignment Grants to help 15 local school districts improve the alignment of their Career, Technical, and Agricultural Education programs with local industry needs.

Through the one-time-only program, GaDOE and GDEcD will award a total of $203,564 in grants to the Clarke, Clayton, Crisp, Effingham, Fulton, Harris, Jackson, Murray, Muscogee, Oconee, Peach, Savannah-Chatham, Tattnall, and White County school districts along with the Marietta City school district.
The grants were awarded through a competitive allocation process.
Governor Kemp said the awards reflect the prioritization of “preparing the next generation of hardworking Georgians.”
Each awarded school district will conduct an in-depth review of local industry needs and work to address gaps between CTAE pathway enrollment and workforce needs.

Working with the University of Georgia’s Carl Vinson Institute of Government, the districts will develop and begin implementing an action plan to better connect their CTAE offerings with in-demand, high-wage, and growing industries in Georgia.
State School Superintendent Richard Woods said it is their goal that every student in Georgia will graduate ready for their next step after high school.
Woods stated the Why Georgia Works Alignment Grant will help graduates pursue fulfilling, high-paying careers without having to leave home.
With GDEcD’s support, GaDOE’s CTAE office will provide training and implementation support to schools throughout the grant period.
In the fall of 2023, GaDOE and the Carl Vinson Institute of Government released an updated Georgia Alignment Toolkit to guide schools and districts in connecting education and industry.
The project was sponsored by the U.S. Department of Commerce – Economic Development Administration through Award ED22ATL3070005 as a single-year grant opportunity.
source_title: Effingham, Tattnall Among SE Georgia Counties to Receive 'Why Georgia Works Alignment Grants' • The Georgia Virtue

facts: ALBAform Inc. held a ribbon cutting ceremony attended by the Czech Republic’s foreign minister and ambassador in Flowery Branch, Hall County in September 2013.
The company develops and manufactures custom metal components for the automotive industry.
ALBAform represented the first investment by a Czech firm in Georgia.
At its Hall County outset, the company started with one Czech engineer and one CNC bending machine to bend wires for car seats and other components.
The company initially opened in Dallas due to personal relations between founder's daughter Monika Vintrlikova and a fellow foreign exchange student from Dallas.
The company relocated to Flowery Branch after taking over a facility there because Dallas was too distant from potential clients in Alabama or Georgia.
When moving to Flowery Branch, the company left behind three contract workers in Dallas.
Upon arriving in Flowery Branch, president Jan Vintrlik hired four employees immediately, all of whom had metal working backgrounds.
President Jan Vintrlik considers the move to Hall County positive due to its international environment.
Vintrlik noted that the high concentration of foreign-owned companies in the area makes it easier for non-locals to settle and focus on production.
The firm’s client base has grown to include Daimler, BMW, VW, and Tesla.
In Europe, the company's main customers include high-volume carmakers like VW, Skoda, BMW, Mercedes, Hyundai, and Kia, as well as low-volume brands like Rolls-Royce and Bentley.
The number of employees in Flowery Branch has grown to 55 and the company is looking to employ 10 more.

The company needs to expand its 30,000 square feet facility by 20,000 square feet to 50,000 square feet to meet a 20 percent anticipated growth in production of metal components for seats and other auto parts.
The extra space will be used to house more machines and stock products.
President Jan Vintrlik stated that if he has to move, he won't leave Flowery Branch because the company wants to ensure the same location for its employees.
The firm is hiring for the quality department, technicians who can program CNC machines, maintenance people, and operators for new machinery such as CNC bending machines and welding robots.
The company provides special training for employees because the machinery is specialized, though a metal-working background is an advantage.
The company promotes itself at job fairs in surrounding counties and encourages employees to recommend friends.
ALBAform is in touch with Lanier Technical College and posts job placements on the Internet.

President Jan Vintrlik noted that U.S. employees are happy for company success but leave if they find work closer to home or motivated by better wages, requiring more communication about company plans.

Vintrlik contrasted U.S. workers with Czech counterparts, stating Czechs are stable because they do not move for work, are harder to motivate, get suspicious if talked to too much, and usually do not speak up directly but talk among each other.

Products made both in the Czech Republic and in Georgia are exactly the same, utilizing copy-and-paste technology, quality standards, and research/development to supply European seat makers in the U.S. and Europe.
The company continues to conduct research often at the request of customers who try to find savings within production, consolidate products, or find better surface treatments.
ALBAform asks suppliers for more reliable machines with faster production times to allow more complexity for its products.
The company owns its own tool shops and advanced quality checking tools.

The company asks employees to think globally and speak different languages to gain a competitive advantage, with staff attending development meetings in Germany, assembling welding tools in the U.S., and reporting economics to headquarters in the Czech Republic.
ALBAform tries to add value to its products by asking suppliers for faster production times allowing more complexity.
Monika Vintrlikova's father Jan Maderic visits the company annually and participates in telecommunication conferences.
source_title: Czech Firm Positive About Move to Hall County, Looking for More Space and Workers - Global Atlanta

facts: Anovion Technologies will invest over $800 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
The project will create over 400 jobs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Anovion Technologies will build a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
Anovion is a supplier of premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
The project will create more than 400 jobs and over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that job creators are recognizing every corner of the state has the resources and workforce needed for success.
Anovion is a climate tech-driven advanced materials company.
Anovion is one of the first qualified U.S. suppliers of synthetic graphite anode materials for use in e-mobility applications.
Graphite is the largest battery material used in electric vehicles by mass, more than copper, nickel, manganese, cobalt, and lithium.
Eric Stopka, Chief Executive Officer of Anovion, stated the company is opening its first large-scale expansion manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Eric Stopka noted that Georgia’s strong technical workforce and top-tier schools will help advance research partnerships and contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline.
Eric Stopka highlighted the region’s growing availability of clean energy, which will result in increased sustainability for Anovion’s manufacturing process.
Anovion’s new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet and feature proprietary furnace technologies.
The facility is located at Downrange Industrial Park in Bainbridge.
The facility will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
The facility will produce synthetic graphite for use in batteries that power EVs, electric storage systems, electronics, military applications, and other products.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in late 2025.
The company will be hiring for a variety of technical, production, and engineering roles.
The company will also be hiring for administrative and management positions.
Bainbridge Mayor Edward Reynolds welcomed Anovion to the city.
Mayor Edward Reynolds described the $800 million investment and creation of over 400 high-paying, quality jobs as a huge win for the community.
Pete Stephens, Chairman of the Decatur County Board of Commissioners, stated that Decatur County leadership has focused on attracting quality companies and jobs.
Pete Stephens noted that diversifying the industry mix is necessary to remain competitive and grow.
Pete Stephens described the announcement as a step toward diversification, with new green industry jobs representing a growing segment of the economy.
Project Manager Taylor Walden represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the City of Bainbridge, Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County, University System of Georgia, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that much of the critical supply chain for e-mobility and manufacturing is currently sourced outside of the U.S.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson described Anovion as an innovative industry leader filling that need and delivering a critical component to Georgia.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $21.9 billion in investment and 28,000 jobs in Georgia.
More than 10 of these projects are battery manufacturers, recyclers, and suppliers.
The battery-related projects represent approximately $8.5 billion in investment and 6,000 jobs.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois.
Anovion Technologies is a U.S.-owned and U.S.-based advanced materials business.
Anovion is currently America’s first commercial-scale manufacturer of premium, cleaner anode-grade synthetic graphite.
Anovion's existing manufacturing footprint is located near Niagara Falls, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
Anovion began commercial production in early 2021.
Anovion is among a limited number of graphite anode producers in America to have successfully gained qualification for EV applications.
source_title: Anovion invests in GA facility creating over 400 jobs
