facts: Boogook Georgia, LLC is a Korean automotive supplier specializing in fluid-carrying systems for vehicles.
The company will launch its first location in the United States in Macon, Ga.
Boogook Georgia will redevelop an existing building within the Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority (MBCIA) Industrial Park located at 2600 Weaver Road.
The company plans $300,000 in leasehold improvements at the former B&W property.
Boogook Georgia will make a total capital investment of $6.8 million to support its operation.
Joong Suk Oh serves as the Chief Executive Officer of Boogook Georgia, LLC.
Company leadership recognized the unique opportunity to support Korean automotive manufacturing partners more locally.
Expanding operations to Macon will serve as a driving force for serving the Southeast U.S. market.
The decision to locate in Macon was driven, in part, by proximity to Hyundai Motor’s $5.5 billion Metaplant America in Savannah.
The site is also located near Kia’s West Point manufacturing facility.
The location at 2600 Weaver Road offers quick highway access via I-16 and is conveniently located less than two miles from the company’s new site.
Strategic positioning ensures seamless connectivity to key transportation routes for effective customer service and parts access.
Macon-Bibb County Mayor Lester M. Miller stated that easy access to interstates and highways makes Georgia a prime location for industries looking to move products quickly and efficiently.
Mayor Miller thanked the Industrial Authority for ongoing efforts and welcomed Boogook Georgia as the newest member of Team Macon-Bibb.
Boogook Georgia will create 80 full-time jobs when it opens in 2024.
Additional jobs are expected on the horizon.
Employment opportunities will provide local professional roles across management, manufacturing, assembly, quality and assurance, and delivery functions.
Stephen Adams serves as the Executive Director of the MBCIA.
Adams stated that Boogook's decision reinforces the importance and value of growing automotive industry partners in the region.
Middle Georgia is described as an extremely attractive and competitive area for these companies.
Boogook Georgia, LLC is a subsidiary of Boogook Industries CO., LTD, headquartered in Ulsan, South Korea.
The company is a global leader in the automotive tube industry, specializing in automotive parts and materials that carry fluids within vehicle systems.
The parent company supplies products and services to major global automobile manufacturers, including Hyundai Motor Corp., Kia Motor Corp., and others.
Boogook Industries has supplied products and services as a TIER-1 supplier since its foundation in 1987.
Major products include brake, fuel, steering, intake and exhaust lines.
Product offerings include brake tubes, fuel tubes, power steering tubes, vacuum tubes, and battery coolant lines for electric vehicles.
Boogook Industries holds IATF 16949 : 2016 / ISO 45001 : 2018 / ISO 22301 : 2013 / ISO 22301 : 2012 certifications.
Global production facilities include 2 plants in Korea, 2 plants in China, 1 plant in India, 1 plant in Vietnam, and 1 plant in Georgia, United States.
The company achieved a global annual sales revenue of 106 million USD in 2018.
All Boogook Industries employees are dedicated to technology development and quality management to expand business.
The Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority works with local, state, and national stakeholders to attract new business investment to Macon-Bibb County.
The Authority creates a business-friendly climate that results in an increased tax base for the county.
Catalyzing job growth and strengthening corporate infrastructure at various industrial parks are vital elements of the Authority's economic development record.
source_title: Korean Automotive Supplier Boogook Industries to Redevelop Existing Building in Macon, Georgia - MBCIA

facts: Korean automotive supplier Boogook Industries plans to open at the former Brown & Williamson Tobacco facility in 2024, creating 80 full time jobs.
The Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority (MBCIA) reports that the county has seen almost $2 billion in private investment since 2017.
The 120-acre Airport North Industrial Park was certified as Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD), allowing businesses to hit the ground running and save time and money.
Industrial Realty Group purchased the 2 million-square-foot former Brown & Williamson Tobacco property in 2021 and created a multitenant option for MBCIA to market.
The former Brown & Williamson Tobacco facility was once Macon’s largest employer before its redevelopment.
Greater Macon Chamber of Commerce launched the Greater Career Works digital platform to assist employers with hiring and help job seekers explore careers, registering 13,000 users over the past year.
The chamber developed Future Workforce Fridays, a program livestreamed every other Friday into middle and high school classrooms to make kids aware of job possibilities.
Bibb County schools executive director Cassandra Washington used a $15,000 grant to launch the CD2 program, training counselors to connect students with local industrial parks and career pathways.
The chamber dove into workforce development in 2022 to address the need for a skilled labor pool to sustain incoming industrial opportunities.
Bibb County schools hopes through various workforce programs to touch at least 2,500 students a year and increase the number of local people going into community industries.

facts: DTNA announced two significant investments at its headquarters complex on Swan Island in Portland, Oregon: a new Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE) training center adjacent to Electric Island and a new engineering facility.

The EVSE training center represents the next phase to accelerate testing and development of charging infrastructure for commercial vehicles since the first-of-its-kind public charging station was built in partnership with Portland General Electric and opened in 2021.
The new training center will be used for the DTNA dealer network and as a resource center for community stakeholders interested in the clean transportation industry.

The anticipated project for the training center will cost $3 million, with half of the costs being funded through a forgivable loan from Prosper Portland, the City of Portland’s economic development agency.

The new DTNA engineering facility will be approximately 110,000 sq-ft and will consolidate testing activities under one roof with a focus on research and development for emerging propulsion technologies, such as battery electric and hydrogen-fueled vehicles.
The engineering facility project is anticipated to cost $40 million and will be partially funded by the State of Oregon with a $700,000 forgivable loan from the State’s Strategic Reserve fund.

John O’Leary, president and CEO of DTNA, stated that DTNA is proud to call Portland, Oregon home and appreciates the City and State’s support of expansion plans to promote the development and proper testing of advanced technology.
John O’Leary added that DTNA looks forward to collaborating for many more years on training and employing a future-ready workforce.
Business Oregon Director Sophorn Cheang stated that Oregon is the location of choice for businesses leading clean technology innovation in the U.S.

Sophorn Cheang noted that the partnership with local and regional economic development leaders to support DTNA’s investment adds to the region’s competitiveness and will provide opportunities for skilled workforce in manufacturing and engineering for years to come.
Portland City Commissioner Carmen Rubio expressed thrill at DTNA’s decision to invest significant resources and continue to lead heavy-duty vehicle technology in Portland.
Carmen Rubio acknowledged and thanked Governor Kotek for her commitment to the effort and building the clean economy.
Carmen Rubio recognized the Prosper Portland Board, Kimberly Branam, and the outstanding team at Prosper Portland that has worked closely with DTNA for many years.
Carmen Rubio noted that Portland is where Freightliner was founded more than 80 years ago, DTNA is an anchor employer in the city, and today’s announcement is a proud moment for all involved.
Carmen Rubio stated that the future of heavy-duty vehicle technology in Portland is very bright and very green.
DTNA’s history in Portland dates back to the founding of the Freightliner brand in the city in 1942.
When Freightliner was acquired by the former Daimler AG in 1981, the manufacturer established its North American headquarters for medium- and heavy-duty trucks in Portland.
Since then, DTNA has based all research and development activities for North America in the region.
The Portland Truck Manufacturing Plant is located on Swan Island and has been in continuous operation since 1969.
The Portland Truck Manufacturing Plant today produces the battery-electric Freightliner eCascadia and eM2 as well as the Western Star X-Series.
DTNA employs over 3,000 people in the Portland region.
Daimler Truck North America LLC is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, and is a leading provider of comprehensive products, services and technologies for the commercial transportation industry.

Daimler Truck North America designs, engineers, manufactures and markets medium- and heavy-duty trucks, school buses, vehicle chassis and their associated technologies and components under the Freightliner, Western Star, Thomas Built Buses, Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp and Detroit brands.
Daimler Truck North America is a subsidiary of Daimler Truck Holding AG (DTG), one of the world’s leading commercial vehicle manufacturers.

facts: Daimler Truck North America (DTNA) delivered 20 battery-electric Freightliner eCascadia Class 8 tractors to Reyes Beverage Group (RBG), a beverage distributor in California.
The delivery marks the first phase of RBG’s plan to transition to a zero-emission fleet in California by 2039.
David Carson serves as Senior Vice President of Sales and Marketing for DTNA.
David Carson stated that the Freightliner eCascadia is built with maximum productivity in mind for fleets switching to efficient, zero emission vehicles.
David Carson noted that DTNA is the leading manufacturer of medium- and heavy-duty trucks in the U.S. and is committed to reducing emissions with partners like RBG.
RBG’s broader sustainability efforts include a recent transition to renewable diesel and an ongoing idle-time reduction programme.
RBG expects these EVs to contribute significantly to their goal of reducing carbon emissions by 60% by 2030.
Tom Reyes serves as President of RBG West.
Tom Reyes commented that RBG is among the first in its industry to adopt these electric vehicles and sees it as a significant step toward sustainability goals and state regulation compliance.
A typical driving range of the eCascadia is 220 miles.
The eCascadia can recharge to 80% capacity in as little as 90 minutes.
The eCascadia offers multiple battery and drive axle configurations with ranges between 155 and 230 miles.
The eCascadia features a Detroit proprietary ePowertrain for power, efficiency, and reliability.
The eCascadia includes standard Detroit Assurance with Active Brake Assist 5 Suite of Safety Systems.
DTNA offers comprehensive fleet electrification solutions, including Detroit eFill commercial chargers and Detroit eConsulting services to assist customers transitioning to electric vehicles.
The delivery represents a significant step in the commercial transportation industry’s move towards more sustainable operations.
source_title: Daimler Truck North America delivers 20 electric Freightliner eCascadias to Reyes Beverage Group | MT Article | Motor Transport

The facility is located in Redford Township, Michigan, near Detroit.
The site operates as a diesel engine manufacturing plant.
The expansion and modernization project will create 436 jobs.
The investment aims to upgrade the Detroit Diesel plant to boost research and job creation.

facts: Nearly 600 manufacturing Daimler Truck North America employees are temporarily losing their jobs in the Charlotte region.

Daimler Truck North America is temporarily laying off 573 workers in Gaston County according to a N.C. Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act report filed Friday with the North Carolina Department of Commerce.
The mass layoffs will be effective Friday at the Custom Truck Services facility located at 1803 Main St.
The mass layoffs will also affect the manufacturing plant at 1800 N. Main St.
Daimler Truck North America spokesman Andrew Johnson stated that 546 employees will be temporarily laid off in Mount Holly.
An additional 27 employees will be affected at Daimler’s components and logistics site in Gastonia.
The Gastonia Components and Logistics facility is located at 1400 Tulip Drive.
Andrew Johnson cited a notable slowdown in new truck orders, particularly in the medium-duty, on-highway, and electric vehicle segments.
Daimler Truck North Carolina Human Resources Manager Steve Griffin stated the job cuts are due to a sustained reduction in orders and diminished build rate.
The temporary layoffs are expected to last for an unknown duration.
Affected employees will receive payment at their regular pay rate for the period between Friday and Sept. 9.
The majority of affected jobs are truck assemblers, material handlers, office supervisors, technicians, and painters.
There are about 1,400 employees at Daimler’s Mount Holly truck manufacturing plant.
The company has approximately 6,500 employees across North Carolina.
Daimler Truck North America implemented workforce reductions across several facilities to align with current market conditions.
In total, about 2,000 employees at five sites will be impacted by the temporary layoffs.
Other impacted locations include Detroit, Portland, Oregon, and Saltillo, Mexico.

Daimler Truck North America designs, engineers, manufactures, and markets medium- and heavy-duty trucks, school buses, vehicle chassis, and their associated technologies and components under brands including Freightliner, Western Star, and Thomas Built Buses.
The Mount Holly plant produces medium-duty Freightliner trucks.
The Gastonia Components and Logistics facility provides stamping, metal fabrication, and sub-assembly of cab and chassis parts.
Daimler has other plants in the Charlotte region, including Daimler Truck Remarketing Corp. at 2477 Deerfield Road in Fort Mill, S.C.
Another regional facility is the Cleveland Truck Manufacturing Plant at 11550 Statesville Blvd. in Cleveland, N.C.
In 2016, the company cut more than 900 jobs due to a diminished build rate.
Around that same time, Daimler temporarily laid off about 700 employees at its Mount Holly facility and about 550 at its Cleveland plant.
Daimler Truck North America is a subsidiary of Germany-based Daimler Truck Holding AG.
The parent company operates 40 production sites globally with more than 100,000 employees.
source_title: Layoffs hit Daimler Truck workers in Gaston County NC | Charlotte Observer

canonical_name: US Electric Vehicle & Autonomous Truck Market
facts: Daimler Truck North America has debuted a demonstrator electric semi-truck with autonomous driving technology.
The electric autonomous Freightliner eCascadia technology demonstrator is based on a production electric Freightliner eCascadia.
It is equipped with Torc Robotics’ autonomous driving software and the latest Level 4 sensor and compute technology that will eventually enable Level 4 autonomous driving.
Level 4 is considered fully autonomous, meaning the truck can handle most driving situations independently, but a driver still needs to be behind the wheel.
Torc is a self-driving truck specialist based in Blacksburg, Virginia, and it’s Daimler Truck’s independent subsidiary for autonomous driving technology.
Daimler’s goal is to develop this research and engineering project into a modular, scalable platform for different trucking applications to offer bespoke solutions for customers.
The electric Class 8 Freightliner eCascadia went into production in 2022 and is now being driven in more than 55 fleets in the US.
It comes equipped with the proprietary Detroit ePowertrain.
Several battery and drive axle options are available with range options of 155, 220, or 230 miles.
The battery can be charged to 80% in as little as 90 minutes.
The autonomous sensor suite and computer power are packaged to fit the eCascadia’s smaller day cab configuration.
Daimler Truck North America’s engineering team developed an advanced prototype air-cooling concept for the compute stack, which is positioned between the driver and passenger seats.
Customized software provides the autonomous system with control interfaces and feedback on vehicle status.
Cameras, lidar, and radar sensors are above the windscreen, and the in-house designed sensor bar cover protects from damage and soiling.
Four additional 12-volt batteries provide enough power to ensure uninterrupted operation and increased efficiency and safety.

Daimler says the autonomous eCascadia could be used on shorter, repeatable routes, and it’s currently testing the autonomous electric semi-truck demonstrator between freight centers along US highway corridors.
Daimler Truck has been developing and testing autonomous truck technology since 2015, and it aims to enter the market with production SAE Level 4 autonomous trucks in the US by 2027.
The Mercedes-Benz CLA EV 350 has an EPA-estimated driving range of 312 miles, but in real-world tests drove close to 400 miles on a single charge.
The 2026 Mercedes-Benz CLA EV is available in two variations: a 250+ rear-wheel-drive (RWD) and the more powerful 350 4MATIC all-wheel-drive (AWD) model.
Both models are powered by an 85 kWh battery pack.
The EPA-estimated driving ranges for the CLA EV 350 4MATIC and 250+ are 312 miles and 374 miles, respectively.
During Edmunds' recent EV Range Test, the CLA 350 drove 385 miles on a single charge, beating its EPA rating by 73 miles.
The CLA EV 350 used 25.7 kWh per 100 miles during testing, beating its EPA estimate of 29 kWh per 100 miles by almost 13%.
Mercedes states the CLA 350 EV delivers a peak charging rate of 320 kW, but testing reached 349 kW.
In 9 minutes and 11 seconds, the electric sedan added 100 miles of range during testing.
The 2026 Mercedes-Benz CLA 250+ starts at $47,250, while the CLA 350 4MATIC starts at $49,800.
Covenant Logistics completed a two-week evaluation of the Tesla Semi in California, including a loaded run over the Grapevine pass on I-5.
Covenant Logistics VP of Sustainability and Innovation Matt McLelland stated the driver was amazed at the performance of the Tesla Semi and felt a high level of confidence compared to diesel trucks.
The evaluation tested the 500-mile long-range Tesla Semi with a larger customer in California.
The Grapevine test involved running northbound with a loaded trailer over a steep grade and southbound requiring constant braking attention, which was mitigated by regenerative braking on the EV.
Regenerative braking on steep descents allows the Tesla Semi to recover energy instead of burning through brake pads, eliminating brake fade risk on long downhill stretches.
Covenant Logistics Group reported $1.16 billion in revenue for 2025 and operates one of the larger truckload fleets in the country.
ArcBest logged 4,494 miles over three weeks and averaged 1.55 kWh per mile on typical dispatch lanes.
DHL Supply Chain completed a 3,000-mile trial out of Livermore, California, averaging 1.72 kWh per mile at full load, and has since taken delivery of its first Semi.
CEVA Logistics ran a West Coast trial and reported avoiding 4.38 metric tons of CO₂ emissions.
MDB Transportation and AiLO Logistics both launched three-week pilots in late April focused on port drayage operations.
Tesla started volume production of the Semi at its Nevada Gigafactory on April 29.
Tesla has already landed a 60-truck order from port drayage fleets.
source_title: Daimler Truck just debuted an autonomous electric semi-truck - News IO

facts: Daimler Trucks North America delivered the first vehicle in its Freightliner Electric Innovation Fleet, a Freightliner eM2, to Penske Truck Leasing on December 20, 2018.
DTNA president and CEO Roger Nielsen ceremonially handed over the eM2 key to Penske Truck Leasing president and CEO Brian Hard during an event in Carson, California.
The Freightliner eM2 is an electrified solution for local distribution, pickup and delivery, food and beverage delivery, and last-mile logistics applications.
The Freightliner eCascadia is a Class 8 tractor designed for local and regional distribution and drayage.
Both the Freightliner eM2 and eCascadia enter series production in 2021.

The eCascadia and eM2 are part of Daimler Trucks' global electrified truck initiative, joining the Thomas Built Buses all-electric Saf-T-Liner eC2 school bus, the FUSO eCanter, and the Mercedes-Benz eActros.
Earlier in 2018, DTNA formed the Freightliner Electric Vehicle Council composed of 30 customers with strong use-cases for electric trucks, including Penske Truck Leasing.

The customer council co-develops deployment strategies for battery electric vehicles, covering applicable use cases, current legislation, facility requirements, charging infrastructure, and service support.
Starting in December 2018, Penske Truck Leasing will install 20 high-power charging stations across five of its California locations as the first step in infrastructure deployment.
In 2019, Penske will put an additional nine medium-duty electric eM2 trucks and 10 heavy-duty eCascadia electric trucks into targeted service in California and the Pacific Northwest.
The Freightliner Electric Innovation Fleet is partially funded with a nearly $16 million grant from the South Coast Air Quality Management District (SCAMQD).
The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and the ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach also contributed to the grant funding the fleet.
DTNA is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, and manufactures, sells, and services commercial vehicles under the Freightliner, Western Star, Detroit, and Thomas Built Buses nameplates.
Penske Truck Leasing operates more than 300,000 vehicles and serves customers from more than 1,000 locations across North America, South America, Europe, Australia, and Asia.
source_title: Daimler Trucks North America Delivers its First Battery-Electric Commercial Truck to Penske Truck Leasing - Newsroom - Penske

facts: Daimler Truck North America (DTNA) has produced its 850,000th truck at its Cleveland, N.C., manufacturing plant.
The milestone vehicle is a Western Star 47X.
The milestone truck was delivered to Alamo Group.
The truck will be provided to Alamo Group’s snow and ice team, a manufacturer of heavy-duty snow and ice removal equipment.
David Carson is the senior vice-president of sales and marketing at DTNA.
David Carson stated that Alamo Group has been a valued partner for many years and expressed pride in supporting their mission with advanced vocational trucks.
The Western Star 47X is purpose-built for demanding applications like snow and ice management.
The 47X is powered by a Detroit DD13 engine.
The 47X is engineered for durability, safety, and performance in extreme conditions.
Jean Philippe Bourque is the group vice-president of Alamo Group’s Snow and Ice Division.
Jean Philippe Bourque stated that they are honored to receive the milestone truck and continue their strong relationship with DTNA.
Jean Philippe Bourque noted that the Western Star 47X is a critical part of their portfolio and that customers value its reliability, performance, and support.
DTNA’s Cleveland Truck Manufacturing Plant was established in 1989.
The Cleveland plant is DTNA’s largest heavy-duty truck plant in the U.S.
The facility spans 1.2 million square feet on 178 acres.
The facility employs approximately 2,000 people.
The facility currently assembles the Fifth Generation Freightliner Cascadia.
The facility currently assembles Western Star 47X and 49X models.
Jeff Allen is the senior vice-president of operations and specialty vehicles at DTNA.
Jeff Allen stated that reaching 850,000 trucks is a testament to the dedication of the Cleveland team and the strength of manufacturing processes.
source_title: DTNA rolls out 850,000th truck at Cleveland facility - Truck News

facts: DTNA has a labor contract with approximately 7,000 United Auto Workers (UAW) members who build heavy trucks and buses at plants in North Carolina, Georgia, and Tennessee.
The UAW master contract with DTNA is set to expire at midnight on April 26.
DTNA workers are demanding wage increases, improved health insurance coverage, and an end to tiered employment structures.

Thomas Built Buses operates plants in High Point and Archdale, North Carolina, which together constitute the largest school bus manufacturing site in North America with approximately 1,700 workers represented by UAW Local 5287.
A mass rally for DTNA workers was held on April 2 at Local 3520 in Statesville, North Carolina, attended by UAW president Shawn Fain and rank-and-file members from six union locals.

The DTNA labor struggle is occurring amid a rise in electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing in North Carolina, described as an industrial boom encompassing lithium mining to battery production, with one EV auto plant predicted to employ 7,000 workers.
The UAW announced that 10,000 autoworkers signed union cards at 13 worksites across the South.
Workers at the Volkswagen plant in Chattanooga are set to vote on joining the UAW from April 17 to 19.
Workers at the Mercedes-Benz plant outside Tuscaloosa, Alabama, petitioned for a union election expected in early May.

The Georgia General Assembly passed an anti-union bill that denies state incentives to businesses that voluntarily recognize labor unions instead of holding secret ballot elections; it is awaiting Governor Brian Kemp's signature.
Alabama Governor Kay Ivey characterized the UAW's organizing efforts at the Mercedes-Benz plant as a looming threat to the state's historically anti-union, business-friendly economic model.
Tennessee Governor Bill Lee used scaremongering tactics to dissuade Volkswagen workers in Chattanooga from joining the UAW ahead of their election.
South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster vowed to fight unionization efforts in the state all the way to the gates of hell.
DTNA posted record-level revenues of nearly $6 billion in 2023, an increase of 10 percent compared to the previous year.
On March 8, 96 percent of UAW workers across DTNA voted to authorize their leadership to call a strike if contract demands are not met upon expiration.

A worker-led coalition including the Southern Workers Assembly, Truckers Movement for Justice, and Tamexun was formed to support DTNA workers and engage unorganized truck drivers and facility workers.

Percy Payne Jr, a 24-year veteran at Thomas Built Buses in High Point, highlighted that workers in the company's truck plants make $4 more per hour than bus plant workers for the same position due to tiered employment structures.
Adam Foley, who performs quality inspections at the DTNA plant in Cleveland, North Carolina, cited cost-of-living adjustments and stagnant wages despite record corporate revenues as a primary demand.

North Carolina ranks almost dead last in union membership according to the US Department of Labor, following early adoption of right-to-work legislation and laws banning public-sector collective bargaining.
The DTNA contract battle is being closely watched by nonunion workers and bosses across the South as a potential catalyst for labor organizing in the region.
source_title: Daimler Truck Workers Are Strike-Ready in the Anti-Union South - ZNetwork

facts: DENSO Manufacturing Athens Tennessee (DMAT) officials announced the company will expand production of its gasoline direct injection (GDI) capability in Athens by building a new 224,000 square foot facility at its existing location.
DENSO will invest more than $85 million and create 400 new jobs over the next three years in McMinn County.
The announcement marks DMAT’s third major investment in an 18-month period.
The overall expansion effort will represent a total investment of approximately $190 million and more than 700 new jobs.
Production at the new facility will begin in July 2016.
Tennessee Gov. Bill Haslam stated that the announcement brings Athens another step closer to the goal of making Tennessee the No. 1 location in the Southeast for high quality jobs.

Economic and Community Development Commissioner Randy Boyd noted that Tennessee’s automotive strength is reinforced by companies like DENSO that make quality products for suppliers throughout the United States.

Brian Nolen, General Manager of DMAT’s Gasoline Products Manufacturing Division, stated the project is critical to support the growing need of gasoline direct injection in North America as automakers turn to this technology to meet fuel-efficiency and emissions regulations.
The localization of gasoline direct injection technology is resulting in new jobs, new production lines, new approaches to training, and new growth among supply partners in North America.
DMAT currently employs a workforce of more than 1,450 and has been producing fuel injectors since 1998 for the North American region.
DENSO’s latest generation of GDI technology is geared to deliver more power and lower emissions while being more compact.
Athens Mayor Ann Davis expressed delight at the expansion, noting it has meant many new jobs for citizens and encouraged continued efforts to ensure a skilled and trained workforce.
McMinn County Mayor John Gentry highlighted that DENSO Manufacturing’s latest expansion reinforces the positive relationship McMinn County has enjoyed with the company for the past 17 years.

TVA Senior Vice President of Economic Development John Bradley stated that TVA and Athens Utilities Board are pleased to partner with the state of Tennessee, city of Athens, and McMinn County Economic Development Authority to support DENSO’s expansion and major investment.

Frank Snyder, chairman of the McMinn County Industrial Development Board, described DENSO as a wonderful corporate citizen whose expansion is a big boost for McMinn County and surrounding communities.

DENSO Corporation is headquartered in Kariya, Aichi prefecture, Japan, and is a leading global automotive supplier of advanced technology, systems, and components in thermal, powertrain control, electronics, information, and safety.
DENSO’s customers include all the world’s major carmakers.
Worldwide, DENSO has more than 200 subsidiaries and affiliates in 38 countries and regions and employs nearly 140,000 people.
Consolidated global sales for DENSO for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2014, totaled US$39.8 billion.
Last fiscal year, DENSO spent 9 percent of its global consolidated sales on research and development.
DENSO common stock is traded on the Tokyo and Nagoya stock exchanges.
In North America, DENSO employs more than 17,000 people at 32 consolidated companies and affiliates.
Of DENSO’s North American facilities, 28 are manufacturing locations in the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.
In the U.S. alone, DENSO employs more than 14,000 people across California, Michigan, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Kentucky, Georgia, Iowa, Ohio, Alabama, and Arkansas.
DENSO’s North American consolidated sales totaled US$7.9 billion for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2014.
Tennessee was named 2014 and 2013 “State of the Year” for economic development by Business Facilities magazine.

The Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development’s mission is to develop strategies to make Tennessee the No. 1 location in the Southeast for high quality jobs, attract new corporate investment, and facilitate expansion and economic growth.

facts: DENSO Corporation announced plans to begin local production of inverters in India and Thailand.
Inverters are key components that convert electrical power in electric vehicles.
Production is expected to start in India as early as 2026.
Production is expected to start in Thailand in 2027.
DENSO aims to establish a robust supply network in the ASEAN region and the Global South.
This move aligns with the “China Plus One” strategy, which focuses on diversifying manufacturing locations to enhance resilience.
New inverter production lines will be set up at DENSO’s existing facilities in India and Thailand.
Demand for hybrid vehicles (HVs) and plug-in hybrid vehicles (PHVs) remains strong despite plateauing growth in battery electric vehicles (BEVs).
Inverters convert direct current (DC) from on-board batteries into alternating current (AC) to power electric motors.
As EV motors become increasingly powerful and efficient, inverters must be capable of handling high current and minimizing energy loss.
DENSO has been manufacturing inverters since 1997.
DENSO’s inverter technologies are widely adopted by global automakers, including Toyota Motor Corporation.
The company holds a leading share in the global inverter market.
In Japan, inverter production is carried out at the Anjo Plant (Anjo City, Aichi Prefecture) and the Hirose Plant (Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture).
Globally, DENSO operates inverter production lines at eight sites, including in North America, Europe, and China.
In 2024, the company launched new inverter production lines at DENSO Manufacturing Hungary and DENSO Fukushima (Tamura City, Fukushima Prefecture).
DENSO aims to boost annual inverter production from 3.49 million units in 2022 to 19 million units by 2030.
source_title: DENSO to Localize Inverter Production in India and Thailand to Support EV Demand

facts: Die-Tech Industries Inc. was started in Carrollton, Ga. in 1987 by third-generation tool- and diemakers Tom and Jerry Wysoczynski.
The company began accepting production stamping jobs for its presses in 1999 to fill idle time between testing and offset fixed costs.
President and CEO Tom Wysoczynski stated that global competition hit tool and die shops hard, causing margins to shrink to the point where awarded contracts raised questions about quoting mistakes.
Die-Tech purchased servo feeds, coil handling equipment, and straightening systems from Dallas Industries in Troy, Mich., to automate feed presses for production pace.
The company determined it needed accurate, flexible servo feeds to remain competitive in quality and productivity.
Servo feeds allowed fast and simple changeovers in the job shop environment.
Dallas' feed equipment handles tough applications, such as stamping an automotive fan hub that must be held flat to 0.005-inch total indicated runout (TIR).
Die-Tech acquired a Dallas Space-Saver™ servo feed that reduced required floor area by up to half compared to conventional feed system layouts.
The space-saving equipment eliminated the cost and need for material looping pits and saved additional floor manufacturing space.

The production line comprises a powered reel and load car mechanism, a combination coil restrictor/peeler/threader/debender, and a high-powered single unit combining servo roll feed and pull-through straightener.
The line is attached to a 500-ton press with a 132-inch-long bed and is used primarily for progressive dies with multiple in-die nut and stud staking applications.
The system handles coils weighing up to 30,000 pounds and material widths from 12 inches to 36 inches, with diameters up to 72 inches.
Large coil capacity allows the stamper to make fewer coil changes, resulting in more uptime and less start-up scrap.
Die-Tech achieves approximate feed and press speeds of up to 83 strokes per minute (SPM) at a 6-inch feed length and 240-degree feed cycle.
The company implemented automatic, self-centering capabilities to reduce die mis-hits and quality defects caused by improper setup.

The powered reel is self-centering with a variable-speed AC drive, dual-pressure adjustable air brake, coil width scaling arms, hydraulic-powered mandrel expansion, push-button support arm expansion, and a fail-safe braking system.
A combination coil restrictor prevents coils from springing when restraining bands are cut, while the peeler lifts the leading edge and the threader guides material edges into the straightener.
The debender removes leading and trailing edge bends from coil material to prevent mis-hits, breakage, and unexpected repairs.
Tom Wysoczynski emphasized that accuracy is critical for staking operations, noting a mis-hit could cause up to $1,500 in damage to replace a staking head.
For rear hatch mounting brackets with six staking heads, an inaccurate feed could cost up to $9,000 to repair the die, plus downtime and labor.
The straightener unit includes a piloting head that pivots upward to release pressure on material as die pilot pins precisely locate the stock.
A full-function control system with a touchscreen interactive programming unit slashed setup times by guiding operators through sequential steps.
Subsequent setups for repeat jobs are completed quickly by entering an identifying code or part number, setting all line systems to predetermined parameters in less time than die setup.
The company typically performs three to four setups on the line and press in a 24-hour period.
Die-Tech developed its own performance-based software package to track operations because the stamping business grew too fast for existing service capabilities.

The software tracks die and press maintenance schedules, setup performance, material requirements planning (MRP) status, routing and logistics, downtime, repair mean times, projected run times, and scrap rates.
Operators cannot clock onto a job without receiving proper training, and all quality check sheets are printed electronically to ensure team members have the latest copies.
The software system controls scrap, sorting results, daily quality bin inventory, rework hours, and training hours.
Dock audits and employee training audits are conducted electronically using wireless scanners, with results emailed immediately to supervisors, human resources, and quality control managers.
If a defective product occurs, the company can identify the date, shift, employee, and steel heat within 10 minutes for immediate investigation.
Die maintenance and machine maintenance are tracked on a predictive database using data generated directly from parts produced or time used.
Schedules are created based on manufacturing use of tools or machines to minimize maintenance costs and create a predictive maintenance environment.
The company's main business now comes from production stamping and assembly, while upholding die manufacturing standards.
Die manufacturing continues to be a good income generator for the company.
The company currently runs approximately 40 presses ranging from 45 to 800 tons around the clock.
Die-Tech has more than 550 customer dies and tool sets on hand to run on a contract basis.
Stamping income has soared to levels nearly 10 times that of 10 years ago.
source_title: Die shop transitions to production stamper

facts: Dongwon Autopart Technology plans to invest $30 million in a new production facility in Emanuel County, Georgia.
The automotive project is anticipated to create 200 new jobs over the next several years.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment and stated that Georgia's thriving automotive industry is creating new opportunities for Georgians statewide regardless of zip code.
Dongwon Autopart Technology was established in 1971 and supplies components such as doorframes, seat frames, and battery frames to automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company currently supports more than 500 direct jobs in the Southeast, including at facilities in Hogansville, Georgia that will remain operational.
Dongwon Autopart Technology has been operating in the United States since 2003.

Heather Hollstein, Human Resources Manager at Dongwon Autopart Technology, stated the company is proud to be part of growing the state's automotive industry and remains committed to supplying internal and external customers with high-quality products and service.
Construction on the new manufacturing facility will take place at the Hwy 297 Industrial Park near Swainsboro.
The company will be hiring for positions including management, system leads, and production personnel.
Hiring is expected to begin in 2026.

Emanuel County Development Authority Chairman Will Donaldson welcomed Dongwon Autopart Technology to the county, noting that the project pays off the authority's time, energy, and investment in economic development.

Ken Warnock, CEO of the Emanuel County Development Authority, assisted the company in locating the site and described the facility as the single largest new economic development project for Emanuel County in over 30 years.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce team, represented GDEcD on the competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Emanuel County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the automotive industry is expanding outward along the I-16 corridor as suppliers choose strategic locations with access to OEMs and Georgia's ports.
Dongwon Autopart Technology's new facility will be its second location in Georgia, reflecting the robust growth of the state's automotive industry over more than 100 years.

facts: Elan Technology offers several varieties of steatite, alumina and zirconia advanced ceramic materials.
Steatite ceramic is a magnesium silicate material used in insulators/enclosures for electrical components, and in appliance, aerospace and automotive applications.
Alumina ceramics are advanced ceramic components used in a wide range of applications such as electronics, pump components and automotive sensors.
Zirconia ceramics are commonly used in applications such as valves, bearings and filters.
96% Alumina (102-04) has a density of 3.72, flexural strength of 353 MPa (51 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 330 GPa.
96% Alumina is the most widely used composition of alumina that Elan Technology offers.
94% Alumina High CaO2 (102-03) has a density of 3.64, flexural strength of 362 MPa (52 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 300 GPa.
94% Alumina Low CaO (102-02) has a density of 3.63, flexural strength of 364 MPa (53 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 290 GPa.
85% Alumina 14% SiO2 (102-01) has a density of 3.35, flexural strength of 185 MPa (27 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 245 GPa.
The listed alumina grades are commonly used as electric insulators in electronics, pump components & automotive sensors.
Steatite (101-01) has a density of 2.75, flexural strength of 178 MPa (26 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 111 GPa.
White Steatite (101-02) has a density of 2.56, flexural strength of 139 MPa (29 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 97 GPa.
Low Loss Steatite (101-03) has a density of 2.91, flexural strength of 152 MPa (22 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 113 GPa.
The steatite grades are commonly used for insulators/enclosures for electrical components.
Cordierite (103-01) has a density of 2.26-2.30, flexural strength of 92 MPa (13 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 220 GPa.
Cordierite is commonly used in appliances, heating element supports, refractory supports (ovens/kilns) & thermocouples.
Yttria Partially Stabilized Zirconia (104-01) has a density of 6.02, flexural strength of 976 MPa (142 psi × 103), and Young’s Modulus of 220 GPa.
Yttria Partially Stabilized Zirconia is commonly used in pump components, cutting instruments, valves, bearings & filters.
Elan Technology's engineers can assist with application support and material selection.

EVCO Plastics invests in design for use, design for manufacturability and design for competitive advantage.
EVCO Plastics believes its success is determined by maximizing client success through the critical design and engineering process.
Partnering with clients early in the design phase is identified as one of two most critical factors for success.
EVCO Plastics focuses on five keys to success for engineering out cost and engineering in quality and optimal performance.
The first key is Part Design, which involves understanding product application, intended market and performance, end use objectives, and use environment.
The second key is Mold Design & Build, focusing on mold efficiency, longevity and performance.
The third key is Molding Process, covering injection molding technologies, cycle times, material flow and employing scientific injection molding.
The fourth key is Quality Assurance, focusing on tolerances, part inspection, steel safe mold and more.
The fifth key is Automation in the injection molding process and downstream to make it simple and lean.
source_title: Design For Manufacturability | Conveyors & Conveying Equipment | Evco Plastics

facts: EVCO Plastics is a plastics injection molder with nine plants in the U.S., Mexico and China.
Based in DeForest, Wis., EVCO recently purchased a facility near its CAL 1 plant located outside Calhoun, Ga.
The newly purchased facility is dubbed CAL 2.
CAL 2 adds 40,000 ft2 of space.
CAL 2 includes room for 20 injection machines of up to 500 tons.
CAL 2 includes a 725-ft2 metrology room.
CAL 2 will introduce EVCO’s first white room, measuring 22,000 ft2.
CAL 2 will also introduce EVCO’s first LSR molding capability, measuring 2100 ft2.
Both the new white room and LSR molding capabilities are aimed to support medical and commercial lighting customers.
CAL 2 is expected to be fully operational this fall.
When fully operational, CAL 2 will be a food-grade compliant facility with highly automated “lights-out” production.
The addition of CAL 2 brings EVCO’s total footprint to 1 million ft2.
The company employs over 900 people to operate more than 140 injection presses from 28 to 3500 tons.
EVCO is currently adding 13,000 ft2 to its AMP facility in DeForest.
The expansion at the DeForest AMP facility provides room for more machines as well as storage of raw materials and finished goods.

facts: Evco Plastics Inc., a family-owned injection molder, has purchased a building near Calhoun, Ga., where it will add white room and liquid silicone rubber molding capabilities.
Evco Plastics provides product design, injection molding, and plastic packaging services for businesses.
Estimated annual revenue for Evco Plastics is $100-500M.
Estimated employee count for Evco Plastics is 1.0-5.0K.
Dale Evans serves as President & CEO of Evco Plastics, with a CEO approval rating of 90/100.

facts: EVCO Plastics and Kistler have formed a partnership for zero-defect production in injection molding.
EVCO Plastics is a globally recognized leader in injection molding and additional technologies.
EVCO Plastics is a family-run business founded in Wisconsin in 1964.
EVCO Plastics has a workforce of over 1,800 employees spread across eleven facilities in the US, China, and Mexico.
Anna Bartz serves as the Director of Communications at EVCO.
Following recent growth, EVCO operates more than 260 molding machines.
EVCO offers technologies and services across the entire value chain, including automation, packaging, labeling, and more.
Precision Molding is one of EVCO's four main divisions.
EVCO's products and solutions cover diverse industries ranging from agriculture and powersports to packaging and consumer goods.
EVCO explicitly does not produce automotive parts or implantable devices.
Medical technology accounts for roughly 20 percent of EVCO's annual sales.
Two EVCO sites are equipped with an ISO Class 8 Clean Room: the headquarters in Deforest, Wisconsin, and a production facility in Dongguan, China.

Fanello Industries has expanded its facilities and capabilities in order to maintain leadership in the southeast metal industry.

facts: Flambeau’s plastic manufacturing facility in Sharon Center, Ohio commissioned a 3 lb. Sterling dual head blow molding machine, increasing production capacity.
The Sharon Center plant is one of eight facilities across the United States.
A brand new 1,900-ton injection molding machine (Engel Duo press) was commissioned at Flambeau’s Columbus, Indiana facility, increasing the range of existing injection molding machines.
Flambeau, Inc. continues to be named one of the top plastics manufacturers in North America, rising in rankings for 2022 and holding positions in others.
Production was ramped up to full capacity on new injection moulding machines processing plastic parts for Makita power tools such as planers, drills, grinders, and saws.
Flambeau, Inc. reached its 75th year of business in 2022 after being founded in 1947.
Associates at the Baraboo injection molding plant achieved a zero-accident milestone of 365 days and 251,134 hours worked by September 30, 2021.
Flambeau EuroPlast committed to a substantial investment in its Ramsgate manufacturing facility with four new Haitian™ injection moulding machines to manufacture plastic case assemblies for Makita.

Surges in demand led Flambeau to invest significantly in Middlefield operations, adding three new presses ranging from 330-ton to 730-ton dedicated to stock boxes and cases for Flambeau Outdoors™ and ArtBin®.
A new 750-ton press was added to Columbus operations primarily for proprietary products including Flambeau® Brute™ tool boxes, gun cases, and tackle boxes.
Two new presses were added to Madison operations to increase production of stock and custom products for fluids and packaging divisions.
Flambeau’s plant in Saltillo, Coahuila, Mexico received a 2020 quality award from PACCAR for exceeding its “10 ppm” quality standard.
In the 2020 Plastics News Magazine ranking of blow molders, Flambeau came in at #20.

Plants in Madison, Georgia, and Saltillo, Coahuila, Mexico manufacture thermoplastic molded parts for the automotive industry and received General Motors (GM) Supplier Quality Excellence Awards for 2019.
The award from GM is given only to top-performing suppliers.
For the Mexico facility, it was the sixth straight year receiving this recognition from GM.

Flambeau expedited engineering and tool making processes to produce a key component of personal protective equipment (PPE) for frontline healthcare workers at its Baraboo, Wisconsin injection molding facility.

facts: FREYR Battery (NYSE: FREY) is a Luxembourg-based battery manufacturer focused on sustainability.
FREYR Battery has announced plans to open a new manufacturing plant in Coweta County, Georgia.
The proposed plant site is located approximately 40 miles south of Atlanta.
The project represents a total capital investment of approximately $2.6 billion.
The manufacturing facility will be known as Giga America.
The exact location of the Giga America facility was not disclosed at the time of the announcement.
The site is reported to span 368 acres.
The site will be situated near distribution centers for Amazon and Shopify.
The site offers proximity to Interstate 85, which connects Atlanta with Coweta County.
Community and company leaders expect to create more than 700 new jobs over the next seven years.
A specific timeline for project completion was not disclosed.
FREYR Battery considered more than 150 sites across 25 different states before selecting the Coweta County location.
The company’s products are designed to decarbonize transportation and energy systems, including as components for electric vehicles.

Tom Einar Jensen, co-founder and CEO of FREYR Battery, stated that the company expects to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and develop localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S. to enhance energy security and economic activity.
Georgia has recently established itself as a hub for electric vehicle production.
In December of the previous year, California-based Rivian Inc. (NASDAQ: RIVN) announced plans to build a $5 billion facility in Rutledge, Georgia.
The Rivian facility is located about 50 miles east of Atlanta.
The Rivian project has since been met with environmental concerns and backlash from local residents.
In May of the current year, Hyundai Motor Group revealed plans to develop a $5.5 billion manufacturing hub for its electric vehicle division in Bryan County, Georgia.
The Hyundai facility is located near Savannah in coastal Georgia.
At the time of their announcements, local leaders estimated the Rivian project would add about 7,000 jobs.
At the time of its announcement, local leaders estimated the Hyundai project would add about 8,000 jobs.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp's office cited the state’s prime location, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and business-friendly climate as primary factors attracting companies like FREYR Battery.
Katie Kirkpatrick, CEO and president of the Metro Atlanta Chamber, welcomed FREYR Battery to metro Atlanta, noting it has quickly become a hub for battery cell research and production.
Kirkpatrick stated that FREYR Battery will benefit from the region's vibrant research ecosystem and the diversity of talent that stands ready to support the company’s next-generation efforts.
FREYR Battery’s stock price opened at $14.40 per share on Monday, Nov. 14, marking the first full day of trading since the announcement.
The stock price represented an increase from $10.92 per share a year ago.
source_title: FREYR Battery to Open $2.6B Manufacturing Plant in Metro Atlanta, Create 700 Jobs - REBusinessOnline

facts: FREYR has selected and purchased a site in Coweta County, Georgia for a multi-phase Giga America clean battery manufacturing project.
The project is expected to be developed based on the next-generation SemiSolidTM Lithium-Ion Battery Technology platform developed by 24M Technologies Inc (“24M”) in Boston, MA.
The initial phase of the project will be a battery cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
The initial phase is based on a preliminarily estimated capital investment of $1.7 billion.
FREYR plans to undertake detailed plant engineering in the coming months to finalize plans for the initial Gigafactory module.
The company and key stakeholders are evaluating additional value accretive upstream and downstream modules as well as a second cell production phase.
Total capital investments for the project are expected to bring to more than $2.6 billion by 2029.
Over the life of the project, FREYR and its partners expect to create more than 720 U.S. jobs for highly skilled workers in Coweta County.
FREYR purchased land for development at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site in Coweta County, GA.
The industrial zoned tract is 368 acres with options secured for further expansion.
The site was selected through a rigorous evaluation process based on key operational, logistical, and financial criteria.
The Giga America site already offers substantial room for additional upstream and downstream development projects to strengthen cost position and improve the carbon footprint of produced batteries.
The development of Giga America positions FREYR to meet rapidly growing customer demand for Energy Storage Systems (ESS) applications with U.S.-based conditional offtake partners.
FREYR is seeing very strong interest for increased volume commitments through ongoing customer dialogues.

FREYR is accelerating its previously planned U.S. expansion based on strong tailwinds in U.S. renewable energy development, grid stability initiatives, and recently announced tax incentives associated with the Inflation Reduction Act.
Projected U.S. demand is being augmented further by increased interest from existing and new customer dialogues in the commercial mobility and electric vehicle segments.
FREYR is intensifying and broadening its financing efforts to support expected capital commitments for the Giga America project.
Financing efforts are running parallel with the ongoing construction of Giga Arctic in Mo i Rana, Norway.
The State of Georgia and Coweta County are collectively providing strong financial incentives for the Giga America project.
FREYR intends to apply with the U.S. Department of Energy for packages that could potentially include a grant and/or direct loan to assist with development.
FREYR is evaluating clean power supply solutions for Giga America with utility providers in the region, including potential development of a dedicated solar plus storage facility.

Giga America will leverage 24M’s SemiSolidTM platform, which simplifies lithium-ion battery production and enables highly capital, labor, and energy efficient manufacturing at scale based on U.S. technology originating from MIT.
FREYR’s U.S. project team intends to replicate major elements of the Giga Arctic development by maintaining a continuous technical dialogue with key personnel in Norway.
The announcement was made from New York, Oslo, and Luxembourg on November 11, 2022.
Upon completion of all contemplated construction phases, the Giga America complex is expected to be one of the largest battery cell manufacturing developments globally.

FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen stated that expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of the company's long-term strategy and expects U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV, and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade due to the Inflation Reduction Act.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that job creators bring operations to Georgia for success and welcomed FREYR as a valued addition to the state's No. 1 business ranking, looking forward to its impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.

Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman Bob Blackburn stated that Coweta County is quickly becoming a premier destination for top-tier manufacturing industries and looks forward to the innovation and success FREYR will generate.

The total package of financial incentives for Giga America includes robust assistance from The State of Georgia and Coweta County plus expected production tax credits associated with the Inflation Reduction Act.
The U.S. energy storage market is already the largest in the world and is expected to experience exponential growth over the next several years.
According to Rystad Energy, cumulative collective storage demand of 4.5 TWh from the U.S. and Europe will be required through 2030 to satisfy a 1.6-degree scenario.

Under the same scenario, Rystad expects annual collective storage demand in the U.S. and Europe to reach 1.4 TWh by 2030, representing a 34-fold increase from projected corresponding annual 2022 demand.

Jensen added that FREYR is deeply committed to decarbonizing transportation and energy sectors and expects to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and develop localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S.
Giga America will be based on the SemiSolidTM technology of FREYR’s U.S.-based licensing partner, 24M.
The SemiSolidTM platform enables capital and energy efficient production of lithium-ion batteries at scale.
FREYR is currently evaluating clean power supply solutions for Giga America with U.S. Independent Power Producers (IPPs).
The company’s long-term ambition is to replicate the strategy employed at Giga Arctic to produce next-generation batteries that are among the cleanest anywhere in the world.
A launch event will be hosted live from Coweta County, Georgia at 10:00 am EST (16:00 CET).
The webcasted presentation will be streamed online in English, with a recording made available after the presentation on FREYR’s website and YouTube channel.
FREYR Battery aims to provide industrial scale clean battery solutions to reduce global emissions and is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under ticker FREY.

The company has commenced building its first planned factory in Mo i Rana, Norway and announced potential development of industrial scale battery cell production in Vaasa, Finland, and the United States.
FREYR intends to install 50 GWh of battery cell capacity by 2025, 100 GWh annual capacity by 2028, and 200 GWh of annual capacity by 2030.

A cautionary statement notes forward-looking statements regarding the project's ability to deliver an initial battery cell production module of approximately 24 GWh, among other potential benefits and risks.

Information about factors that could materially affect FREYR is set forth in its Registration Statement on Form S-3 filed with the SEC on September 1, 2022, and annual report on Form 10-K filed on March 9, 2022.

The Giga America project will be developed in multiple phases, beginning with an initial battery cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
The initial phase requires a preliminarily estimated capital investment of $1.7 billion.
FREYR selected the Bridgeport Industrial Park site through a rigorous evaluation process based on operational, logistical, and financial criteria.
The site was chosen for its suitability to co-locate modular upstream and downstream projects with the Giga America cell manufacturing facility.

facts: T1 Energy is a rebranded entity formerly known as Freyr Battery that has pivoted from battery storage to solar manufacturing.
T1 Energy formed a partnership with Corning involving a $1.5 billion investment in polysilicon and wafer production.
The partnership secures hyper-pure polysilicon and solar wafers from Corning's Michigan campus.
Polysilicon is a foundational material for solar cells that has historically been sourced overseas, exposing projects to geopolitical risks and trade tariffs.
The partnership ensures raw materials, wafers, cells, and modules are all produced domestically, creating a closed-loop system.
The closed-loop supply chain aligns with the Inflation Reduction Act's (IRA) domestic content requirements.
Analysts project that the streamlined supply chain could reduce solar module costs by 15–20% by 2030.
Cost reduction drivers include economies of scale and the elimination of intermediaries.
T1 Energy's G2_Austin facility is set to produce 5 GW of advanced TOPCon solar cells annually.
T1 Energy's G1_Dallas module plant is already generating 443 MW in Q1 2025.
Trump-era tariffs on foreign solar imports have inadvertently accelerated domestic manufacturing.
Avoiding reliance on Chinese components ensures eligibility for the IRA's 30% Investment Tax Credit (ITC).
The IRA's domestic content bonus could add up to 10% to the credit.
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) mandates 10-year recapture rules for ITC claims.
T1's partnership with Corning includes wafer production at Hemlock Semiconductor, a U.S. polysilicon leader.
T1 Energy made an $850 million investment in Austin.
T1 Energy made a $340 million acquisition of the Dallas module plant.
The company abandoned its Georgia battery project, which was a $2.6 billion venture.
The U.S. Department of Energy emphasizes solar as a “low-hanging fruit” for rapid decarbonization.
T1 Energy maintains a diversified energy portfolio in Europe.
The U.S. solar market is projected to grow at a compound annual rate of 12% through 2030.
Market growth drivers include corporate sustainability mandates and the AI-driven energy demand surge.
By 2026, T1's Austin facility will supply 5 GW of cells annually.
Corning's Michigan plant will produce enough wafers to support 10 GW of module production.
The partnership has job creation potential for nearly 6,000 roles across Michigan and Texas.
Job creation aligns with the Biden administration's industrial policy goals.
Success of the Austin facility hinges on timely construction and operational efficiency.
The solar market's post-2028 outlook depends on sustained demand and regulatory stability.
source_title: T1 Energy's Solar Supply Chain Transformation: A Strategic Bet on U.S. Energy Independence

facts: Battery has become a crucial component thanks to the adoption of EVs and energy storage systems.
Freyr Battery, a Norway-based manufacturer, is establishing the first supply chain in the country with contributions from its Asian partners.
The company aims to leverage cheap energy prices and optimized production processes to make cost-competitive products.
According to the International Energy Agency's report, demand for EV batteries will surge from 340GWh to 3500GWh by 2030.
Various companies have or plan to build battery factories in Europe or the US due to the growing awareness of securing supplies locally or regionally.
The situation creates opportunities for startups such as Freyr.
Freyr has moved rapidly since its establishment in 2018.
Listed on the New York Stock Exchange since July 2021, the company is constructing a production base in northern Norway.
The project's first phase includes a customer qualification plant (CQP) and two gigafactories, Giga Arctic, that would cost around US$1.7 billion.
Production is scheduled to start in the first quarter of 2023.
The CQP will host one industrial-scale production line instead of a pilot.
Tom Einar Jensen, Freyr's CEO, said the company will utilize the facility to produce sample cells, test raw materials and train its operators.
In short, the CQP could help Freyr achieve high production in the gigafactories later.
According to the company, eight production lines will be installed at Giga Arctic with an annual capacity of 29GWh.
Operations will start in the first half of 2024.
To secure material supply, the company has partnered with Aleees of Taiwan, aiming to build an LFP cathode plant in the Nordic region.
Jensen said the joint plant would be the largest outside China, with an initial capacity of 10,000 tonnes per year by 2024.
Battery startups like Freyr face multiple challenges at their early stage, including finding the right technology, competence, investors and how to scale up quickly.
Jensen said scale is essential for driving down production costs and for a company to stay competitive.
As a Norwegian company, Freyr will leverage the economical renewable energy to produce clean batteries.
Jensen said 100% of the industrial electricity onshore in the country comes from hydro or other green power sources.
Therefore, Freyr probably pays the lowest power prices in the world compared with other battery makers, giving it a competitive edge.
According to the company, producing batteries with green energy can cut down carbon footprint by 25 to 30%.

Jensen added that if Freyr could also make cathode materials with renewable energy, it would reduce more than 50% of carbon emissions during the whole battery production process than many China-based manufacturers.
In addition, Freyr has licensed the 24M SemiSolid™ production platform, which is useful for producing cost-competitive products.
The platform was developed by 24M Technologies, an MIT startup.
Jensen said the platform enables an optimized production process with smaller and simpler facilities while making much larger and thicker electrodes.
Moreover, Freyr only needs less than half of the operators that a conventional battery plant requires and would be able to make three times more batteries per employee.

"So, half the CapEx (capital expenditure), 20 to 25% lower OpEx (operating expense), and a localized supply chain enable us to be very competitive while having the cleanest battery solutions on the planet," the CEO concluded.
Starting from Norway, Freyr is on track to expand.
For example, it has formed a joint venture with Koch Strategic Platforms to build a gigafactory in the US.
Jensen said the duo has looked into more than 150 sites in the country and narrowed the list down to five.
Freyr will also expand to Finland in the City of Vaasa.
Jensen said the country has abundant renewable energy, minerals and metals and the workforce competency it needs.
Those advantages could benefit Freyr's planned battery factory in the area.
The startup recently opened a technology resources campus and business unit in Japan.
Jensen said the new office could enable Freyr to access battery engineering proficiency, next-generation solutions and many other resources in Japan.
The company also looks forward to building local partnerships to scale up rapidly.
As it gets close to product commercialization, Freyr has secured initial customer agreements with companies focusing on energy storage systems (ESS).
For instance, it announced on August 30 a battery cell sales agreement with Nidec Corporation of Japan.
Jensen said Freyr's technology is particularly suitable for ESS.
Still, the company has also received significant interest from EV and commercial mobility segments, with two times the contract volume for ESS.
Freyr has an ambition to reach 50GWh of annual production capacity by 2025, 100GWh by 2028 and 200GWh by 2030, according to the CEO.
He added that the company is considering building anode manufacturing facilities besides producing cathode materials with Aleees.
It will also diversify the technology platform beyond 24M to offer a broader set of battery technologies.
And, of course, Freyr is looking for more partners.
"We have no restriction as to who we partner with," Jensen said. "If companies out there have something to offer, and they see that we could be a relevant partner. They're all welcome."
source_title: Freyr Battery poised to scale up clean and cost-competitive battery production

facts: FREYR Battery has acquired land in Georgia to built a $1.7 billion battery cell manufacturing facility.
The company partners with 24M to use and scale up the latter’s SemiSolid lithium-ion battery platform.
FREYR announced plans for the Giga America production plant in Coweta County at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site.
Norway-based FREYR Battery’s initial goal is create 34 GWh in battery cell production capacity at the industrial park site once production begins.
Eventually, FREYR plans to spend close to $2.6 billion on several battery cell lines of production by 2029.
Co-founder and CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated that expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from our inception.

Jensen noted that with the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, they expected U.S. demand for energy storage systems, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
The project could create more than 720 U.S. jobs, the company predicted.
Both the state of Georgia and Coweta County offered financial incentives for the project.
FREYR intends to apply with the U.S. Department of Energy for packages that could include a grant and/or direct loans to assist with the development of Giga America.

The announcement follows several other battery manufacturing plans in the United States revealed since passage of the Inflation Reduction Act which offers incentives such as production tax credits for tightening the clean energy supply chain surrounding the U.S.
Early this year, technology giant Honeywell detailed a deal to buy 19 GWh of FREYR battery cells between 2023 and 2030 for its energy storage applications.
FREYR will also leverage Honeywell’s technology such as field instrumentation, integrated automation and security integration in the manufacturing process.
Prices for battery energy storage systems are predicted to fall by many market observers, spurring greater market development.

Early this decade, the energy storage industry forecasted some 100 GW of new BESS capacity by 2030 as developers increase solar power installations and seek balancing assets for intermittent renewable energies.

Deutsche Bank analyst Edison Yu considers Goodyear a 'relative winner' of Trump's trade wars because most of its U.S. demand is filled with domestic manufacturing, making it less exposed to import tariffs relative to peers in this industry.
Replacement tires make up 85% of Goodyear's revenue and are set to benefit from auto tariffs as consumers will stick with their vehicles longer, leading to more tire changes.

facts: A Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. plant in Georgia faces about $70,000 in fines for failing to follow federal workplace safety laws that endangered workers.

Investigators from the federal Occupational Safety and Health Administration imposed seven serious citations against the company in January after inspecting its plant last year in Social Circle, Georgia.
Federal officials found the company failed to provide proper protective gear to workers processing rubber through hot metal presses with temperatures exceeding 350 degrees Fahrenheit.
Investigators discovered hazards from unguarded machines, rainwater and leaking equipment that put workers at risk of slipping on the production floor.
William Fulcher, the OSHA area office director in Atlanta, stated that the inspection found multiple safety deficiencies that put employees at risk of serious injury or death.
Goodyear has contested the fines, according to an OSHA spokesman.
Laura Duda, a Goodyear spokeswoman, wrote that the company cooperated with OSHA during its inspection and is following the standard administrative process for responding to citations.
A December investigation by Reveal showed how Goodyear’s lax approach to safety contributed to the deaths of motorists on the road and workers in its plants.
Goodyear ranked among the top five manufacturers in the United States for worker deaths since 2009, according to Reveal’s analysis.
At least four motorists over the last seven years have died in accidents after tires made at Goodyear plants failed.
Reveal’s investigation found that intense production demands and leaks in the roof of Goodyear’s plants have endangered both workers and consumers.
Court documents and former workers from Goodyear’s plants in Danville, Virginia, and Fayetteville, North Carolina, detailed how water leaked through the roof and onto machines.
Workers are trained to avoid moisture in tire production because it can prompt treads to separate, causing a blowout.
Former employees of Goodyear’s Danville and Fayetteville plants reported feeling pressure to put production before workplace safety.
Some former employees recalled a quota-driven motto invoked on the shop floor: “Round and black and out the back.”
Hours after Reveal’s investigation was published, Goodyear issued a statement acknowledging it fell short of its own expectations for safety over the past two years.
Barbara Hatala, Goodyear’s communications manager for the Americas Operations, called the safety failures unacceptable and mourned the loss of valued coworkers at two U.S. manufacturing plants.
The Trump administration has rolled back and postponed Obama-era workplace safety protections in keeping with goals laid out by the National Association of Manufacturers.
Richard Kramer, Goodyear’s chairman, chief executive officer and president, serves on the board of the National Association of Manufacturers.
Since October 2008, Goodyear has been fined more than $1.9 million for more than 200 health and workplace safety violations, far more than its four major competitors combined.
Besides the most recent proposed OSHA fines of $69,058, Goodyear’s plant in Social Circle, Georgia, has been previously cited for three workplace safety violations, two of them serious.
Jordan Barab, former deputy assistant secretary of OSHA under Obama, described the most recent fines as “deeply discouraging.”

Barab stated that despite multiple preventable deaths and serious injuries in plants across the country, Goodyear never seems to learn that it is legally and morally required to provide safe workplaces for its employees.
