entity_name: Hyundai-LG Georgia Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced plans to increase their joint investment in a Georgia battery manufacturing plant by an additional $2 billion.
The total investment for the Georgia battery manufacturing plant will reach $4.3 billion.
The plant is expected to produce approximately 300,000 electric vehicle batteries annually.
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution intend to allocate a cumulative investment of $7.59 billion over eight years in Bryan County, Georgia.
The broader strategy encompasses both the battery manufacturing plant and a distinct electric vehicle manufacturing facility.
Vehicle production at the electric vehicle manufacturing plant is scheduled to commence in January 2025.
The EV manufacturing facility has a projected annual output of 300,000 vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group is the world’s third-largest automaker in terms of vehicle sales.
The combined manufacturing facilities are collectively referred to as the “Metaplant.”
The Metaplant has been strategically incentivized by the 2022 U.S. Inflation Reduction Act.
The Inflation Reduction Act offers a consumer tax credit of $7,500 contingent on electric vehicles being produced within the United States.
The Act introduces stringent sourcing criteria for critical minerals and battery components, emphasizing domestic production.
The law includes provisions for substantial U.S. battery production tax credits to foster domestic battery manufacturing growth.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs utilizing cells from the Georgia plant.
These assembled battery packs will be supplied to Hyundai Motor manufacturing facilities across the United States.
The supplied battery packs will support the production of electric vehicles under the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp expressed enthusiasm for the investment, highlighting the state’s ability to attract suppliers in support of the manufacturing initiative.
Hyundai’s Chief Operating Officer Jose Munoz previously indicated that the Georgia plant has the potential to eventually produce up to 500,000 vehicles annually, contingent on market demand.
Hyundai Motor announced a distinct $5 billion electric vehicle battery joint venture in the United States in collaboration with SK On, the battery unit of SK Innovation Co.
source_title: Hyundai and LG to Invest Additional $2 Billion in Georgia Battery Plant, Create 400 Jobs

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp signed a sales use tax exemption for projects of regional significance at the future site of the Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County.
The South Korean automaker's electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility is scheduled to open in late 2024.
The facility expects to employ 8,100 workers.
Kemp was joined for the signing ceremony by Georgia Speaker of the House Jon Burns, an Effingham County native who owns a farm near the site.
Kemp was also joined by Georgia Sen. Ben Watson, a Savannahian whose district includes Bryan County.
In addition to the tax exemption, Kemp signed the state's Fiscal Year 2024 budget into law at the event.
Georgia's state budget tops $32.4 billion.
House Bill 19 centers on raises for law enforcement and teachers, with teachers receiving a $2,000 raise and law enforcement seeing a $6,000 increase.
Kemp stated: “We are investing in a pipeline, from the classroom to the boardroom,” noting more money is being put towards education than ever before in state history.
Kemp announced the Hope Scholarship will be restored to 100% of tuition for students attending public universities and technical colleges.
The budget includes $52 million for Georgia Pathways to Coverage, the state's Medicaid waiver program.
Individuals earning less than 100% of the federal poverty level are eligible for Georgia’s Pathways to Coverage if they work, attend school, or perform community service at least 80 hours a month.
Kemp stated the administration is working to successfully launch the Medicaid program on July 1 of this year.
Another key aspect of the bill includes hiring an analyst to work alongside the prosecution unit tasked with tackling human trafficking in Georgia.
House Bill 408 extends a sales use tax break for regional projects meant to spur investment.
Kemp called Hyundai Metaplant America and another battery plant coming to Bartow County “transformational projects” and cited them as reasons why House Bill 408 is imperative.
Kemp noted that most communities benefiting from the tax exemption tool are located outside the metro Atlanta area.
Kemp reminded attendees that suppliers related to the Hyundai project exceeded financial projections.
Kemp announced over 4,500 new jobs in roughly $1.96 billion dollars of investment for the Hyundai project's site suppliers.
The announced supplier investment number is nearly double the original estimate because the company chose Georgia.
source_title: Georgia Governor Bryan Kemp signing ceremony Hyundai Metaplant Bryan

facts: Hyundai Metaplant America is an EV plant located in Bryan County, just southwest of Savannah.
The Hyundai Metaplant America build-out is ramping through 2024–2030.
The facility projects 8,500+ direct jobs.
The project includes 7,500+ indirect supplier-and-construction jobs.
The surrounding Tier 1 supplier ecosystem for Hyundai Metaplant America represents the largest emerging vending opportunity in coastal Georgia.
Hyundai Metaplant America runs vendor onboarding through facility operations and is typically inaccessible to outside operators once operational.
The construction-contractor and supplier ecosystem is ramping in advance of the Metaplant opening between 2024–2030.
The Port of Savannah is the third-largest US container port and the largest single-terminal container facility in the western hemisphere (Garden City Terminal).
A dense 3PL and customs broker ecosystem surrounds the Port of Savannah along the Ogeechee River and I-95.
Gulfstream Aerospace Savannah employs 12,000+ people building business jets and is the world's largest business jet manufacturer.
The Travis Field corridor hosts a business jet supplier ecosystem adjacent to Gulfstream Aerospace.
The Pooler aerospace supplier ecosystem surrounds the Gulfstream-adjacent corridor.
The I-95 distribution belt through Chatham County includes Amazon, FedEx, and a long tail of regional warehouses.
Savannah's metro area grew rapidly from 2015–2024, driven by Port of Savannah expansion and the Hyundai Metaplant America EV plant announcement and build-out.
The Savannah metro contains roughly 16,000 business establishments with a median household income of $60,000.
The machine-to-business ratio in the Port of Savannah-adjacent and Hyundai Metaplant America-adjacent supplier corridors sits noticeably below the southeast average.
High-revenue marquee accounts in the region are locked under multi-year national contracts with Canteen, Five Star, Compass, or Aramark.
Georgia requires no state-level vending operator license, but operators must register a Georgia Sales Tax Permit through the Department of Revenue.
Sales tax is 7% combined in Chatham County (state 4% + Chatham 3%) and 8% in Bryan County.
Chatham County Health Department requires food handler training for vending operations stocking food, accepting ANSI national programs.
First-mover advantage applies to building relationships with construction-contractor offices ramping in advance of the Metaplant opening.
Port of Savannah locations operate on 24/7 shift volume requiring high-volume value product mixes.
Gulfstream-adjacent locations require curated product mixes with cleared-workforce overlay.
Downtown Savannah Class A and B mid-rise tenants, Historic District commercial, and surrounding hospitality contractor offices drive tourism and corporate demand.
SCAD has 14,000+ students, while Savannah State University and Georgia Southern Armstrong campus together exceed 25,000 students.
Compass Group holds the Port of Savannah, Gulfstream Aerospace, SCAD, Savannah State, plus major hospital concession contracts.
Canteen has a downtown Savannah Class A presence.

Local Georgia operators dominate the second tier, including the Garden City Terminal-adjacent 3PL and customs broker corridor, the Gulfstream-adjacent business jet supplier ecosystem, the surrounding Hyundai Metaplant-adjacent supplier corridor, the downtown Savannah Class B mid-rise, and the I-95 distribution belt.
The biggest emerging zone for supply chain and logistics opportunities is the Hyundai Metaplant America-adjacent supplier corridor in Bryan County.
source_title: Best Vending Machine Locations in Savannah, GA: 2026 Operator Guide

facts: LG Energy Solution Ltd. and General Motors Co. will spend 2.7 trillion won ($2.4 billion) to build their second US electric vehicle battery plant in Tennessee.
The new Tennessee plant should double their EV battery output in the country.

Ultium Cells LLC, the joint venture between LG Energy Solution and General Motors, aims to complete the new Tennessee plant with an annual production capacity of 35-gigawatt hours (GWh) by the first half of 2024.
LG Energy Solution and General Motors are already working on a $2.3 billion EV battery plant in Lordstown, Ohio.
The Ohio plant has an annual production capacity of 35 GWh and is set to be completed by the second half of this year.
The combined 70 GWh capacity of the Ohio and Tennessee plants will be enough to charge around 1 million high-performance, all-electric EVs that run for more than 500 km on a single charge.

LG Energy Chief Executive Kim Jong-hyun stated at an April 16 event announcing the new plant that they will put efforts into building a stable US supply chain from R&D to raw materials procurement to battery production.
Kim Jong-hyun added that LG Energy and GM will contribute to the success of the US Green New Deal policies together.
LG Energy became the latest EV battery maker and global automaker to make new investments in the US, aligning with President Joe Biden’s green transportation policy.
Early last week, LG Energy reached a settlement with domestic rival SK Innovation Co. to end a two-year US legal dispute over trade secret infringement and battery-related patent violations.
Under the settlement agreement, SK Innovation will pay 2 trillion won to LG Energy, consisting of one trillion won in cash compensation and one trillion won in royalties.
SK Innovation is currently constructing two battery plants in Georgia that will supply EV batteries to Ford Motor Co. and Volkswagen AG.
The first SK Innovation plant in Georgia is set to begin operations in the first quarter of next year, with the second plant scheduled for mass production in 2023.

Since being named the sole battery supplier for GM's first mass-produced EV, the Chevrolet Volt, in 2009, LG has provided batteries for GM's flagship models, including the Chevrolet Spark and Chevrolet Bolt EV.
LG Energy's other clients include Tesla, Volkswagen AG, Hyundai Motor Co., and Kia Corp.
On April 16, SK Innovation announced a joint development of hybrid electric vehicle batteries for Hyundai Motor Co. and Kia Corp.
source_title: GM, LG to build second US EV battery plant in Tennessee - KED Global

facts: Kubota’s newest facility will consist of engineering offices, workshops, and testing buildings, as well as large-scale grading and development of several outdoor test courses for turf, utility vehicles, tractors, and other construction and agricultural-related equipment.
The facility is the fourth and final link in what will complete the global chain of Kubota R&D facilities in Japan, Thailand, Europe, and now North America.
The new facility enables Kubota to realize its goal to have products under development 24 hours per day for markets around the world.
Haruyuki (Harry) Yoshida is President and CEO of Kubota North America and Kubota Tractor Corporations.
The investment in this new facility marks a giant leap into a new era of growth and success in North America for Kubota.
The facility will make it possible to design new products in Georgia, manufacture them there, and sell these new products all across the country.
The equipment manufacturing industry is a core pillar of the Peach State’s economy.
The equipment manufacturing industry supports nearly 39,000 jobs for hardworking Georgians.
The equipment manufacturing industry has a $4-billion economic impact each year in Georgia.
Governor Kemp joined Kubota Tractor Corporation for the groundbreaking of their newest facility as they expand their footprint in Georgia.
Governor Kemp thanked Kubota for their continued commitment to creating opportunity for the people of Georgia.
source_title: Georgia Governor Joins Kubota for Gainesville Facility Groundbreaking - Dixie Contractor

facts: FOTON has established major logistics centers in China and overseas, featuring advanced warehousing systems and efficient global parts distribution.
FOTON partners with Daimler, Cummins and ZF to produce high-quality trucks, diesel engines and advanced gearbox systems globally.
FOTON IBLUE develops EV, hybrid and hydrogen commercial vehicles with core battery, motor and ECU technologies across multiple models.
Foton Motor was founded on August 28th, 1996, and is headquartered in Beijing, China.
Foton Motor's business scope covers a full series of commercial vehicles including medium and heavy-duty trucks, light-duty truck, mini truck, vans, pickup, bus, and construction machinery vehicles.
Foton Motor has become a leading commercial vehicle manufacturer in the world.
Foton Motor’s new energy technology leads the world, especially in electricity and hydrogen fields.
FOTON Cummins and Aucan offer stylish design with powerful performance for both work and family use.
The company meets diverse passenger transport needs while supporting cargo and specialized transportation solutions.
FOTON provides world-class logistics products and comprehensive solutions for urban and regional transportation.
Efficient mini trucks are designed for urban last-mile and light cargo transportation.
Products are powered by FOTON’s Super Power Chain, providing efficient solutions for refrigerated transport, dumping, hazardous goods and municipal operations.
The company provides customized products and solutions for infrastructure and Belt and Road engineering projects.
FOTON matches the power of Futian Conominus and Oukang to meet the needs of work and family cars.
The company addresses multi-scene passenger car needs while taking into account freight and special categories, providing multi-level passenger solutions.
FOTON delivers world-class quality and logistics products for urban trunk lines and branch line logistics transportation products and services.
To meet urban end micro-freight needs, Dora quickly runs to achieve efficient operation, with micro cards being the first choice.
The Futian Motor Super Power Chain Product Line meets needs for refrigerated transportation, dumping, dangerous chemical transportation, municipal sanitation, and road rescue.
The company meets customization needs of infrastructure engineering and provides product and service customization solutions for "Belt and Road" international engineering customers.
source_title: FOTON Commercial Vehicles | Pickup, Truck, Van & EV Supplier

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Kia will invest more than $200 million and create nearly 200 new jobs at its West Point plant to accommodate assembly of the all-electric 2024 EV9 three-row SUV.
The production line transformation will make the EV9 the fifth model assembled at Kia Georgia, joining the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs, and the K5 mid-size sedan.
EV9 assembly is expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Governor Kemp stated that Georgia's longstanding partnership with Kia has led to generational job creation and growth for the West Point area.
Governor Kemp noted that the project will bring more opportunity to hardworking Georgians and help propel the state toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
Currently, more than 40 percent of Kia vehicles sold in the U.S. are manufactured in West Point.
The addition of the EV9 to the lineup will further increase the facility's output.
The EV9 is the brand's first three-row EV SUV and the first Kia EV to be assembled in North America.
The EV9 serves as the flagship vehicle for Kia's global Plan S strategy focused on sustainable mobility leadership.
Sean Yoon, President & CEO of Kia Motors North America and Kia Motors America, stated that the EV9 has the potential to be another change catalyst for Kia like the Telluride.
Sean Yoon described the EV9 as the most innovative vehicle Kia has ever built and a standout in the EV market and on the road.
Kia Georgia is a certified Georgia Made manufacturer.
Kia Georgia will continue to utilize the services of Georgia Quick Start, the nation's top-ranked workforce development program, which operates the Kia Georgia Training Center.
Georgia Quick Start's comprehensive, customized training services will prepare new employees with the skills required to perform successfully on the new assembly line.

Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, stated that the Kia Georgia team looks forward to continuing its tradition of being the best of the best as the plant begins production of its first EV.

Stuart Countess highlighted partnerships developed statewide with the Governor's Office, the Department of Economic Development, and Georgia QuickStart, as well as local partnerships with the City of West Point and Troup County.
City of West Point Mayor Steve Tramell stated that Kia has been an outstanding corporate citizen and community partner for seventeen years and looks forward to a strong future together.
Mayor Tramell noted that by providing employment opportunities and fostering economic growth, Kia has helped improve the livelihoods of many people in West Point.
Senior Regional Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce team on this competitive project.

The project involved partnerships with the West Point Development Authority, the City of West Point, Troup County, Georgia Center of Innovation's Manufacturing and Energy teams, and Georgia Quick Start.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that since Georgia recruited Kia in 2006, Kia has exceeded expectations as a partner by giving back to its adopted community.
Commissioner Wilson thanked Kia, partners in West Point, and state and local agencies for working together to meet the needs of today and tomorrow's leading industries.
To date, Kia has invested more than $1.9 billion in Georgia.
Kia Georgia is responsible for more than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region.

Earlier this year, Governor Kemp and state lawmakers recognized January 31 as "Kia Day" to commend Kia's vehicle manufacturing and impact since the 2006 announcement of a state-of-the-art vehicle assembly facility in West Point.
The 2006 announcement marked one of the biggest single foreign investments in Georgia's history.
From Georgia, Kia ships vehicles to sales markets around the globe, including the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, Middle East, and Pacific nations.
Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire EV supply chain, resulting in more than $22.7 billion in investment and the creation of over 28,400 jobs since 2020.
source_title: KIA to invest more than $200M, hire nearly 200 as it starts ...

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and its affiliate suppliers announced a $5.54 billion investment five months prior to the article's publication date of 25 October 2022.
The project includes plans to produce a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia electric vehicles (EVs) alongside a new battery manufacturing facility.
HMGMA will create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years.
The company aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV batteries and other EV components in the U.S. market through this project.
HMGMA's new smart factory is expected to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated that Hyundai and the people of Georgia share qualities including respect for history, ingenuity, creativity, and determination to improve the next generation's world.

Euisun Chung noted that Hyundai's EVs are recognized as best in class and expressed determination to become a global leader in electrification, safety, quality, and sustainability through this partnership.
Euisun Chung added that with the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the company will evolve beyond an automaker into a leading global mobility solutions provider.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that the state's economy is reaching new heights due to record jobs, investment, workforce development programs, and infrastructure benefiting Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp said the partnership with Hyundai Motor Group and the groundbreaking of this innovative facility exemplify Georgia's unprecedented economic success.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed anticipation for continuing the partnership and observing its positive impact on the local community and the entire state.
Approximately $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
Hyundai Motor Group aims to lead the U.S. auto market's EV transition with the new facility.
The Group has a global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030 as part of its accelerated electrification efforts.
To achieve its sales target, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network ensuring a stable worldwide supply of EVs.
With additional U.S. EV and battery production capabilities, the Group aims to maintain its position as one of the top three EV providers in the United States.
The Group plans to lead the EV market through advancements in design, technologies, and mobility solutions optimized for the electric mobility era.
The investment aligns with the U.S. government's roadmap to accelerate electrification, coinciding with other global OEMs expanding local U.S. EV production.
Last year, the U.S. government announced an initiative requiring zero-emission vehicle sales to account for at least half of OEMs' new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
The Georgia facility will operate as an intelligent manufacturing plant.
All production processes, including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production, will be optimized using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers.
As part of its sustainability commitment, the plant will primarily rely on renewable energy sources and use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.
In addition to job creation, HMG announced new connections with the local economy to enrich community health through corporate social responsibility programs.
Corporate social responsibility donations to date total $1.54 million.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group breaks ground on Metaplant America dedicated to EV and battery production

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Battery Recycling Grants
canonical_name: DOE Battery Materials Processing and Battery Manufacturing and Recycling Programs Funding Round
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy has announced over $3 billion in grants for 25 projects meant to help boost domestic production of batteries and battery materials.
Several lithium-ion battery recycling projects are part of the new funding round.
American Battery Technology Co. received a grant of $150 million as part of the awardees with recycling projects.
Cirba Solutions received a grant of $200 million as part of the awardees with recycling projects.
Clarios Circular Solutions received a grant of $150 million as part of the awardees with recycling projects.
This funding round is the second from the DOE’s Battery Materials Processing and Battery Manufacturing and Recycling Programs.
The agency expects to invest a total of $16 billion from the two DOE programs to fund battery recycling, processing and manufacturing initiatives.
The grants are funded through the $1 trillion infrastructure law passed in 2021.
The most recent round of DOE grants continues a long-term effort to bolster the country’s supply chain for critical materials contained in batteries.
The Biden administration says such research and investment is important to reduce reliance on other countries.
A prior round of DOE funding offered $1.82 billion for 14 projects.
American Battery Technology Co. plans to use its $150 million grant to help construct a new commercial scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in South Carolina.
The South Carolina facility is expected to process about 100,000 metric tons of battery materials a year from battery manufacturers and automotive partners.
The South Carolina facility will produce “battery grade” minerals including nickel, cobalt, manganese, copper, aluminum, and graphite.
The South Carolina site will be the second commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility for American Battery Technology Co.

American Battery Technology Co.'s first facility is a battery recycling facility near Reno, Nevada, which opened in the fall of 2023 and had also received grants from the DOE and from car manufacturers.

CEO Ryan Melsert stated that the company is greatly appreciative of the confidence and support received from partners and is energized to move forward with feedstock supply, product offtake, and strategic stakeholders in this critical expansion of domestic capabilities.

Other battery manufacturers and recyclers will use an infusion of DOE funding to advance recycling plans, upgrade plants or start new partnerships for recycling specific materials needed in battery manufacturing.

Clarios Circular Solutions plans to spend its $150 million grant for retrofitting its existing South Carolina facility to be able to recycle lithium-ion battery production scrap into components needed for new batteries.
Clarios Circular Solutions expects the retrofitted South Carolina facility to have an annual output of 20,000 metric tons per year.
Cirba Solutions’ $200 million grant is expected to help it build a facility in South Carolina meant to process over 60,000 tons of batteries annually.
Li Industries will spend $55.2 million to convert a former manufacturing plant into a recycling plant in partnership with General Motors.
Ascend Elements received $125 million to advance its recycled graphite production facility in Kentucky, which it says is the first facility of its kind.

Blue Whale Materials received $55.2 million for a recycling facility in Oklahoma meant to process EVs, consumer lithium-ion batteries and battery scrap for further downstream recovery of cobalt, nickel and lithium at metal refineries.
source_title: DOE’s $3B funding round includes battery recycling projects | Waste Dive

facts: Battery recycling and manufacturing company Ascend Elements (formerly known as Battery Resourcers) is planning to open a battery recycling plant in Georgia.
The facility is scheduled to open by August of 2022.
The plant will recycle lithium, cobalt, and nickel.
source_title: Ascend Elements to open battery recycling facility in Georgia | GoElectricDrive - Accelerate the Good, Powered by EDTA

facts: In July 2024, the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) intended to solicit responses to design, construct, install, finance, operate, and maintain electric vehicle charging infrastructure compliant with the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Program.
Georgia's NEVI Deployment Program: Round 2 identified 33 locations in a Request for Proposal (RFP).
The installation of electric vehicle charging infrastructure at these 33 locations will fill gaps in the state's Alternative Fuel Corridors charging station network.
source_title: NEVI Example RFIs & RFPs – EV States Clearinghouse

facts: EnviroSpark won a National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) funding award from Georgia to build eight DC fast chargers.
Four of the new DC fast chargers will be installed at a Waffle House in Tifton, Georgia.
The remaining four DC fast chargers will be installed at a Shell station in Metter, Georgia.
EnviroSpark secured two of the five eligible sites awarded in Phase 1 of the state’s NEVI awards.
All eight stations will be hosted on EnviroSpark's national charging network.
Construction and installation of the chargers will begin later this year.
The project marks the second collaboration between EnviroSpark and Waffle House.
In February, the Tennessee DOT selected EnviroSpark to build stations at a Waffle House in Lakeland, Tennessee.
Stephanie Luque, co-founder of EnviroSpark who also leads the company’s federal initiatives, stated they are honored to partner again with Waffle House.
Luque noted that NEVI-funded projects represent huge steps toward reducing drivers’ range anxiety and moving closer to mass EV adoption.
According to GDOT, Georgia is among the top 10 states for EV sales.
GDOT reported that three of the top five largest pending investments in the growing EV supply chain are in Georgia.
Georgia has around 1,200 public charging stations.
Only 14 stations currently meet both the distance and power requirements under federal rules (minimum of four 150 kW ports and located within one mile of an Alternative Fuel Corridor).

Andrew Heath, Georgia DOT Deputy Chief Engineer, stated that the Georgia NEVI Deployment Program outlines the state’s approach to using federal funding to address EV infrastructure gaps in Alternative Fuel Corridors.
Heath noted that Georgia is turning to the private sector to deliver innovative infrastructure at strategic locations across the state through public-private partnerships.
source_title: Charged EVs | EnviroSpark wins Georgia NEVI awards to install DC fast chargers at Waffle House and Shell station - Charged EVs

facts: A Shell station in Metter, Georgia, will soon be home to four new DCFC electric-vehicle chargers from EnviroSpark.
EnviroSpark is building the chargers using National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) funding awarded by the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT).
Shell is No. 45 on CSP’s 2023 Top 202 ranking of U.S. convenience-store chains by total number of company-owned retail outlets.
EnviroSpark won two of the five open Phase 1 eligible sites for Georgia's NEVI deployment.
EnviroSpark was the only company selected to build more than one location in the first round of funding of Georgia’s NEVI deployment.
In addition to Houston-based Shell, EnviroSpark will also build four chargers at a Tifton, Georgia, Waffle House.
Atlanta-based EnviroSpark will begin construction and installation of the eight NEVI-compliant DC fast chargers later this year.

Stephanie Luque, co-founder of EnviroSpark who leads the company’s federal initiatives, stated that collaborating with Waffle House, the Metter Shell station, and GDOT exemplifies all parties’ shared commitment to driving innovation and sustainability in ways that benefit drivers and communities.
Stephanie Luque noted that NEVI-funded projects like these represent huge steps toward reducing drivers’ range anxiety and moving us closer to mass EV adoption.
Georgia is among the top 10 states for EV sales, according to GDOT.
Three of the top five largest investments in the EV supply chain are in Georgia, according to GDOT.
Georgia has approximately 1,193 publicly available EV charging stations.

Only 14 stations have DCFC ports that meet both the distance and power requirements under federal rules (minimum of four 150 kilowatt ports and located within one mile of an Alternative Fuel Corridor).

Andrew Heath, deputy chief engineer at GDOT, stated that the Georgia NEVI Deployment Program outlines the state's approach to using our federal funding to address EV infrastructure gaps in our state’s Alternative Fuel Corridors.

Andrew Heath noted that in order for the program to be successful, GDOT is turning to the private sector to deliver innovative infrastructure at strategic locations across the state through public-private partnerships.
The Georgia NEVI Deployment Program is funded by the 2021 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law enacted as the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The program provides nearly $5 billion nationally over five years, which started in the fall 2022.
Georgia’s allotment from the formula program is approximately $135 million to develop its portion of the national network.
Shell serves about 8 million customers per day with a brand presence at approximately 12,000 fueling stations across 49 states.
Globally, Shell serves around 32 million customers per day at mobility sites for quality fuels, EV charging and convenience-store and non-fuel products and services.
source_title: Georgia Shell Station Awarded 4 EV Charging Stations From NEVI Funding

facts: The State of Georgia is seeking reimbursement for the legal fees it incurred in multiple citizen lawsuits concerning Rivian’s $5 billion factory in Atlanta, arguing that the litigation was merely intended to delay the construction process.
Local media outlet The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported on Tuesday that the case returns to the Morgan County court this Wednesday, with the first hearing on the state’s request.
The government, joined by several counties’ local development authorities, is seeking over $540,000 from the six landowners who sued the State last year.
According to several First Amendment advocates cited by the outlet, this is an unprecedented sum of money in a lawsuit between government agencies and citizens.
In early 2024, a Morgan County judge dismissed the lawsuit, where citizens argued that the state of Georgia was taking over the property to get around local zoning rules and land disturbance permits.
The plaintiffs stated that their lawsuits raised legitimate legal concerns and that the reimbursement request constituted “government intimidation.”
Attorney John Christy stated: “It’s really to put the fear of God into these poor citizens to sit down, shut up and stop bothering us,” and “And if you don’t, we’re going to squash you like a bug.”

The Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties wrote in an op-ed that while citizens have the right to voice concerns, it is not a legitimate exercise of that right when the legal system is weaponized to block a perfectly legal project.
The JDA added that lawsuits filed by plaintiffs were designed to delay progress, costing taxpayers and the community.
According to Georgia law, governments may pursue litigation costs when lawsuits are deemed “frivulous” — in other words, when it has no legal merit. It is up to the judge to evaluate that.

Kara Murray, spokesperson for Georgia’s Attorney General Chris Carr said: “This wasn’t a legitimate challenge to a public matter,” adding that it was an attempt to delay economic development by filing several meritless lawsuits against the State, costing taxpayer dollars.
The state is seeking to recover attorney fees as part of its responsibility to manage public funds. The recovered money will be used to support education, infrastructure and public services.
The lawsuit dismissed last year was not the first time the landowners brought Rivian plant’s zoning before court, since plans for the factory were announced in late 2021.
The Georgia state and the JDA assumed the project site in early 2022.
As local zoning rules do not typically apply to government-owned land, critics said that by doing this, the state was averting a potentially challenging rezoning vote.
In 2022, the citizens in Morgan County asked the court to issue a stop-work order to halt site grading, which was refused.
The judge cited a clear “attempt not to address irreparable harm, but to stop the contruction of the Rivian production plant.”

The group later filed a similar lawsuit in Fulton County. In 2023, Fulton County Superior Court Judge Thomas Cox Jr. ruled the lawsuit frivolous and unlikely to succeed, ordering the plaintiffs to prepay nearly $365,000 to continue the case.
Although the plaintiffs’ obligation to prepay was later overturned on appeal, the lawsuit’s zoning claims were ultimately dismissed by the court as well.
Rivian said back in 2021 that the Atlanta factory would employ 7,500 workers.
The project was set to be completed in 2024, however construction was delayed.

The new facility is expected to take approximately 36 months to build, with partial operations projected to begin in the third quarter of 2027 and sales of its production expected to begin in late 2028.
CEO RJ Scaringe said earlier this year the plant is set to “provide an additional 400,000 units of annual capacity for R2 and R3 once fully built out.”
Last month, the Irvine-based EV maker announced it will establish a new East Coast headquarters in Atlanta, Georgia, as part of its broader investment in the state.
The new office will open in late 2025 and expand further in 2026 alongside construction progress at the factory.
Rivian expects to employ about 100 people at the new site by the end of next year and up to 500 once the headquarters is fully operational.
source_title: Georgia Gov. Seeks $540K Compensation in Rivian Plant Lawsuits | EV

facts: Rivian expects to achieve its first positive gross profit in Q4 2024.
Rivian delivered 14,183 vehicles in the final three months of 2024, bringing the annual total to 51,579.
The delivery total met the company's full-year guidance of 50,500 to 52,000 vehicles.
Rivian delivered slightly more than the 50,122 vehicles it delivered in 2023.
A supply shortage beginning in Q3 caused Rivian to cut its 2024 full-year production target to 47,000 to 49,000 vehicles, down from an original 57,000.
Rivian produced 49,476 units at its Normal, IL plant in 2024, topping its revised target.
Deliveries and production include the R1S, R1T, and electric delivery and commercial vans.
The previously discussed shortage of a shared component on the R1 and RCV platforms is no longer a constraint on production.
CFO Claire McDonough told analysts Rivian expects "a modest gross profit" in the final three months of 2024.
The positive gross profit will be mainly driven by regulatory credit sales, lower costs thanks to plant upgrades and improved supply contracts, and other revenue outside vehicle sales.
Rivian's net loss fell to $1.1 billion in Q3, with a gross profit loss of $392 million.
The company lost around $39,000 on each vehicle delivered in Q3, a drastic improvement from losing over $139,000 per unit in 2022.
Including a $1 billion convertible note from Volkswagen, Rivian ended Q3 with $6.7 billion in cash and equivalents.
Wall Street expects Rivian to post Q4 revenue of $1.4 billion, up from $1.3 billion in Q4 2023, and a loss of $0.68 per share compared to a loss of $1.36 per share last year.
Rivian launched a new joint venture with Volkswagen worth up to $5.8 billion, which CEO RJ Scaringe called a "meaningful financial opportunity."
Chief Software Officer Wassym Bensaid stated that other OEMs are now "knocking on our door" about similar supply deals for EV tech and software.
Rivian is preparing to launch its mid-size electric SUV, the R2, early next year.
The R2 will initially be built at Rivian's Normal, IL facility, but production is expected to significantly expand with plans to open a second plant in Georgia.
The R2 will start at around $45,000, or nearly half the cost of the current R1T ($71,700) and R1S ($77,700).
Rivian will also build a smaller, more affordable R3 crossover and high-performance R3X at the Georgia facility.
Rivian plans to build the Georgia plant in two stages, each adding 200,000 units of annual production capacity.
Rivian says the R2 and R3 are "critical drivers in the company's long-term growth and profitability."
Rivian secured a $6.6 billion federal loan for the new EV plant just before Donald Trump took office.
The funding is now in jeopardy after the Administration announced plans to freeze federal loans.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp told Channel 2 news that Rivian "secured that loan at the tail end of the Biden administration and, you know, I think there's no secret that the Trump administration is taking a look at all those things," adding, "So I don't really know where that stands right now."
Rivian is confident the funds will be there next year when they go to draw them.
A Rivian spokesperson said the company is "working hard to onshore US manufacturing, providing thousands of American jobs here in Georgia."
Rivian's stock is down 12% over the past 12 months and 90% from its all-time high shortly after going public in November 2021.
source_title: Rivian (RIVN) eyes first positive gross profit in Q4 earnings

facts: Rivian secured up to $6.6 billion in funding for its second EV manufacturing plant.
The new facility will be home to Rivian’s more affordable midsize R2 electric SUV.
Rivian announced it closed its loan agreement with the US Department of Energy (DOE) for up to $6.6 billion in financing for the new EV plant in Georgia.
Rivian has already begun hiring for construction and management positions in the area.
Several other positions are open across the state.
Recruitment is expected to ramp quickly as it prepares to begin construction on the facility.
Rivian’s loan will be split into two phases.
The first phase is expected to include a loan of up to $3.4 billion.
The remaining $2.6 billion will come in the second phase.
CEO RJ Scaringe said the loan will help accelerate the launch of the Georgia plant for R2 and R3, providing thousands of jobs in the state.
Construction is expected to begin next year.
In 2028, Rivian plans to start producing customer vehicles.
The Biden administration announced a conditional commitment in November of up to $6.57 billion to finance the development and construction of the new GA facility.
The financing is part of the DOE’s Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing (ATVM) Loan Program.
The ATVM program provides loans for manufacturing ATVs and related materials that improve fuel economy in the US.
With Trump taking office, the program will likely be challenged again.
In his first term, Trump already asked Congress to remove it.
The incoming administration is now looking to alter it to finance fossil fuels.
In November, Rivian said the loan would provide significant funding for production of the company’s midsize platform.
The midsize platform underpins the R2, a midsize SUV, and R3/R3X, a midsize crossover.
Rivian’s upcoming R2 and R3 vehicles will be critical drivers in the company’s long-term growth and profitability.
Rivian plans to build the plant in two stages.
Each stage will add 200,000 units of annual production capacity.
The site is 1,744 acres and located 40 miles east of Atlanta.
The site consists of 44 parcels of land.
Through 2030, the company is expected to create around 7,500 jobs.
Another 2,000 construction jobs will be established in the region.
Rivian unveiled the midsize R2 and R3 models last March.
Starting at $45,000, the R2 is nearly half the cost of an R1S and R1T.
A filing with the DOE in October revealed Rivian planned to begin operations in GA in Q3 2027.
Full capacity was not expected until 2028 according to the filing.
According to the filing, Rivian could break ground as soon as Q2 2026.
The first shop is expected to be ready by the third quarter of next year.
The DOE said Rivian’s new plant, which will produce 400,000 EVs annually, would help reduce overall national emissions of air pollutants and human-caused GHGs.
Last year, Rivian produced just under 49,500 vehicles at its Normal, IL plant.
Since reporting third-quarter earnings in November, Rivian’s stock is up over 40%.
RIVN share prices are still down around 20% over the past year and over 90% from their peak in November 2021.
The announcement is expected to be finalized at any moment.
The article was updated on 1/16/25 at 5:00 pm to include Rivian’s official announcement, which includes specific details and comments from CEO RJ Scaringe.
source_title: Rivian (RIVN) secures billions in US funding for new EV plant

canonical_name: Global Automotive OEMs and Tier 1/2/3 Suppliers
facts: The automotive industry in North America has rebounded significantly with new models hitting showroom floors and sales ticking upward.
OEMs are concerned about whether their suppliers can keep up with increasing demand.
New car sales in the U.S. were on pace to exceed 15 million in 2013, according to an IHS SupplierBusiness editorial.
North American vehicle production was expected to exceed 16 million in 2013 and 17 million in 2015, per IHS Automotive.
Michael Robinet, managing director for IHS Automotive Consulting, forecasted about 135 vehicle launches per year through 2016.
Vehicle launches averaged 85 per year in 2010 and 2011, compared to the projected 135 annually through 2016.
Suppliers face significant pressure due to a strained supply base, with potential for launch delays if they cannot handle the pace.
Many suppliers are reluctant to expand capacity due to fears of another automotive fiscal cliff.
Larger Tier 1 suppliers are responding to OEM demands for expanded capacity and capabilities, while smaller Tier 2 and 3 suppliers struggle to maintain their presence.
Smaller suppliers face increasing difficulty as a small group of huge global suppliers secure larger contracts from OEMs.
Large global Tier 1 suppliers possess the financial resources to meet OEM demands for both capacity expansion and enhanced R&D capabilities.
OEMs are demanding increased supplier investment in research and development of new technologies and international facilities.
International Automotive Components (IAC) Group, a large global Tier 1 supplier with over 90 manufacturing locations, developed new plastics processing technologies for vehicle interiors.
IAC provides instrument panel solutions including air distribution ducts, air outlets, glove boxes, decorative appliqués, and other trim components.
IAC developed unique surface materials like SprayPUR, TAC 2, and FastCast to meet consumer demand for comfort and craftsmanship.
Smaller suppliers unable to provide high-level R&D or meet capacity demands are becoming targets as OEMs consolidate their supplier pools.
Ford allocated 65% of its global purchasing to companies on its Aligned Business Framework list of 104 preferred suppliers in the prior year, up from 55% in 2010.
Ford aims to continue increasing this share while reducing its total number of suppliers from 1,250 to 750.
OEMs are requiring suppliers to accept shared responsibility for potential product liability, warranty, and recall claims.
The emphasis on shared responsibility has extended from automakers down through all three tiers of the supply chain.
Mold manufacturers face increased risk of being dragged into product liability lawsuits due to today's consumer advocacy climate.
Tier 1 suppliers are questioning whether their Tier 2 and 3 suppliers are financially viable to cover warranty exposures or claims.
Most suppliers were running three crews or shifts to meet current demand, leaving little slack in the system.
Rapid supplier expansion carries pitfalls, including compromised part quality that could lead to increased product liability claims.
Tier 2 and 3 suppliers must focus on reducing risk within their automotive customer base as OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers work to mitigate supply chain risks.
source_title: Automotive OEMs tell suppliers to be prepared to play, or go home

facts: Scott Ledford is the owner of Commerce, Georgia-based waste and recycling company Metro Site Fabricators.
Metro Site Fabricators received a $31 million settlement from SK Battery America Inc. after a 16-month litigation process.
SK Battery America Inc. is an electric vehicle battery manufacturer that operates a north Georgia factory.
The settlement was awarded following the improper disposal of batteries that sparked a devastating fire at Metro Site's material recovery facility.
Ledford shared his story at C&D World in January, and Waste Today reported on the fire in its March issue.
Metro Site lost its entire facility after the lithium-ion battery fire.
Investigators believe the blaze started after a drop-off of more than 200 batteries short-circuited upon contact, causing thermal runaway.
Ledford alleged SK Battery mishandled the batteries because Metro Site is not permitted to handle batteries among its recyclables and did not know it had received them.
SK Battery America Inc. is a subsidiary of Korean conglomerate SK Group.
SK Battery did not admit liability as part of the settlement but agreed to pay $20 million in punitive damages and $11 in tort-related claims and damages to Metro Site.
Before the fire, Metro Site had segregated waste received from SK from the rest of the waste in the facility.
Ledford stated that his team was able to identify lithium-ion battery pouches as being manufactured from SK's facility, noting this is rare for identifying fire origins in recycling centers.
As part of the litigation process, Ledford pushed for a non-confidentiality agreement so he could discuss the case and help other operators prepare for fire hazards.
Ledford advises operators doing business with groups that deliver hazardous materials to hire an attorney to draft agreements covering responsibilities and liabilities.

He recommends that in the event of a lithium-ion battery fire, operators should secure all evidence, ensure it is handled by proper authorities, and quickly hire an attorney well-versed in this type of litigation.
Ledford noted he spoke with his attorney seven days a week for 16 months during the case.
Moving forward, Ledford plans to increase his visibility by speaking at waste industry meetings and conventions and making himself available to others with similar experiences.
source_title: Metro Site wins $31M settlement after lithium-ion battery fire - Construction & Demolition Recycling

facts: It is Hyundai Motor Group's first state-of-the-art U.S. smart auto manufacturing facility.
Production is scheduled to begin in late 2024.
The Metaplant will manufacture models from the Hyundai, Kia and Genesis brands.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA)  - Bryan County Chamber of Commerce

facts: Hyundai is chasing its fifth straight record sales year in the US and has bigger plans over the next few years.
New US auto tariffs and an ICE raid on its battery plant in Georgia have not swayed Hyundai from its plans to play a bigger role in the market.
Hyundai Motor’s CEO, Jose Munoz, stated during an interview with the Atlanta Business Chronicle that the company will increase production capacity in Georgia beyond what has already been announced.
Hyundai confirmed it remains committed to a previously announced $26 billion investment to boost US sales over the next four years.
Around $2.7 billion of the total investment will be funneled into Georgia, in addition to the $12.6 billion earmarked for the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and other facilities.
Hyundai plans to overcome US tariffs, which were recently lowered to 15% from 25%, by boosting local production, a strategy CEO Jose Munoz described as “very simple” and “localization.”
The remarks follow an ICE raid in September that detained over 300 South Korean workers at the construction site for a new Hyundai and LG Energy Solutions battery plant.
Hyundai’s investment in Georgia, the largest in state history, is expected to create around 40,000 jobs across manufacturing and battery plants, as well as all of its suppliers.
As production ramps up at HMGMA, Hyundai will continue hiring until it reaches full capacity.
Hyundai is working on multiple projects on different fronts, including robotics and AI.
Although initial plans called for the plant to exclusively produce EVs, Hyundai is expected to begin building hybrids in 2026.
Hyundai is taking actions to remain competitive and optimize costs by localizing both its production and its supply chain.
Through the first 11 months of 2025, Hyundai sold nearly 823,000 vehicles in the US, up 8% from the same period last year, putting it on track for a fifth consecutive record retail sales year.
Hyundai fully expects to achieve five consecutive record retail sales years (“5 for 5”) in 2025.
Hyundai is compensating for the loss of the $7,500 federal tax credit with some of the largest discounts among US automakers.
The IONIQ 5 electric SUV is among the most affordable EVs to lease in the US, starting at just $189 a month.
Hyundai builds the IONIQ 5 and the three-row IONIQ 9 at the HMGMA facility in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai is emerging as a dark horse in the US

facts: The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced that Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by economic development business publication Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that thousands of jobs were created across Georgia thanks to the work of economic development teams in all four corners of the state.
Kemp noted that GDEcD's efforts helped Georgia land record-breaking investments and hundreds of thousands of jobs for its communities.
Kemp attributed Georgia's continued status as No. 1 for business to a pro-business approach, an unmatched workforce, and world-renowned logistics.

Area Development’s annual Gold and Silver Shovel Awards recognize state economic development agencies that demonstrate excellence in creating a business-friendly environment, fostering innovation, and implementing strategies that successfully attracted high-quality investment projects.
All states were invited to submit information to Area Development on major groundbreakings, investments, and business expansions occurring in 2023.
Andy Greiner, Editor of Area Development Magazine, stated that the awards highlight the exceptional efforts of state economic development teams and their commitment to building strong economies.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted Georgia’s diversity of industries leading to success, specifically naming advanced manufacturing, aerospace, e-mobility, life sciences, and fintech as instrumental in achieving a Shovel Award again.
Wilson reflected on back-to-back record-breaking years and expressed anticipation for celebrating upcoming milestones with project partners.
Wilson congratulated Governor Kemp, the state legislature, the state economic development team, and all partners across Georgia for their dedication to creating long-term community opportunities.

links.context: Information on 2024 Gold and Silver Shovel Awards

source_title: Georgia's New Business Investment - The ATL Airport Chamber

facts: Over the past six years, solar, energy storage, and electric vehicles have grown at an exponential rate.
Renewable energy technologies provide economic advantages by creating jobs and using primarily indigenous resources, allowing most energy dollars to stay in the country.
Arkansas' economic potential is increasing because of transportation electrification.
Electric vehicles are laying the groundwork for a strong Arkansas EV economy by lowering the cost of ownership and operation, and benefit from some of the lowest electric rates in the country.
Electric vehicle charging serves as a cheaper alternative to fueling up at the pump.

Today's Power works with cities and utilities that strive to lead by example on behalf of their communities, demonstrating that promoting low-carbon projects such as solar and EV charging fosters clean energy, efficiency, good employment, investment, and environmental friendliness.
The recently passed infrastructure act will provide more Americans, particularly those in rural and underserved areas, with opportunities to benefit from the renewable energy economy.
Arkansas is set to receive approximately $54 million to build out a statewide network of EV chargers to support EV adoption across the state.

With ample charging infrastructure and the availability of larger EV models such as electric trucks and SUVs, utilization rates of EVs and other renewable energy technologies are expected to increase over the next few years.
The top two technological advances expected over the next five years are improved large-scale EVs and more cost-effective battery energy storage.
Personal EVs are grabbing market share, and commercial fleets and municipalities could rapidly follow suit.
The total cost of ownership (TCO) for electric delivery trucks could soon be on par with diesel-run trucks due to increasingly cost-competitive and available EV infrastructure.
E-buses have higher acquisition prices due to upfront battery costs but offer a lower TCO due to independence from pricey diesel.
E-buses eliminate local particulates, including SOx, NOx, and CO2, which are major issues in most cities today.
The new era of EVs has rapidly expanded the market for lithium and cobalt batteries while significantly lowering their cost.
Expanded battery markets are increasingly being used as energy-storage solutions by industry and utilities beyond just EVs.
Falling battery prices are proving useful in lowering power costs, increasing reliability and resiliency, and making power systems more flexible to operate.

links.context: Related article about Jennah Denney's appointment as EV Charging Infrastructure Manager

source_title: Economic Development 2022: Jennah Denney, Today’s Power

facts: The American arm of South Korean automaker Hyundai Motor Co. plans to commence production operations at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America by the fourth quarter of 2024.
The official opening of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America facility is targeted for the first quarter of 2025.
The Metaplant complex is currently under construction on a 3,000-acre site in Black Creek just south of I-16 East near Blitchton.
The Metaplant is projected to eventually employ 8,500 people on-site.
Parts suppliers building factories in the region, including some in Bulloch County, expect to employ almost 6,000 more workers.
Hyundai Motor Group projects that the Metaplant and off-site suppliers together will create a total of 14,476 jobs at full production.
HMG Metaplant America CEO and President Oscar Kwon stated during an October 2023 press tour that they were on track to start production in early 2025.
Hyundai Motor North America president and CEO Josè Muñoz attended the plant's groundbreaking ceremony in 2022 alongside Hyundai executives from South Korea and Gov. Brian Kemp.
On Monday, Muñoz joined Kemp and other elected state leaders in Atlanta for “Hyundai Day” at the state capitol.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group has been an incredible partner to the state and highlighted the historic benefits of the project for Georgians.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia, with further announcements expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and involve more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of the Metaplant.
Josè Muñoz stated that Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Four building exteriors at the Metaplant site are complete, and outfitting for internal operations is underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant, including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and other amenities for employee use.
Full-time Metaplant employees are being hired at a rapid pace for training and skills on machinery, office and clerical work, and numerous other plant jobs.
The Metaplant currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the current Metaplant workforce is from the greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 Metaplant employees are from Georgia but outside the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on Feb. 8.
The Metaplant has partnered with Ogeechee Technical College and other area technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, and Savannah Technical College.
source_title: EV production to begin Q4 2024 - Statesboro Herald

facts: HMGMA and Kia America celebrated the production of the Kia Sportage Hybrid on June 2, 2026.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated mass-production Electrified Vehicle plant.
The all-new manufacturing facility is located just outside of Savannah, Georgia.
The facility features a highly-connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production are optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system is designed to create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
The plant is designed to build more than 500,000 vehicles per year.
Production includes Hyundai’s luxury brand Genesis.
HMGMA is home to the award-winning IONIQ 5 and the IONIQ 9 three-row SUV.
HMGMA offers training opportunities to employees.
HMGMA is located approximately 25 miles from downtown Savannah in Bryan County, Georgia.
HMGMA welcomed a new fire truck with a traditional wet-down ceremony to strengthen on-site emergency response.
Savannah Technical College and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America commemorated a landmark apprenticeship partnership with an official signing ceremony.
source_title: HMGMA - Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America
